Mirror's Edge. The brightly colored,'EA'-esque, parkour Portal-substitute (as I would like to call it). It's as if EA/DICE got high and took the 1st person feel of Portal and mixed that with ♥♥♥♥ing Crayola markers and Parkour....and it worked. Mirror's Edge puts you into a very immersive story of Faith, a type of parkourer who now has to run from the law and save her sister as well as the 'parkour organization' she is with. I very highly recommend this game to you or really any gamer out there.

I definitely recommend this game! It is very original and fun to play, but the script/ending leave a lot to be desired. I'd give it a 6/10. Here are some more details:  Pros: The whole dimension-flipping mechanic is very unique and original, and frankly makes for some brilliantly designed puzzle levels and fun gameplay. If you liked Portal, then you will definitely like this game! The graphics are also well done and gives the whole game a fun, 'cartoony' atmosphere.  Cons: The talking will drive you insane. The writers clearly wanted to create the same witty, 'punny' sort of one-liners as in Portal, but its not funny at all, and the only voice actor in the game completely messes up the delivery. Moreover, this game will BABY you through every level. 'Look up there!' 'You're gonna want a battery here!' 'Maybe you should like heights, hint, hint' and yes they literally say 'hint, hint.' This gives you almost no sense of accomplishment after completing a level, and it made me feel like the developers thought I, as the player, had the intellect of a 1st grader. Also the ending is a huge buzzkill, big build up to nothing... All the same, this game was worth it to me for all the slow-down scenes :P

Dirt Showdown is in my opinion a bit underrated and really misunderstood by some people. They consider the game as the successor of Dirt 3. However, Showdown is more a spinoff, that has nothing to do with the other Dirt games besides the 'Dirt' name. That being sad, I think this game is really good in what is does. Just mindless 'demolition derby like' fun. The car handling is just right for this type of game in my opinion. It also has great graphics and sound. There's single player with a career mode, wich will keep you busy for a while. It has enough variety with many different gamemodes. And then there's another SP mode, called Joyride, were you have to complete a series of tricks and collect all the Showdown logo's. Well, if you want those achievements at least :) But the game really shines in multiplayer. Unfortunately, the MP isn't really active anymore. Often you find yourself in an empty lobby. The best idea is to look on the forum for people to play with. Because of this, I wouldn't recommend the game just for MP. But like I said, there's enough fun to be had in SP alone.

I ignored this game for a long time because the screenshots did not appeal to me. The screens do not give it justice. When playing the game the graphics work very well.  This is my 'go-to' game on iPad. It is one of the best games I have ever played. The way the game systems interact together and the multiple ways to upgrade and specialize your ship. Do you want a ship that hacks the enemy systems while you teleport your tough crew on board to take over? Do you upgrade your shields and pilot skills to defend while deploying drones to take out the enemy? Or load up on powerful missiles to just blow them up?   The frantic feeling of being a starfleet captain giving orders to put out the fire in the oxygen room, repair the weapons systems, heal the crew, fight off enemies boarding the ship is all done extremely well.  The game can be random and the final boss is insanely difficult. The randomness helps each game feel fresh and forces you to make different tactical dicisions. The final boss challenge makes it very rewarding when you actually get there and win.  If you like rogue lite / like / random generation games, if you like space, strategy, choose your own adventure, oregon trail style games. This is one of the best and has a ton of content to keep you busy for several hours.

I think that Half Life 2 is an immersive, visually stunning, and exicting first person shooter experience.   Valve did so many things right with this game, such as the wide variety on weapons with each one being unique in its own way, and I think that upgrading the weapon to a completly different weapon with the same basic idea, but with more power and more traits is a unique, well excuted concept.  The storyline does take work to follow, but it is still very immersive and very well written.  The controls are great and easy to learn, feeling very smooth and easy to execute.  This game has lots of little things that make the overall experience more enjoyable, like the vechials you aquire as you go through the game, which not only make the game faster, but fun to move around, and gives you the sense of freedom, and it is fun to gun down your enemies in the process.  Also I like how there are no real cutscenes throughout the game, but there is still a story, and when a crucial plot element is in progress, you get to freely move around and experience the part from all angles, something that I don't believe that many other games do.  And to put it bluntly, the game is fun as hell.  I would without a doubt reccomend this game, and is worth the nine doller price.

Thomas Was Alone  is one of the best games I have ever bought. It's masterful understanding of mechanics, and simplistic beauty is astounding. Also, the game has a LOT of levels, and so far seems to be very replayable.  Thomas Was Alone is a game that encourages the gamer to  think, but not in the 'AHH this is hard' way . If you can play portal or Half Life 2, then you can play this.  The characters themselves never appear to speak, but it doesn't seem to be needed. As the narrorator speaks, the characters come to life, without even having to change his speach.  Thomas Was Alone shows an amazing indie game, where it's noted that games do not need the highest graphics in the world, but only serve to tell a story.  10/10 I highly recommend this game.  *Notes: I bought this game on sale for ~$3. I would pay 25 dollars for this game if I had to.*

From the ability to disguise yourself as a certain faction bringing different reactions from characters in the game, to wielding a plethora of powerful lazer and plasma weapons, the possibilities are unreal. From manslaughtering a town, to rescuing one, to joining up with a certain faction to manslaughter, to joining a faction aimed at rescuing one. There are an infinite number of possibilites from traits to paths taken, there is really no limit. I haven't even done most questlines, usually I end up shooting the quest giver or the target before I need to at all. Casino wants me to give them MY guns? Wielding from an amazing, overpowering Super Hammer, to a plasma version of the Incinerator, there is no telling what kind of insane weaponry you are able to wield to overcome ANY enemy. This game is jam-packed with questions, answers, paths, choices, weapons, anything else an amazing game needs? I don't think so. This game was created by the gods of Bethesda, and should be played and bought by everyone in the ♥♥♥♥♥♥♥ solar system, as long as the aliens have played this game I will gladly welcome them into my home.

If you enjoyed Fallout 3, you'll love Fallout: New Vegas. Think of it as Fallout 3 with a few popular PC mods and a defaulted orange UI. The good old 50s music is back, the setting is different and the world is still wrecked. However this game was made by the folks who worked on previous Fallout titles unlike Fallout 3. So the atmosphere is the first thing you notice is a LOT different. Instead of it being a destroyed, depressing and bleak atmosphere, it's a western styled desert. Where society is pretty much back on it's feet... Kinda. Enemies are still lurking all over the huge world, all of the same enemies in Fallout 3 can be found in Fallout: New Vegas and it's DLC.  If you played previous Fallout titles but didn't like Fallout 3, there's hope for you yet. As this game was made by a lot of the folks who worked on previous titles, all the easter eggs are there, the same old comedy and absolutely extraordinary quests you have to go on have returned.  So whether you're new to the Fallout series, a long time veteran of Fallout games or you just played Fallout 3, you'll find that this game is very enjoyable no matter how you look at it.  The world is large and full of content, you can literally play for hours on end and still not find everything in the game, there just is THAT much.  So get the best 'bang for your buck' (I'm English, we don't say that here in England) and get this game today, literally... You won't be disappointed.  WARNING: it's made on Bethesda's infamous Gamebryo engine (aka Creation Engine now) which is known for crashing, bugs and glitches, just save often and you'll be fine.

Riding virtual trucks turns out to be a surprisingly relaxing experience!  Unlike most simulations, this one looks polished and not outdated at all. The only point of the game is driving cargo from city A to city B and there is very little to do besides that. You can upgrade your truck and hire other people who you will never see in-game, but this gives little satisfaction. Driving, on the other hand, is very pleasant. Just watch out for traffic lights, speed limitations and other drivers, keep your lane and enjoy the soothing scenery!  My biggest problem is the diversity of road elements, buildings and cargo that you come across. Eventually you'll start noticing that some of them are duplicated across the whole map and that can somewhat shatter the illusion of a huge game world, though there was still enough diversity to keep me seeing something new once in a while after 30 hours of gameplay. Another issue is that the radio player in Linux is broken and the developer doesn't seem to care (though you can always play the stream you want from a browser).  In conclusion, it's a nice and relaxing game where you can forget your worries, turn off your brain for a while and chill behind the wheel. Just don't forget to tune in to your favourite podcast, or else you're risking to lose too many brain cells in the process.

This game is a shining example of how free-to-play all too often = pay-to-win. Eternally in 'Early Access', this game is yet another sad reason why Steam's 'Early Access' program is an overall failure.  Big money weapons are completely OP (e.g. M1/M2 Carbine, Thompson, etc). If you don't pay, good luck spending a few weeks with a catheter and hot pockets and without seeing the sun to earn them without spending cash.  Balancing classes and levels for both teams in skirmish games is a joke. Sometimes it will give more/better tanks to one side or give one side tons of paratroopers. It also doesn't balance player levels properly so newbies often get seal clubbed. It makes for imbalanced and frustrating gameplay if you are a newbie.  Also, the game is riddled with cheating. There are several cheating programs that you can easily find on the Internet which reveal the locations of all enemy players and puts boxes around them, making camoflage/cover almost useless and allowing you to always get the drop on an enemy. I've seen people with 20 or even 30 to 1 K/D in a game. As it is so easy to die in this game, I can imagine that those players were using these programs.  Overall, this game really isn't worth investing any serious time into unless you like pay-to-win games and have the cash to pay so you can win. If you're looking for a free-to-play game that doesn't bone you for not spending tons of cash, look elsewhere. This game is not for you.

(6 hours played, retrospective)  I was bait-and-switched into this game, but I'm okay with that.   Your character has to explore an unfamiliar mansion at night, during a thunderstorm, to uncover clues to the whereabouts of your missing family. On the surface, this sounds like a horror/thriller adventure, which is why I can see why many people are turned off by this game. However, I'm a fan of unexpected genre shifts when done right; like the way Alfred Hitchcock subverted Psycho from an embezzlement crime drama to a slasher film.  The similarities can be drawn further by recognizing the voyeuristic undertones of searching the house top to bottom for clues. You'll be raiding your parents' and sibling's personal effects to piece together their lives, as well as their wherabouts. Despite the moody atmosphere, you'll realize by the end that  MASSIVE SPOILER:    your family isn't in danger  . The ominous undertones still serves the purpose of creating a layer of tension, which helps motivate the player forward. I can't imagine the game being as compelling on the first playthrough without it.  With 'playing' in mind, I wouldn't consider this a game as much as an exploratory narrative in the same vein as Dear Esther - another polarizing, but critically acclaimed interactive story in it's own right. The more rewarding aspects of this game is finding objects that trigger a voiceover from the player's younger sister. Her voice is the only one you hear throughout the entire game, and her journey in establishing her identity during the tribulations of high school is captured beautifully.  Another thing I'd like to point out is the time period. It's set in 1995, since cell phones would mitigate this game, altogether. It also important to factor this in when you discover a central theme to the story. You'll know it when you see it. The importance didn't dawn on me until months after playing the game. This also probably means they were targeting a specific demographic of gamers in their 30's. I spent my formative years in the 90's, so this game gives me a fresh perspective on things I didn't have the maturity to realize back then.  All in all, I think this isn't a game for everyone, especially younger gamers. Otherwise, you'll have to play it yourself. Personally, I found it very engrossing.

An exellent, albeit linear, narrative constantly interrupted by flawed turn-based combat. The Banner Saga boasts great visuals and beautiful soundtrack, character design is spot-on, reminiscent of older cartoons, while still being true to the nordic theme and as historically accurate as a fantasy saga can be.  The flaws lie in the gameplay, particularly in the fact the game concentrates too much value on Renown. It's the currency with which you buy food, amulets and level up heroes. It's also not that easy to come by, since it's mostly rewarded from killing things and winning battles (although you, very rarely, gain some for a choice).  The game alternates between frequent combat sections, where you and the enemy alternate in turns, regardless of numerical advantage. Combined with the fact strength is both HP and how much damage you can deal, this system encourages the player to not kill oponnents, but, instead, harm them until they all have low HP, only then should the player start the killing.  The fact renown is used for everything also limits character choices and story decisions. You'll be encouraged to be a psycopath, partaking as many battles as possible to gain extra renown. To make matters worse, there are no real consequences for such choices.  Morale is almost meaningless, only awarding a few extra willpower points at the start of the battle. Supplies are also meaningless, seeing as fighters aside heroes have no effect on war outcome, as long as you win your battle (usually a charge, since it puts you against more enemies, which equals more renown), a team of 5 heroes could win a war against thousands of dredge.  Downed heroes come back with injury whoose duration and severity depend on game difficulty setting. Ironically, these same immortal heroes can die arbitraly during text-based events while travelling.  While I haven't been able to finish the game yet (always get bored by the game before being able to do so), I've seen users complain the story actually ends abruptely, leaving space for an obvious non-advertised sequel. The Banner Saga is actually just a first episode, despite not being advertised as such.  EDIT: I have finished the game on a new playthrough, and the ending is indeed abrupt, with a short (non animated) cutscene right after a rather easy boss fight (just ignore every dredge and focus on the boss). Even if it's part of a trilogy, a game must feature a relatively complete story (compare it to a movie such as 'The Hobbit: The Desolation of Smaug' and you can see how this kind of ending is unsatisfying). A bit more recently, the store page was updated to reveal banner saga as the first of a trilogy, despite that not being the case on release date.  To summarize: The Banner Saga has great visuals, soundtrack and (linear and relatively unfinished) story, but it's held short by bad gameplay which ranges from silly to frustrating, removing almost all immersion or tension the well written story can create.  If things don't get better in the sequel, I think stoic should give up on making games and start writting novels or animated short films.

This game is great fun, and easily addicting. I can confirm that, as I have hundreds of hours on record over the years it has been up (Steam playtime doesn't come close). There's so much depth for what looks like such a simple game, with 8 classes with genuinely different playstyles, dozens of companions (and counting) with very different attributes, a story that is engaging and funny at the same time (which is nowhere near done as of yet), and too many items to name, which is what wikis are for. The community is great and very helpful, like a little family as Marona said, and really gives the game the value it has.  It really is a fantastic game, with a friendly and open-to-discussion dev team and competitive and friendly player base. Whether you're fully engaged in boss battles with team mates, or passively grinding while doing something on the side, this game is 142% worth the money and time.

Binding of Isaac was one of the best games ever made.  Binding of Isaac Rebirth is less good but still decent. Definitely an improvement in technology for the gamplay and engine, but the addition of some many extra items makes a lot of the items you get a 'sabotage' item that ruins your playthrough. Likes:  -Graphics seem more uniform and polished. Weird, considering they're pixel now, but I find the team did a great job in translating the original vector flash graphics into a more stylized version of itself. It's probably not as smooth looking, but the little embellishments of background animations, extra details, and ambient animation and lighting give the game a bit more class versus just any old Flash game you'd get on the Internet.  -Challenging, but approachable. I still can't win at the original game (with or without DLC). I've won once in this version. It's not easier, I still have a ridiculous number of deaths, but there's something less daunting about the challenge of it. You can get further before you die.  -Fusion of old and new. A lot of the classic game, with a mixture of new room types, new bosses, and new items. A lot of the old has been re-balanced and refreshed, and some of it is still the same. So pretty cool to have a mixture.  -Controller support. No more Joy2Key required. Most BoI players swear by keyboard and mouse, but being able to use Xbox controller, and even having it on the PS Vita/PS4 has made the game much more accessible to me.  Dislikes:  -Vague about controls. The original game used to have the control scheme on the ground of the opening room. The instruction graphics have been retained, but not the actual controls to do each thing. Not a problem for old BoI players, pretty confusing for new people. Don't get me wrong, it's not a hard game to control, it's just kind of an unnecessary bit of initial confusion. For the controller it took some wasted bombs and items and peeking back into options to figure it out as well. Don't get me wrong, the controls themselve are great, but it'd be nice if they could have made that graphic on the ground adapt to what you were using.  -Womb filter is horrific The game uses a new system of visual filters for atmospheric effects. It's lovely for lighting, but one filter in the womb just drives me completely up the wall. It's a moving animation of blood cells in a blood stream moving in the background. It reminds me of eye floaters or a flourescent light flickering really badly. It kind of gives me a headache and makes me a little motion sick. It's a shame to have to turn off filters just because of that one thing, but trying to get through the womb with it going is really difficult.  -No Steam online play That's kind of a bummer for me, but I guess it's because they didn't feel the need to do so, what with player 2 controlling the familiars. But, it'd be nice if there were some Steam compatibility. Eh, well, I guess it's always been more of a single-player game. I just feel kind of sad that the seeds are really only to share an experience for someone else, as they don't contribute to achievements.

First of all, im reviewing the single player 'seasons' mode, as i have not played multiplayer. I was hesitant at first to buy this game, as an arcade style game for 20 dollars seemed like a bit too much, but i have to say, this game turned out to be much better than i thought. It runs smoothly with little to no lag, and the controls are smooth (Using a wired Xbox 360 Controller) and that is something that a game as chaotic as this one certainly needs. The customization options for your vehicle are very fun to play around with, and you can end up with some pretty crazy looking cars! And you unlock the items just by playing matches, which makes it very easy to get new items. The camera options are very convenient, Allowing you to change the FOV, Height, Angle, and distance of the camera. Now, for the actual game, the seasons mode is very fun and competitive, allowing you to pick from different teamates, and to choose how many cars are on the teams. 1v1, 2v2, 3v3, and 4v4 are the option. 4v4 is my favourite so far though, as it's rather chaotic. Possibly the best thing about seasons mode is that even by yourself you still get very competitive, and that's something that i dont think many games deliver on. The music is a bit garbled and im not sure whether or not its supposed to play all the time, as it usualy stops within a few secconds. Overall, for a game that was just released, it is very fun and i'd recommend it to everyone, whether they like sports or not. I think this game would be even more entertaining with friends, but i don't have any friends that own this game.

Lichdom: Battlemage is a decent, but monotonous game.  As I started playing my first reaction was: 'Why I can't jump!?'. It turns out - this game has no jumping mechanics. You can shoot fireballs, lightnings and other stuff, but you can't jump - it's to difficult for them mages.. I guess.  This game is very linear. Sometimes it has open spaces, but there is only one way to the main objective all the time. You can press a button to see the line where to go (that's always the wrong way if you want to explore the area and find its secrets ^^, but that's why I liked it, because you always know the right path + you will never miss something valuable if you go the other way).  The story is ... baaa... oook... decent enough. Nothing spectacular. The problem is how it is told. It's always in the same way - through some holograms from the (recent) past. Some dialogues/monologues are just dull and without any value. Everything feels so stretched out that in the end it just becomes really boring.  The graphics of this game is really good. Lighting, spell effects, enemy animations - everything is really well done (this actually saves the game from being bad).   Visually pleasant, but overall just ... empty game.  You can use 3 out of 8 different sigils (spell types) (can switch between them at checkpoints). Usually you can combo them for more efficient and faster killing. There is no loot in this game. You automatically collect all the 'items' ... a.k.a. augmentations from the enemies you kill. You can use those to upgrade or craft new and better spells. It can be a bit hard to understand how all this works, but this game has some automated crafting system, so you don't need to waste much time here if you don't want to.  The gameplay itself is very straightforward. All you have to do is kite all the time and juggle through 3 different buttons. In other words - shoot, run, shoot, run, shoot, run (and sometimes die because of stupid bugs (knockback + damaging aura combos from the enemies doesn't help either)).  This game has really stupid bugs, You can make the enemies teleport from one area to another (randomly) by using one of the spells, but sometimes it happens that they teleport outside of restricted area and you can't kill them anymore and then you can't progress any further. Then you have to go back to the main menu and restart the area and waste another 5-7 minutes doing the same thing you just did. Or you can fall off the stairs and get stuck between the wall and the stairs. Remember, that you can't jump in this game. Yeah. Time to reload once again...  I really liked those little hidden challenge areas. They each provide massive rewards but first you must fight through several waves of enemies and only then you will be able to collect all the loot. Most of them I managed to do in one go, but some are really challenging and you can die once or twice.  Oh, and this game has swamps too. Why so many games have swamps? I hate swamps. They should be deleted from the games forever. uhhh. But hey, it's linear so it's not THAT bad.  I only recommend this game if you are prepared to do the same thing over and over again for 20 hours and if you get this game on a big sale, otherwise - it's just a waste of your time and money.

Im just going to cover the zombie pvp modes because thats what I mainly play.  Zombie pvp modes were fine up until about 4-5 months ago until they decided to add Dna perks and allow you to upgrade to lvl 3. You're probably wondering what zombie Dna perks are. Well, basically you can use up to 9 of the 20 available most of which just increase your HP and armor, but one in particular really ruins zombie pvp modes that being on-hit-boost. This perk basically resets zombie abilities at a low chance. Not a very low chance if you ask me because i've used zombies with healing abilities and had it reset 6-7 times in a row healing thousands of hp in seconds and then infecting everyone.  Now besides Nexon making zombies even more powerful most rooms are just FULL of people using light zombies and sting fingers. Basically they're really mobile skinny ♥♥♥♥♥es that have broken hitboxes that most people love to use with bhop macros such as autohotkey making them almost impossible to hit.  One final thing I want to cover with zombie pvp modes is what I like to call 'Lag abusers' these are people that usually live in brazil or russia with dialup internet having 200+ ping and they teleport lag around the map infecting players from 2-4x the normal distance. Sometimes they even ignore knockback and stun from weapons.  With all of that said the only other thing I want to mention is the decoders. Now while I don't think this game is pay to win anymore due to zombies being extremely overpowered now if you actually want weapon variety be prepared to pay hundreds or thousands of dollars. The chances for some cool weapons such as the warhammer, reaper, etc are low like a 1 / 600 chance and with decoders being $5 a piece you can see how stupid that is. At least in CSO which is the eastern version of this game they pay $0.80 - $1.50 for a premium type decoder.

After ~ a decade abstinence and sick of teh two big FPS shooter franchises these days I gave Q3 a go again, as I remembered the fun I had with it at LAN-partys during the early 00's.  Well, the gameplay is faster than i remembered - You spawn, you get thrown into the fight, you die. The learning curve is pretty flat if you are new to the game, or were gone for a long time just like me. You have to learn the maps and item spawn points (again). You have to train your aiming and foresight. You have to train gameplay mechanics such as strafe- , circle- and rocketjumps to be fast and agile on the map. I still do train with bots, because I am pretty sure I still can't compete with real people.  Maybe this is a downer for new players: If you do not train, you get your ♥♥♥ beaten up pretty hard. And if you do, you still get yourself beaten up for a long time, even with bots. But this is rewarding at the same time. Once you get better you feel like you actually achieved something.  But I have one big problem: Almost every server shown by the ingame server browser is empty. Once the bots are no real threat anymore, you have to have friends playing it too. No possibility to join a random server in QuakeLive at least for me. Any useful advice on this is much appreciated.  From what I read in the forums the veterans are ♥♥♥♥♥♥ due to the steam-only conversion. As I did not play Quake Live actively before this, I don't care that much. I guess it's the same for new players, too. To me it was worth the 10€ and I can recommend it. I think i will have to convince some RL friends to get into it at a LAN again.

So, most people will think this is a ♥♥♥♥♥♥ pay 2 win game, right? Well, to that i say NO. With the 30,000 SP the game gives you at the start, you can buy some good guns (that are not paid with real money), you'll rank up faster than a machine gun's fire, and you'll kill all of those VIP players and fancy guns paid with real money. With that said, let's go over the pros and cons.  Pros: -The maps are great -There are a lot of fun gamemodes like Hexa, in which the players are put into a glass arena, and they have to kill eachother using weapons from the Mario-like looking cubes. -The graphics are stunning. -The anti-cheat software used for the game is much better than Valve's VAC. -The gameplay feels like Call of Duty Black Ops 2 (for me atleast). -Like i said above, you don't need to spend a penny to be good at this game.  Cons: -There are no cons.  I rate this game 11/10, literally, it's that good.

Seems similar to the original, with the same plot and everything. Doesn't seem like anything new has been added so far, but I suppose that's because it's just remastered graphics?  The graphics seem ok, not fantastic. The faces seem very... ugly. I'm sure my character wasn't that ugly in the first game, and other characters also seem to have lumpy, mishapen potatoes for heads. some people have weird eyes that seem to glow, which is odd. All the cutscenes and animations like in the spell book (when you go to upgrade abilities, you see a little imge of the spell in the bottom right corner) seem to be from the old game, and are quite blurred. Which is odd, seeing as this is supposed to be an upgraded graphics game.  The controls are abysmal. I can't remember the original fable have these weird controls. It's very difficult to shoot bows and dodge and everything at once, which I can't remember happening in the original. The menus are terriblle and nothing is consistent. (As default) You walk using WASD, then the menu switches to using insert/home, delete/end and arrow keys.  No mouse is allowed in inventory/menu screen. It just makes it more difficult to use, and seems like a crappy console port.  Aging and scars are terrible. As a young adult in this game, I shouldn't look like an 80 year old man who's been whipped and tortured all his life. The slightest nudge of a leaf, falling gently from a tree, causes horrific and irrevesible scaring over 90% of your hero's body. I'm all for the rights of people with disabilities and disorders, but surely this guy shouldn't be a hero and would be better employed stuffing feathers into pillows or some other profession where you're unlikely to be maimed because a butterfly looked at you funny.

A great little game. You play as a frog who gets yelled at by his boss, but gets to blow stuff up to make up for that.  Kero Blaster is an action platformer with an arcadey feel to it. You die in a few hits, and health is a bit scarce, making the game fairly challenging. Coins collected during the campaign can be used to upgrade guns into more powerful and satisfying ones, as well as increase your max health.  While the game only took me about two hours to complete, the excellent enemy design and pacing (as well as a hard mode unlocked after finishing the game) provide high replay value.  The soundtrack is also fantastic.

I don't think it's very useful as most people already finished the game by now.  Seriously, just a little more work put into this game (eg. improving AI and gameplay experience and creating a proper finale) and it could have been a nominee for the Game Of The Year award.  It was fun to play it but Lucius II is definitely not what most people would expect for 20 Euros. :(

At first I felt like it's an experiment in leveraging word of mouth to sell an abstract meaningless creation by telling people it's deep and meaningful and watching them repeat that nonsense just so that they themselves do not seem shallow.  But in the end it's not just that. It's an exercise in solving problems you do not have for the narrator you do not care about by making forced choices that do not matter whenever told to fo so by the narrator. There's no gameplay, no meaningful win condition and, in the end, no aesop to be made.  The creator should have taken some effort to make the player care about the narrator or the narrator's obsession or, at least, to explain why the 'games' make any sense from a sane person's point of view and, thus, should be played by the actual customer.  P.S.: Now that I think of it, the game takes less than 2 hours to complete and, technically, can be refunded upon completion. Not refunding it would be an act of paying up for your own mistake of buying it on faith.

Hatoful Boyfriend. A game that when I murmur its name in the cold wind of a winters morning - I almost hear a reply. When I play it i feel as though I, a small part of society and the world can actually achieve something greater than myself. This is what this game does; it comforts me, protects me, acknowledges me. It's like the older sister i never had.  Inspired by 'Pigeon, the untold story' and the fantasy adaption of the manga 'Hatsui Fantabolian' this game delves into the struggles of the kawaii female heroin <insert chosen name here>. She's in pursuit of senpai - the buff hot pigeon who's got all the girls interest. But will it be too much for him to bear?  Pros: +Fantastic graphics +Engaging subtitles +A good plot  Cons: -Senpai is often unattentive -Pigeons can be overzealous at times -No DLC  Overall this game is a breath of fresh air in the shoujo and reverse harem genre - giving its fanbase a new perspective on love, life and school in all of its glory.  9.4/10

review_text: Will you be Sith or Jedi? Let your choices decide. You will find combat reminiscent of D&D if you have ever played. Once you decide on your actions it plays out in a similar manner as you get feats, skill points, etc.

More Wang for everyone!  Take: the combat mechanics from the first game. Add in four-player co-op a-la Borderlands, socketable weapon upgrades a-la Diablo and hotswappable weapons a-la Aliens Colonial Marines (the only good thing about that game), and you get a ridiculous gorefest that still feels fresh and enjoyable in a post-DOOM world.  This game is to DOOM what Duke Nukem 3D was to Doom - similar awesome hyperviolent shooter action, but with era-appropriate humour thrown in. Like the previous game and unlike the original 90s Shadow Warrior, the humour isn't merely dumb movie quotes and sexist remarks, as while Wang still attempts to make them, he gets called out, burned in response, and sometimes even admits defeat. It's the most offensive yet least offending game of the decade! Top it off with character development for Wang himself after what he experienced in the first game, and you get a gorefest that you don't want to cringe from. And that's amazing in this day and age.  (Still reminds me of Blood II too much for comfort for some reason, like its predecessor did)

review_text: Wow....I'm almost at a loss for words. Almost..but I really need to recommend this game, so here goes.  I absolutely loved everything about this game. The art, gameplay, routes, and especially the writing. I can't get over the writing. I could hardly sleep the 4-5 days that I put into this game. I literally drowned myself in it.  The story made me laugh, cry, and gasp in shock. Almost every route ripped my heart out at some point. After finishing all but one 'secret', I am drained.   I truly long for the game to continue. That is how greatly I recommend this wonderful work of art.   I don't know what to do with myself now, without these dolls to play with.  As a side note, if you haven't played the developer's other game 'Dandelion', I also recommend that, and I would play it first. The gameplay is different than this, and there is a small tie-in to this game that's more fun if you've played 'Dandelion'. Just my opinion of course. Both are my favorite games of any in my collection, and the only ones I've bothered to review.  So, what are you waiting for?! :)

I have only written one other Steam review before, and no idea why I have been tempted into it today - probably because I found it difficult to decide how I felt about The Division.  First things first, I dont play many FPS games because Im not very good at them, not am I a co-op or PVP player as my life doesnt really suit teampspeak etc!   I was attracted to The Division by the rpg elements and the environment that it its set in, and I thought that it had a potential as a single player game so long as I wasnt going to be permanently being slayed by level 50 masters of the universe (as I tend to be in Elite!). So I guess this review is for people asking the question whether its a game for the generally single and possiblt rather casual game....  1) The set-up experience This was odd, with a character generator that was about as basic as some games from around a decade ago. In particular, if you are a woman or chosing to play as one, the female character options are particularly poorly made.  The tutorial section of the game is generally good, but there isnt enough on the fairly complicated multiple menys under which you manage your inventory etc. The most bizarre element of this phase of the game was when in the virtually your first mission you have to register on a computer in the safe house - this involved forming a queue with around 20 players and waiting in turn to use the one pc (reminds me of lining up to use a payphone in my youth!).  Other than this, i think the game does well at taking you by the hand and getting to a level that you can broadly work out where you should be going and how.  2) The look and feel The look and feel of this game are gorgeous. Even on my not exactly stellar hardware Manhattan looks great, and the winter setting feels fresh. The day/night and weather transitions are worth standing and looking at (so long as nobody is gunning for you).  The character models of NPCs are fairly limited, as is their dialogue, and I suspect that this will get wearing after a while. But I dont find it an more jarring than the same limted palette in Fallout 4.  3) Gameplay I have only tried the PVE sections so far, and may not ever venture into the PVP zone. Generally id say its fun and atmostpheric, though the number of bullets it takes to bring down a vanilla enemy is ridiculous. I have buddied up on a couple of missions through the random matchmaking and the players have all been friendly and keen to work in a team (even without voice chat).  I havent met with a situation so far that cant be played single player, and It seems to me that the game scales to reflect how many people are in your team. Maybe its just me, but it somehow feels better to be wandering the empty streets alone, rather than in a group. But clearly the game can be played as single player if thats your thing.   I have been getting killed alot, but thats more to do witj me being poor than the game I suspect, and the respawn points are generally well placed close to resupply boxes so you can keep trying sections until you click.  The RPG elements work for me - its good to see you base building up, and the morale of people in and around it improving as you do more missions. There is a reason to explore the streets as there are lots of items around at street level, in buildings, or higher up, and this means there is more to do than just jump from one scripted mission to another.    4) Conclusions I think it will have some longevity for me, and I may even venture into the PVP at some point (so long as I can find a good group to guide and educate me).  Its definitely not a 'must buy' if you are a single player game fan, but well worth a look if you like an atmospheric and slightly different take on the genre.

There was Fallout 3, which was quite good; and then there was the epic expansion New Vegas. Things were looking great. The franchise went into hibernation after that with vague promises of things to come. Those promises have been fulfilled in Fallout 4 and things are looking better than ever. Brotherhood? Check. Minutemen? Check. NPCs to partner with, like, hate, and love? Check. New game mechanics like settlements to build and maintain? Yup, they got that too.   Now, the advance press promised a better shooter than previous iterations of the game, some even claiming to be in line with a 'Destiny'-like shooter. Did they manage? Not quite. Combat is better, but V.A.T.S. is still your second-best friend after more ammo. The frantic '♥♥♥♥ shit ♥♥♥♥ shoot from the hip cuz I'm backing up before I die' combat is better though and not to be ignored.    Overall, the game is WOW better than before. Storytelling, combat, questlines, voiceacting, all of it.  There is, however, one major problem: Diamond City. The game forced me...a lifetime Yankee fan...to fight my way to Fenway Park. Say it ain't so....

This review contains spoilers regarding Half-Life 2.  Riding on the high octane ending of Half-Life 2 and acting as a direct continuation, Half-Life 2: Episode One manages to be a safe, if not fulfilling, addition to the Half-Life series.  Sticking with the known working formula, in Half-Life 2: Episode One you resume control of Gordon Freeman. Throwing the ending of that game to one side, Gordon finds himself back in City 17 with Alyx and a Citadel on the verge of leveling the entire city. Throughout this episode you venture back into the Citadel to contain the core and then proceed to try and escape City 17 before its destruction.  The issue with this game is it brings very little new to the table. The environments are all rehashes of what was seen in HL2 - the Citadel, the streets and buildings of City 17. The only original feeling portion of the game is where you have to fight through a hospital, constantly being assaulted by Combine and Zombies, with an assault ship opening fire through any window you pass by. Amongst the zombie horde is a new creature: the Zombine. This headcrabbed Combine soldier is stronger and faster than the other zombies, possessing the ability to pull grenades out in a kamikaze run. It spices up the variety little, but only a little, for the most aprt they still act like everything else you've encountered so far. The sections with the Citadel are interesting; now a degenerating and highly unstable building that the Combine are fleeing, but ultimately it's not too indifferent from the final stages of HL2.  The final, and largest, criticism is the length. This game can be completed in roughly five hours. For a game that offers little advancement in plot, instead focusing mainly on combat, there's not a lot here to make it an essential play. Don't get me wrong, it's worth playing if not just to have an excuse to dip back into the HL2 world, but this game struggles to have any sort of re-playability value when HL2 does everything this game does - only better.

I'd rate this game a 4/5. Dishonored 2 is a well polished stealth game with more features than Dishonored 1. This game absolutely lives up to being the sequel to Dishonored. The game supports more playstyles than its predecessor.  However, the game falls short of being a 5/5 for several reasons.  The biggest one being how it handles new game mechanics. Dishonored 2 introduces brilliant new game mechanics and then restricts them to only certain parts of the game. I'm hoping DLC will help fix that.  Another reason is how many upgrades in the game cater to only 1 or 2 playstyles. I found myself tons of upgrades I could never use because I was relying on stealth.  That leads into the other complaint I have. Maybe I'm just not very good at stealth, but it seemed like getting detected once or twice heavily impacted what playstyle the game thought I was playing even though a large majority of my kills were stealth. That led to a very frustrating experience for me.  Overall, Dishonored 2 is a fantastic game and if you loved Dishonored 1, then you'll love Dishonored 2.

app_name: The Amazing Shinsengumi: Heroes in Love
review_text: Recommendation: Maybe+  I've played a few Dougenzaka Lab games, mostly on my phone cause they are...well...mobile games after all. Mobile to PC ports. So I'm not expecting something on the same level as say: Hakuoki or Nameless.  It's cute, I love seeing the various forms the Shinsengumi members take, especially Souji and Hijikata. Saito is my waifu though forever always and I played his route first. I will say the naughty parts certainly use some creative word usage, I'll give them that much... not exactly classy. I've read fanfiction with well written saucy bits more impressive than what this gives. It isn't my favorite Dougenzaka Lab game. Pub Encounter and Men of Yoshiwara, imo, are stronger otome visual novel games than this (I played those on my phone and bought them again on steam on sale)   I bought this on sale, for like....5 bucks? Well worth the 5 bucks but not the 15.99 price tag. Though more convenient to buy on steam than on the mobile platform just because they charge, what, 4.99 or something per route, then 2 bucks or something for the extra chapters too. So horse a piece I suppose.

L.A Noire is a very successful hybrid between an early 90's adventure game such as Monkey Island and Broken Sword with the open world and combat/driving mechanics of GTA. You play as Detective Cole Phelps, a WW2 veteran who is trying to work his way up the law enforcement ladder of the Patrol/Traffic/Homicide/Vice/Arson division all while trying to deal with the hostility of his co-workers and the inner corruption of the police force. If you've ever watched a Detective Noir movie like L.A Confidential then you pretty much know what to expect plot wise.  The adventure part of the gameplay will be familiar to anyone who's played adventure games in the early-mid 90's. You explore the crime scenes of your cases looking for items and clues that will help you in your investigation and then questioning the suspects of the case with the evidence that you have accumulated. The interviewing part is well done with some great voice acting and animation although the facial expressions can be a bit TOO subtle at times, you pretty much can't lose though as the story will still progress even if you get all the answers wrong.  The driving/shooting aspect is similar to any other open world game and you will be chasing and gunning down plenty of criminals during the 20-25 hour campaign. The theme and style of 1940's LA comes across really well with a brilliantly detailed city that has plenty of locations to explore, old fashioned cars to drive and a great albeit short period soundtrack. Anyone who is a fan of Noir movies and open world games with a mix of old school adventure will have a great time playing L.A Noire.

One of my favourite stories ever and one of the most touching and engaging stories I've ever followed in a long while, despite not having AAA graphics, no voice acting and basically no gameplay whatsoever (do I really have to count the puzzles and those silly action parts...?).  You are literally guided through the story and since I can't spoil it, it's definitely a game that will ease your heart and will make you feel great upon finishing it...however I have few complains, considering how much I loved it.  1)No replay value...and for replay value I mean that, unless I'm wrong, I didn't find any secondary or alternate ending, meaning that once you finish it you're basically over with it, as there's no real other paths to choose: this is definitely a flaw because in games like these you're often given the choice of multiple paths to choose, like in Visual Novel games, resulting in a different ending or just flatout different part of the game. In here nothing of this is present, and unless I haven't looked properly, I don't think there are any.  2)Inappropriate Dialogues: the two characters are pretty good, but for whatever reason they will OFTEN have situations where they'll just flatout say silly, if not completely useless lines of dialogue, and this could work if the game wasn't extraordinary serious and actually kinda grimdark: we are talking about a dying old man and you'd think they'd have more appropriate dialogues in those scenes. Silly dialogues can work yes, but when the moment asks and not just when the game wants to break the dark mood and make it cheerful: it results in a total contrast of the current situations (it's hard to explain though: you'll have to see for yourself).  3)It's a non-visual novel. Even if the game looks like you can do a lot of stuff, move around and interact with the enviroment and so forth, you're actually unable to do many things...in the end this is basically a Visual-Novel-Styled game because you'll won't be doing much at all but follow where the game will lead you: you have no control on most of the parts of the game and essentially see what's going to happen (again, do we have to mention those silly puzzles and those useless action parts?).  I will say though that this is not a flaw per se, but remember that some players WANT to play a game and not just read dialogues and see cutscenes: if you are one of these players you might not even bother because, despite having a great story behind, you might be bored to death from basically doing nothing but follow the lead of the game. Everyone has different tastes so I had to mention this too.   But for those who really are curious, you should really have a look at it, especially if you love story driven games where you actually care more for the story than the gameplay. The story is good, some might not even like it, but that's all about tastes, and if you don't mind once in a while to play a 'Novel Game' (not much of the visuals if you ask me), I'd say give it a look and see for yourself.   Has flaws, but they're easily ignored.

This game was fun, addictive, and... well... like almost every other game thats out there. This games, back when it was good, brought an old concept into new light. When people fought in teams, they fought together as a team. Everyone knew what to do and they would do it in the smartest and, mostly, risk free fashion. People would group together and use each other's abilities to overcome the other team. When someone was in danger of being overtaken 2v1, others would help that person out and push the other team back. Victory felt well desurved and defeat kept you pushing for more. I admit there were problems with the game before, but it was still fun despite. Teamwork was what made the game fun. The game has been change almost completely since then. 'change is good,' some might say, but with change comes new problems. The game gives people too much power. Strategy isn't what it is anymore. People lack weakness and planning is almost out of the picture. For example: when there was a 'tank' that was much more powerful than the rest, the smaller ships would either distract or damage 'the tank' so the other ships could destroy 'the tank' while its weak. That 'tank would deploy counter-measures or bring a few friends along with him to stop these attacks. Most matches now, people will go off on their own or in small squads, and ignore the rest of the team. Now, the problem everyone is talking about: real world cash for ingame money. This game was heading into this direction from the start, but its hit the point of utter annoyance. From over-powered moduals and weapons, to even just wanting to change you name. Sure, if you cram you can get anything without paying for it, but for somethings that would take years. This game was great before, but, like everything I've explained, this has changed. The developers have spent so much time trying to add to the game that they forgot to fix the problems. I wish I could say something good about the game, but with every groundbreaking improvement is an equaly annoying and ruinous problem.
This game is awesome, I'd buy it again :)

I have to admit, I find this game a little frustrating. It still is fun, though.   The goal of this game is, as the title says, to not starve. More specifically, it's a survival game where you find yourself in a strange world having to scavenge food and create things to survive the nightfall. It is in a sense a sandbox game, only a very challenging one, because food is not all that plentiful, and supplies are scattered around a randomized world full of danger. The goal is to see how long you can hold out and survive in this environment.  There are three meters you have to maintain to survive. Your health, your hunger, and your sanity. Of these, health is the hardest to manage, while hunger can be sated with food, and sanity can be restored by making clothes and such. A lot of the game is finding out which items in the world help maintain those levels, or what you can do with items to make them useful in helping those levels, such as cooking food.   It's an enjoyable game, but very difficult to survive unless you are fortunate to find the right resources soon enough.

I choose 'Yes' for recommend, because of the price. Download it and try it. It's a FTP ARPG, better than other FTP, but not AAA level.  There isn't enough of a 'hook' for me to keep playing much. Sometimes I go back thinking it will improve. It hasn't yet. Though I haven't played too much. (I'm at level 12 or 15 or something) The thing that annoyed me most when going back, is figuring out the quests I need to complete. The directions were weak. Other than that there is nothing wrong with the game, just other ARPGs seem to do it better, (thinking abou the loot, and combat, and skill system you know, the heart of an ARPG)  The new thing about this game vs other ARPGs (Diablo, Torchlight, Path of Exile, sort of) is the MMO aspect, everyone is in the same areas clearing out the same enimies all the time, with short respawn timers. There are instance dungeons for important story points and random ones for extra loot that are solo, or group based.  The free to play model here is not terrible. You can beat the game without spending anything. You can pick from a very small set of characters to start. (I think: Storm, Thing, Scarlet Witch, Daredevil, Iron Man, but don't quote me) You do get random character drops in game, but they are quite rare. You get crafting items which will allow you to gain access to other characters, but again, it takes a LONG time. (think: 100(s) of hours per unlock) Characters cost about $5-$15 each. Bank space and cosutme changes also cost money.
Make yourself a favor ( if you enjoy RPG ): Grab a beer ( or any good beverage of your taste ), invite a friend and play this ♥♥♥♥ing game, seriously! Thank me later.

Reasons you want to play it: 1. The visuals are pretty and extremely cute in that anime way we all love. 2. Presentation reminds you of classic arcade titles. 3. Fights are very button-masher-friendly. I consider this a con, but some people like to mash buttons and win. 4. Final boss is challenging in the way that DOA bosses are challenging. ;) 5. Not many other fighting game options on Steam.  Reasons you may not want to play it: 1. The story mode plot is not animated and is hard to get into. 2. There are no voiced lines in the story aside from moments where they say 'Nooo!!!' or something similar. 3. Fights are very button-masher-friendly. 4. Final boss is frustrating in the way that DOA bosses are frustrating. 5. Combo animations get old fast.  I recommend it only as a cheapie. If you can get the game for $7 or less, it is a good deal, because while it is a nice fighting game, the amount of content is low.

I played the Hunter well before it went to Steam and thoroughly enjoyed the game, even more so competitively hunting against other players I knew in multiplayer. So many of the Steam reviews revolve around two themes: this games isn't F2P, and it doesn't deliver instant gratification.  There is no such thing as a free lunch. I wonder how many of the negative reviewers have purchased premium time in WoT, WoW, AW, or War Thunder? Or have spent $$$ on the Lowe in WoT? Maybe a premium ship in WoW? Of course you should feel entitled that they conform their payment model to other games, that bare very little simularity to what they have created. /sarcasm This isn't an arcade game like a Cabellas special, it actually makes you have patience and some skill. I think they know that the masses aren't going to find this title appealing, that it won't be Far Cry Primal. If you want to participate in something unique, you have to pay the entrance fee. The best part is that they have constantly expanded the content and listened to community feedback. And when you start looking at the content, you start seeing that you don't need every piece of it to have fun. You could hunt almost any animal in any reserve with a 12 gauge pump. I personally like the classic muzzle loader. I have a few guns and a bow, but they compliment what I like to hunt, as does my gear. I don't have to have or expect all of the content. The most important part is that license. $35 gets you 12 months to hunt any species. That's not $14/month for premium time in some other games. I think people are conveniently ignoring that they can selectively buy the content and experience they want.

I've been playing the entire Millennium series several times since the first part was released and loved it from the start.  There's this young girl, determined to change her fate against all odds, and the story is well told, so that you feel with her the injustice that happens to her and her family.  The main quest of the game is to find people who are willing to fight alongside Marine and I've always been curious about what the next character will be like, what abilites they are going to have. Their strenghts and skills are very diverse and it's fun finding out how to use them best and what they can do. Also in their personalities the characters are very different, which is typical for Aldorlea games. Each one of them is special and I loved reading the dialogues between them. The gameplay is very well balanced. You can choose the difficulty mode that suits you best, and an 'ordinary' player like me can do perfectly on normal mode. Fighting in that mode isn't too easy so that one would get bored, but also not annoyingly hard, and grinding isn't necessary at all, which is something that I appreciate. Being granted the choice whether to play with visible or invisible encounters is a great feature too and increases the replay value a lot in my opinion. What's typical for Aldorlea games as well is the big variety of equipment. You often find great stuff in secret rooms, but also get to buy good things. It's so much fun to find new weapons and enjoy seeing the difference they make in fights, as well as other special accessories.  A big (albeit totally optional) part of the game is finding secret rooms. I love and hate them. It's always satisfying to find one, but it can become just as frustrating when missing one or more.  There are also a lot of side quests to do, some easy, some quite tricky. So this is a game where you shouldn't just rush trhough, but take your time and explore thoroughly. Then it's fun to figure out how to solve those quests. As for visuals, I find the maps very appealing and beautiful. What's special in MM is that there are maps where you can only walk left and right and jump up and down to other platforms. I like that, it's different from common maps. Also you do a lot of crazy jumps to reach places, that's awesome.  The character artworks are probably the most beautiful of all Aldorlea games, they are simply amazing. The music too is wonderful for the most part, at least to my taste.   All in all I very much recommend this game to all people who love old school RPGs and are not biased against RPG Maker games.

This game is absolutely amazing. It completely draws you in, your heart will be beaten, mashed and crushed but you will feel some of the deepest emotions you've ever felt in a video game.   My own personal story: I had a small group of survivors. We'd been fairly cohesive and effective for the first couple weeks of the war, then we got a new survivor to the group. She was badly hurt and sick but I felt this deep compassion after reading her story. She's barely escaped with her life when soldiers (Never learned if they were government or rebel soldiers) burnt her village and killed her family. I knew we needed to help her, so I sent Marco out to gather some medical supplies so we could nurse her wounds and treat her infections.  Marco crept through an abandoned hospital to find our desperately needed supplies. There was a chance there would be soldiers but that was the only place left with medical supplies. Marco found the last of the supplies that were desperately needed. He made his way towards the exit when suddenly, a group of soldiers burst in from the way he came. Panicking, Marco sprinted towards the other exit as gunfire and curses were shot in his direction. The exit was within arms reach as two bullets lodged themselves in Marco's back. He collapsed dead on the floor. I was in shock. This character, this person, I had grown attached to and heard their story was now gone, forever. I sat there and read as everyone in the group tried to come to grips with Marco's death. They cried the entire day, no one could be motivated to do anything, I cried with them. For a solid ten minutes I sat in front of my computer weeping over Marco, this friend I'd felt I'd known as lost. All the while the song from the soundtrack 'We keep going' played in the background.   I cannot recommend this game enough to people but be forewarned, this game will leave you feeling some of the deepest emotions a game has ever made you feel.

I'm sure you played the flash version of this online, I mean who hasn't? If you were thinking this is anything like that then you are wrong. The game has inhanced graphics, better mechanics, (mortar tower isn't annoying to upgrade anymore) new towers, an assortment of new gamemodes, and no premium shop. (kinda) The new towers are fun and easier to figure out and understand (besides the helicopter) for example the bloon chipper, sucks in bloons and cuts layers off. The helicopter though, I don't get it. Maybe it's because I don't use it since I have strategies with the old ones or that it's complicated but I never see it being used. Here's a list of pros and cons though.  Pros - Easy to understand interface and nothing is ever cut off or hidden. - Shortcut keys in this feel super useful versus never needing them on the flash version - Impoppable gamemode is impoppably hard, it's a good thing! - New towers are fun and easy to work into any endgame strategy - Enhanced graphics are beautiful and work with the maps - Charming art style - Lots of replay ability - Premium shop doesn't let you 'pay to win', it should really just be called the shop  Cons - Helicopter tower may be bugged, may be useless, idk - The game gets very monotonous very fast - Daily challenge on impoppable difficulty can be impossible, either towers cost to much and it can't reach an area or  something else along those lines can happen. - You can't cross a Ninja Kiwi account with this one so you must restart - Ranking up is very slow once you hit like 25  Over all it's a pretty good game with a lot of things going for it. 8/10 would recommend to everyone  Also if anyone could tell me how to use the helicopter that would be much appreciated

A great open world, a ton of quickly accessible and varying mini-games and side missions, and a combat system that is half in a similar style to the Batman Arkham series and half GTA's gunplay make for a wonderful experience, but be prepared to reconfigure the controls (defaults suck).   Highly recommend despite the porting/control issues (see below), but I do suggest you spend some time to rebind the controls and make them usable!  PROS: -World is a good size with events consistently near each other (not too much pointless running) -A good number of side missions and mini-games that use different mechanics (fighting, racing, number puzzles, gun fights, etc). -Good variety of *meaningful* collectibles. Collectibles unlock additional melee combos, health bonuses, etc.  -Cash sinks throughout the city (buy furniture for your various apartments from various vendors); clothes are also presented as a cash sink but due to the 'preset outfits' (DLC?) they become meaningless within the first hour or two of playing. -Radio and music selection (multiple stations, good banter, many types of music) -Excellent 'Social Hub' incorporating 'multiplayer' (arcade style) scores to actions within the game, such as Longest Jump, Fastest track times on races, and a flurry of other stats you can compete for. Integration of this is well done while ingame, but seeing what boards there are could be a little better done (overall its great and I really wish more games do this! It should be standard in the industry) -Excellent Cop/Triad score mechanic in missions promote the player to win without destroying everything but doesn't punish you much if you do. Outside of the missions, unless a cop sees you do something wrong, you do not get penalized for hurting civilians (this is a minor, but important point; to compare, Watch Dogs also has a reputation system but in that game hurting civilians outside of missions hurts your reputation which basically forces the player to be extra careful to the point of annoyance....Sleeping Dogs has the same 'good guy encouragement' but none of the annoyance.)  NEUTRALS: -Clothes are cool, but since certain sets give massive values compared to the rest, you will basically wear the +50% damage dealt or -50% damage taken outfit in almost every scenario. -'Food' shops around town make the town feel much more alive and provide a mini-cash sink....but the long unskippable animations will make you skip them unless you are on a really difficult area and absolutely need the buff.  -Very minimal immersion issue: Your character is a 'deep undercover' cop, but half the missions are 'police' missions which involve you knocking out criminals, etc. In the police shooting missions at least you wear the swat mask, but in the other missions you just run around punching people.... but everywhere else in the game everyone in town instantly recognizes you... -'Preset outfits' are way too strong compared to the in-game clothes. I assume these were released at DLC at some point, but it greatly modifies the balance of the game. (For example, one of the early outfits gives +5% melee damage....or you can buy the 'preset' outfit that gives +50% melee AND +50% grapple damage....compared to 5%, yea...) -Gunplay is weak. Bringing up 'aim' mode gives a huge reticle, you can't carry more than 1 weapon at a time, and, in general, if you have a gun you can wipe out armies of enemies until you run out of bullets. The entire 'gunplay' experience feels very forced in this game; given the gangster environment of the game, I can understand the need to include guns but they are certainly the weakest aspect of Sleeping Dogs.   CONS: -CONTROLS!! The control scheme for this game is clearly designed for a controller. There isn't much that could have been done to remedy the problem when bringing it to PC because the problems are deeply embedded in the game design itself, however the MOUSE5 button issue could have been prevented and that 1 extra button would have gone a long way in remedying the other issues.  ---'Aim mode' while driving seems to share a key with 'enter/exit vehicle' by default for some reason. When I encountered the part in the game where you are meant to shoot while driving, my character kept jumping out of the vehicle.....HINT: In the controls setup, under 'Vehicle controls' (push CTRL), there is a separate entry for 'aim mode'; you can use this entry to bind a different key so you can actually use aim mode while driving without diving out of your vehicle.  ---Camera view keeps repositioning on its own if you move....It is possible to look around, but its far more difficult and annoying than it needs to be. ---The game thinks MOUSE4 and MOUSE5 are the same key. Thus, you cannot bind anything to your mouse5 key. ---The 'sprint' and 'parkour' button is the same key (most PC players use shift to sprint, but space for jump mechanics). This also means you often have to stop sprinting when running in a tight area because if you are sprinting and get too close to a wall, he might parkour over it unintentionally. ---There is a 'lock on' button, but it is not a toggle (and no option that I know of to make it a toggle). On a controller this may be fine, but holding LCTRL or similar while moving with WASD is silly. Luckily, the lock-on feature is useless in melee combat (I never used it).  ---Similar to 'lock on', when you are holding a gun this becomes 'aim mode' instead. Again, a toggle would go a LONG way. This is playable, but annoying at best; however since the game's gunplay is extremely easy and shallow, you won't spend much time with this. ---'Aim-assist' ....sigh. Thankfully there isn't much shooting in this game and it can be mostly disabled.  -Minor BUGS! Audio lines will get cross/double played quite often. Rare occasion game crashes for no apparent reason  -'Camera' missions (final step): you need to watch the criminals through a camera and identify the one to be arrested. The problem is, there are no clear mechanics how to do this. Sometimes you can see a handoff so you know who the supplier is, but other times its the guy who has a briefcase or the guy who shoots someone....or sometimes nobody does anything and you just wait 50 seconds and the game tells you which one it is....??   -Fast travel system: you can only fast travel via taxi that are randomly driving around the city (a GREAT idea on its own) but there is NO other way to fast travel and you can only fast travel to certain missions or stores. You can NOT fast travel to your own apartment (yea you read that right).....not even if you are already in a car yourself (I guess he can't drive himself? lol). You have to drive around until you find a cab, pull over, and then pay the cab for a ride to your apartment....thats just silly. -Voice acting often doesn't match the character and is almost all in English with awkward accents (some accents are clearly American though the character has never been to America, other accents are borderline racist)....this is made weirder by the fact that you can get the SUBTITLES in Mandarin (??) but you can't get the spoken audio in Mandarin with English subtitles !! Why!?!? Mandarin spoken audio would have gone a long way.

The online is terrible. Dont buy unless you will only play offline. 5 bars connection does not matter because the problem is not the internet speed, but the way the game handles the traffic itself. There is one-sided rollback, meaning the two players are never synced but one is experiencing the 'correct' game, meaning your opponent is experiencing all the lag if your game appears fine, or vice versa. I have not heard the devs are addressing this issue; fatal flaw for an 'online' game.

I thouroughly enjoy this game. There is such a wide variety of what you can do, I can barely stick to one route (Not that you have to). This is the kind of game where you work to get bigger and badder equipment, regardless of how you choose to work. Right now, I'm working as an intergalactic cargo runner of rare commodities. I like this career because you really have to work a stock market and route planning. Not to mention, I get to fight off pirates trying to get their hands on my booty. It's very interesting because you have to make a balance between protection and cargo capacity/long distance speed. The more I fight off pirates and make trades, the better ships I can get.  There is much more to this game than cargo running though, you can also take you ship to bounty hunting and kill some of the biggest bad guys out there. If killing isn't your thing, you can spend your days taking in the sights of the galaxy and do some exploring. Still looking for something else to do? Take some mining lasers and blast away at some asteroids, scoop up the fragments, and sell 'em at a station. If you want more excitement in your space life, you can work for a faction and take part in large battles with lasers flying every which direction and explosions in every corner of the battlefield.  My buying point of this game was that you play in a 1:1 scale of the universe, and that is just amazing to me.

A highly enjoyable, casual multiplayer shooter with a fun and lighthearted style that endears itself to me.   The Mean Greens is a 5v5 multiplayer shooter which sees the two teams, the Greens and the Tans, square off in one of many different and surprisingly unique gamemodes. What makes it stand out is the fact that you play as those cheap little plastic toy soldiers that you would see being sold at the dollar store, and the maps take place in all sorts of everyday household locations, ranging from the inside of a freezer to an arts and crafts table even up to a large saltwater fish tank.  The gameplay is simple enough. You've got your standard 2 teams, an objective and the undeniable sense that you should just go over there and shoot their plastic nuts off. There are no classes or character progression systems, each and every player has the same loadout as you do at all times, meaning there are no picks and counter-picks, just an evenly matched lineup in which the best team wins.  Although, there are some cheap-shot handholding weapons like the rocket launcher which in essense is a one-shot one-kill weapon. But it's relatively balanced since there are no ammo pickups on the maps, and once you shoot the rocket launcher it will go on cooldown for a ridiculously long time before you can use it again. In fact it is quicker to just die and respawn to get the rocket launcher back. Many will think the rocket launcher to be a cheap weapon, and they'd be right, but its use does require some thought and timing, since you cannot instantly pull it out and fire away, instead you have to wait for a moment for your character to get it ready, giving enemies ample time to shoot you down or close the distance so you cannot kill them without killing yourself. The cheapshot weapons like this may seem like they could ruin the game and cause the most skilled players to get constantly wrecked by the biggest shooter virgin alive. But it actually doesn't ruin anything, since you also have the same weapons as they do, making this in my opinion a very casual multiplayer game. (As of patch 1.4, the rocket launcher cooldown is now spread out between lives, meaning you can no longer die and have it ready to go after an 8 second respawn time instead of waiting a minute or so to have it back)  The gamemodes and maps are fun and visually interesting in their designs, bright colors and massive backdrops create some outstanding environments that aren't too distracting from the gameplay itself. You've got your standard multiplayer maps like King of the Hill, Capture the Flag and Free-For-All, but also some unique gamemodes that takes full advantage of the fact that you're playing small toy soldiers in a big world. Like the birthday cake map where the objective is for one team to use their flamethrowers to light as many candles as they can before time runs out, while the other team has to defend the cake, switching at the end of a round, with whatever team having the most candles lit wins.   Does it have its flaws? Yeah... The third person perspective I feel doesn't really do the game many favors, it's difficult to see which weapon you're holding in the middle of an engagement, for example the M-16 and the sniper rifle, since they lack a distinctive silhouette when your soldier is holding them. Also, because of the third-person view the most important HUD elements are subdued, being difficult to notice when you're in the middle of a firefight. How much ammo is in your currently selected weapon or how much health you have doesn't show all too well in the middle of gameplay.   I feel like this game would benefit so much more if you were allowed to at least toggle between first and third-person views, like you could in Tribes: Ascend, and I really think the HUD needs an overhaul. But all things considered, it's nothing that completely ruins anything, because like I said, it's casual multiplayer. If you're looking for some simple, easy to pick up and play multiplayer fun with an interesting design twist, then the Mean Greens - Plastic Warfare is right up your alley.

Eh, there's just not a lot to it, even for the price. Sure, there's that initial rush to grab as much as you can, so it lives up to its name fairly well. but 60 seconds of gameplay is an apt description. 'Scavenge hunts' is just sending a living family member up with a chance to eventually come back with a few random items. Everything else is whittling down objects and feeding or not feeding people. Little to no replay value, really needs some beefing up on actual gameplay.   It's a fun game to an extent, but there's just nothing. to. do.  I love the art style, I love the music, I love the theme. It's got a lot of character to it..... but these things can't replace gameplay. I was honestly hoping for this style, carried over to maybe a more cartoon-like version of This War of Mine, a fantastic example of resource and party managing, survival, scavenging, and keeping the home front safe and comfortable.   On a side note, it bugs me that if the Dad doesn't make it to the shelter in the countdown, you instantly lose. Why is it if he dies later, it's fine, just keep someone in the family going? If the mom or kids are down in the shelter with food and supplies, why can't I play a game with them?
