LotF is a game I won’t be revisiting in the long term, but I definitely enjoyed it enough for the time I have spent with it up to now. I’d love to recommend it, but it falls short of expectations.  LotF is a game that (predictably) uses a combat system similar to that in the Souls games. You control Harkyn from the 3rd person perspective and probe enemies for patterns & weaknesses before moving in to finish them off using a weapon of your choice. There's a relatively large selection of weapons & armour to choose from, with the game slowly allowing you access to more powerful items as you continue with the linear narrative. The combat is functional and usually fun, but can be a little clunky at times, and the spells are often completely underwhelming and are more useful for tactical purposes. If you like to play RPGs as a mage then honestly you should probably avoid this game. You have access to one long range weapon which you can use in a variety of ways, but I found it most useful for pulling single enemies out of larger groups.  The game looks pretty good with a very consistent art-style, and the two major areas had very distinctive visual themes. The enemies were well designed and the environments were pretty, even if you could tell how constrictive they were. The armour designs were not to my taste (far too large and “showy”) and were a bit reminiscent of the kind of over-sized armour you see on space marines from 40k. The enemy animation was good enough for me to easily distinguish between what attacks they were winding up which allowed the combat to flow. The sound was not up to the same standard as the visuals: the scripting and voice acting in LotF are simply atrocious. Conversations often have sentences that don’t really follow on from the last, being said in tones that don’t follow the mood, on subjects you couldn’t care less about. The poor voice acting really impacts on the narrative too, as it is delivered in a very obvious and linear manner through conversations and cutscenes. The story simply plods along from A to B with few surprises. The audiobooks add meat to the narrative and are delivered with far more convincing speech than that from actual characters in the game, without them the story would have been paper-thin and completely forgettable (which is why so little of this review is dedicated to the story).  I have a few more gripes about LotF as well. The biggest issue is that after finishing, not much changes in NG+ except that enemies have far more health, which really kills the replayability. My second largest issue is the scaling/levelling seems very oddly balanced: even when not using checkpoints for long periods of time to get extra XP, I rarely built up enough experience to increase Harkyn's capabilities in a noticeable manner; sometimes adding a point into Strength actually reduced the damage of my equipped Strength-scaling weapon and I have literally no idea why. Thirdly there are some technical problems: occasionally the game would outright crash, resulting in complete loss of everything you’ve done since last touching a checkpoint; the frame-rate would often bomb to unplayable levels; and sometimes the load times between areas would take unusually long.  It probably sounds like I’ve panned LotF, but I did manage to enjoy my time with it despite its failings. If you like 3rd person fantasy RPGs then this is worth the price of admission in a sale.

I carried a buddha statue halfway across the ocean floor, only to have to incinerate it for science.    The game was short. This was partly due to its simple nature -- there were no in-game mechanics such as inventory or character management. You essentially walk around, open doors and avoid monsters.  The visuals were great. The game ran pretty smoothly.  The audio was fantastic, especially the foley.  Scary at some points, but doesn't feel desperate or urgent, or even very deadly. This was not nearly as scary or desperate as a lot of Amnesia: The Dark Descent. I am reluctant to compare other horror games to it, however, because when I played Amnesia I was definitely more naive and knew much less about games than I do now. Games in general seem to get less scary as I get older.  Story seems overexplained by the main character, he wasn't very likable and the script seemed to shove all the interesting parts in your face. It seems as though the developers were worried that people wouldn't understand what was going on, so they spelled everything out.  The stealth is very basic. The enemies had variety but were never difficult to get past or particularly frightening.  No replay value, expectedly.  SPOILERS: At the end,    some plot points seem rushed. While the choices you make throughout the game don't have consequences, they still provide some moral satisfaction (killing life-supported people, the robot guy in the beginning, destroying the WAU)       Overall, a decent experience. Interesting things are tackled in the story. Mildy scary. Immersive.

One of the best strategy games developed. It does need more tweaking in the mean time, the developers though are doing an amazing work on that end. The AI needs balancing and goverments need adjustement, turns are slow (15-20s) even on high-end machines. However it is fun and engaging in its current version. Graphics are amazing. Many buildings and wonders too. Adding districts and redesigning goverments are both a huge improvement.  My only two complaints are about the AI wars and UI. The defense tactics of the AI have been remodeled from release, nice. However, units are now easier to destroy, the AI spam-build them but only move them without attacking, speed epic and difficulty emperor. Too bad. The UI is redundant and some windows are cluttered with useless information, thus becoming themselves useless. All the numbers should be fit into graphs for better forcasting and resource management.  The tech tree design and implementation are outdated and incomplete. The tree itself can be better visualized. The technologies are incoherent, and should have been reworked.  The civilopedia is useless, so much information deliver no insight nor story. How could the developers have spent all their budget on Sean Bean but not recreating a civilopedia that is both informative and helpful. Mr Bean becomes boring to listen to after 50 hours of play.

I really wanted to like this game.  But the dull combat (compared to something like say D&D), boring art style that feels archaic and makes me appreciate modern gaming (I am looking at you extremely zoomed out fixed camera angle), monotonous unrelenting 'dark and gritty' tone that gets absolutely rediculous and incredibly mundane very quickly (all attempts at breaking it come solely from your companion chatter, and it just feels out of place as a result), pedestrian situations (An attempt at realism in fantasy can be great, but when you have everything else boring in the game you need to make up for it with interesting situations), makes for a boring game that fails to suck me in in any way. Six characters in a party at once is too many. You cannot micro them realistically without permenant slow motion or liberal pausing, and the AI/behavior settings is terrible (How hard can this really be? Seriously, just copy the format from Dragon Age Origins and that is enough, there is no excuse for this level of failure).  All in all, I found it almost impossible to get immersed in, impossible to care about, and annoying to play.

This is a lovely little puzzle game, and refreshingly different. You play BUD, a cute little robot whose mission is to grow a Star Plant. To make the plant grow, you have to find the new shoots and steer them towards floating islands full of energy that the plant feeds on, causing it to grow. Along the way there are things to help you (leaf gliders and flower parachutes, for example), and you can collect power crystals that give you boosts. It is very colourful and very pretty. The controls are simple and quite innovative (using both mouse buttons as your hands to climb). It's also quite challenging. As the plant gets taller, you soon discover that it's a long way to fall, and an even longer way to climb back up again! It's a great little game. Well worth buying.

6/27/15: NO.  7/18/15: My biggest issue with this game is that it's not fun to play whether you have a winning team or not. And when everything clicks and a match becomes a fun, balanced battle, it ends just after a quick 10 or so minutes. The lobby then takes way too long to kick up the next match resulting in everyone just leaving. I'm ok with tight experiences but the matches seem too short and too centered around planting a bomb or standing next to an armored vehicle to make it move. These are objectives I'm happy to take part in with games like Team Fortress and Battlefield. In Dirty Bomb, these objectives never feel like a tiny corridor with 1-2 routes to take. With the largest teams being 8v8, I suppose this is by design but it puts a lot more responsibility on everyone playing the objective... I dont know about you but a game should be inherently fun with or without a super e-sport mega team.

I'd rate this a medium - wish it had been more challenging. Missions are completely linear, there is some unnecessary handholding (e.g. hints like 'you might want to be careful here'), you can often recover too easily from accidentally being spotted, checkpoints after each step, and some items later in the game reduce the challenge even further (in particular the invisibility cloak). Also, the story and (British?) humor didn't do much for me, but that could be subjective. On the bright side, it was enough fun for a single playthrough, occasionally getting new items was fun, graphics were simple but flawless, and the moderate pace and low difficulty made for a relaxing experience. Bought this on sale for $5, which seemed about right.

Fallout 4 was a game I was hyped for since 2010. I was never a fan of Fallout 3, but Fallout: New Vegas is my favorite game of all time. The writing of a Fallout game is important to me coming in as a fan of the first two games, and Bethesda's Fallout games have pretty poor storylines and dialogue. Obsidian's New Vegas, however, is one of the most cleverly crafted RPGs ever made and is a game I have played to completion multiple times. Fallout 4's combat is fantastic and is a huge improvement on the last two games, and really feels more action-y, which is what a game like this needs IMO.  PROS:    Excellent combat   Interesting areas   Great graphics for a Bethesda game   Mod support   Some quests are really fun (BoS main questline)   NEUTRAL:   Bethesda's normal scattering of bugs   Crafting system is not something I'm into   City building not really my thing either   CONS:   God-awful story   Dialogue doesn't hold a candle to New Vegas   Poorly written characters   Repeating quests   Minutemen   Little player choice compared to NV   Feels small  THE UGLY   Other than character customization, the voiced protagonist you play as has a very linear path that you can not really change throughout the game (despite the BoS/Institute/Railroad choice), which is a slap in the face to New Vegas fans.   Overall: 6.2/10 (If steam had a 'Meh' button i would use that but for now I recommend it)

When I think of action typing games the most prominent game that comes to mind is Typing of the Dead. A classic and there's been nothing close to it since, until now. Epistory takes you on an adventure through a paper landscape riding on a large origami fox. This game has those feverish typing to kill enemies element but this one is more family friendly.  When you start off it's not clear how or why you're in this strange paper world or what your end game is but that's okay as the game leads you down many paths to uncover your story. The more you explore the more your world expands. As you fight and type your way through enemies you gain points for unlocking more areas. Pretty straightforward right? As you make your journey through the vast wilderness your chronicles are narrated by the girl in a way that seems more like a walk through memory lane.  There are various zones throughout the world that break up the monotony and a couple of magic powers to provide a little variety for your typing skills. While this game is way more family friendly than the aforementioned title that no way diminishes the hardcore difficulty of this game. You will periodically face waves of enemies and the words to defeat foes gets progressively more complex. There are some words I didn't even know existed in the English language. But I should also mention that if English isn't your go to native language don't fear as you can play in several different languages.  So to sum it up:  +Great art style  +Several languages to choose from +Innovative battle system +Ability to make your own word lists  Okay the one issue I kind of had with this game was movement controls were a bit on the clunky side. It's played at a fixed isometric camera view and you use AWSD keys to move but going in a straight line requires holding 2 keys and then there's another button for running. Yeah, that's the only thing I was not a fan of. But for everything else this game is a treasure and a definite must play even if typing games aren't your jam.

After yesterday’s review of a somewhat disappointing platform game, Marcus Level, I didn’t want to set my hopes too high for Dinocide. However, this was very difficult. It’s a game that again appears to have everything that I look for in this type of game being inspired by the NES old-school single player platformers. It’s also not the developer, AtomicTorch Studio, first game on Steam. Generally developers learn a trick or two from their debut game and I hoped that this was the case here. Their first title, VoidExpanse, released in April of last year, was met with a rather poor to mixed reaction by the reviews on Metacritic where the game averages a 59 critic score which is somewhat bogged down by the 40 that GameSpot‘s Kevin VanOrd gave the game. However, Steam players genuinely appeared to like the game (72-28 positive-negative review score as of time of writing). With that said Dinocide appeared to have potential from the outside looking in.  So it turned out to be. A game with a whole lot of potential and one that does a lot of things right. The atmosphere created by the music and the 8-bit era graphics. The nicest touch being the health mechanic that decreases over time but can be replenished by hitting food. I found it to be an incredibly deft decision because of the caveman character and setting that you play. I quite enjoyed the four different dinosaurs that you can utilize and ride with each having their own little attacking move that aides your ability to complete levels and to unlock certain things throughout the game. Arguably the most important advantage for the game is its accessability. This is something that everyone will be able to not only play but find some level of enjoyment from.  I only have two criticisms of Dinocide. First, the game may be a little too easy. I played through the majority of the levels without dying a single time. When I did die it was often because I had attempted to do something silly. Had I used any level of concentration I would have known not to do it. One of the things that really that I believe made the NES games so good was that when you beat them you really felt as though you had earned it. Unfortunately, that’s really not the case here. My other criticism is that the game is over too quickly. With the game being as easy as it is I was hoping for more content but the game can be completed in a couple of hours. The only real replayability is in choosing an alternative route meaning that you will never hit every possible level in one playthrough. Unfortunately at time of writing the game lacks Steam achievements so the only reason to replay the game is to visit those extra levels without any extrinsic value to doing so. Steam achievements would add a lot of value to the game and given the fact that AtomicTorch utilized them in VoidExpanse I am somewhat shocked and disappointed that they weren’t implemented here.  Despite the two pretty heavy criticisms that I have of the game I really did enjoy my time with it. With increased natural difficulty it could have been much better but it’s still a good game. Some may argue that it’s not quite worth its asking price of US$9.99 but it does come close. If I were a price conscious consumer I’d wish list this for now and pick it up when it is discounted or bundled. At that point you’ll receive maximum value and quite likely a bargain. I have never particularly liked the Saints Row series, and I think I was able to articulate why only recently. Here's my problem. In various sandbox games you're thrown into a world with a bunch of toys and you let loose, the amount of fun you have is really up to you, the player. Saints Row likes to shit all over this concept.  Whenever I play Saints Row I always feel like I'm being forced into the fun, like, the game is screaming 'HAVE A LOT OF FUN' at me and tossing me into stupid situations with stupid 'weapons' and cartoonish characters. It doesn't do it for me for some reason.  Saints Row is like that one good-natured autistic friend you had as a kid who tries really hard to be funny and they might sometimes draw a chuckle out of you, but in the end comes off as overly annoying.

PAYDAY 2 is a very immersive first-person co-op adventure game designed to educate students interested in the financing buisseness. The game teaches you to work together with your team to complete goals set by your various clients. Along the way, you are supported by your mentor, Bain, and his co-workers Gage, Alex, and Bile. This game also teaches you how to operate and repair machinery, and how to make good decisions in time-sensitive situations. I recommend this game for anyone looking for a job in financing, or anyone with experience in said line of work that is willing to support a new generation of crooks, theives, and robbers.

Payday 2 is a very special game. Sure, it's easy to look at the screen shots and videos of the game and have it screen 'meh' as a shooter, but it's oh so much more. In the game, you and up to three other people must plan and execute daring heists ranging from ripe jewelry stores, secure banks, seedy meth labs, and even daylight on the streets. It's you versus the law, and you'll need your wits, gadgets, and (in most cases) plenty of ammo to survive! The game features some random factors thrown in with varying layouts for each heist, plus lots and lots of fully customizable weapons. The game also gives you leeway on different ways to approach heists, either trying to be sneaky and not alert the authorities, or go guns blazing against an increasingly hardened police force. With 5 distinct difficulty levels ranging from 'normal' to 'death wish' and unlockable skill trees, and customizable masks you'll keep coming back for more. The only drawbacks to the game are that some of the later levels are a bit 'grindy' in that it takes a long time to level up, and the rewards system is almost completely random, but the game is intense and fun and you won't even start to care about those things after a while. A must buy!

I have to let you guys know upfront that I'm really a fan of the 80s vibe. Seriously man, something about the pastel teal and pink puts the lead in my pencil. The very first time I saw Hotline Miami's menu, heard it's music, before I had even PLAYED the game, I was hooked. To my great relief, the actual game is just as dope as the aesthetic.  Gameplay in HM isn't particularly deep. You are dropped into an area and must kill every mother ♥♥♥♥er in there to advance. This is harder than it sounds: the face of death smiles on your character if an enemy so much as sneezes on him. You generally have to observe patrol patterns to formulate the most effective strategy. In my mind, I think of Hotline Miami as a puzzle game. Instead of moving blocks, you take lives in the most effecient manner possible.  The game grades you for your performance, and there are some sexy unlocks that are earned through high grades and exploration. At the start of each level, you choose which of your masks you will don. They all have a different effect of varying potency with the more powerful ones generally having to be found in their levels. You also unlock weapons and letters for a secret message (these must also be located). No spoilers, but it's in your best interest to try to locate those letters.  The story is surreal, chaotic and awesome. The game already has atmosphere in spades, the sharply written dialogue and trippy twists put it over the top. Basically, Drive: The Videogame, or at least how it looked from the Driver's perspecitve.  With everything else the game has going for it, I was surprised to find that my favorite part of the whole thing was the music. Seriously, the music in Hotline Miami is incredible. It would be incredible to listen to by yourself in a leisurely manner, but what makes it truly remarkable is how well it flows with the game. Try to play the game without music - you can't, really, truly can't. I cannot think of another game where this has been the case.  So, in closing, the game hits every bullet point. It can be frustrating, but is never dull. Occasionally shallow, but always fun. Extremely aesthetically pleasing, in both the graphics and music. A story you can sink your teeth into, and the gameplay to go along with it. Great experience for any gamer, provided they are not put off by the (extreme) 8-bit violence.  8.2/10

To the casual player. A thumbs down.  To the Speedrunner. A thumbs up. ---  Casual player-  If you're into dry parody type humor, and don't mind the graphics, then you might like the game. If not, then the game doesn't really have anything else going for it. It tries to be funny and forces you to go around collecting coins to 'unlock DLCs' to get abilities, to explore more areas. It's rinse and repeat until you beat the game. Which only takes about -1 hour- or less to complete on your first blind playthrough. ----  Speedrunner -  Your time is recorded and there's an overall scoreboard of best times at the end. So if you're into speedrunning then this is a very short and easy game to pick up and learn. Especially if you're new to speedrunning. You can choose the order of coins and DLCs you collect along the way, which gives option to different routing paths. Which is good if you want to improve your routing ability if you've never routed a game before. On top of that, it does require some good execution. You can get punished and lose some time for missing some jumps or accidently talking to certain NPCs too quickly. Intentional deaths and checkpoints can also shave off a ton of time from your runs if you use them properly. Overall though it's a very simple and easy game to speedrun.  They also have an any% category and 100% category. So the game might be fun to run for a day or 2 if you're an experienced speedrunner. ----

In the past I've heard a bit about how cool Homeworld was, and I have to say I am thoroughly impressed. Being a big fan of the RTS genre, when I heard about this game I immediately wanted to try it out, but alas, it was unavailable. So when I saw 'Homeworld Remastered' on Steam's front page a few weeks ago it went straight to my wishlist, and I ended up buying it the day before release.  I was concerned that I might have impulsively spent $30 on something that wasn't really worth it, but now I have no idea why.  Having played around 4 hours of this game over the two days since release, I've only gotten more endeared to the game and how epic it feels. Building a huge fleet of frigates, destroyers, and battlecruisers and then proceeding to wipe out some poor AI... Awesome.  Some Quick Pros and Cons PROS: -- Phenomenal feel, moving ships around in 3D space doesn't feel confusing or difficult. While I have yet to employ it significantly, I'm sure it will make things quite interesting. -- AI is, for the most part, pretty smart. While it makes some dumb moves from time to time it doesn't give up unless all is lost. -- Oh my word, the visuals are incredible! Next time you have a large group of fighters engaging the enemy, select one of them, focus and zoom in. Large-scale, small-scale, everything looks beautiful, even on my puny GT 630.  CONS: -- There's some minor bugs, like when moving ships I'll occasionally lose my mouse cursor for a second or two. Every so often the sound skips and repeats 1-2 second snippets of a clip.  NEUTRAL: -- Gameplay moves a bit slower. It requires patience to build up a sizable attack force and deploy it to wipe out the enemy, but once that combat starts you really grasp the benefit of taking the time to work.  I may expand on this review as I spend more time playing the game, but if you're still doubting whether you want to buy this, let me eliminate any doubt. The Homeworld Remastered Collection is an awesome piece of work. For $35, you get an incredible RTS, its sequel, and the remastered versions of both. Hopefully Gearbox will provide a demo at some point, but if you're a fan of the RTS genre, purchasing this shouldn't be a question.

This game reminds me so much of Castlevania I & 4 that if you're a fan of those game I know for sure you'll fall in love with this game as much as I have. The game is linear, you go through the stage and collect sigilis to gain more health and find the powerups hidden along the way. Selena's human and wolf form have 3 different special skills that get upgraded twice as well as two other upgrades for attack power. At the end of each sigil collecting stage is a boss battle. Also, if you miss any hidden sigils or powerups, and you will, you can replay the levels to get the ones you missed. The graphics aren't high-end but I'm assuming it's a stylistic choice because I find them absolutly charming. The voice acting of the narrorator is amaing.  The only con I have is that there's knockback damage - no one in gamer history has ever asked for this!   If you're in the mood for old school action platformer then give this game a shot. When I play games I always ask myself if my gameplay experience was worth what I paid for and in this case I will say absolutly. I find the mixed reviews harsh. It's only 1,99 USD, come on! I got this on sale but I would gladly pay the full, modest price for this game, double even. When I looked through the FAQ to look up how to get my direct input controller to work, I felt like the developers really cared about what their customers thought and had a lot of love for this game. I hope to see more from them in the near future.

They're big part of the game even if the game doesn't revolve completely around them.  3. Not for people who hate (yes, you'd have to _hate_) adventure games, either.   4. Certainly not for emotionally void people, you wouldn't benefit much from this game. There's hardly any brainstorming.   For everyone else: I HIGHLY RECOMMEND THIS GAME! (Far better if you've played the first season)

Why do I keep getting kicked in the balls?? I have made the same mistake of pre-ordering and I keep telling myself not to do it. You will get kicked in the balls, I keep thinking. Again, I pre-ordered and just like x-rebirth and minerwars 2081. I GOT KICKED IN THE BALLS.  Now, for why this happened to me:  The game is definitely a game. No doubt about it. It has all the mechanics needed to be a game, but that is about it. They threw as much as the could to make the shell of the game, then they said 'Game is done' and charged us $60.00 US American dollars (Living in North America).  After playing for roughly 40 hours. Here is what I find wrong with the game so far:  1. When I take off from my ship, randomly, I will be shot up into space. No trusters, no 45 degree angle into the horizon. It seems like I have some kind of antigravity field on my ship because that sucker just takes off vertically and puts me right into the stratosphere. All complements of a weird random bug.  2. Every trading post, colonial outpost, pod, transmission towers or basically any place I visit in a planet is the same damn rehashed building with the same ingame assets. You get crafting recipes from manufacturing outposts, you get stranded ship beacons through transmission buildings, you get multi tool recipes through abandoned shelters. Everything is incredibly cookie cutter. Procedural generated.. My left foot.   3. The game ran like a dog limping home after being hit by a bike. After going to the forums and doing this and that - Opening up text files changing words from true to false, telling my video card it can't use shadows, overclocking my computer (but not too much because else it will freeze again) - The game still ran like the turd my dog took after arriving home limping. I did the experimental thing and upgraded my video card drivers and now it runs a bit better. Here is the kicker, and it is still something that still happens. I can count the amount of times I go in and out of my ship and there must be a magic number of times I go in and out until the game starts to stutter as if my computer just ran out of it's last amount of ram. My only solution is to restart the game.  4. The thought that went into solving puzzles to get rewards is outstanding. I go to a transmission building and you have to solve the puzzle of what's next - 1212, 2121, 1212, xxxx - Take a WILD guess what number you need.  5. The way the game handles the user interface. WTF! - When I find another ship the first thing it prompts me 'Compare'? Uhh yeah. Show me the god damn ship. Why should I even press the compare button. It should just show you the ship and decide if you want to take it. Now here is the kicker. I want to dissasemble the items on the ship I found and transfer them to my ship. I can't do that without me accepting the second ship as my own. WHY CAN'T I JUST TAKE THE ITEMS AND PUT THEM IN MY SHIP'S TRUNK? I also can't access my exosuit inventory while I do this, so I am forced to jugle inventory around just to be able to take everything.  6. Please give me a damn compass. Which way is the magnetic north of some of these planets? At least give me Dora the explorer here. She might have a better idea of where to go. Or give me some damn smoke signals so that I know which way I am going. Let's say I find a good source of gold ore, or I just loaded up my ship's trunk with all this stuff and now I need to sell it. Well, I can't find the trading outpost because the way point has just dissapeared (WTH). It is very difficult to find your way around because you can barely find your bearings. Sure, you can travel faster than the speed of light but these ships interior look like my 1977 Nova without a guidance system (how does that even work?) - Again, if this wasn't enough. When you visit a place you get this damn cut scene that lasts about 5 seconds as if saying 'ohh you have found this! WOW' but after the 200th time doing this you almost don't feel like even going to the saving pole. Once I leave the bulding and I want to go back to it there is a way point. The way point has a deep color if it hasn't been discovered and it is grayed out if you have already discovered it, but there is a damn bug that shows you the way point as fully deep color until you get close to it. Then it becomes grayed out 'THE RAGE.....' that is how I feel 'RAGE' when I travel for 5:45 minutes just to find a way point has already been found by me because this thing can't tell me if I was there or not before.  7. Talking about all these places you can visit in a planet. Well, if you spend enough time on a planet looking at all the buildings. Your hud will start to get filled with markers. SO MANY MARKERS to the point where you can't read what each says or how long it will take to get there. I used to work for quality assurance at a big video game company and this would've never flown (you see what I did there. Spaceship - Flown).  8. The ship's controls are completely unintuitive. Even when you manage to move the ship around with a mouse, your yaw movement with keyboard is useless. It just helps you straighten out the ship - Hell, if they are already not allowing you to crash the ship (as you can see, the ship moves out of the way when you are about to meet a flying boulder) why not just make the ship self stabilizing and only allowing you to move spin if you put it in manual mode.   9. Which takes me to one other thing. I landed my ship close to two big flying boulders. The ship decided there was no space to land and moved forward underneath the two flying boulders. Guess what happened when I tried to take off? My ship kept on hitting the boulders until it blew up. Over and over and over with no way to go in reverse or move out of the way because of these clunky controls. I had to restart my game from an earlier save. - Not cool.  10. The unecessary grind. This one is not a bug but it bugs me. Because if the game was noble enough to not insult my intelligence it would be fine to grind for that shinny new object. Make is smart, make it worthy of something. But when you are releasing a game that will make you upwards of 50 million dollars have the decency to not treat me as a stupid consumer. I have to grind in a very methodical mathematical manner to obtain upgrades in a linear fashion. Terrible. An incredible uninspiring design.   All in all, I did have fun thinking what the next corner was going to show me. What will the next planet bring and I am still working my way to a fully stocked ship before I start to really see everything the game has to offer - I just have a bad feeling it is going to make me grind towards the center somehow.   I give this game a solid 5.7/10 - I had to give it that .7 to express to you that it was almost a 6. This could've been so much more. I will update this review as I play more or find more to say.

This is at its base a pretty fun game - it plays pretty smoothly, especially once you get the knack of jump boosting, the weapons give you a good range of tactical/playstyle preference options, while not perfectly balanced they are all viable options with benefits and weaknesses. The game modes cover all the basics - Capture the Flag, base defense, team death match, free for all, but there is nothing really innovative here.  The arenas are graphically fairly diverse, but probably most of them are a bit too similar tactically, while others are a bad idea if you plan on having bots due to the limited routes and the poor AI pathing - if set to low levels you should be able beat up on the AI with its slow reactions and poor aim, if set hard the bots are pretty challenging as long as they don't get stuck on scenery, or failing to find a route to the target when playing CTF or base defense.  Generally I wouldn't buy this to play against the AI, and decent random online games are rare nowadays, but if a group of friends wanted a fun lightweight action game to play against each other regularly, this would be a decent option.

I'm trying really hard to like this game. Was super excited about it, having watched a quick look of it and dug the overall feel of it. But, having got my hands on it I can't really recommend it. The controls are super clunky and unresponsive at best, to the point where it seems like a design choice in order to make the game more difficult. I was playing on a wireless 360 controller but even when I switched to mouse n keyboard it was still a major factor. There are big sections where a well timed jump is necessary and it often seems touch and go as to whether the game registers it or not. This goes for other controls like the roll as well. With it being a 2D environment, both of these are essential in defeating enemies but neither can be relied upon in a pinch. Thats not even factoring in the difficulty in actually memorising the (seemingly random) attack patterns of bosses and their ability to spin on a dime mid combo. I'm also finding the colour scheme difficult to look at for long periods of time. Even if I know a trap is somewhere I can still have difficulty locating it and feel like I'm constantly straining my eyes. Not really liking the general enemy design, especially the blob enemies. Their attacks seem cheap and too easy for the AI to land. I'm gonna persist with the game, and would probably give the game a 'maybe' rating if I could. If you can, try a demo.. Wish I had

I saw most of the negative reviews were just nonsense rage reviews. Things like 'game sucks' with no reason why, the game looked good and i heavily suspected noob rage for all the downvotes.  ---Pro's--- So on a good note, most of the negative reviews were just noob ragers. Ive played a few MOBAs and was able to figure out the game pretty quickly, won the only 2 games i played.  the game DOES have tutorials, i saw one reviewer blatantly lie about this  The item hero set up is unique and interesting  The map is decent enough, Que times are very short, games are also pretty quick.  Plays just as well with mouse and keyboard as it does with a control pad, thats an impressive feet imo.  a ton of potential 'IF' the developer makes some key changes(see the 1st con) and works hard towards a more polished product  ---Cons--- Sadly the cons are really really bad. First off the game is VERY pay to win. You can buy in game gold with money, then use that gold to buy massively powerful buffs, or insta rez during a match. You can buy the same buffs with in game earned gold, however if you paid for gold you will have enough to spam the buffs and insta rezs constantly as apossed to a few buys here and there.  The interface is also terrible, partly becuase it was made to be usable by a control pad, however it was poorly done. The main problem with the interface is that its confusing to look at and know where to find what you want.  Only 2 cons, its a good game. but the 2 cons are really bad ones. if the game wasnt pay to win i would easily vote yes on recomenndation. The pay to win is pretty bad though, a decent player with enough money can nearly outright buy a win.  -----final thoughts----- The devlopers had a gold mine here, its really honestly a good start to a great game. Sadly they got greedy and ruined the entire thing. Ill be sad to uninstall such a promising game, but the devloper trashed it with P2W. I wont be back.

app_name: DARK SOULS™ II: Scholar of the First Sin
review_text: I love dark souls 1 it was much fun to play through the game for the first time and i have to say that i love dark souls 2 just as much mainly because the combat was more refined, polished and fluid. It wasn't as difficult as the original mainly because i had much more experience with the series and the areas in the game were less frustrating then the original or maybe the developers just made the game easier then the original on purpose i can't really tell. Another big bonus for dark souls 2 is the steady 60fps throughout the whole game, the game just felt soo much more better then the original where it had a 30fps cap and would drop to 15 fps in areas like blightown.  The level design is not as memorable as the original. You remember areas in the original like Anor Londo, Sens Fortress, Ash lake, The painted world etc. The areas in dark souls 2 are not as memorable or interesting. There are a few exceptions of course like Majula which is just beautiful and a much better main hub then firelink shrine. The world of dark souls 2 is not as connected as the original it just has branching paths from majula where in dark souls 1 every area of the game is interconnceted which each other and all link back to the main hub firelink shrine.  The pvp is alot better then the original because not everyone is trying to backstab you every fight and it overall just feels more better making it more fun. It took me 38 hours to beat dark souls 1 and it took me the same amount of time to beat dark souls 2 which was a funny coincidence. The crown trilogy dlc also adds more hours of fun and shows off some great level design and locations. I have to mention that beating the ancient dragon was one of the most satisfying things ever in a game.  9/10 (Amazing)

This the first indie title I have played and I was very impressed. The thing about this game is it revolves around understanding the mechanics and patience. I am a big fan of stealth games so I loved Party Hard. It has cool mechanics, a nice story that you learn about between levels and it presents a real challenge. The game is nice because random events take place, but the more you play you understand how they are mostly not random. Someone saw you stab that guy? if something happens to them before they get the phone then you are fine, with more events like this. A note to the developer: While I know this is an indie game I did notice a few things that dont make sense, for example when you are discovered and they tell the cop as he is leaving he just gets in the car and you are fine, and on some levels you get discovered immediately with the ninja. I noticed that this is because events happen in the game as the loading screen is still there. I could move, get discovered and die in that time frame as the ninja depending on the level spawn. Other than that I give a 9/10 because the game doesnt tell you all the controls   It took me roughly 10 hours for me to beat the main story, but the extra levels are a nice challenge.

Steam, we need a neutral button, for the love of Pete's sake.  I would much rather have seen this idea professionally adaptated into an anime series (under the right people, of course) rather than a point and click adventure game. At the end of 2.5 hours, my first thought was 'Well, there goes 2 hours I'll never get back.' I very rarely say that about the game. That said, 2.5 hours isn't bad for a $2.00 price tag. But honestly it felt like they took what could have been a very interesting concept and watered it down into something else entirely.  One big issue I had with the gameplay was that once you die, you either start over completely from the beginning of the entire game and have to hold down your mouse for literal ages (and re-choose from previous dialogues), or you have to start from where you left off at the last save point. The latter of these options still consists of holding your mouse down for ages.  Speaking of the mouse thing, there is a LOT of dialogue. If you like reading, you might like it. I struggled with reading it all, but did, and while the story itself was okay, the scripts and playout in the end were not so much to my liking.  This really comes down to personal taste and by all means, if you think you'll like it, you just might. I'm really on the fence about saying yay or nay and just out of that experience I had I have to go nay. But it's not a concrete thing. The game reminds me of Hell Girl in a sense, and that's why I feel it would have done well as an anime. But as a game, it left me looking for closure. And things without closure are hard to enjoy for me personally.  It's not bad for what it is, it just isn't for everyone. I can't recommend it unless you're okay with a lot of reading, okay with backtracking when you fail, okay with holding down the mouse for long periods of time and repeating many scenes, or, alternatively, you're going for the achievements/cards/whatever and following a guide.

Borderlands mixes the first person shooter genre with roleplaying games, set in the desert world of Pandora. You play as one of four classes of vault hunters seeking to uncover the legendary vault, led by an enigmatic voice guiding you forward.  The blend of the genres and gameplay consisting of shoot & loot is fun, although the story elements fall short of desireable. You get one ability to complement your arsenal and perks that influence your stats or ability, but there's little else of customization. Do not expect to see much variety in the environment either.  Although you can skip this game entirely and play Borderlands 2 (improves a lot) without missing out on much, it is still a fun game.  This review is based on the standard game, the DLC missions were not tried.

A solid story with a charming, quirky art style reminiscent of old school RPGs. This game is good for fans of interactive storytelling, point and click adventures, or just quality writing.  My only knocks are (1) the questionable pathing around many of the levels, and (2) the pacing of the story.   For my tastes, the level pathing has too many twists and turns seemingly for no reason other than to be complex. The controls in general are on the weak side, whether using keyboard (recommended) or mouse (NOT recommended).   The pacing of the story is a bit draggy. It is redeemed by the overall high quality of the story, but there are areas of the game that feel disjointed, and occasionally I wondered how long it would take for me to uncover a meaningful piece of the mystery. Much of the first half of the game can feel meaningless and tedious since you really have no idea what's going on. Maybe it's just me, but the game could benefit by a bit of tightening up.  Still highly recommended, especially when it goes on sale in summer or for the holidays.

app_name: The Witcher 2: Assassins of Kings Enhanced Edition
review_text: Awesome continuation of The Witcher series that is a large improvement from its predecessor by a long-shot with better graphics, combat system, animation, and acting. It only makes me more excited for The Witcher 3 to be released. My criticisms for the game include the inability to increase inventory size, zero warning as to when side-quests will expire, limited animation during dialogue, and controls that are not very keyboard friendly.   THIS GAME WILL NOT HOLD YOUR HAND FOR YOU. You are responsible for upgrading your armor/weapons, building your traits, and timing your potion intake in order to avoid having a overly hard-time with the game. Didn't spend enough points on your signs? Good luck. Have only 30 seconds of potion when the boss battle starts? Oops. Didn't craft the sword that's twice as powerful as your current one? Tough.   Also, THANK GOD the Romance cards are no longer a thing. That was a major problem I had with the first Witcher game, in that the cards had no value and served only as fan-service for horny male gamers. It kind of made me feel dirty as a male. In The Witcher 2, there is one sex scene with Triss. I hear you can sleep with the ♥♥♥♥♥s too, but I didn't mess around with that (too busy killing Drowners).

app_name: DARK SOULS™: Prepare To Die Edition
DARK SOULS™: Prepare To Die Edition
review_text: Dark Souls is like a drug. You play and play, until you get so frustrated that you feel like breaking something, but then, just before you give up and ragequit, you get that one little thing, like a ring, or weapon, that just makes you go 'I'll play for a little while longer' The rush of satisfaction and accomplishment you get after you defeat a boss is the best thing ever. You feel like a god. When I beat Drake and Josh (Ornstein and Smaugh) I shouted so loudly that my throat hurt for hours. This is a really amazing game, recommend 100%. It's also fun to listen to the misery of your friends while they play it, too.

The graphics are nice, the planets are diversified and the towns look lively and I already spend hundreds of hours on it. But. I think there's still a lot of room for improvements. First are the Techs. I miss those from the Beta. Now, even though we have the choice between 4 different for each category, I only use and had needed of 1 of each. Maybe 2 in some very specific circumstances. The playstyles now. You can only go melee or distance. That's all and it's a bit boring, especially because a lot of times, the melee fighter will be disatvantaged and will have to rely on firearms against a lot of bosses. Now, even though the game tells us we can spend the whole game growing crops, well... it's like telling me I can spend all my hours in Zelda in the fishing mini game (I did spend dozens of hours doing that admittedly...). You can grow and sell crops, you can build cities, but it won't help you finish the game.  Last, the worst point was the underwater. My hyotl doesn't swim any faster. And she drowned. Going underwater is a real chore and it's awfully slow. There should be a tech to swim at the speed you run on the surface instead of a tech to float.

This is honestly one of my favourite FPS's. It's a mixture of the tactical planning/slower pace of Red Orchestra without as much stalematey gameplay. Rounds can move quickly, but the map size ensures that you never miss the action. It rewards going after objectives over getting kills by awarding 10x the points for capturing an objective. Each role feels important, from sharpshooters to machine gunners. Finally, like any semi-tactical fps it can suffer from having bad teammates that don't communicate, but the community is overall pretty decent.  TL;DR- if you like Red Orchestra and Arma but want something a little more CQ and fast paced definitely pick it up.

THERE IS NO STOPPING METAL  This game left me speechless so many times but I will try to muster some words for this review.  Where do I begin? Oh yeah, WITH THE GENERAL ♥♥♥♥ING INSANITY THIS GAME DELIVERS  Seriously, I cannot think of a more, pure, unadultered Bad-♥♥♥ game. Nothing really tops this game on the 'hardcore' scale.  -------------------------------  Enough chatter, time for the game.  The game is surprisingly well-made, and no wonder since Double Fine, the dev team, made some perfect classic action games like Psychonauts. They had the experience and you can see it in-game.  I have to say the best thing about this game is the audio. MOTHER♥♥♥♥ING METAL. Apart from the astonishing soundtrack, the voice acting is godly and it features sacred metal beasts like Jack Black and Ozzy Osbourne, to name a few. Solid, solid voice acting and music. Your ears will experience ♥♥♥♥♥♥s in this game.  JUST LISTEN  http://www.youtube.com/watch?v=5WS57pNIq9A   A very, very, very strong point about this game is its story. I won't even go into detail as to not spoil it, but it's absolutely GREAT. Characters are very well portrayed. Cartoonish elements and humor are abundant, giving this game a one-of-a-kind flavour. One of the best cutscenes in the gaming industry. I don't even know if you can skip cutscenes or not, but no one is complaining! I never felt the need to skip a single cutscene!  Game-mechanics wise, I must say it's pretty good. It has visceral combat, a progression system, customisation, free-roam, missions, metal solos. Double Fine did a great job and the game is just fun to play, though I must say the game itself is not the center of attraction here, like many hack-n-slash action games.  Game graphics are just great. Some eye-candy moments throughout the games, but the character animations and expressions are the best. The universe is 100% awesome.  -------------------------------  The game delivers a moral, and that's the passion for metal, which I was not a big fan when I first stepped in and now, well, let's say I had a change of heart.  THE HEART OF METAL  ♥♥♥♥ING BRUTAL GAME AND YOU SHOULD KEEP AN EYE FOR IT  8/10

Nice game.....BUT it is riddled with bugs, even now.   Gameplay is a fun action game, which as everyone explains, and it is the best way to put it, you are essentially a space ninja. With friends it can be a lot a of fun, and be a good time waster, but alone it can get repetitive fairly quick. At first glance it seems like the game has a lot of areas to explore, the truth is a lot of them are recycled, much in the way it was in Dragon Age 2 (If people remember that annoyance).   As for the bugs, there are A LOT, and some are even game breaking, such as; Melee randomly stop working on both keyboard and controller (I had this happen multiple times), Chat locking up and making you unable to access any of the menus, If an alert ends while you're in the mission sometimes doors won't function, Clipping issues with some parts of the map (Fell through and above the map several times), a huge lag issue with pvp mode (both sides said everyone was telephoning around). And last but not least, not a bug but just a pain....No way to get rid of unwanted Kubrows beyond killing them off, which takes 10 days, and shouldn't have to come to that or being forced to buy another slot. It is just a waste.

I have not found one thing to really complain about in this game, and that is a rarity. The only thing I can really knock it for is the load screens, but I understand completely why they are there given that they have to make money from the game, so it has to be able to run on older hardware. I look forward to the day when such load screens are obsolete.

I see many, many reviews of this game saying how the 'difficulty spike' ruins the game for them. I am not, in any way a 'Hardcore' gamer. I hate punishing difficulty as much as the next guy. But this game didn't feel punishing to me. Every death I learned something. And every death I learned to avoid things. Every death taught me more of what Ori could do.  Finishing this game was one of the most enjoyable game experiences I've ever had. So, yes, bits get really hard out of nowhere. But finishing is so worth it.

What you get out of Offworld Trading Company depends heavily on what you expect and how much time you're willing to put into it. OTC is based around 25-40 minute skirmishes (or 10-15 if things are very lopsided) rather than being a sprawling epic. As such, there aren't a tremendous amount of different mechanics available, but what is here is very well balanced and allows for deep, varied games. Make no mistake: there is a very steep learning curve here, and a lot of the depth isn't necessarily readily apparent because there's so much going on.  Note on the learning curve: the basic concepts are fairly simple, and getting to the level of competence only requires a few bits of knowledge (that aren't necessarily obvious). Really mastering the game takes a fairly deep understanding of the game, though, and really good players can exploit small advantages to open up large leads (though those can also be countered by black market and other tools if players stay attentive).  Offworld absolutely delivers on the promise of being an RTS without units. In place of the usual micro-heavy unit management, there are myriad map quirks, prices, opponent actions, and random events to pay attention to and try to take advantages off of long-term. Even with over 700 hours put into the game, mostly playing multiplayer with some of the best players in the community, there are still plenty of things in a single game that I will fail to notice. Success at this game, rather than being reliant on high APM, depends on having good awareness and a good understanding of how and why prices will move, how to appropriately react, and especially countering your opponents.   Single player is somewhat more difficult to evaluate. The AI is relatively well-made and able to put up a fight or pose a serious challenge for newer players. With experience, there are some predictable aspects and flaws to the AI that let you beat them consistently, but until then, skirmishes against the AI should prove somewhat interesting and useful as a learning tool.   If you're a competitive person, going for the best times on the Daily Challenge can be a way to keep single player fresher. Trying to figure out what strategies work or the exact way to manipulate prices to make the AI do what you want it to do can be interesting, and with the variety of maps and restrictions set for each day's challenge, it's nontrivial to find optimal strategies. Being able to see how other players approached the same challenge (using the in-game replay feature) is useful both for learning new strategies or figuring out how to make small optimizations.  The campaign is essentially a series of skirmishes where you start with limited production and are able to unlock perks and better production as you advance. All but the final week of the campaign are a bit different from your standard skirmishes; rather than race to buy out your opponents, you are competing at investing in the central colony. You can choose from 9 different executives to play as, and a lot of work has been done to make them play differently. Once you find a working strategy and manage to build a solid foundation from the first few weeks, though, applying the same strategy over and over until the final week is fairly viable, so the campaign can drag a bit. Once you get to the final week, the standard skirmish with a different set of tools than you're used to can be fun.  Basically, if you're willing to put a lot of time into learning how the mechanics of the game interplay and are interested in multiplayer, this game will definitely reward you with a deep, consistently interesting experience. Single player can be played more casually and is enjoyable in its own right, but doesn't necessarily have a great deal of lasting appeal if you reach a point where the AI isn't particularly challenging. If you're not willing to put time into overcoming the steep learning curve, a lot of the game can seem overwhelming and incomprehensible (or else pointlessly simple if you play on very low difficulty levels), and probably isn't worth your time.  tl;dr - Buy the game if you want a rich multiplayer experience and aren't worried about the learning curve. Buy the game on sale if you're mostly interested in single player and the game's concept. Look elsewhere if you're looking for a game with an epic scale or with mechanics that are simple to master.

After playing and reviewing the original Hexcell, I realised it was the first game of a trilogy. I bought it and was pleased to see that my main complaints in Hexcell were fixed in the following games. (First Hexcell review here :  http://steamcommunity.com/profiles/76561198068982092/recommended/265890/)   Hexcell Plus is a Minesweeper-like, with hexagons instead of squares, line indicators, ' ? ' marks, etc... But the one thing that makes the Hexcell games shine is the level design! Unlike minesweeper, the levels are not random but designed. Because of this, you'll never encounter a case where you have to take a random choice and have 50% chances of winning. There will always be a path to the end of the level and we you get stuck, it's more both frustrating and motivating because you know there is a path to find.  Hexcell Plus introduce some features that were not present in Hexcell and is a nice improvement on the difficulty. Hexcell was easy for me untill the end of the game but Hexcell Plus got harder sooner. I even had to replay some puzzles at least five times to get the perfection.  If Hexcell was too easy for you but fun enough, this might be more entertaining. A fun minesweeper inspired puzzle of average difficulty!

app_name: METAL GEAR SOLID V: THE PHANTOM PAIN
review_text: Upon completion of the main game:  Graphics: 10/10: Not much to say here, these graphics are some of the best I've personally seen.  Graphical Smoothness: 9.5/10: -0.5:I counted maybe two instances where the game would slow down because of graphics, but they were just cutscenes, minor ones at that.  Gameplay Smoothness: 8.5/10: -1: Many instances Snake would start sliding on random pathetically small inclines, and many times a situation would present itself where Snake should be able to climb/drop down from somewhere, but it refuses to happen. -0.5: Vehicles 'door' areas seem to be very picky, and since the Fulton is defaultly on the same key as the action key, trying to get in a vehicle can be a tad bit difficult.  Story: 7/10: I loved the story for the most part, game did a fantastic job of making me care about the characters, the reason I butcher this score however: -1: Forced plot device with severe consquences, I'm sure people know what I am referring to. -1: If certain rumours are to be believed, the proper ending of the game was cut. -1: The game left me wanting so much more, the ending slapped me in the face with a trout, and I really don't want to wait for another full priced game for the rest of the story.  Audio: 10/10: Its all good, nothing buggy or off with it, enough said.  Music: 11/10: Love the Metal Gear music like always. +1 For managing to provoke a reaction with it too.  Online-FOB: 5/10: On the face of it, the idea seems pretty interesting, but there are a lot of problems with it. -1: Grief potential, cause a inflitration totally involves running around with an anti-material rifle and quick scoping the guards. -1: Managment issues, trying to get your good staff off the FOB into Mother Base so they don't get kidnapped just for being there is a nightmare. -1: Mandatory To Participate in, game won't let you get by a certain point without building one. -1: Almost mandatory to be there during an inflitration cause relying on the AIs to stop someone is a bad idea (Because Reflex mode is a thing too). -1 Flawed deterrance system, you are never truely safe should someone decide they want to grief you.   Thoughts: Overall, a fantastic game, would recommend, I just hope that work keeps getting put into it, and that maybe some updates/DLC will add on more to the story for reasons that I do not want to spoil here, yet I'm sure people who have completed the story know what I am probably referring to.

Since people really prefer to have these pros/cons type of reviews at the Steam pages i'll keep them like that and easy to read.  + Great graphics/cutscenes. + Good music. (It's a Square game after all.) + Interesting story that isn't as nearly as convoluted as some others in the series. + Japanese voice acting included, and it's really really good. + Most of the characters are rather likeable, except ironically the main protagonist and one spineless coward called Hope. + Tons of content and quests to do. (The game is about 50 hours long.) + If you know how to fix the game it runs fairly stable and crash free...  - ...Although that WILL require some fiddling with. - Seems like at least the first 15 hours are tutorial sections. - The game can feel like it plays itself sometimes. - Character customization extremely limited. - Game really rewards you only after you've finished the 50% mark.  Now, the PC stuff.  * Although all the negative things i'm about to say, the PC version is STILL the optimal version to play the game. Offering a stable 1080p/30fps on most rigs compared to the attrocious 720p/15-25fps average that was offered on the consoles. Just make sure you apply these fixes to both FFXIII and FFXIII-2 so you'll avoid the game breaking issues.  = Optimization is terrible, expect to play this game with locked 30fps to avoid frame dips all the time. = Disabling Steam Cloud is mandatory since it can cause your game to crash while saving and corrupt your save file. = Playing with the keyboard is not recommended and you really should own a 360 pad to get the best experience.  Overall, a pretty good JRPG plagued with technical issues and minor annoyances. I'd still recommend it.

I have spent hundreds of hours on this game, and I have fairly mixed feelings about it. On the one hand, I love the story, the lore, and the overall fell of the game. It's great to explore a fully open and realized Tamriel. But, on the other, there are some major cons. For example, I felt that, if you don't sink money into this game, you will not have as much fun as the game offers with all of it's DLC (which, by the way, is amazing, having their own well crafted main/side storylines and addictive radiant quests). Another example is the farming. Oh, the farming. This game was my first MMO, and the only one I have ever played. I am not used to farming, and do not like it. From what I see from other games, grinding seems like an optional thing to do to level up quickly. In ESO, it's almost a necessity to even level at all. There are numerous other flaws (including bugs), but I feel that these are paled by the games amazing main and side quests (except for the Daggerfall Covenant. That storyline is boring as all hell), engaging and beleivable world, and the overall charm of exploration I hold dear in every Elder Scrolls game.  Overall review: 8/10  PROS: -Great main plotline -Numerous unique and engaging side quests -Fantastic DLC -Great multiplayer experience -Polished gameplay  CONS: -Bugs. Oh so many bugs. -Farming -Game feels lacking without DLC

I put off reviewing the game for some time fearing the negative backlash I'd almost surely recieve. Nowadays it's become a bit more accepted to dislike Undertale, or simply have it be 'not your thing', but giving criticism to LISA, its darker, more 'mature' counterpart it's often compared to, seems to be entirely blasphemous.  That said, I tried my very best to like this game. I thought I would abslolutely love its handling of serious topics, gameplay, and story. The reviews on the front page affirmed my assumption, with one in particular I recall describing the game as...  'If Earthbound and Mad Max had a baby, and then David Cronenberg became that baby's abusive alcoholic stepfather.'  Earthbound and Mad Max, I suppose I can see. Like Earthbound, the game has some rather fun and engaging gameplay, more innocent and simplistic visuals despite a dark plot, and odd humor interlaced between the game's seemingly more serious segments. It's Mad Max in the sense of its setting and world, and that's about it.  But there is nothing 'Cronenberg' about this game aside from its body horror. It's missing the artistry, actual decent writing, and above all, subtlety, that makes Cronenberg so great.   Here lies the biggest problem in this game, which so many others regard as being its greatest strength -- LISA's writing. Despite masquerading as something meaningful, the experience is artificial and lacks the competence to actually pose any real or unsettling questions. It hides behind its simplicity of plot behind a deep-curtain comprised of extremely out-of-place humor, and lazy absurdity.  Prior to writing this review I read several interviews with the game's creator, Dingaling, and was especially hooked by and interested on a particular write-up he did regarding his process of telling stories. Seeing as I hated how he told stories, I felt it would give me insight and explaination as to how his methods succeeded so well with so many others, and not with me. Having read it, I got just what I was looking for. If you're interested in taking a look yourself, the link is below:   http://dingalingboy.tumblr.com/post/129856421848/lisa-storytelling   It pretty much told me what I already knew -- Dingaling is not a subtle storyteller. He tells things only as they appear to be, rather than what they imply. For many people this is enough, but for me it doesn't work. There is no right or wrong way to tell a story, but in the context of LISA, with its serious subject matters (abuse, ♥♥♥♥, genocide, death, etc.) these kinds of subjects are just not suited to such a simple approach. This is why the game fails, and why its unfitting absurdist humor, on top of its lack of subtlety, is the nail in the coffin.  Earthbound, I believe, succeeds where LISA fails. Its dark tones were a very fascinating juxtaposition to its upbeat and innocent exterior. Its off-the-wall oddball humor added to its themes, rather than muddled them. LISA is all over the place, and on a narrative level doesn't work on any front it sets out to explore.

I have only played 8 hours of this game so i have not even experienced the entire content the game has to offer but i can say that this game is highly addictive, and highly recommended for all those who are fans of simulation/management games.  The aim of the game is to take ingredients and alter them in order to create cures for specific illnesses and then sell them to make money to make more cures etc etc.  The game has a very steep learning curve, especially in relation to the later tier drugs that involve mixing. Getting these to work in a fluid manner (maximising profits) is quite challenging and requires a lot of trial and error so i highly recommend after the tutorial loading up the mode which has all the research and drugs unlocked so that you can play around and learn how to create a fluid setup of the machinery and adapt to the different building structures (there are only a few so even though its completely random you will get familiar with each one eventually).  I did read alot of reviews before purchasing this game and a few mentioned the randomness of the drugs you recieve at the start of the game and size of the building being annoying/limiting the ability to play the game. However i dont think this is as much of an issue, the game seems fairly balanced and gives you plenty of time to complete scenarios regardless of the spawn. I can see the spawns being an issue if you are an achievment hunter who wants to complete all the levels on master (the time limit is much much less), however this can be worked around if you just restart the mission a few times so in my opinion the randomness is not a big deal for those who read those reviews and got worried that the game is broken.  I also especially like the addition of a 'global economy' aspect in relation to the competing companies whereby you need to ensure that the drugs you make are better than theirs by fine tuning them or you will not sell as many units loosing you money or manufacture ones that the ai have not considered thereby creating your own monopoly of the drug (can also be done by patents).  Also a must have are the mods for this game, only ones i have found so far are the ones supplied by the actual Big Pharma website, they are very useful (example free conveyor belts).  Overall, great game, very challenging and i can see many hours of my life being eaten by it.

I have put a lot of time into this game and I have no idea why. The story is utter garbage. The new dialouge system, and talking in general, have been dumbed down. The combat is mediocre at best and the settlement building feels shallow.  ---Story--- I would set a spoiler warning in the beginning but this story is so predictable that i realy don't see the point. When I awoke from the cryo-pod I could say almost emidiatley that quite a substantual amount of time had passed since the kidnapping and Shaun wasn't a baby anymore, I wouldn't have guessed that 60-70 years had passed i'll give you that, my guess was around 30-40, but it wasn't all that unexpected. The twist that the father was Shaun was obvious for at least a couple of hours prior. The endings were all ♥♥♥♥, and the only difference was who you fight in the end and a couple of slides in the ending with no seeming difference in the world except that some types of enemies are gone.  ---Dialouge--- The dialouge system has for some reason been dumbed down from previous fallout games, although I have only played Fallout 3 and New vegas. Compared to those games this is objectivley worse as in the previous games you could see what your character is going to say and you could have less or more than four speech options and any learned skill could find a use in dialouge, being amazing with guns could be good when talking to a gun nut. In Fallout 4 you can only see a word or short frase that is maybe, perhaps describes what your character is going to say. 'Sarcastic' comes to mind, it appearently meaning to shout loudly or to make a bad joke or anything really. The only factor in speaking to anyone is charisma and you could succeed anny charisma check with charisma 1, described in fallout NV as 'Misanthrope'   ---Combat--- The combat is allright, at best. It is better than unmodded Fallout 3 and New Vegas, but modded it is more or less the same, grenade hotkey is good and the cone of fire indicator is apreciated, although this feature could be removed as it makes combat a wee bitt too easy, although having gutted your speech options, Bethesta probably thought that you should have one way to deal with the situation. The leveled feel of the game, meaning that as you level up, stronger monsters spawn, makes every enemy bullet-sponges as I quite frequently had to empty an entire clip from a decked-out combat rifle, one of the more powerfull semi-auto weapons, into a raider, an enemy frequently described as cannon fodder.  As a side note, weapons have little to no uique characteristics from other variations of the same weapon, you could give me a maxed out 10mm pistol and it would still feel the same as the starting one. this means that in vanilla there is, acording to the wiki: three different types of pistols. Two shotguns, ONE SMG, wat?, Five rifles. Advent Rising is one of those games that tries to tell a very good story behind some relatively good 3rd person dodge shooters with some fancy-shmancy powers. The result is a bit of a mixed bag though, but for a 10 year old game it held up pretty well. Unfortunately, the game is sort of a sleeper hit that never was popular enough to finish the other two games in the trilogy.   The story penned by Orson Scott Card (Ender's Game, etc.) has the Wyeth Brothers (you control Gideon, the younger brother) be the pilot taking the ambassador to a first contact with a race of aliens who worship the humans almost as Gods, and they bear bad news... while they are perfectly peaceful... their enemies, known as the Seekers, are about to wipe out all the humans, and they will be here in days.   And just as the shuttle head back for home, the Seekers arrived early with a full fleet, and started to invade the human space station. Fighting through the chaos and waves of aliens cutting through the hull and marines defending till the last, Gideon witness the Seekers appear to be scanning humans as they execute them. He was able to get his brother to escape pod, but his fiancee Olivia had been captured or lost.   Pod landed on the planet, but there's full enemy action there too. Fighting through more enemies, they arrive at the city, fought through more enemies, reached the general in charge, but was told they need to find a ship off the planet... Seekers are using asteroids... any one not getting off are dead. As the asteroid bombardment begain, Wyeth brothers have to run through a collapsing base to locate the last available ship...   Well, I've barely covered 1/3 of the game so far. Later Gideon discovered he has powers that he barely knew how to tap, and with the help of some friendly aliens, he may survive long enough to figure out what is going on... And let's just say there's enough plot twists left and right to keep you guessing.   The game itself is third-person with some vehicle sections. Basically you control this guy, very athletic that can jump, pinwheel, dive and roll, and so on. He can also dual-wield two long weapons (or short) and achieve phenomenal accuracy if you lock on to the targets. So the game basically is a lot of shooting and jumping around, even a few fisticuffs when you have no weapons (and later, fight with your powers). The problem is, you only have two active attacks, and if you need a third or a fourth you have to remap them and things get dicey, and in the late game boss fights you will need more than two attacks. Consider this a hint. :)   You do get to drive a vehicle sometimes, and the 'Scythe' is almost a bit like predecessor to HALO's Warthog. Your brother or allies will get in and man the gun for you. And it handles pretty well. It also has a bit of a booster for the more difficult jumps that you may sometimes need to make. And yes, you can run enemies over. Seekers also have hovertanks that you can borrow. There are also sometimes stationary turrets you can appropriate against Seeker gunship/dropships.   Graphics is obviously a bit dated by current standards, but not excessively so. Animation is decent, and while architecture is a bit square-ish it's not too bad.   Good story, lots of action, though a bit cliche-ish at times. This game really needs a bit of love, even though it's a decade late.

app_name: Angels That Kill - The Final Cut
review_text: In Angels That Kill, players have the “privilege” of playing the role of two indistinguishable characters in Noll City, set in a Noir-crime theme. The quietness of the game makes it difficult to be attentive to most of the dialogue since subtitles seem to be overly missed in contrast with the dark, gray scales and obnoxious bright white that populates the game. Most of the time, determining what character is being controlled at the moment is difficult unless players are gifted in multitasking and can diligently watch the sky for birds while reading the subtitles that tend to show without warning. This takes much of the immersiveness away from the game since the story tends to be overlooked in favour of the gameplay.   The basic environmental structure is bland to say the least, which is mostly the case for Noir themed media; and ironically “Bland” is a brightness option in the settings but changes are barely visible. Despite using the old-style theme in Angels That Kill, the tones utilized in the game are intense and harsh. Some players may find the game tends to strain the eye and that headaches ensue.    The gameplay is standard and simple with the options to pick up items, examine surroundings and run slightly faster than the default speed. The game implements a hunger bar but players have the option to switch this mechanic on or negate it from the beginning of the title. When the hunger bar is in effect, players must search for edible food in dumpsters to replenish it or fail to do so, which will result in your death. The hunger bar doesn’t really affect the gameplay much if players are quick to complete each chapter before the player even has to worry about it. Controls are sluggish and can be frustratingly annoying -- especially when one character is suffering through a drunken stupor -- and forces players to navigate the streets with inverted “WASD” controls that seem to interchange when you turn down a street.    Someday I would like to make another playthrough of the game if changes ever occurred.   Down below is a short video preview of the game! Check it out or don't!   https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=IThb1XIaMF4
