This is my favorite game this generation.  Just a word of warning to game hoarders; it's a game that only shows it's true value if you invest time in it. If you play it as you'd play any light platformer ('I just want to finish this thing'), you'd be missing everything that makes it so great.  This game is made for people who like to get the most of their platformer action games, not just finish them and toss them aside (not that there's too many of them worth a try nowdays). It's for all of us who were having a blast with Mega Man 9 and 10's challenges, time attack and endless modes, or were aiming for no-death runs on Hard Corps: Uprising. It expects you to take your time... learn from it, get good at it, and try all of its difficult challenges.  And by my experience, it's the modern action platformer that best achieves this feeling. After all, its a gameplay concept that's been evolving for a long time by the hand of its developers.  Inti Creates' Mega Man Zero series introduced the next step of what I felt was the natural evolution of the Mega Man X series (more so than Capcom's own in-house later entries, X7 and X8). It continued refining the ranking system and performance-based powerup prizes first experimented with in Mega Man X5, and as soon as they reached the first sequel, Mega Man Zero 2, they changed the way you play these games altogether. Now the game rewarded you with missable powerups based on your performance. In a way, it taught you how to play it well rather than just play it by 'surviving through it', so by the time you got the unlockable Hard Mode, you'd be up to it. They were somewhat short games, but thanks to these mechanics, they gained a lot of replayability.  They kept rewarding skill with Zero 3 (good times), but later softened a bit with Zero 4. Maybe they started to hears some criticism? That would explain why later, with the Mega Man ZX series, they tried something different. They kept most of Zero's core mechanics, but lost some of its pace and feel due to the introduction of interconnected maps you had to traverse 'by foot' instead of a select stage screen. It was a weird mix of slow-paced exploration feel ('thing you only want to go through once') that didn't mix well with the well achieved learn-and-repeat replayability the game was based on.  After the abandonment of the Mega Man franchise by Capcom, and Keiji Inafune's collaboration with Inti Creates as an independant game creator (he's credited as the 'action director' of this game), we finally got the next step of Inti Creates signature gameplay's evolution with Azure Striker Gunvolt in 2014.  If you've tried any of the games I just mentioned, you know what kind of tight controls and challenging-yet-totally-fair stage design and combat you can expect. There's shots, there's dash, there's wall-kicking climbing... all the best action platformers standards. Although this time your attacks are not your gunshots primarly, but a combination of shots that 'tag' your enemies, and a forcefield that'll hit tagged enemies as long as they're on screen. It's hard to explain before trying it, but it has a good feel to it, and it's a mechanic that makes this stand out among the usual run-and-shoot deal.  Even if the forcefield does the killing, you'll still get different guns as you go on, which change the way you tag your enemies. So now, instead of arbitrary 'rock beats scissor' damage charts, you get actual visible mechanics as to why one weapon is better than other depending on the situation (ex: for shielded enemies, you'll want a weapon that tags through obscables; for multiple enemies onscreen, you'll pick the weapon that tags as much enemies at once as possible; for enemies clogged together, the one that spreads behind after its first contact)  Besides the action itself, what makes this game stand out as a performance-based action game is the Kudo system. A plain score system, and the basis of how your performance is measured. The catch is: i) they multiply exponentially (the more you accumulate in one shot, the better); ii) you lose all of them if you get hurt; iii) you don't automatically get them, you have to 'cash them in'. For that, you need to either use an (optional) special offensive skill, or touch one of the (optional) checkpoints throughout the stage. Or, of course, reach the end of the stage without losing them.  What this produces is a constant gamble where the bets are your own skills. A constant wondering of 'am I good enough to skip this checkpoint, so I can keep adding Kudos until the next one'? Then there's another layer of decision: extra score multipliers on top of the total depending on how fast you finish the stage. 'Should I rush through, risking taking a hit, or take my time to build up Kudos safely?'  Which makes this work so well, is how the 'retries' system is handled: there's no extra lives. No penalties for retrying, or damage taken. All that matters is your Kudos. So if you decided to take a checkpoint, you can retry the next section of the stage as many times as you want. However, for the absolute best ranking, S+ (which is the goal of the most rewarding in-game challenges and Steam achivements), you'll need to multiply your Kudos as much as possibly... that is, no-damage (or almost no-damage) runs.   But it manages to make it less tasking than it sounds, because you have plenty of less demanding challenges to practice and learn the stages from. Besides, you'll get betters skills and equipment over time; yet another incentive to keep practicing. You'll be grinding money and equipment components on each stage run: the better your performance, more generous the prizes.  With all that, the feared no-damage runs become very achievable to anyone who goes through everything the game has to offer.   This is not one of those games you'll finish once, then find a youtube video of someone doing a speedrun and think 'that's just crazy'... The game is designed for you to reach that point gradually. And it feels awesome.  Overall, that's the point. This is a game designed to make you feel awesome while playing it. Besides guiding your way up to the more difficult challenges, there's a lot of tiny details that enforce this. The sound effects are satisfying, the animations are beautiful, the UI is sexy (just seeing that huge Kudos score grow ends up being addicting), etc. The cherry on top of all: the regular BGM changes to some very pumped up, DDR-style, J-POP tunes when you're above a certain point of Kudos. The feeling of 'you're awesome, just keep at it' is enforced, not only by the score system, but every aspect of the game.  Even though the enemy variety is slim, I think it's better for it. Having a limited amout of hazards reinforces the fact that these are obstacles you're supposed to learn from easily. It works great as it is.  As for the Steam version: I played this game first on 3DS when it launched two years ago, and now I'm having a blast doing all the challenges all over again. The port is not perfect, it has some flaws (locked on 30fps; you have PC-specific setting on the launcher and others on in-game menus; the controller customization is limited, I had to take Xpadder to assign the special skills to actual buttons because they're locked to either F keys or the controller's analogue)... but it feels really good to be able to play such a powerful action game as it should: with a proper controller in my hand, with a proper screen in front of me... instead of on a crummy handheld.  (Theres new game mode too! And a more faithful retranslation with restored dialogues cut from the 3DS ver.)  So I IMPLORE Inti Creates that they port to Steam the upcoming sequel as well. After having my favorite action platformers locked in small handheld screens for so long (since the Game Boy Advance days, that it), having Azure Striker Gunvolt on PC is a godsend.

There are not enough accolades that I could ever try to give this game.  Look...I know you'll hear a lot of people calling this a mix between space invaders and galaga...and,I suppose I can see it,galaga wise, with the well evolved power up system...but,to me...and I don't think there are any space invaders influence discrepancies...and bonus on them for using my favorite space invader as their Mario....but I see,hear,and feel centipede as the rightful mate of space invaders in all of this.  Let me just say that my love for this game is based upon the endless mode. The wave mode is wonderful...but simply not as gripping...although the boss battles are epic.  While you may not dedicate your life to this game, it will turn you out every time that you come calling. It will leave you breathless... and you will feel your heart begin to beat again as the alien mushroom clouds and explosions subside... and you see your own mushroom cloud... the air returns to your lungs...  This game is intense.  9.11.15.   Ok.so I have dedicated more time to the wave mode.  I now find it equal to endless.  You would miss out quite a few interesting characters by not embracing it.     To the Devs:  All of the bright explosions...especially when the screen is full and the aliens are dying like flies in a fire...  is like Jedi mode for me...relaxing amidst a sea of chaos. Just feeling where I am.  And...the screen shift... I never really noticed it until I saw someone mention it in a review.  Thank you for your obvious aches and pains of love that delivered this work of art.  I look forward to more from you.

When I first downloaded it, I seen the 40mb download size, and thought, 'This is either going to be really simple, or really bad.'. While the game itself is simple, the caveat of actually learning it is daunting. I downloaded it last night, and already have 5.5 hours on record (Rolling the galaxy as a rush delivery courier in dual Carrier's). The graphics are simplistic, (I recommend the high DPI DLC), the actual gameplay is more taxing. Constantly balacing mortages, and crey payments on a day to day basis, while maintaining a high cash flow is what takes up most of my time. Now for the pros/cons list.  Pros: Small download Straight forward gameplay Massive amount of NPCs to interact with. Sound is good considering the game's theme of emulating a 2002 game. The size of the area you are in. Randomly generated map on each new pilot. Skippable tutorial.  Cons: New players may buy the Sparrow thinking I'm just going to go around and kill everything like a pirate, then find out EVERYONE is much bigger than you, with bigger weapons. Some trial and error is involved. Hyperspace jumping is one tile at a time, so it takes quite a long time to get anywhere Buying anything but a cargo ship at the beginning basically means death, as the real money is made shipping things. Tutorials are a bit lacking, even including the wiki. Outfitters are few and far between. Not telling players 'BUY THE STAR MAP WHENEVER YOU SEE AN OUTFITTER' makes new players a bit lost on how to get around.  All in all, after you get past the learning curve of how to play, the game is quite enjoyable. 4.5/5

This game is amazing! It's like a mix of Devil May Cry, Legend of Zelda, Portal, and Fable The Lost Chapters and balanced nearly perfectly. Combat is fluid and fun and the insane amount of combos and moves makes itfresh and not repetitive (unless you make it repetitive by just spamming the mouse button :P) Power ups are cool and are utilized well IMO. There's a lot of action but also a lot of puzzles and many oppurtunities for exploration. It's an open world and many paths to other areas require puzzles and such and most of the puzzles don't feel tedious and are challenging but very intuitive. There are few exceptions (freeing Azrael took FOREVER and was kinda tedious). Boss fights were a highlight in this game. Bosses were tough but had patterns that you'll pick up on after a while. But using the patterns to your advantage takes skill and precise timing. There are collectibles that help you, like finding artifacts that you can trade in for currency, 10 pieces of armor you can find, chests that include either currency, wrath, health, health or wrath shards to increase your total capacities, trinkets that show you where chests and artifacts are in the area, keys, etc. Cool gadgets and gear offer more variety and also help in puzzles such as a grapple, the voidwalker which is essentially a Portal gun, the shadow mask to go back and forth between the shadow realm and your realm (no this isn't Yu-Gi-Oh in any way), etc. Plus you get to ride a horse that can even help in combat. Controls are great IMO, and I played on the keyboard. Left Alt for dash felt weird at first, but after finding a good placement of my hand on the keyboard it became easy and flowed well. It took me about five minutes or less to get used to. The using CAPS LOCK + 1, 2, or 3 for abilities was the only thing I thought was a bit tricky. I rarely felt the need to use them, though. Also underwater movement was fluid and fun, which is rare I think in a game. The story was really engaging. The ending also had a great little twist. Voice acting was great, save for a few parts, with Mark Hamill doing a full-on Joker voice for the Watcher and Liam O'Brien, the guy who voices Gaara in the english dubbed Naruto series sounding like an ancient version of Gaara as War, the main character. Yes, it's just as awesome as it sounds. The added ability to pick up items in the environment such as cars or telephone poles and use as weapons only added to all the chaotic fun. The boss death scenes were all brutal and soooooo satisfying. I highly recommend this game, it is amazing and the exploration and hidden places and objects adds to replayability.

Pros: Neat color palette - i like the art style Equipment can be upgraded Offline mode was designed, in response to requests from users Online play means you can team up with others Enemies are kinda neat, and there's some cool elemental equipment you can craft, such as traps and bombs  Cons: Very repetitive. I mean, SUPER repetitive. Seriously. It's basically the same map every mission, which consists of: Kill animals for food, hunt Wesen (supernatural baddies, ie werewolves), repeat. Speaking of Missions, they generally consistsof 'Collect food' or 'Kill baddies.' Occasionally its 'Find NPC on the map somewhere with a limited form of radar. Scour every corner of the map for about an hour, click the NPC once, then leave.' Every town appears to be the same. I might change my opinion on this game in the future, but untill that time this is my current stance. To best describe this game, you have to ask yourself a simlpe question: How many times are you comfortable restarting a game for a desired effect? I'm not talking about game crashes, I'm strictly talking about restarting a mission or backing out of a non-restartable mission to try it again at a cost. If you answered yes, I pose another question: are you willing to pay actual money (for optimal play, around $30 in DLC) to have the chance to complete a mission the way you want to? IF at any point you said 'no, that stupid and rediculous.', than you understand how I feel about this game after four months of playing. I found that there are very few missions that are actually worth playing, such as Shadow Raid, Hoxton Breakout, Ukrainian Job, and the basic bank heists. The rest of the missions are rediculous either due to their difficulty, payout, or lack of alternative methods. Aside from all of that, I really like the masks. Alot of the maks are really interesting and stylish depending on who you play as. However, I still have gripes with the thing I like most about the game. There is no way to redo the masks if you want to change them later and you have to earn most of the masks, paterns and even colors through playing a crap ton of little missions (i think the odds of an infamous item is 1 in 100 or so). I have nothing wrong with earning masks, but I do have a problem with the lack of customization and not being able to (dear god, I cant believe im saying this) buy/sell/traid masks via steam or main menu store. Masks are simply cosmetic, unlike the safe weapons, so I dont see a reason why it can't bee a thing. The final point I want to make about Payday 2 is that if I could add one thing and one thing only to this game I would add a 5th pager, because if there is one thing that would make more of the stealth missions more tolerable or wothy of play, it would be that. In conclusion: play it with friends/solo with booze, or you will have a bad time.

First of all, a bit of background. I've played every single Battlefield game since 1942 up to 3. Every single Call of Duty since the start up to MW2. I quit FPS games a while back because they grew stale and boring and only centered around one area for fun and that became an area in which I needed change.  I do play FPS every now and then, primarily when it's not a bread-and-butter FPS game. CoD and Battlefield remakes stretch on to the horizon and very few have any flair or charm or anything that really pulls you into the game short of just basic, solid shooter mechanics.  . . . This game doesn't even have that.  This game attempts to recrete historical battles in WWI by enacting trench warfare. This is what got my attention. It was an area that had not been really explored much by the FPS genre (excluding the new Battlefield 1 coming up soon) and the premise really intrigued me. That is ... till I played this abomination of a game.  I could continue waxing poetic-like and spinning a story about how bad this game is but I'll get down to the basics. Pros and cons. Good staple.   Pros:   + True trench warfare. You stand up, you die. Hugging the ground and hiding behind obstacles are your best chance at survival. + Mostly one shot bullet mechanics. These were slugs. Bullets in WWI were not aerodynamic and thus were more forceful. The game holds true to this. + Squad based mechanics. You are rewarded for listening to your NCO. Are rewarded for doing tasks that benefit your role, such as covering fire for the machine gunner.  Now, onto the ...   Cons:   + Terrible audio. Impossible to tell what directions things are coming from or flying half the time. + Bullets rarely go where you aim them. I understand this might be to 'replicate' the feeling of this historic time but it only serves to frustrate the player. + Exceedingly slow transition time from standing-to-crouch-to-prone and vice versa. Going from one to the other is horrendously slow. + Slow time to get into looking down the sights and back again. Takes almost a full second to look down the sights and another to get back. + Cannot reload unless completely standing still. Not even moving is allowed otherwise it stops reloading. + Registry feels off. Unsure on this, I gave up before I could test this more (primarily due to my bullets not landing where I was aiming) + Repetitive maps (yes, already I am saying that) + No killcam. (Some may dislike this but it assists players in learning what they did wrong and what the other player did right.) + Sluggish/Archaic controls. Crouch/Prone is a toggle. Cannot change this. All movement feels as if you're slugging through a bog.  In summation, this game is something I had hoped to really enjoy. I tried giving it a fair shake to learn the ins and outs of it. However, it's extremely two dimensional. It doesn't parade to be anything else but a basic, bread-and-butter shooter with a theme: Trench Warfare. It manages this, but only barely. Even with all my vehemence for EA and their ♥♥♥♥♥♥♥ child: DICE, I truly hope that Battlefield 1 does a better job at trench warfare than this abortion of a game.

This game, in many ways, mirrors some of the accomplishments of F.E.A.R. (for obvious reasons); for instance, it forces players to choose and use a small selection of weapons at any one time, and the game has a sense of immediacy in its control scheme lending itself to direct action (I half expected to nail enemies with flying kicks). The otherwordly effect of a child's voice conjured experiences of Alma Wade, as does the duality found within the persona and actions of the antagonists' structures. With these similarities in mind, is Betrayer a good game?   Yes.  Betrayer is a good game, and stands on its own merits: a strong narrative, striking presentation, and effective use of audio. The dependence on audio cues is rewarding. Dialogue between the player character and non-player characters are accomplished through simple trees, whose options are unlocked when clues are discovered. Roaming the world of Betrayer harkened to Silent Hill, but was animated and alive in a way that allow a player to imagine themselves in the midst of a breathing, sighing forest, tinged and tainted with subtle blood-red cues. When action happens, the player's speakers tend to explode with a rushing, beating heart, and enemies are deadly.  The challenging enemies serve to emphasize the theme of the game, and its narrative. There is a lot of text in Betrayer, and it is well-written; the investigations often reveal cruelty or tragedy, and all tie into the eponymous conceit of the game. I'm not sure if spoken dialogue would have improved things, because voice acting on a budget can do more harm than good, and often precludes narratives from achieving the depth text otherwise provides.  While well written and compelling, the text of the game can sometimes be confusing. Players have to backtrack and search areas again, which made the later game feel systematic rather than fluid.   Stick with it and its rewards will yield themselves.

A truly fantastic RTS, this game really should go up on the shelf alongside Command and Conquer, World and Conflict, and all of the other classic Real Time Strategy games out there. Wargame: Red Dragon is the refreshing 2014 installment and sequel to the 2013 Wargame: Airland Battle. Red Dragon is the third installment in the series and provides new features and choices for gameplay while retaining the best features of the Wargame: ALB. This time the Cold War moves from it's Theater of War in Northern Europe to the Pacific and Eastern Asia. Fans of the previous Wargames franchise as well as players new to the series will be really delited with this latest sequel as developer Eugen Systems debuts it's 2014 installment in the Wargames series. Oh Yeah, did I mention they added Naval Combat?!  Singleplayer: For those of you who have played Wargames before, whetehr it be Wargame: European Escalation or Wargame: Airland Battle, you will know that the plot line for the franchise is set in a ficticious alternate historical timeline set in the late 1970's and well into the 1980's. Wargame: Red Dragon features four new campaign plot lines set during the cold war in Asia; each is set in a different time with a different faction. The reason there are four different campaigns is because of the fact that each campaign goes along with a difficulty setting: command the South Korean military forces in order to repel an overwhelming North Korean invasion force on Easy Difficulty. Try reigniting the Sino-Soviet border conflict as the Chinese on Medium Difficulty. Repel an immense offensive by the PLA of China in an attempt to reclaim Hong Kong as the British on Hard Difficulty. And for the finale, lead a conquest of the Japanese mainland before the American forces can arrive in order to support their comrades in distress as the Soviet Union on Very Hard difficulty. So, with all of these different campaigns the player is given more choice in how they go about tackling a situation then in most RTS campaigns which I found to be really enjoyable since the game conforms to your playing style. I think that the developers of this game did a great job in not just the way the game plays but also in the storyline in order to make it as realistic as possible but still balanced enough to fight a fair war. Choosing the late Cold War as a time to set an RTS is really interesting because weaponry and warfare are still balanced at this point between either the PACT faction or the allied NATO forces. The context of the setting takes place before the information era so weapons systems haven't quite yet reach a point a point where a drone strike can whipe out a unit of hostil forces with the push of a button from all the other side of the globe. This games storyline and multiplayer provides two different ideologically parted empires waging violent and destructive skirmishes across the globe. While Wargame: Red Dragon's campaign is a fantastic embodiement of these features and incorporating them into it's storyline, the only thing that bugged me a bit was how in the singleplayer storyline the conflicts didn't seem to have much connection to each other and should have been drawnout longer.   Multiplayer:  While the Wargame series is infamous for it's setting during the Cold War, the embodiement of these factors is better brought out through the multiplayer experience. Set on a number of maps throughout Asia (I presume) the multiplayer element feels the same and doesn't stray too much from Wargame: ALB with the exception of new Asia based factions and units added to the game. In terms of map diversity however, the game does feel a bit different in the details. The more traditional maps of the first two Wargame installments were set in European countryside towns with some Urban presence including buildings for infantry to duke it out in, but usually it would be forests and farms. Wargame: Red Dragon's maps are different both visually and aesthetically. Since the addition of naval warfare to the series, the maps in Wargame generally revolve around coastal areas with dense jungle and plots of rice farms. The forests have now generally been replaced by jungle. One of the latest items of the maps for this game I was excited about was the introduction of denser maps which means more urban areas and denser jungles which really allowed for the tank battles to take place further away then the infantry battles. The overall experience of Wargame multiplayer feels like it's gotten a refreshing new update and I would definitely recommend this game to anyone looking for a good pop-in and pop-out RTS.

It's the city-builder that you wanted but didn't think existed. There are a number of factors to making a successful city, all of which logically make sense once you know what to do. Don't expect your first city to be a hit, infact go into it planning to fail and just try to develop your strategy for success. Most likely you'll be running short of funds and be anxiously awaiting your income ticks so you can build your important buildings like Fire/Police Stations, etc. But that just adds to the satisfaction when you finally build a good size town and can start expanding at will. One of the cool features is the District Drawing Tool, which lets you assign different laws to different portions of your city. Want to have a ghetto slum area near your factories? No problem! High class wealthy skyscrapers with recycling and legal weed? No problem! Plus there are Wonders like the Space Elevator that require special monuments that can only be built when you accomplish certain criteria. Worth purchasing but wait until it goes on sale unless you NEED a city builder this very second.

I don’t usually write game reviews, but I’ve enjoyed Hyper Light Drifter so much I feel compelled to do so. Additionally, I’ve taken care to keep this review spoiler free.  At first glance the gameplay is familiar, and is something akin to A Link to the Past. This coupled with the old-timey graphics gives it a nostalgic feel, but HLD is much more than a reskinned Zelda clone. First, the game is challenging and at times down right hard. However difficult, I found the game to be fair. In fact, HLD rewards good planning and practice. For instance, practicing timing drifts/attacks, and making thoughtful use of each firearm will make or break some fights. There were some battles that I was simply overwhelmed the first couple of attempts at them, but after some reflection and planning, I would breeze through what I previously thought to be near impossible.  What struck me next was that HLD is an explorers dream come true. The game is packed full of secret areas to uncover. In these areas could be anything: new customs, currency, weapons, or something more mysterious. In fact, the element of mystery and discovery is a major theme in HLD. The story in HLD is not told in a simple spell it out for you sort of manner. Quite the opposite actually. Instead, you are left to piece together cryptic clues about the broken world your character lives in. Again HLD reward being thoughtful and meticulous. It is entirely possible to traverse the entire game and not understand what is happening.  Finally, the world is perhaps my favorite aspect of HLD. The music and the masterfully crafted zones invoked powerful emotions. At times I felt excitement, wonder, but also sorrow. By the end of the game I felt connected to the world, its inhabitants, and most importantly compelled to uncover its darkest mysteries (an ongoing process for me and many others). Really my only complaint is that of any great gaming experience, the journey while enjoyable is over far too soon.  TLDR: Hyper Light Drift has great music and pixel art, is challenging but fair, and drops you into a mysterious shattered world that begs to be explored end to end.

Don't let the negative reviews fool you, the game is moderately RNG based, but it is fair.  -Pros- Amazing artwork Sounds, narration, etc. is done very well Allows many combos and rewards (or punishes) you handsomely for taking risks It's difficult, in a good way. Like Dark Souls Allows a variety of ways to customize your characters, and even allows changes mid-expedition Is never truly 'unfair.' RNG can be a cruel or benevolent god The game warns you before you load in to the actual game. It tells you that it will be tough, your heroes will die, the game won't hand you everything on a silver platter. You have to work for it, which makes it fun  -Cons- If you are the type of person who wants everything to go well and for all your characters to live, this game is not for you Only for those who can handle a game meant to be difficult RNG can seem to be a bit unfair at times. Emphasis on 'seem' Failure will result in a huge setback. For some that may be a con, for others it may be a pro  Overall, if you love a difficult game, this is the game for you. If you can't handle your characters dying, think twice about this before buying it

This is a two button game. When an enemy approaches you on the left, you left click to kill them. Right click for enemies on the right side. And that's it for controls. The game could have stayed that simple, but it quickly ramps up with enemies that take multiple hits to kill, boss fights, QTE based brawlers, equipable skills, weapons, and even more crazy stuff. But as the complexity grows and the enemies get faster and faster, you're still just clicking left and right. It's kind of unreal, and legitimately exciting and tense when the speed ramps up. This game has no business being as good as it is, but ♥♥♥♥♥♥♥ if it isn't impressive what they did with so little.

Well this is a risky review, but all I can do is give an honest opinion on my experience.  Let's start with this game is not something you have seen before, don't try to fit it in with minecraft or elite dangerous, don't compare it to FO4 or COD, it's not like anything you've ever played before. It's a true work of art that masquerades as a game, look at the science involved in the generation, real world science.  I'll start with cost as that's a beast, Hello Game is a British company, if you have any idea of what's going on in the world I imagine you know what Brexit is and how it's raised the cost of everything in my Country, this is a £25 game pre-Brexit, sorry about my fellow idiots, they did mess everything up for me as well.  I've been waiting for something to stratch the exploration itch, when this was released it was also mentioned that there would be base building, for me that's what I was looking for, a galaxy to explore and being able to call somewhere home. When the Foundation Update dropped and I saw what it included, that was all I needed to try the game out. I'm not even close to being finished with it, but I think that I've put in enough time to now have an opinion.  It's a space exploration game, yes it's a bit grindy, yes it's beautiful, there's quests, upgrades, ships, tools, space freighters, pirates and aliens. Every planet is very different, even when they initially look the same, the detail in the generation is incredible and unfortunately appears to be overlooked by most, I've discovered hundreds of stars, planets, plants and animals in my travels through space, I'm busy learning three alien languages to allow me to understand the races I meet. There's moral puzzles, maths puzzles, language puzzles and puzzles based around the races lore.  An example, I found a monolith on a planet, when I interacted with it, it began to melt and I could see a creature frozen in the ice, the monolith gave me two options, I could leave the creature trapped or try to free it, I chose to free it, wrong choice, I was actually distubing a sacred tomb and it lowered my standing with the race faction. It's a one off choice, there is no changing your mind or having another go.  I actually love that I made the wrong decision for the right reasons and was punished for it, it's just not something you see in games, the entire galaxy is full of little events like this, where choices on a human level are just wrong. As I explore further I keep finding little snippets of lore that teach me about the races, their culture and history, which in turn enables me to actually start to figure out the moral choice of the race rather than my own.  All through this I'm on a mission to gather as much knowledge about the galaxy as I can, this in turn gives me access to better everything, but best of all it happens naturally just by exploring.  Yes, there's space battles, land battles, creature fights, the sentinels (space police), looting freighters, environments that'll kill you, but even on the harshest planets, devoid of life, there's beauty to be seen, exploration to be had, and knowledge to learn.

This game is beautiful. It reminds me of when I used to play Fate: it's a simple dungeon crawler, and yet it feels like so much more.   The artstyle of the game is fun, and it makes you enjoy looking around, even if there isn't much to interact with (from what I've seen so far). And the music is stunningly wonderful. While repetitive (which of course it will be, if you're in an area for long enough), but it's just so good. Some of the dialogue is entertaining, and a little of it got a chuckle out of me, while other bits tugged a heart string or two. The game has different times of day, as well as weather, from the looks of it. Which makes it pretty immersive. You, every NPC, every enemy, and every torch give out a radius of light around them (as does your mouse when you scroll around the map!). So you can see where everyone is even if it is dark or you're in a cave.  Leveling up is quite simple: just accrue enough XP to level up and it does so automatically. You get to customize your character as you see fit (though I'm not quite certain what everything does yet). Even earning the currency (Silver, in this game) is pretty simple, if not a grind. Simply slay monsters and sell the loot you get (so far, that's all I've been doing).  The only thing I am slightly put-off by is the lack of a map scrolling. The only way of looking at the map is opening it up (the button on the right, right below the minimap), and even then you cannot zoom in or out. The combat is wonderful, but it will take some getting used to for some players.  Overall, I have definitely found a good game, and I'd like to congratulate the developers on a job well done! If anyone remembers playing Fate, you might very well like this game too! And for this price, it's a steal!

All I can say is that this game is the most philosophical game on Steam so far. I can easily recommend it if you feel like entertaining the intellectual part of your mind. This game deserves at least one reward.

Unlike most on Steam I cannot give this game a bad rating simply because there are features missing that do not effect my gameplay (minus the online).   So as far as the gameplay goes, I enjoy this game very much despite lack of 'next-gen' effects and online. It feels smoother and the 4K boost is very nice comparing it to my console versions. All the costumes are there as promised, however controller support needs some work... though it is playable, there are a few buttons that are not mapped properly.   local co-op is a nice touch especially most PC games do not integrate this feature into their titles. Lack of online, achievements, and other main Steam features is disappointing considering the game was delayed for more than a month and most of the game was ported from the PS3 version.   As far as the lack of features go... If Team Ninja was outgoing, they would have made a proper PC version with beautiful textures and launched with the key features. It would have sold nicely and brought more attention to the Steam version. Hopefully in the near-future they will patch the game with current-gen textures and effects so it will get the attention that it deserves.  If you're like myself and enjoy a good fighting game for the enjoyment then I recommend this, however If the game is not fixed within a timely matter than I would wait for a price drop. I will also edit my review after proper alterations are made to the game.

Space Hack is a Diablo-esque ARPG made by RebelMind. It was released pretty late in the whole Diablo hype, though, and is kind of lost in the many games that exist today. The game may have been great in it's prime, but in the modern age of gaming, this game fails to impress, and I do not recommend it. Though the game is a dollar, so the choice is up to you.   Space Hack claims to offer '50-80 hours of gameplay!' Note that this is on the retail box. While this does sound like a great reason to buy it, I was pretty bored within the first 5 hours. The game does some pretty low-blow crap, like having to travel out for an objective, get severely hurt, travel back to camp, heal, head out, get hurt, return to camp, heal, ect, ect. This system was obviously created to make the game longer. And it's a ♥♥♥♥ing pain. The music has no specific genre, and really never tries to create an atmosphere. The visuals are pretty poor, even for the time period that the game was created in, and the game is very inconvenient to play on a modern computer. The game runs at a really low resolution, somewhere around 720x600, and if you have a second monitor, it'll ♥♥♥♥ around with it. Even if there weren't these minor inconveniences, I still woulden't recommend this game. While the gameplay is decent, it basically borrows 80% of gameplay from Diablo 1. You might as well play that.   At the end of the day, I tried to enjoy Space Hack, but I just coulden't. Since it's a dollar, though, the choice is up to you. Maybe you'll enjoy it more than I did.

As a seasoned gamer, I usually approach games with a clear assumption and expectation that I can complete them somewhat straightforward. This illusion was completely shattered and humiliated after just one minute in the fast-paced and unforgiving world of Velocibox. The amount of deaths was nearly proportional with the amount of seconds played, and after just ten minutes I seriously questioned my abilities to even reach level two, let alone complete the game in level eight.  Though, while Velocibox share the same rouge-like elements (no pausing or resetting) as other endless-runners, then Velocibox is very unique due to the controls and graphics. You are allowed to utilize the ceiling and walls (in fact, you are more or less required to do so). This means that all obstacles may be completed in different ways – all depending on the next obstacle, certain approaches may be preferred. And with 70 different patterns there are  a lot  of options – and a lot of frustration when your plan fails miserably.  Additionally, the cubes needed to advance to the next level are rarely placed in line; thus forcing the player to hunt these while spinning and dodging obstacles. The player is required to collect six cubes to advance to the next level, though these may be collected when the player see fit – the level will continue to run until the player catches six cubes or dies. Your score, however, is depending on how fast and how many boxes are collected – the more cubes as fast as possible, the more points. The player is thus encouraged to take chances for a higher score.  Mastering the controls while comprehending the camera movement and constant speed is a strong indicator that Velocibox indeed is a  skill-based  game – somewhat similar to games like Super Hexagon. It requires fast reflexes and a seemingly impossible overview – though, with enough tries the player learns how to approach the individual obstacles and master the controls properly. However, once the player finally advances to the next level, the aforementioned colours turn out to be a real enemy; because while the controls are essentially the same, the drastic change in colours means a loss of the vague overview. Similar, each new level introduces new obstacles – something which ultimately means the player is forced to slowly progress as they master each level better and better. The better they master the first level, the easier they may approach the second, and so forth.  Velocibox is an extremely challenging and fast-paced game with minimalistic graphics and a very intriguing gameplay. Advancing through the levels requires patience and a great amount of concentration – and despite the thousands of tries, the game still remains fun, entertaining and extremely challenging. The key is undeniably to master your reflexes and understanding the art of re-orientation, and doing so successfully is highly rewarding. Don’t be put off by the first many failed attempts, but rather try and experience the satisfaction by reaching level two (and three, and four, and…).   Good luck!

ADOM was the first roguelike game I ever played and I've tried many since my first encounter with the game in the early 2000s. I've yet to find another game with similar depth and the ability to suprise me on most playthroughs, it's still my favorite roguelike game ever!   The facelift with the graphical tiles is a welcome change, but if you enjoy the old-timey ASCII graphics, they're still there for you! Druid's duel is a game where you have to manage your resources called mana that you are allowed to spend each turn based on the number of squares you possess on the board and the goal of the game is to eliminate your opponent from the board. There is 3 classes of druids that can transform into a specifc animal, the wolf that can travel through a good amount of terrain quickly however cant attack while tansformed, the eagle that can jump into enemies or simply drop on a square in firing range and it is the only ranged unit and the bear that can travel 3 squares and kill enemies that are present in them while transformed and in his normal form he can travel 2 squares or simply walk to and kill an enemy in a 2 square range. The single player isnt very interesting the story is mostly irrelevant and the bosses straight up cheat by having incredibly long range and multiple lives and you have a few matches agaisnt normal enemies in between and sometimes you will face the same dull bosses again. I honestly got bored so I stopped halfway through the campaign. The multiplayer is okay but matches with more than 2 players usually means that one of the players will probably not continue it and most of my matches ended up in people just leaving because the players close to the leaving player region get free boarding control wich gives them the advantage.  The game has asynchronuos matches and real time matches however unless you set it up with some friends or with someone in the foruns you will most likely never get to play a live match.  Also this worth mentioning the highest ranking player is the dev nobody else bothered to get that high rank and I bet most people simply got bored along the way. Also one of the biggest complains I have about the game is the repetetive soundtrack the single player has some varianhts however the multiplayer doesnt. So overall the game might look interesting but his lacking in complexity and it is extremely repetetive.

This game was kinda meh to me, but it had some redeeming factors.  Pros : - The price of admission is cheap - The game has some pretty unique mechanics - It has a sombre and eerie atmosphere, if you're into that kind of thing - A few scary moments - You play as a little robot helping a diver survive, which is cute - Lack of weaponery creates a sense of vulnerability when faced with enemies  Cons : - Takes a while to understand what things are and how things work (no tutorial) - Confusing, unhelpful U.I (User Interface).  - The game is one big escort mission. Your guy can easily get stuck/killed which is frustrating. - No checkpoints. Especially frustrating when the level is long and tedious and you die to something stupid. - Essentially boring. The game has a very slow pace and lacks excitement of any kind. - Suffers from Missing Key Syndrome (MKS). Progress can be blocked because you couldn't make out a black item next to a black wall and black background... - Hacking system does not have a mini-game. You simply wait and you have a % success rate. Kinda lame.  So basically, get this game if is on sale for super-cheap for and interesting experience.

453 deaths. In under 10 hours. I beat dark souls three times.   Welcome to DeadMaus!  The biggest difference between this game and dark souls is the ability to not die! There are five primary reasons as to why you will die in deadmau5:  - no estus flasks; you'll find items that heal you but they are all consumed - the vision is limited not only by your screen, but you can't see anything that's not lit up by a light source; this game is dark in a very literal sense of the word  - Traps. Are. Everywhere. - 90% of the time, you will not see the traps (that. are. everywhere.) until you spring them - the enemy AI's are evil geniuses operating under the evil power of the evil Mother Brain evil evil  And yet, after all all of this death, I still recommended this game! Dark Maus is not fun because it directly mimics the Dark Souls games, but because it flawlessly emulates I Wanna Be The Guy via great use of the mechanics of the Souls series. In consequence of using Souls's mechanics, the game offers plenty of replay value through its weapon variety, self-handicaps and how you take care of baddies. And, because it's like IWBTG, you get to die a lot in a lot of hilariously unfair ways. This game's a very odd kind of blast, but a blast nonetheless.  Its only real shortcomings are its lack of story and its fairly plain (albeit spoopy) sound track. But this game costs $10 and was made by one guy, so there's that.  I definitely recommend this game to anyone looking to jump right into a challenging game for cheap, so long as they don't mind leaving behind their MLG KDR.   8/10. Jump right in! Good times are to be had at a critter's expense!

Cthulhu Realms is is a deckbuilding dueling game.  Unlike say Magic the Gathering or Hearthstone, you do not preconstruct decks, you start with a fixed set of cards and then modify your deck as you play, balancing getting cards that add to your economy with those that do damage or thin out weak or useless cards.  It using most of the underlying concepts of Star Realms, but brings its own twists and flavour. There is a lot more interaction with the ability to remove or abjure cards and significantly more complicated reactions and symbols which does make it a less readable game.  There is are two AI based single player campaigns, one of which you get for free. To play online, or to play the second campaign you have to buy the DLC. Worth noting that the purchase and account is shared with the phone apps, buy it once play it anywhere. Also keep in mind that the multiplayer is turn based, there are no interrupts and so you can comfortably play a game out over the course of a week or so, or if both you and your opponent are about and actively playing then you can knock a game over in as little as 10-15 minutes.  I have spent a lot more time on Star Realms than I have on Cthulhu Realms but this does make for a nice variation.

If you love adventure, RPG, and total war then this is your game. I have owned this game for over four years with almost 900 hours on record and I still play it. What makes this game very playable is the MOD's you can download. The base game is fun at first, you learn the games mechanics and how to play the environment. Afterwards it get's repetitive, lucklily there are tons and I mean tons of mods that range from changing certain game mechanics like the diplomacy mod for instance is a very popular one, all the way to toal game conversions. Some MOD's take place during historical medieval europe, japan, revolutionary war, civil war etc. Beyond that there are a lot of fantasy mods which I enjoy the most personally. They have a decent collection in the steam workshop, but I always went to  moddb.com  to get all the mods I most enjoy, but be careful, there are some mods that have not been updated or abandoned for years and may contain viruses. Other than that, this is a game that you can use cheats and still put hundreds of hours into a campaign. For me that is always fun, although some people differ on that. But playing the game without cheats is just as fun. Lastly, this is also a game you don't have a powerful rig to pay, this game can run decently on a dual core with a 8800gt and 2 gigs of ram. It won't run fantastic, but it will do the job. Hope this helps, the next installment: Bannerlord will be coming out hopefully soon, and it will knock your socks off!!!!  1,000,000,000/10

Positives: Essentially a revamp of the original Mount and Blade refining a great game. Taleworlds creates a pseudo-fantasy world based on medieval Europe which immerses you in the aesthetic of the time. The player can forge their destiny as either a King, Vassal, Simple trader or merchant, even a bandit or perhaps just a bounty hunter. The close combat melee system is difficult to grasp at first but shortly becomes efficient and ruthless. Cavalry and Mounted units are simple to control and efficient at dealing with vast enemy groups or parties. The formation orders and micro-control of soldiers on the battlefield makes for tactical and realistic combat. Archery, Crossbows, Swords, Two handed weapons, polearms and shields give a customisable aspect to warfare. Each individual faction has its own diverse culture and backstory leaving much of the story to the imagination and interpretation of the player. Player kingdoms are a challenging addition which allows for a greater goal, for those completionists who'd like to conquer the fictional world of Calradia. <<<<JEREMUS>>>> Items such as armour/weapons are balanced and not too easily obtainable. Generally the tone of the game as well as the music are essential to immersing the player in a believable world.  OK: The companion system is slightly frustrating due to the arguments within your party, however this does add realism and character to the game, as most NPCs are robots. You see friendships arise between your teammates and as well as thi- <<<<JEREMUS>>>> The graphics in Mount and Blade warband, are not the greatest, but I found that not concentrating on the individual textures worked well enough and actually the shaders were decent.  Negatives: Sadly there are pitfalls in M&B, the siege battles for instance are painstaking, with entire armies ascending a single ladder. Thankfully sieges will be improved in the sequel: Bannerlord!!! Minor annoyance- but when the character jumps or touches certain objects in game, they will freeze and then start walking again slowly, this can be annoying as you literally cannot hit the ground running in this game, eehehehheh. Bloody Party Morale..... HOW LOW CAN YOU GO, 'Oh whats that 7 fully trained Swadian Knights? Youre deserting me? WELL OK' Food and resources for your party have to be bought individually and cant be stacked or stored as they go rotten and then you run out of food within a week! IRL a king didn't personally carry all the grub for his soldiers, why should I? I managed to encounter QUITE a few bugs and glitches, namely to do with textures and loading screens, as well as random crashes.  Other than these nitpicks really, I'm gonna be honest, warband is WELL worth your time. And hooooly ♥♥♥♥ will it take up your time, I still had fun sacrificing sunlight and social life to get revenge on ♥♥♥♥♥♥♥ King Harlaus.  10/10 Would raid Chide again.

TL;DR - The game was fun at first, then it got really competitive and then it began to suck.  I downloaded this game last year, it seemed really interesting and fun so I thought i'd have a few games. Didn't really understand it that much but I was having fun! Fastfoward a few months and me and my friend are really into it, watching Dota 2 streams, getting items, playing the game for hours on end etc. and it was probably some of the best playing time i've spent on a game.  Spent about a year on the game on and off, had the few bad games which made me want to stop playing but i persevered. However, recently I thought I'd pick the game up again after a few months of inactivity. Lot's of changes were made, and I had lost my touch, but the main thing which put me off the game completley was the amount of childish, pathetic, greedy and incompent people in all of the 17 games I played. I understand that is what online gaming is like, but the consistency of this was impeccable and not only did it annoy me that I couldn't win a game due to 1 or 2 people, but it made me give up.   So there it is folks, read all the reviews you want and agree with whoever you want, these are just my findings after putting in over 1000 hours. Did you enjoy the original Bioshock? with meaningful plasmids and weapon upgrades, a variety of tools at your disposal, and a more of a roam from point to point adventure, solving puzzles and fighting crazies along the way?  You did?  Well go ♥♥♥♥ yourself. We're here to deliver the Halo shooter experience to PC, complete with a two weapon rule and glowing gold bullet shield!   Those meaningful plasmids I asked about? Those are gone, There are three useful plasmids in this, murder of crows, posession, and windblast. Every other plasmid has done nothing to the enemy other than make them an angry person with a tentacle wrapped around them instead of just an angry person. Every secondary plasmid power is a landmine. Because ♥♥♥♥ variety.   The storyline is something ripped out of the 90s (See 'TimeCop') and very similar to a few other games who have implemented it much better. (Amnesia the Dark Descent has similar twists, as well as the playstation exclusive inFAMOUS 1)  The nonsensical storyline attempts to hide behind multiverse theories (You know, the thing that everyone groans about when DC comics tries it) but then throws in time travel whenever it feels like without ever bothering to explain it in the slightest. Mulltiverse and time travel are two different things. You cant just throw one in and expect it to cover both.  While the game warrants a second playthrough just so you can see how blatant the foreshadowing was the first go around, and how nonsensical the lighthouse scene is in general, it is nonetheless a solid shoot em up, adhereing very closely to the Halo Combat Evolved formula. Not much you can mess up there.  The racism in this game is unbelievably blind to itself. It portrays a powerful black woman who becomes a heartless cruel animal just like the white masters said they would if left to their own devices. These scenes had me sick to my stomach at the sheer ignorance of them.   But, if you want a pretty, pseudo intellectual PC ready version of Halo, here it is.  graphics 9/10  story -1/10 for racism enabling characters  Voicework 10/10 all people perfectly played their  World 3/10  Narrative consistency 1/10  Being a bioshock game 0/10  Being a 1940's Halo game 10/10

If you are in the market for a tactical, semi-realistic shooter with a sandbox element, look no further than Operation Flashpoint: Dragon Rising. This isn't, however, a substitute for ARMA 2 or games of its ilk. Yes the game strives for realism, with bullet drop-off, realistic(for the most part) weapon specs, pretty realistic weapon damage and all that jazz, but it's no ARMA 2. And that's what makes this game one of my favorite FPSs. It gives you enough reality to make things interesting, but remembers that it's a game and games are supposed to be fun. It doesn't sacrifice fun for realism or realism for fun. The AI is as dumb as a post though. Also the game is a blast to play with friends. You will get some great war stories out of it. I highly reccomend this game to fans of tactical FPSs.

app_name: Realm of the Mad God Exalt
review_text: This weird pixel graphic action mmo dungeon crawler with rogue-like elements was created a long time ago by a small company called wildshadow. After years of awesomeness they had to sell it to kabam to keep the game going, kabam kept it going for some more years also, and now finally it's in the hands of Deca(Thankfully being revived by them after kabam's dark era). I've been with this game for so long i keep coming back to it, the 1000+ hours i have here are only with the steam account(had 2 older accounts before this one). I'm mentioning this so you get the idea that this game is very hard to kill, the people just wanna play this ****. app_name: DARK SOULS™: Prepare To Die Edition
review_text: Three-hundred hours in I guess it deserves a review...  If you just start playing this game blind without watching four or five 'How to play Dark Souls' videos on youtube, this is a terrible game. Dense, dense, dense. It doesn't tell you how to move, how combat works, or what the game is even about. You've heard that the game is hard, and it is, but before you get over this hump it is technically impossible to play. Some say this is horrible game design, some say it's dense on purpose because it's for 'hardcore gamers.' I'll leave it up to you to decide whether not explaining the mechanics of the game is terrible or hardcore.  Ok, now you know how to actually play the game. Now it's no longer impossible, it's just rage inducingly difficult. It almost seems unfair. I mean how are you supposed to fight three enemies one once? This is ♥♥♥♥♥♥♥t! Then, on the fifth or sixth time you reach that encounter you realize that if you just strategically take them out, have a plan, think it through, you can take them all out. Sneak up and backstab this guy. Parry then riposte that guy. Roll, block, then strong attack the other guy. This is starting to work. I'm gittin' gud a this!  Wait! What in Zeus' ♥♥♥♥♥♥♥♥ is that giant thing!? It's 28 feet tall! It just one shot me!  Five or six tries in you begin to see a pattern. Around fifteen to sixteen attempts you start actually getting it down near half health. On your eight-millionth attempt you are like Neo in the Matrix, rolling out of the way of huge one-shot capable attacks. Dropping two handded attacks on it that take a fifth of it's life off. You get it. You are in The Zone. You now understand how to play this game. It dies. A luminescent beam streams onto your silhouette as dust motes dance in the ribbon of light around you. A chorus of angels sing. You are God.  Then you find the next area and you begin crying.

I almost feel bad for giving it a negative review.  Graphic issues (virtual artifacts), connection issues and terrifyingly terrible AI (often, a swarm of enemies when spawning will simply stand idle and do nothing) make this otherwise fun, affordable and great looking game frustrating to play.   It's a shame, as the game has a level of potential that can easily compete with games such as L4D and Warframe. Graphics are fantastic, the atmosphere's good, the combat is simple though satisfying. To top it off, it seems the devs are really out to upgrade the game past its base level and release addons, which the devs announced would alternate between paid and free add-ons without dividing the playerbase. The first one's already here, it's free, and its a good sign of things to come.  I have faith that the developers are on it and will try to resolve the issues, but until that time, you're probably better off waiting a few months before purchasing.

The good: This is a fun little logic puzzle game, and you can experiment and have some creativity with your character and your tools. The art style is cute. It's not a long game (there is one achievement in particular that requires a lot of grinding, and I think this accounts for the number of hours a lot of people have in this). Some of the puzzles are challenging, and all have extra collectibles to find or things to do.   The bad: Poor damage & combat mechanics make the game somewhat more difficult than it needs to be. You can kill yourself quite easily with lightning or fire, and if any monster gets ahold of you it is generally lights out quite quickly, and with no checkpoints or saves in levels, this can mean some frustrating restarts.  General info: You play a little stick person trying to rescue your friend (coincidentally, another stick person) and questing through a sketched world. The actual artistic skill required to play this game is zero (all your 'tools' can be straight lines if you wish), but it's definitely more fun if you take the time to sketch and enjoy. Replay value is low, once you've figured out the puzzles.   Overall: I don't know that I'd purchase it at full price, but if you pick it up during the Steam sales, I would recommend it! (Please take mind of the first paragraph of thie review before commenting on it)  + Some of the best gameplay of the series + Proper Rush maps - Poor server population (at least in Europe) - No new wave of players to provide some breathing space for begginers + All-kit weapons balance out all the classes, preventing one class from dominating the game - Most of your opponents are going to be level 50 Generals of the Army, who have been playing the game  since launch.    While I still believe that Bad Company 2 is the mechanically best game in the Battlefield series with its implementation of classes, weapons, all-kit weapon balance, and map design, as a 'playing' game, it has not stood the test of time, so please take note that I am giving a negative review of the game in the state it is in now.  If you're interested in the Battlefield series and in general online shooters with a modern military theme, I'd recommend going for the next instalment in the series proper -- Battlefield 3, which still has, three years after its release, a somewhat lively mid-level population, something that Bad Company does not. And that's the very first wall you will run into playing BD2.  True, there are many people coming back to BD2 and you will run into low-level players from time to time, but the *vast* majority of players on most, if not all, servers are level 50 Generals of the Army, who have been playing the game for years and know every map like the back of their hand, and all the various expliots in the game proper. As such, it is next to impossible to compete with these players.  Since sniper rifles in BD2 do not have scope glint to give away their position and scope sway to balance out the amazing killing ponential of bolt-action sniper rifles, camping is pathological and prevelent on the few remaining populated servers. Most matches quickly disintergrate into sniper team vs sniper team situations, which makes arming MCOMs and capturing flags extremely difficult for both teams, resulting that most matches simply run out with the first team to capture flags as the winners.  In some such situations it is impossible for Attackers in Rush to advance beyond their deploy, because the whole of the other team is keeping them in the proverbial pen with sniping and camping next to exits out of the deploy, and there are only a handful of servers with active admins that stop such behaviour with kicks and bans.  In short: the game has reached the end of its play cycle, and is no longer the game it was in back in the 'glory' days of 2010-2012.

app_name: METAL GEAR SOLID V: THE PHANTOM PAIN
review_text: The gameplay is nothing short of excellent. The visuals are superb, and optimized greatly for all types of hardware. It is the next true evolution in the Metal Gear Solid franchise in terms of gameplay and tactical stealth (or guns blazing if you're that boring). Every new mission that is started is another tale to be told because nothing ever, EVER goes the same way twice. The AI is some of the best I have ever encountered in a video game, while bending to some levels of absurdity just so certain gameplay devices can work. Audio is also masterfully done, even though there's a total lack of options in-game.   But I have to not recommend this game to any longtime Metal Gear fan, who will obviously be expecting heavy heaps of story, just not as much as MGS4. This game has two wrong things with it. Two very big wrong things. Not only is it actually an unfinished game - with the latter half composing almost entirely of filler missions - but the quality Metal Gear cutscenes and story we've come to expect were either cut entirely out or moved to cassette tapes. There are some cutscenes that are incredible, no joking, but there is a definite lack of them. You will acquire a huge amount of story cassette tapes over the course of the game, when they could have just served as cutscenes instead. Indeed, Keifer Sutherland does a good job as Venom Snake, but fails to deliver any lines where they would really be needed, and by that I mean he is a silent protagonist for more than half the game. The main story also takes a nosedive into nothing once the first chapter is completed. There are only three main missions in chapter 2 that actually progress the story. The rest are harder versions of chapter 1 missions. Then you get the final mission and it's just over.  This game was truly made for non-hardcore fans of the series, in terms of gameplay and structure. Perhaps story as well, because gameplay is just absolutely everywhere, you cannot complain. I would recommend this game if that were the only case, but in reality, the game is incomplete. Chapter 1 ends like it was the end of the entire game. It should have probably stayed that way, with how Chapter 2 stretches itself out and ends.  I actually do recommend this game if it's on sale and you're a noob to the Metal Gear series. It's a load of fun and perfects the 'fun' parts, but also removes what series veterans have come to expect from this franchise. This game is the most playable in the entire series, but even Ground Zeroes had a better story than this, and that game could be played normally at two hours.
A big fan of the Civ Series, I love the new gameplay changes of Civ 6. I think the art style is a fine choice.

Full Review based on the game in its original form plus the free add on for making new items...tldr: Yes, if you enjoy a challenge.  EL= Endless Legend There are lots of references to Civ so players who have played Civ type games can understand some comparisons. If you have never played a strategy game, get this one, and watch some tutorials.  I bought EL when it came out and barely played it because it was overwhelming. This is after playing many hours of Civ 5 and beating everything but Deity to give you some perspective. When I saw that recent reviews were mixed I figured it was because a bunch of people participated in the recent summer sale and couldn't handle it. I have read about the Chinese issues and I do hope that the game expands into other languages, but am glad they take a hard stance on cheating. There is no point in cheating, earn it, and if you want something easy to play there are lots of mindless FPS.  This game is beautiful. The artwork is amazing, the minor details that appear while playing and remain active (like pollen flowing from flowers (anomaly tiles), is prestine. The music fits but like any strategy game that can take 300, 400, or more turns, I would like to hear an evolving music system. This does not mean the music is in anyway bad because it is very good. I just want more of it.  Starting positions are genuinely quite random, unless you change some settings, but I enjoy it. Out of all the games I've started I have only ever had 1 great starting position. You must earn your keep in this game because it is very easy to be destroyed, and most of the time it will be by your own ignorance/mistakes.  The ability to customize is great. Customizable factions, very customizable terrain options, and so on. I really enjoy trying out different versions of what the creators deam starting traits for 'faction'. I like that you can take penalty items like less food per tile for more points in something else like dust growth (like a currency). Civ definitely has more factions, major and minor, however the uniqueness of the game play and the factions themselves are so different it makes up for it. Of course, they have released some expansions with 2 new factions. There is another that was free but I do not count them, the Mezari, because they are vaulter copy cats, and that's honestly nonsense (but they're still my favorite because they're from outer space, PLEASE GIVE THEM THEIR OWN OUTER SPACE FALLEN STORY LINE ETC!!! <<<<33333). Back to faction uniqueness...in Civ I could play most any faction the exact same way if I wanted with very little change and that kinda sucks because its just a grind session over and over. Sure there are many ways to win but the process of achieving those things are all too similar. In EL you have to play the factions to there base way of 'life' or you will fall behind very quickly.  Going to cut this short b/c I'm hungry AF and want some mexican.  Remaining list of things I'd like to see (talking to developers now, others buy the game and move along, feel free to add me). Also, if any of the following are already included in the expansions I've yet to purchase please ignore/let me know, thank you.  -Rally points for units, especially those being built -Ability to retrofit purchased UNITS (nothing worse than having a lvl 6 healer at half potential) -ACTUAL MULTIPLAYER TEAM PLAY... -More music (it's already all awesome, but let's not go 200 turns and quit, be the 4K you were meant to be) -True history, traits, etc set up for the Mezari (who would be the true science faction), no more Vaulter knock off...I have some ideas -Make trade more involved  OVERALL GREAT GAME, DEFINITELY BUY, IF YOU LOVE CIV TYPE GAMES YOU WILL LOVE THIS, DON'T MAKE IT A CIV VS EL VS THE WORLD ETC. PLAY THEM ALL, LOVE THEM ALL, BUT THIS ONE IS DEFINITELY MY FAVORITE, TACO TIME!

Holy ♥♥♥♥, Endless Legend is unbelievably good. It is everything I wished that Beyond Earth had been. Absolutely the best game from 2014 I've played yet. Civ 5 and Beyond Earth are officially crap, I'm uninstalling them forever. This is finally a modern game that's as good as Sid Meier's Alpha Centauri™.

You are a mercenary in small Latin American country during a revolution. Your main contract is to hunt down Russian businessmen, and while doing that you will eather protect the govermnent or help the bunch of bearded men with Kalashnokovs to take over the country.   Well, this is not a new game. In fact, it was released in 2009, so don't expect up-to-date graphics. Its gameplay is what makes this game unique. It's the most realistic tactical simulator in the world. The closest example is new JA. Second strength of 7.62 High Caliber is guns. There are tons of guns (pistols, assault rifles, shotguns, smgs, machine guns, rifles, sniper rifles) and lots of attachements (scopes, colimators, laser sights, flashlights, grips, grenade launchers). So, if you are looking for realistic tactical simulator with enormous range of guns - this is the game.   P.S. I would also recomment checking out Hard Life and Arma Realista mods. Hard LIfe makes game more varied, add more capabilities; Arma Realista makes range of guns more authentic for Latin American region (how about 20 types of AK and 15 clones of Colt 1911?)
