If you prefer to watch the review instead of reading it, check out the link below:   https://youtu.be/SrCWcLT--OI   For those who like reading:  Firewatch Review:  For those unfamiliar with Firewatch, It’s the first game developed by Campo Santo studios. It was announced in 2014 giving audiences a glimpse at what the game had to offer without disclosing any major concepts behind the game. It was the game that had people asking, “What the f*** is Firewatch?” And with the wait finally over, the game is out and we get a chance to see it in its true colors. I have to admit, I was really excited for this game because it looked like it had so much potential, but it ended up being a dissapointing game.  Gameplay. Gameplay-wise it is what I feared it would be, a simple and mundane walking simulator. This might be a contreversial opinion to have since some people might catergorize it as an “Adventure” game because it allows you to interact with and collect items, but let me explain myself and where I'm coming from for a sec. To me, a traditional adventure game enables you to collect random or specific items that generally have a purpose in the grand scheme of things; solving a puzzle, accessing an area, main and minute plot progressions and so on. Whereas Firewatch, the only purpose to interact with items, is for dialogue or commentary reasons between your character and his supervisor. Technically, with the exception of 2 or 3 things, you don't even need to collect said items. All you have to do is highlight them with your cursor to bring up a dialogue option. And yet, aside from plot-related journals, the game gives you an option to take or interact with things for no apparent reason. A bottle of liquor that has no purpose, fireworks that have no purpose, a bar of candy that has no discernable effect. And that's probably 20% of the items you will interact with. The other 80% is nothing short of just admiring things for the sake of admiring things. Sound familiar (See: The Order: 1886)?  This game is also short. Most people could probably finish it in under 3 hrs. Normally, I like to place quality over quantity, but I was hard pressed to find either in Firewatch. And it becomes even harder when you consider that it’s priced at $20.00. As short as the game is on its own merits, it is made even more painfully obvious by the large amount of time spent backtracking through the same areas over and over again. Adding a bit of variety to the gameplay would have gone a long way towards cushioning the repetition. Running into a dangerous wild animal, solving puzzles or having to flee from a wildfire before it engulfs you are just simple examples that could have been implemented without having to compromise the overall essence of the game.  General gameplay aside, I did encounter technical problems, most of which are negligible but The one that stood out to me was the save management mechanic. While the game allows auto saving and manual saving, they unfortunately share the same slot AND, you're allowed only one save slot per game. This is problematic because if you unknowingly save in a glitched state, you'll have to start your adventure from scratch. I was deep into my first game when I decided to save my progress, only to find out that I was standing on a small tree stump that I could easily step on, but could never alight. The only solution was to start again from the beginning and losing two hours of my progress.... and that was the only time during my entire play-through that I appreciated how short the game is.  Plot/Writing And for a game that skimps heavily on gameplay, the hope is that it would inundate the player with compelling and robust plot points that engage on several levels. That hope is rightly justified and it is apparent that people behind this game had it at the forefront of their minds. Right from the get go the game entices you with a glimpse of options and consequences using a textual tree of choices that are similar to your standard emotional quotient tests. And as you make your way deeper into the game, you encounter several different plot points that range from simple letters you find in lockboxes to larger geographical areas shrouded in mystery. It’s all thrilling stuff that will keep you cycling through to see how things will unravel...but that's where it starts going downhill. As you slowly approach the conclusion of the game. All potential mysteries and plot points are tossed out and the game relies on a poorly presented discovery that marks an abrupt and unsatisfactory ending that tries to tug at your heartstrings. Which is a HUGE shame because the game really goes out of its way to threaten you with a good time, but never really delivers. It’s tempting to try and justify the nature of this game's plot by analytically approaching it, but When all is said and done, when you sit down and ruminate over your experience from the beginning to the end of the game, the realization that this was mostly an exercise in sarcasm between your character and a disembodied voice will creep in.  Audio And this is where I can feel comfortable throwing some good points at this game. It has some of the best voice acting I've heard in a long while. That goes a really long way in making the mostly nothing that you do in the game tolerable. The conversations that occur between your character and Delilah, your supervisor, is extremely organic and, although contextual, it's believably adaptive. There are moments where you hold a long conversation with each other, and moments filled with quick, dry remarks when one character gets fed up with the other. And as a testament of how great and well thought-out the conversation are, it’s the moments of silence that really haunt you and make you feel vulnerable.  The game also does sound effects very well. From the sounds of the wind blowing to rustling bushes in the background. There are also several animal noises that are extremely convincing but unfortunately, that's broken by the lack of animals you encounter in the game. I think there were only two animals I saw throughout my entire play-through. A raccoon and an elk. Which, brings me to the visuals:  Visuals It’s a vast Wyoming wilderness. An “open” world with intertwining paths that will leave you lost or moving in circles if you don't use your provided map and compass. And that's where most of the fun is to be had. Trying to move around and exploring the area by yourself is very fascinating. The design of the map is cleverly done and almost maze-like and every once in a while you run into a historically relevant site or landmark that make you feel like the next big explorer. The geography itself is beautiful and has a lot of breath taking sites like canyons, lakes, rivers and dense forests that lead seamlessly into each other without breaking your immersion. The map surprisingly gets even more beautiful as the sun begins to set and casts a shed of amber around you. The mornings are often foggy and project a sense of eeriness, and where there is wild fire, the area becomes enveloped in smoke and destructive beauty.  Conclusion So that's pretty much it for the review. This is probably the shortest review I've done, but I can only work with that the game has to offer, which is not a lot. And although I've been critical, I don't want you think that this is a horrible game. I just wouldn't recommend it since there's hardly any meat to the bone. There's some fun to be had here, but it quickly wanes at the two hour mark. And for $20.00, unless you’re excited about the prospect of meandering back and forth around a map to no satisfactory end, you're better off waiting for a sale or using your $20.00 on a game that has more to offer.

I would call this a maze game, due to avoiding anything spinny, pointy, or tinged red, all of which will kill you in a puff of black smoke. The storyline really reminds of a movie titled THX 1138, where a man is trying escape an underground oppressive and controlled society (basically a slave drone like Blink in Mooch). But in Mooch (name of the world??) Blink at least has chance to bring his companion along if he manages to escape. That is the point of the game, in fact, to collect little balls of light (pieces of your companion's soul/life force) as you scan this dark world of simple maze-like corridors while avoiding anything spinny, pointy, or tinged red, all of which are lethal.  There are 3 sections to this game with 24 levels per section. Simple command movements WASD or the arrow keys up, down, right, left are used to move the character and the mouse scans the dark. Patience, strategy and good timing are all virtues in this game. There are also 3 challenge modes, easy, normal and hard.   Each level restarts at the beginning if the player ends up a puff of black smoke, but do not despair, the levels are relatively short and small in size. However, do not let that fool you, some levels are quite a challenge and there are the equivalent of mini-bosses as well as bosses. There is plenty of strategy involved in trying to collect those tiny shiny balls of light. At the beginning of each level the number of lights needed to clear the level is displayed and the number goes down as the light is collected.   Defintely an engaging maze survivor game, with increasingly challenging levels, and challenging enough to be worth the price.

Not only this, but there are tons of achievements that will leave you hunting for more content and digging deeper into choices you may have found unimportant.   Over all, I'm thoroughly impressed by this. As a fan of the first game and one of the kickstarter backers, I am thrilled at how it turned out. Buy it, revel in it, and enjoy.

Guilty Gear Xrd - Revelator - is probably the best fighting game to date. It has the best tutorial in all of fighting games, allowing a simple newcomer to the genre to understand every aspect of the genre and Revelator. It has next to no input lag, allowing for the smoothest experience you can possibly imagine. The netcode is rock solid, with only 2-4frames of lag on good connections, without any noticeable lag spikes or latency issues. The visual and aesthetic design of Revelator is AMAZING. The soundtrack is absolutely astonishing with upbeat electric guitar and drums pumping adrenaline through every match, and the graphical style of Revelator is simple a sight to behold. If you're not that into fighting games, you owe it to yourself to at least buy Revelator, run it the best you can and just stare at the amazing visuals.  The PC port is great, and wont let you down with lack of Dinput support, Xinput support, Rebindable keys for both keyboard and controller and barely any issues with running. I have experienced 2 crashes, one on startup the very first time i booted it up, however this has only happened once, despite trying to replicate it. I also experienced a crash after trying to exit it via Alt+F4, but that was most likely right at the start of an auto save, as the save data was wiped. The game also has a slightly sketchy business model, with 3 characters as DLC on day 1, despite very clearly being in the game. One of those can be earned with ingame currency, but the 2 others are locked behind a paywall.  Despite these faults, the game is still great. Definetly pick this up.

(Finished Wii version, re-boght on Steam.)   BIT.TRIP RUNNER is an auto-runner. You have no control over Commander Video's movement speed, and your only interactions are jumping, ducking, kicking, and blocking objects that block your path. The main difference between BIT.TRIP RUNNER and other auto-runners is that obstacles aren't randomly generated or placed in every stage, and instead appear in a set rhythm and melody to the music. As you collect gold bars and dodge obstacles, you fill in various bits of each stage's song, and the more power-up cores you find, the more intense the music gets and the higher your score will soar.   The game is HIGHLY focused on immediate reaction, and if that's not your cup of tea, then your pattern memorization skills will be put to the ultimate test. RUNNER has a relatively smooth start to try and ease you into the experience, but if you're to complete the game then your execution is going to have to be near-flawless. There are no mid-stage checkpoints to save you, and one missed jump means being thrown back to the beginning of a stage to try again. It's easy to get caught up in a flurry of 'just one more try's, but that satisfaction isn't quite the same as you'd get from something like Super Meat Boy or Rogue Legacy. RUNNER can be rote almost to a fault, and it'll probably take people that enjoy very specific types of linear challenges to enjoy it.   If there's one major issue with the game it's that there are some crazy intense swings and spikes in difficulty. Stage 1-11 - ODYSSEY will probably be enough to shut down most that aren't dedicated to trying to see the game to its end, and there are more than a few other bad apples in the bunch whose level of challenge can feel a bit excessive considering the game's lack of checkpointing. It's all doable with the right amount of practice, but again, if that kind of rote memorization isn't for you, it may be best to pass on this one. Otherwise, game on. It's a fantastic game with great music, a fun aesthetic, and engaging gameplay for those of us that like a little something different in rhythm games.

Alright, so after 18 hours of playing this game, I'll give you guys my humble opinion about my experience so far. If it's great graphics and high end quality you look for, get away from this game. But if you're looking for a game with a (very) rich storyline and diversity (the game has loads of diversity thanks to both space and ground action). It isn't really MMO either, most of the stuff (storyline) does fine playing solo, although friends always can tag along.  Do not expect a FPS on the ground, it still is a sort of archer-like RPG experience, although it does have a good tactical depth in skills and equipment. The space combat experience can compete with EVE's, keeping your shield, energy, and skills distributed, and facing the enemy from the right side through a whole battle always is a nice challenge.  Ship diversity is high for all the sci-fi fans out there, with nice customization for them aswell, they also come supplied to you for free until lvl 40, every 10 levels, so that is a pain in the wallet less. You don't really need to grind for 'good' equipment until endgame I'd say.  Just bare in mind that this still is a F2P game with some server issues (they can be a ♥♥♥♥♥ when they're heavily loaded), and sure, all RPG's get repetitive eventually, but this one does a great job at keeping that barrier very far away indeed.

It is a shame, how many flaws this game has. Let's start with the waypoints-scripts. If you don't follow the way, like it was meant by the developers, you will find yourself exploring the whole area, without shooting even once.  Another thing is, that you can hit a enemy in a duel to a non-vital area, like the shoulders, and you still score a kill.  The bullets have no realism inside. You can aim from one corner of the map to the other corner right at the head and you will get a headshot kill.  Most of the enemies look the same, which makes me believe, that there wasn't spent much time making them.  And the most annoying thing about this game is, that it just feels like 'Chicken Shot'. You run around and all that...but fighting the enemies is like shooting chickens in Chicken Shot....boring like hell.  Don't get me wrong...there are still good sites on this game. Like the duels...which are all the same, but still fun...one or two times... If you want to play a good Call of Juarez, then play the first one...and maybe the second...but after that the franchise just went down!

This can help you decide the purpose of the planet be it research, manufacturing or wealth etc. It can be annoying sometimes when you have a set of three habital planets and on one you get a bad synopsis, but thats what makes the game interesting, you never know whats around the corner.  I am still getting into the game so don't have much more to say, but I like it. Also the worshop feature is pretty good, so if your a fan of Star Wars, Star Trek, Battlestar Galactica, you can download additional ship prototypes, I really like that.  One negative I will say, you can't switch on auto turn like on Civilization. The amount of times when nothing happened you and you have to keep pressing enter or using the mouse, get a little tiresome. Apart from that, I am really enjoying the game.

Look - I'm no newcomer to old-school dungeon crawlers. I have played and finished Dungeon Master 1 & 2, Eye of Beholder trilogy, Realms of Arkania trilogy, Lands of Lore, Wizardry 7... and of course Grimrock 1. Some of them were pretty challenging, especially W7 and DM2. Yet it was nothing compared to this game. The combat is not the biggest issue. It's quite hard and I have to admit I suck at real-time micromanaging everything - attacks, potions and spells while running around avoiding enemies and traps. But at least you can restart on easier difficulty. The biggest problem is the design of the puzzles. Especially in the second half the game starts to throw puzzle after puzzle at you, each more and more complex and cryptic. With extremely vague clues, sometimes with no hints at all. I don't think I've ever needed a walkthrough while playing Grimrock 1 but here I had to check it constantly. I mean, in the final area I wasn't able to solve a single puzzle! And after looking up the solutions I was pretty sure I would have never figured that out. EDIT: The first rule of good puzzle games is that they have to provide sufficient number of clues. So that I don't have to rely on guessing and brute force.  This was also great - to access one of the maps you need to use a specific weapon located in a completely different map on the other side of the game. But the weapon is not marked as a quest item, it's just a standard staff. And because it sucks, I did the only logical thing - I threw it away somewhere. Imagine my surprise ten hours later when I discovered the staff is required to progress. I actually had to spawn it using a developer console to be able to continue.  This could have been a great game like the first one. Instead it turned to be such frustrating experience I actually had to force myself to finish it.

If you think this is too long of a review, you can see the summary at the bottom (tldr)  I really like the story and the ability to choose different options. The tutorial is very good and the ongoing tutorials as you learn new things are very good. gwent, the card game, is also pretty good too. It's not hearthstone, but it's good (not that hearthstone is the best game ever).   The combat is very tedious and boring. Once you click to attack you can't get out of it, You have to wait for the flourish to go before you can block again. Many games have successfully implemented changing an attack into a block or dodge. Also, you get a crossbow that does ♥♥♥♥ poor damage. It's a crossbow! It does about as much damage as a punch. It's ridiculous. They could have made it so it takes a while to reload (as crossbows do irl and in most fantasy games) but nope, it just shoots the equivalent of an arrow with a boxing glove on the end and is fairly quick to reload. The leveling of the various spell trees you get doesn't really do a whole lot to change them. Boring percent stat increases similar to borderlands, with the rare skill change which lets you do something new with the spell.   Enemies have very similar combat styles. Rush in, attack, back off, repeat. If you attack they will dodge, parry (if possible) or block. Sure, that sounds like it could result in some variety. They tend to do a lot of standing around and waiting. They also will just block a lot sometimes and do little else. Mostly it's the same pattern of going in for an attack, and then backing off. It's a pretty simple system. It's very easy to parry attacks. Very easy to dodge attacks that they do. Some have ranged attacks. Even the first witcher contract is tedious. The beast has very standard pattern of attacks. It's little to do with combat skill and everything to do with memorizing the pattern, which is a simple one. It looks totally badass though. All in all, the combat is very well polished but lacks variety that even much older games have. The AI is not brilliant for sure. It doesn't feel like it's a step forward in gaming or even for the series itself. If a game isn't improving upon itself, then by the designers own standards, they aren't doing so well.   Humans should be all rushing you at the same time. It's typical for one or two to approach while the rest file in behind them. Wolves tend to attack in groups a lot better than the humans.   Health bars - so, I chop an enemy 10 times in the head and it's totally fine, but that 11th hit kills it? Shouldn't it get progressively slower and/or weaker the more beat up it is? Shouldn't it run away or attack more fiercely? Shouldn't it give up?  No fear - I kill 9 out of 10 guys and the 10th guy is like 'yup, I'm gonna win this one for sure!' and he's wrong and I kill him too. Why wouldn't he surrender? Maybe he offers a bit of gold or just runs off?   Nobody gives a ♥♥♥♥. For all the stuff I did for the soldiers and townspeople I should be a hero! Farmers still spit when I try and talk to them. What you do doesn't change the game all that much. Sure, having different dialogue for all npcs could be hard. However, most of them share similar dialogue. That could just be changed to a more positive version once you prove yourself to them. Still, the story is good enough for me to want to do the things.    No nonlethal way to kill dudes? Yah, geralt, lets just kill EVERYBODY. How is there a good ending for this game? I ♥♥♥♥ing killed everyone but it's ok cause they are 'deserters.' I tried my fists but it keeps switching me back to my main weapon automatically. Sure, I love killing bandits and rapists and thieves and all that. Don't get me wrong. Maybe a guy who stole some money during wartime to feed his family is not so bad though. Plus, wacking people upside the head with the flat end of a sword would be funny.   I get that the combat is similar to how it was in the witcher 2. That doesn't mean it's not 'poop status.' Ok, maybe it's a very polished poop.   IF you are into the story, this is the game for you. Mostly though it's just quests to go kill some things. That'd be great if the combat wasn't poop. Once you experience a few battles, you've seen all the game has to offer for combat outside of the big bad boss fights. You might say that the combat does change down the line but I've killed hundreds of every type of monster except for the corporeal ones. Even they were just like wolves except they could teleport. IF you have kill tons of enemies the same way to see a slight difference in combat, that slight difference is going to get old as you'll then see that a hundred times.  To me, it's 2010 combat in 2015. It's borderlands-level of dull. Very sword-spongy. I chopped a guy with an enormous sword and I'm the size of captain america. I've had a lifetime of training and my enemy is not wearing any armor. He's tough though so he can take four or five of those godly hits. I think it must have been all that training he did growing up as a farmer. Farmers are tough.    tldr: Combat = yawn Story = hearts of love

app_name: Heroes of Might & Magic III - HD Edition
review_text: Okay, so all the negatives you've heard about this game are true.  Calling it 'HD' is a severe overstatement. It's less blurry. That's basically it. It's no remake.   Yes, it has literally 1/3rd of the content of the Heroes III Complete pack, and doesn't have the all important random map generator (because it was added in one of the expansions).  However! It is Heroes III, and it does look just fine in full screen 1080p. Is that worth $15 to you? Because it definitely is to me. I love Heroes III, and while I don't mind playing the old version, my eyes prefer this one.  And let's be honest here. Even without the expansions, there's a TON of content included. Easily 100 hours of stuff. Eventually you'll run out, but by then you'll get your $15 worth.  Definitely recommended, despite the flaws.

This game is quite the throwback to every metroidvania you can think of and delivers on a lot of the same vibes while subverting your expectations at the same time. The story is interesting, and just the overall feel of the controls are tight as heck. In general If you're having that metroidvania itch this will satisfy it on literally all possible fronts but one  and that's with enemy design.  The vast majority of the game is thoroughly enjoyable, but at the same time the vast majority of enemies end up being bullet sponges, surprise punches in the face, or tiny ultra mobile whisps that chunk your health away. Regardless of the guns or upgrades you end up having they end up being a frustrating and demoralizing thing to be constantly fighting as you backtrack (or progress) through the world.  The saddest culmination of the bad enemy design is definitely the bosses, most of which either end up being 'stand in one spot and shoot' or 'find this very specific way to cheese it out'. Neither of which would be a problem if there was something inbetween those two difficulty extremes.  In general I'd still say it's a great game with some cool inventive weapons, the sole issue is with enemy design, and it pains me to pick fault with it because of how incredible the game is otherwise. I would still say it's a game that you should experience if you can despite all of that

This is a good dark fantasy rouge-lite twin stick shooter with atmosphere.  The intro movie is interesting and sets up the setting for the game. From there you have a pretty standard rogue-lite shooter. Use gold to buff yourself in the current run, and dark crystals to buy long term boosts that will impact every game going forward.  The RNG can be a little high in this one, sometimes early on in a run you find yourself up against a level boss that you just don't have the tools to deal with easily. Sometimes you will breeze through to the end. Also it is not always clear what terrain will block shots and what won't, which can lead to some minor frustration.  Controls are tight, sound and music add to the experience. Overall a very decent game.  8/10

Full disclosure - I've been a fan of the franchise since I discovered BattleTech in grade school, right around the time Mechwarrior 2 came out.  I have a ton of fun playing Mechwarrior Online. There is a huge variety of 'Mechs and wide scope for customizing each 'Mech. I'm mid-tier 3 right now, and matchmaking is fairly quick even in the early hours of the morning. Balance between the Clan and Inner Sphere tech bases is getting better as they tweak it. The Community Warfare/Faction Play part of the game is very much in development, but has additional features en route and I have high hopes.  Whether you're in Faction Play or Quick Play/Solo Queue, teamwork is essential. Stick with your buddies, focus fire, and reap the rewards. Get separated from your team and focused by the red team and you'll have a bad day.  That said, it is an imperfect game, partly due to the difficulties of translating a turn-based tactics game (BattleTech) into a real-time first person stompy shooter. It's coming along, and it seems that PGI has been a lot more responsive to/involved with the community lately when it comes to making changes.

Shao Jun is an assassin and her mind is set on revenge after the Templars destroyed the Chinese Brotherhood she belonged to: you'll be relying on your superior training, athletics and a small array of different weapons and gadgets to travel through levels and make sure the Templars pay for what they've done, one assassination at a time. What follows is a game similar to Mark of the Ninja in gameplay, but less engaging overall: there's enough to enjoy in the great art style and fluid stealth but the bland story and so-so combat drag Chronicles down a bit.   PROS:   -gorgeous art style looks like an antique watercolour  -good soundtrack  -fluid controls paired with a satisfying moveset   CONS:   -bland and cliched story even for Assassin's Creed standards  -flat characters  -open combat feels a bit too awkward to be enjoyable   http://steamcommunity.com/sharedfiles/filedetails/?id=632697554   Chronicles: China is a pretty decent experience, after all there's no denying that pulling out a perfect stealth section or even just assassinating a few guards unseen is satisfying. The great art style makes levels truly gorgeous and they'd fit well in an antique piece of art: level backgrounds, rooms and character models are all very nice to look at.  The actual overall gameplay is, well, decent but nothing more: you get a few new gadgets along the way and some new enemy types will make your life more difficult but the level design and small number of actual reliable tactics make the experience a bit repetitive in the long run. Open combat is pretty meh and basically boils down to parry and then either riposte or jump over an enemy to try and hit their backs, it works but it's pretty awkward and unsatisfying.  The actual story and narrative are pretty yawn inducing even for the series and it's difficult to feel engaged when all the characters-Shao Jun included- are paper thin. The game is only 6 hours long though, so that helps it not overstay its welcome.  Chronicles: China is a decent game overall and not a bad purchase if you're looking for more Assassin's Creed and/ or some decently done stealth action: just be mindful of its shortcomings.

Good job Kathy Rain. A welcome addition to the 8 bit point and click sub genre. Very inspired from Wadjeteye. The voice acting on this game is superb. The puzzels are sufficiently difficult but not over the top hard. A strong focus on story over action. Game plays a bit like a choose your own adventure book because it is pretty text heavy but not in a bad way. A really interesting look at how to create stories about trauma through horror and the supernatural. A bit Jessica Jones in the Netflicks style: Motorcycle, black hair dye job, leather coat, bad attitude but good heart, trauma gonna fix it, man who identifies with a colour. Not bad just interesting.  Some flaws: Could have used more action, faster pace. Lack of backstory/clear explanation as to the cause or history of the antagonist. Kathy's teenage trauma is really not fleshed out well enough. Suddenly at the end you're like, oh yeah I think I remember that. Was she even tramatized by that? Game didn't bring it across.   Curiosities: About that hair Kathy, I was a bit confused. It's 1995 but Kathy has a emo haircut with underdye job from 2005? Kathy is wearing a get up from 2015 too. Also, where did Kathy get the money for her custom chopper?   Score: 7.8. Speed it up next time and make the origin story and goals a bit clearer. Let's put some pressure on for a little more motivation. See you in episode 2 Clifftop.

initially avoided this game because of how its been blowing up the internet, but after LittleV did a cover of one of the songs and TB did a sort of review i decided to get it. I've only had it for about a week, but its already my 2nd favorite game of the year (the 1st being the Witcher 3, obviously). The possibilities are a breath of fresh air from most games today where their ideas of choice are a handful of vague/empty dialogue that all reach the same conclusion (looking at you fallout 4) and the multiple difficulties are a great way to keep you coming back to it, in fact coming back to it is a vital part of the game itself. Don't think you can get away with murdering everyone and then reloading, the game knows and it won't put up with your ♥♥♥♥.   Basic monsters can get a little repetitive after awhile but besides that the 'combat' is rather interesting, essentially it's turn based bullet-hell. Most characters are really in-depth and have lots of backstory, although I may be one of the few who didn't get attached to Toriel that much, only after going through the True Pacifist ending did I actually start to like her. I did get attached to many of the other characters though, and I still don't think I'm capable of going back for a Genocide run, maybe after a few months or so.The game can get really serious, sometimes downright disturbing, but overall its a comedy game. Humor is a hard thing to interpret, so really it depends on what you find funny. Not liking the style of humor can possibly kill the game for you, so just bear that in mind. Game also gets a plus for the ♥♥♥♥ing amazing soundtrack, possibly my favorite alongside Metal Gear Rising. So if you want a story rich rpg-lite type game, then Undertale is a great choice, its also rather cheap and will give you more than your money's worth in terms of enjoyment (or in a genocide run, a very bad time)

Like the first Ride, this is one of the best Motorcycle games I've played since Tourist Trophy on the PS2. It is as difficult as you want it to be with scaling that rewards you for turning assists off (much like Forza). It's arcady enough to where anyone can pick it up, and with just enough sim aspects to keep you on your toes if you wish. Race and bike variety is great. Small displacement 2-stroke sport bikes, dual sports, top heavy naked bikes, supersports, touring bikes, classics, etc. The PP (performance points I assume) system is new to this title. Reminds me a bit of Grand Turismo's way of measuring overall performace. Many people do not like it but it keeps the races interesting by having PP limits on events so you can't just max out the tuning on an eligible bike and smoke the AI competition on every race, which was a bit of an issue for me in the first Ride. Tuning and customization options are practically the same as the first title, with just a bit extra. The bike selection and tracks are great and much improved from the previous title. It has a very accurate Nordschleife which combined with excellent controls always makes for a great racing title. I recommend both Ride and Ride 2, but so far Ride 2 is the definitive title in the series thus far.

If you enjoyed the combat systems in Mass Effect II or III or if you've enjoyed the Gears of War series, you'll enjoy the combat in The Bureau: XCOM Declassified. If you're a fan of the XCOM series you can appreciate it for that alone. Unlike previous XCOM titles, The Bureau is very story-driven. I'd go as far as to say that it plays just like a Mass Effect game set in the XCOM universe during the Cold War. You wander around the XCOM base talking to people and completing small sub-missions not unlike wandering around the Normandy in Mass Effect doing the same. Firaxis intends to tie the storyline of The Bureau in to the upcoming XCOM: Enemy Unknown expansion called The Enemy Within due out in November, so I'm eagerly anticipating the big things to come in the XCOM universe.

The story is an interesting deconstruction and the game's world is haunting without relying on cheap tricks to scare you; it's more rooted in building suspense. The ending is disappointing no matter which one you end up with, however, and the final boss is also a bit out-of-place. I do understand the high acclaim this game has gotten though, it's better than most triple-A titles are or were when it was released, and is worth taking a look at.

Hi there! I've been playing since closed beta and I promise that this game hasn't always been this terrible.  The game overall is so very unique, I loved it. The character classes, the environment, the stupid politic shenaniganry. The fun stuff back when this game was fun. I don't think there are or will be any games similar to it, unless someone actually wants to put forth the effort.  I have to say that steam release brought in droves of players, but the lack of Ankama caring enough was something that killed off the player base. They're so focused money, end game content, nerfing every class ten times over, and their other games, that they don't really realize what's happening here (unless they just don't care?). I used to love this game, but now it's just super depressing. The once full streets and markets of the nations are now completely devoid of life. Nothing is geared towards new players or keeping them, everything is for high levels and end game content.   The creativity has seemingly vanished over the years and I have watched this game go from an amazingly unique and thriving game with a great community to... whatever it is now. I have noticed with my return that most of the players above a certain level tend to treat low level players as though they are the scum of the Earth instead of welcoming them or answering questions. I don't know if this is just on Nox or if other servers have the same issue.   The customer service from Ankama is absolutely terrible. When people claim that all old FPS were obtuse labyrinths this is exactly what they mean. These games of follow-up second wave all feature LOL-I-TROLL-U level design, while offering mostly the same gameplay as the first wave of that generation.  This one is basically more of the Duke Nukem 3D, but with asian theme, new weapons and enemies and slightly improved engine. New character is more irritating and by nowadays standards even 'racist', with jokes being even less funny.   Weaponry is somewhat interesting, but again, blast radius of explosives hurts player more than enemies, which renders them less useful. The rest are usual hit-scan guns and couple of projectile ones. So in the end you'll use only 2-3 of the same guns for the whole duration of the game. Usage of voxels could be impressive for the time, but they should spend them on enemies instead of gun models, so you could properly calculate distances and aim vertically.  Foes are unremarkable demons, with only notable one, being also the most irritating. Suicide bomber is often placed in blind spots and then his corpse plays overly long animation of rising ghost, before you could finally hurt it. And if you fail to do that in time, it'll start to fly around, turning sideways while being 2D sprite, so you have additional trouble to see where to shoot.  Turrets and limited vehicles are supposed to be the new gameplay element (but we all know how well it turned out), but they are extremely clumsy, while also being done better in a game released one month prior to this one.  Basically, this game can be recommended only to people who crave more of the same Duke Nukem 3D gameplay or who never played DN3D, due to ignorance or repulsion to the setting, though Shadow Warrior is not that much tonally different.

Damned is a 4 vs 1 cooperative game where 4 survivors are trapped inside a location such as an asylum, hospital, or hotel trying to escape the horror that lies within. In order to escape, keys must be located and boards must be removed to open the final exit door all while avoiding a deadly monster. There are 4 different types of monsters that a group can play against. Each monster has its own unique abilities to locate and kill the survivors and each survivor has a different set of attributes. This leads to a lot of cat and mouse tactics in knowing where to look and when to run and hide.  There's about 10 different maps, a few different game modes, and key placement is randomized each game.  The game has a small learning curve which involves each monster's playing strategy to efficiently locate and kill the survivors, and as a survivor understanding where keys typically are hidden and learning to avoid and escape each monster. It is a very satisfying game after learning the fundamentals, but until that point I felt kind of lost.  There are typically a handful of games going although it is often difficult to find a public game because the game has a very small player base and the majority of games available are private. You may also have to wait several minutes for a spot to open up or people to join your game, but if you already have a small group of friends that won't be a problem.

I realize the game is only in beta and so what I say is only for contructive criticism.   The Bad:  -In it's current state even as a beta the game is terrible to work with and loads way too slow.  -As other's have said it is too difficult to make a straight piece of track.  -I had trouble just building one simple roller coaster that would go in a circle and have a predifined loop.  -I should have been able to easily define my own loop which i could not figure out how.  -I am strongly recommending that you add the ability build a roller coaster piece by piece like in previous versions of the game and give each piece the ability to adjust it to the creators liking so that people of all ages and backgrounds can enjoy the game and not just professionals.  -Should be able to place paths piece by piece like in previous versions and also given the ability to adjust them.  -I should also be able to make paths as large as I want. For example I was able to make two paths side by side but they would not run together. This makes it extremely difficult to create a courtyard.    The Good:  -What I like about the game so far, as poor as it is, the flexibiliy to manipulate the roller coaster tracks.  -The ability to put paths in any direction.  -Planting objects in various locations,  -the queue for rides is great.  -The graphics.    Things to Incorporate: -snap to grid -build coaster and paths piece by piece -ability to create paths as big as you want -dual station that can take two trains for loading and unloading at the same time. Like thunder mountain at disneyland. -an entrance just for boarding and an exit station behind that just for unloading like what can be done in rct3. -transfer track -storage for trains not being used but can be added as the crowds grow. Trains should be added from storage automatically or by the user. -There are other things but I will wait until the second beta to see if they get incorporated as they do not apply to this beta.  Conclusion: I feel like there is a lot of negativity for this beta. I believe there is good reason for it. However, I feel like this game has a lot of potential to be better than any other previous rct game. I cannot recommend this game based on the beta but I can recommend it for it's potential and I am very excited to see the final product.

Wow. Just wow. There used to be NO games that sucked that had Sid Meier's name on it. Now there are two.  I didn't think I'd ever be more disappointed in a game than I was with the steaming pile of franchise killer that was Fallout 4, but I didn't make it out of the calendar year. I have played every Civ game there is and I can safely say that Civ 6 is AWFUL.  The Cons (so many the pros don't matter, I hope I remember them all)  Idiotic, suicidal AI. Wow, it sure is weird that Kongo can have 7 units on my doorstep just a little bit into the game. The AI in this game is actually DUMBER than the AI in Beyond Earth. I know, I know, I didn't believe it at first either.  Barbarians are EVERYWHERE. Don't recruit scouts, they just bump into barbarian camps every couple turns.  The movement system is now just flat out dumb. Want to cross a river? Do nothing this turn THEN move next turn. That's fun.  The gross brown color pallet is nauseating. Land you can currently see is lit up dayglo, land you've seen but don't occupy is a gross faded brown color and instead of fog of war you just have more solid brown.   I currently have 14 government badges and 2 slots for them. Fun!  Did you like playing marathon huge maps on Civ 5? Too bad, the maps, even the biggest ones, are TINY. This is probably the most unforgivable part.  Unit combat strength is practically meaningless early on due to the rock paper scissors nature of things. So dumb. I would expect as units get stronger this becomes less of an issue, but then again I expect lots of things.  You would think the tutorial would cover in depth all of the new features. You think lots of things.  =========  If you spend $60 on this game as is right now, you're crazy. If you're like me, you'll find yourself thinking 'Wouldn't I be having more fun if I was playing Civ 5 right now?' Spoiler Alert: Yes.  I would ask for a refund, but since Steam has its ridiculous 2 hour policy, I am stuck with this stinker.  Worst Civ game ever.   Wait, no, duh, BE was worse, of course.  All I know is Dishonored 2 is on the way, and that can't POSSIBLY suck, right? Right???

This is a gorgeous, polished, thoughtfully-written visual novel that walks a nice middle ground as far as complexity goes - it's hard to 'break' the narrative with one's choices (which is a problem a lot of otome games run into), but there are still lots of those choices and you can really see their impact on how the game unfolds and resolves.   The endings are broken down into four main categories, with a lot of room for variation within those categories that makes for a high replay value. Art is unique and beautiful, and the writing is consistently clever. Pacing is excellent. I finished my first playthrough in 2-3 hours, which felt just about right, given how many more times I want to go through it.  Really my only disappointments were the lack of F/F romance potential, particularly in a so wonderfully lady-centric game (forget the prince; I wanted to marry the fairy!), and what felt like some untapped narrative potential regarding one of the major story branches (without getting into spoilers, there's a lot of vague but explicitly called-out foreboding around that branch, implying interesting and not necessarily pleasant consequences that don't seem to show up in the actual endings).

Do you like movies? Do you like movies about Romans? Did you like the movie Gladiator? Then you'll like -watching- this.   The campaign is good, you play as the handsome yet blindly loyal Marius, the son of Rome whose father is murdered by blood thirsty barbarians... OR IS HE? The plot thickens from there into your predictable tale of lies and deceit, backstabbing and that old 'the gods are playing a game with the mortals again' chestnut. A few good characters, bad characters and ugly characters in the story... all of whom at some point (spoiler alert) die (fine, not a real spoiler)  Some of the best CGI I have ever seen, and the gameplay graphics are not far off that either. Even though the game at times can feel a bit like a Crytek benchmark demo using Roman assests, the story, the voice acting, the production behind it all is solid.  Ryse is generally a group fighting game, like the Batman games, Assassins Creed, Shadow of Mordor etc only not really done quite right. I can't really describe the problems other than certain 'rules' that exist in these other games do not exist in Ryse; for example the number of concurrent enemies that can attack you at any one time appears to be unlimited, making certain blocks impossible - You end up substituting the dive key instead of block just because it works better.  In the thick of the fights sometimes you don't block the opponent who is attacking you, sometimes you don't attack the correct enemy in the group - It's as if the game has not translated from a controller to a keyboard and mouse properly and is totally dubbed down for console jockies.  Which leads me to Combat. Combat is very simple; Only two buttons are needed. Left click to hack or hold for a heavy hack, right click to bash (break defence) or hold for heavy bash. You then have space to block. That is pretty much it. Combat is actually just a set of clicks depending on who you are up against (normal barbarian, shield barbarian, heavy barb... you get the idea). Remember to dive though, not block.  Saying this though the combat is very strangely satisfying, akin to Shadow of Mordor there are finishers and executions and you can even line up two attackers to be executed in a single set of moves once initiated. Once in an 'execute' there is a very basic QTE where you either left or right click depending on the colour that the enemy flashes up... which makes absolutely no difference at all to the outcome of the execution - the enemies death sentence is already dealt whether you click or not; The clicking is purely for the benefit of you current chosen perk at the time of executing. There are 4 perks, health, damage, XP or the mystical 'focus'.  The actual execution animations are movie like, I personally do not tire watching them over and over and am pleasantly surprised when I see the odd 'environmental' finisher where you throw someone at a wall of spikes or over a barrier, kills that you don't see that often.  Some sections of the game could have done with not being there. Such as the 'shoot all the enemies using the big cross bow Marius!' sections, and collectables are pointless given the games level design is more linier than 10 miles of train track. I get the gameplay needed to be broken up, but these sections felt like a big gap filler to me. Should have just put more fighting... and diving.  There is multiplayer and coop and a solo mode where you can go into the coliseum and fight. Customise and level up your gladiator, which is really cool actually... that is until you are up against 15+ enemies and are diving around the floor like total tw*t trying not to get hit because block doesn't work properly. You also have to unlock 'booster' packs to gain any items... a game mechanic I've come to loathe. I hate grinding.  Are you not entertained? All in all, the game is good and entertaining and looks absolutely f*cking stunning and for that reason you should play it at least once. For under fiver :P

I find Sunless Sea a difficult game to review as it is so unusual. I don't think I've ever played a game quite like it. The art is fairly simple yet perfectly evocative. The writing is exceptional. To try to categorize it I think it would best be described as strategic story telling. You attempt to guide your character through the life of a Zee Captain, earning enough money and knowledge to uncover the game's secrets and work out the details of its story. You make choices regarding what resources to stock up on, how to outfit your ship, where to travel, and the actions you take when you get there. Through these actions the story reveals itself to you. Make the right choices and you progress. Make poor choices or encounter a bit of bad luck and you will likely find yourself meeting a colorfully terrifying end.  If you don't mind doing a little reading as you play and perhaps keeping good notes on what you find this is a very enjoyable game with the potential for significant depth (no pun intended) of story. The game may be a little slow paced for some. Advances in wealth and ability are not easily earned. I often find myself precariously balanced between poverty, insanity, and starvation, hoping desperately that my fuel does not run out before I can make it back to port.  Ultimately I find the game to be enjoyable and rewarding.

I bought this game on a sale and would have refunded it if I had payed more for it.  It was sitting there on my wishlist for quite some time and for the price I payed (about 1.99 dolar), it is perfectly ok.  When I bought this game, my expectation was that it would be a crisis management simulator depicted on space.  I like this game idea so much that It could have been a very nice game to play.  The reallity is that it is a complex board game simulator where you play against the odds. Believe me, odds have more influence over the outcome than you would expect.  Don't get me wrong, the game is very well made. Very polished details, I mean.. the team made a great job with their idea.  What I dislike is that this game is based on luck, and I hate playing luck games.. I rather play something that presents a consistent outcome based on your choices and skill.  Let me give you an example on what you will face on Normal game:  First 3 or 4 rounds, you will survive but will have left a couple of less impending problems on the station.   These will suck your dices thus preventing you from clearing next round's major problems that will ultimatelly destroy your station.  On previous rounds, you had to roll your dices to fix the problems and these Voids and Stasis prevented you from accomplishing that, or made it very difficult.  Dices sometimes tend to suck the luck out of you.. although you had 4 dices, you could not fix the problem and there was nothing left to improve your situation using the special abilities.  Sometimes, between rounds you have to choose between two BAD side projetcts that will do you no good.  Finally, you will receive +4 problems on your station in addition to the ones you left.. you know it is the end just by adding the amount of possible perfect sixes your dices could give yout. And you know isn't going to happen..  What I mean is: This game is a combination of 30% strategy and 70% luck. If you deal well with that proportion, play the damn game.  Currently, a very enerving game for me.

Even though it looks and feels like Mankind Divided is a Deus Ex game, it rapidly becomes apparent that something is missing. Or maybe a few things are missing even.   First of all, the scale of the game's story has been deliberately reduced. If you do all the side quests, it's still about the same length as Human Revolution, but rather than touring Hong Kong and whatnot you are limited to one city for the entire game (Prague). You aren't really fighting a worldwide global conspiracy here, but rather just making some chess moves in a longterm game of influences and such. And well... that's okay I guess, but it feels like a step down from the previous installment, ya know?  Second, the game's lacking a certain human element. In DX1, you had your brother Paul, Tracer Tong, pilot dude and Alex who weren't just JC's support crew, but rather his friends. Human Revolution had Malik, Frank and even Sarif who Jensen had real personal connections to. This has... uh. Well, a black lady whose identity and presence in the game is not adequately explained.   I still love Adam to death as a character, but it feels like this whole plot is just him putting in his 9-5. He doesn't feel like he's really involved in this story at a personal level because none of his friends are here.  Third, it doesn't feel like the world is very organic. I remember in the first Deus Ex I looked for three or four ways to approach the weapons dealer Mouse, enjoying how each method got a different reaction and different prices from the guy. In this, it felt much more stiff. You complete missions by either being stealthy or shooting everyone. Anything you do beyond that is sort of ignored.   For instance: Two cops are arresting a neighbour in some ♥♥♥♥♥♥♥t anti-aug crackdown. I took them both down and tried to free my neighbour, but... well, I couldn't. The game didn't expect that so it has zero consequences or impact on the game.   Same thing goes when I see a bunch of augs being held by police later that night. I can't free those guys apparently, even though I had a subquest to free another group of dudes just 10 minutes before. Those NPCs don't have names I guess so they're deemed Not Important.  Now, did I like the game anyway? Sure did. The stealthing is fun, Adam Jensen is a superb character and the plot is quite intriguing. But while I appreciate it for what it is, one cannot help but wish this had been something more.

Before you question the purchase price, consider that the game comes several different and previously-unreleased game modes, including Black Label mode, both of which are things that Cave could have sold separately as DLC.  Resurrection is one of my favorite shoot 'em ups of all time, so my review will be a bit biased here. I've only played the 360 version at conventions and rarely at friends houses in the past, so to have the game to play whenever I like is a boon. I've only ran through the game once, but it plays fantastically-- 60fps with no lag (other than the pre-programmed lag that Cave puts in, which is totally acceptable.)  For those that have never played a Cave shmup before, or for those that aren't into the shmup genre as a whole, I don't really know if I'd recommend it. But for someone that's either a shmup fan or looking to get into it, this is a great place to start-- the music is memorable and addictive, the game as a whole is challenging but not COMPLETELY impossible, and the game is much-better built on launch than Mushihimesama was when it first came out.     Now to grind until I can face Hibachi.    EDIT: If you're having trouble getting used to the Z/X/C/V four-button setup that is the default with the keyboard, try rebinding V to the spacebar. Using space to hit hypers is a lot easier if you're more used to the Touhou style of 'holding in one button for constant fire and holding in another to focus your fire & slow down movements' like I am.  EDIT 2: This initial review was written before the details of the soundtrack being free DLC for the first week were announced. Because of that, I've retracted that part of the review.

DotA 2 Will drain you of your will to keep playing, and yet you will always return. DotA 2 Will make you regret ever playing it, and yet you will always think 'Just one more game' DotA 2 Will take away all your free time, and yet you'll almost feel proud of how many hours you have in DotA 2.  With the time you spend on this game, you could do all sorts. Study on learning a new set of skills to further your career. You could raise money for charity. You could be an aid worker for a foreign country and help improve the lives of others. You could even just play other games, you know, those ones sitting in your backlog, pushed aside by DotA 2.   But you won't. You will always return to this game. The maximum you will learn from playing this game is how to insult others in various languages (mostly these languages will consist of Russian and Peruvian.). This game will become as much a part of your life as eating or blinking. Half the time you will not even remember having played DotA 2 on a certain day. It just becomes natural to you.  If you start playing this game, you  will  become addicted. You will also spend obscene amounts of money on cosmetics. A quick chest purchase here, a few random items on the market there, and boom. Like me you'll have spent far too much on a game which needs no money spent on it. If you start DotA 2, not only will you be subjecting yourself to a motivation-destroying hobby, but also subjecting yourself to a wallet emptying one too.   tl;dr This game will take away your time, money and patience for others. You will become an angry person.  Basically 10/10 GOTY All years

This game has a grocery list of issues and eight years has not preserved this game very well. This game is a poor console port and it doesn't hide the fact. The game will show you an X-box controller for controls when you are sitting mouse and keyboard in hand. The controls are very stiff and the character is a nightmare to control. Every jump or hookshot is an agonizing movement as you cannot control the character until the animation is finished. Switching weapons requires the character to come to a complete stop and then switch weapons. Most of the enemies in the game will sit around and wait for you to to come and kill them, they will rarely put up a fight. The human enemies in this game will sometimes shoot in your direction, but as long as you don't approach within 50 feet they won't pursue you.   The game uses an energy mechanic that if you kill an enemy it will drop a puddle that you can collect. Unfortunately, the puddle won't last too long so if you kill an enemy from too far away you need to rush right for it to collect it. There are moments when you kill a bunch of enemies, then need to spend a lot of time to run around and collect all the energy. This requires you to stand directly on the puddle, no ranged collect, by far the most annoying mechanic for a majority of a game.  Lastly, this game uses waypoint saves with no option of saving individually. This presents a problem during boss fights and bosses that will one hit kill you.

This is quite honestly the best Horde Mode you can play today. Or atleast one of the best. Don't know what a horde mode is? Basically It's a coop throw away mode devs make alot. where a small group of friends( or ♥♥♥♥♥♥♥s who joined and have no idea what the word teamwork means) get together and take on waves of monsters. often zombies.To make an entire game that is 90% horde mode is difficult. You would have to make it fun as hell, goofy at times, Intense, a wide variety of foes, and a good balance between teamwork being essential and relying on the behavior of other players who eventually start to act like total trolls. Killing Floor does all of that. let me just say now that, past beginner difficulty, this game is a challenge to say the least. Your experience of the match can vary based on the tweaks made by the server admin. A full on totally customizable sandbox menu lets you change everything like the specimens you face, waves you go through, and of course a wide variety of maps each being even better than the last. Like all horde modes, your experience would be very very dull unless you got to play with fun toys. KF offers a neat 'traders store' between rounds. Where you can buy guns, armor, ammo, and if you like change your chosen perk. Perks can give you discounts and bonuses on many different things but the big thing is each perk you have offers It's own weaponry. If I have the Berzerker perk I'll mostly only be able to get melee weapons. If I want a shotgun or a m4 or something like that I'll need a specific perk. Choose wisely though because You can only change once between rounds. If you are easily offended by an onslaught of microtransactions and DLC, KF might just ♥♥♥♥ you off. As one can dissect from the selection of DLC it showcases in game and in the tore page, there is blatant use of the sort. for example, you start off with about 5 different character skins to choose from. There are litterally atleast 7x more skins available so long as you got the cash. the same can be said about guns. If you can learn to ignore all these, you can still have a more than satisfying experience while not paying a dime over the cost of the game.  pros: -fun as hell -intense -satisfying mechanics and usually good players to play with -solo mode -fully customizable experience  cons: - load of DLC and microtransaction. -maybe a little too hard for some -can be hard to get a good game going -visuals often below average being a game from 2009(granted still easy on the eyes) -joining a lobby in game can be a chore with a small amount of euros and a damn pistol to use.  overall a refreshing experience that leaves one very satisfied, a game worth the price of admission. [7.9/10]

Mirror's Edge is wonderfully refreshing. It's set up like an FPS, but you rarely use guns. Instead, you focus on complex movements to avoid enemies or hand-to-hand combat with occasional, minimalistic gun-play. The art style is also unique, simple, and stunning. I like the combat and the story, but even if you find them lacking the game will still provide you with a very unique experience. Games like this push the industry forward, even if they don't make it big. I first played this on the 360 and then got it for PC because I loved it so much.

Don't get me wrong - there are moments of true brilliance, both visually and in terms of script, in this game. This is a propper arthouse game, no doubt about that. But to experience it all you must grind for hours and hours on end. The length of this thing is ridiculous. There are five chapters there. Sounds reasonable? Not so, unfortunately. The length extends enormously, because you will die/fail and retry hundreds (literally!) of times. When after a series of lengthy action/platforming sequences you think that you're finally at the end of the chapter, you're usually not even one third way through. The change of scenery helps combat this, but everything brakes down again when you are forced to stay in the new environment for much longer than is actually required. This is irritating as hell. And the emphasis on arcade-like (and even 2D sidescrolling!) timed jumping - mind boggling. There is no proper feeling of flow at all here.  Sure, if you're a hardened gamer specializing in third person action games, get this game. You should have a blast! But if you are not (I myself have 20 years of experience, but mostly in first person action games), think twice. And if you do buy it, don't even contemplate playing it on anything but the easiest mode.   Developers of this thing should point their attention towards regular people next time and get rid of this tedious platforming and trial/error based mechanics of grinding out enormously drawn-out levels.  BTW, Steam says I'm 13+ hours in (actual number may be even higher, as usual), but in the game, I'm about 50% done. So for me this is a 25-30 hours game, and that is way too much for a story driven linnear action experience, imho.

The Good: DirectX 11 visuals look stunning, fun and past paced action, bullet time is used well, superb camera work and cinematography, one of the most engaging stories in any recent shooter, lengthy story mode  The Bad: Some bullet time quirks, last-man-standing has some issues, gameplay is a bit shallow and feels repetitive some times, scripted bullet time events are spread too thin  Max Payne pretty much took bullet time effects mad popular by The Matrix and made it mainstream for games. Back in the day Max Payne was a high-tech graphic noire story by Rockstar Games. It was fun, full of action, and had a great story and character. The second game did the same thing, but felt too familiar to the first game. The third time around is a decade later, but Rockstar now has the tech to do what they originally wanted. Max Payne 3 is one of the best looking games out there, but also features one of the richest and well delivered cinematic stories in recent gaming history.  Max Payne has retired and is just mourning the death of his wife and kid while getting drunk every day. An old buddy from the police force stops by and recruits him for a body-guard job in Sao Paulo protecting a very rich family in a third world country. Things go wrong, like they always do for Max, and he needs to redeem himself and set things straight after so many screw-ups through the game. I can't say much more without spoiling the story, but you're in for a treat on this one.  The game feels familiar once you start getting into it. You can dual wield weapons or use a two-handed weapons. Bullet time is back with bullet dodge as well. You can get behind cover and use bullet time to pull off headshots and kill a dozen enemies that would normally kill you without it. Bullet dodge is fun to when you don't have cover or just want to knock some guys down quickly while moving to cover. My only issue here is that it isn't as fast as previous games. Spinning around in bullet dodge is slower and feels too weighty for me. Max's bullet time meter only refills if you stick your head out and start killing enemies without it so there is some balance. If you get hurt you can pop painkillers, but they aren't in abundance like the last two games. I don't think I ever had over 4 at one time and they are hard to come by.  That's pretty much all there is to the action. There are many different weapons with things added on like laser sights, nightvision sights, scopes, and flashlights. I really wish the gameplay was evolved a little more, but all you do is shoot everything that moves. There are some great cinematic moments that break this up like bullet time shooting in different scenarios which is really fun. I just wish it wasn't spread so thin because a lot of the time the game gets extremely difficult and repetitive with too many of the same type of shootouts in a row. At least the environments change all the time so you have a lot of different scenery to look at.  Max Payne 3 has some of the best camera work and cinematography in a game that I have ever seen. At certain points the camera will snap to the last enemy in the area that is dying so you can pump more rounds into him as he falls. Then the scene will go right into a cut scene seamlessly and then right into another action sequence. I don't think I have ever felt like playing an action movie before more than I have with Max Payne 3. The game is also really long with 14 chapters that will take about 12+ hours to beat. The story is just bursting at the seams with detail and lots of scenarios to make it not seem rushed and incomplete.  There's a multiplayer mode that can be pretty fun as well as an arcade mode to keep you coming back. You can play through the main story again to find all the golden gun parts and clues if you want for achievements. The game also plays better on the PC due to the pin point precision of a mouse, and the addition of the latest DirectX 11 graphics which will require a monster rig to run at a decent frame rate. I didn't find any major problems with the game, just a few annoying ones that were persistent throughout the whole game like the difficulty imbalance, some bullet time quirks, and actually a big issue with last-man-standing. If you have a pill bottle left and you die you get a few seconds to shoot that enemy and Max will automatically consume the bottle. If Max is flopping around or turned around you have to wait for the reticle to automatically face the enemy. If you are out of ammo you're screwed. Some times objects will be blocking your shot and you get screwed there too.  Overall, Max Payne 3 is a wonderful game with top-notch cinematography, superb voice acting, and high-end visuals. Fans of the first game will be pleased with this lengthy shooter, but the gameplay itself can be repetitive, and shallow some times. The cinematic bullet time events are spread too thin, but it can be forgiven due to how wonderful everything else is. Max Payne 3 was well worth the 10 year wait, and here's to hoping there is another one.  ~Score: 8.5/10

app_name: DARK SOULS™: Prepare To Die Edition
review_text: Dark Souls is a JRPG that allows the player to experience entirely what the genre is named after, playing a role. This game allows people to choose entire who they want to be, how they explore they world around them, and how they wish to interact with this world. Many people enjoy Dark Souls for its challenging combat and the satisfaction that follows from conquering these challenges. The combat is entirely based on player knowledge and exploiting this knowledge to give you an edge against enemies, not requiring fast reflexes or inputs. There is also a deep and diverse weapon system within the game that allows players to chose practically any fighting style they are most comfortable with. But aside from the widely enjoyed battle system there is a very sublte narrative that is constantly being offered to the player, however it is very rarely just given to the player via cutscenes like most modern games. The player can either explore the world and interact with the NPCs around them and learn the rich lore of the world they are in, or they can entirely skip it and rush to the end of the game, it's never forced onto to learn anything. Dark Souls has so many deep and fulfilling secrets within it that allows even the most veteren of players find something interesting on another run through the game. I would suggest this game to anyone with the patience to enjoy an adventure, versus just another game title.

What do you know: A proper, lengthy fantasy adventure that's actually quite good, although doesn't really reach greatness.  Perhaps it's because the game is stuck a bit too much in the past, with a metric ton of references to classic LucasArts adventures in conversations, situations and puzzles alike. I'm sure the designers meant some of the puzzles as homages to the originals, but they just seemed like swiped material to me. Also, you don't need to be breaking the fourth wall with a jokey pop culture reference every 15 minutes or so. I liked the game best when it was just doing its own thing, not when it was imitating others.  Maybe it's because the game does begin to falter in its latter half, with puzzles becoming more and more about just clicking everything in sight, locations getting reused and re-reused, and the narrative getting a bit muddled with constantly switching protagonists. I think a more focused approach with a couple of hours of fat trimmed from the gameplay might have worked better.  Anyhow, not a bad adventure game. Some of the scripting could use some work, but technically this was rather nice with polished graphics, unobtrusive music and a smooth interface. Doesn't trump Daedalic, the reigning German adventure-makers, but doesn't stray too far behind.

If your looking for a review on this game you've come to the wrong place, but if your looking for an outstanding lemon bars recipe you've come to the right place.  Ingredients: 1) 2 cups Flour 2) 1/2 cup Sugar 3) 1/4 teaspoon Salt 4) 2 sticks (1 Cup) Salted Butter, Cut Into Small Cubes 5) Filling 6) 1-1/2 cup Sugar 7) 1/4 cup Flour 8) 4 whole Large Eggs 9) Zest And Juice Of 4 Medium-sized Lemons 10) Powdered Sugar, For Sifting  Directions:  1. For the crust: Preheat the oven to 350 degrees F. Grease a 9-by-13-inch pan with butter.  2. Stir together the flour, sugar, and salt. Add the butter to the bowl and use a pastry cutter to cut it all together until the mixture resembles fine crumbs. Press into the prepared pan and bake until golden around the edges, about 20 minutes.  3. For the filling: Stir together the sugar and flour. Crack in the eggs and whisk to combine. Add the lemon zest and juice and mix until combined. Pour over the crust and bake about 20 minutes.  4. Allow to cool in the fridge for a minimum of 2 hours, then sift powdered sugar over the top before cutting into squares.  (Use an 8 x 10 pan if you'd like the layers to be a little thicker.)

This was a great game. Made great by the music and the storyline: thoughtful, provoking, nihilistic yet full of hope, faith, and courage. It had a slightly different feel in terms of playstyle: entertaining, interesting, not unenjoyable. Playing 'Remember Me', I felt as though my enjoyment of simply playing was only half of what was driving me to play. I wanted - no, needed - to know what happened next. So, I played on. I felt the music, felt the rhythm of Nilin fighting through her existence as she pieced together her memories... her life. I needed to know - even though I didn't agree with altering memories, the reality, of those Nilin cared for as well as striking out against other victims of her reality, of her existence - that Nilin's actions justified the end: stopping the atrocities done to those around her - to the world as she knew it. With that in mind, I recommend this game. How could I not. I mean, if you had the ability to never experience pain, would you make it so?... I wonder. Well, that's my time on the soapbox, back to playing a second time through. Cheers, and Remember Me soon.

app_name: Injustice: Gods Among Us Ultimate Edition
review_text: I first went into this on the 360 expecting a janky reskin of Mortal Kombat with a generic tale of silver age Superdickery. On both counts I was pleasantly surprised - this is a rock solid fighter that does a fine job capturing the feel of superhumans braining each other with cars, backed up by a pretty good Justice League story and a hefty amount of extra modes, from the inventive character-themed challenges of the Star Labs to the King of the Hill lobbies online.  On the downside, like many of its kind, the online play is on the patchy side right now - connection quality seems more unpredictable and choppy than on the console versions, and while the lobby browser makes hopping onto a lively winner-stays-in session quick and easy, the matchmaker's a waste of space that just sits there pretending to search for people.

Although the game has an interesting concept, most of the game is just running back and forth. It does deliver a good story, with some nice twists. Depending on your game preferences it could be considered a good game.

This has possibly been one of the hardest games I have ever played. Blood of the Werewolf is a mix of Dark Souls and Mario, for the reason that it is a 2D platformer and will kill you more times than days in a year. It combines the aspects of classic platformers with their unforgiving demeanor, and the looks of a modern medieval-styled game. In Blood of the Werewolf the player takes on the role of Selena, a mother and wife seeking revenge on the various creatures of the night that killed her husband and kidnapped her son. All the events go on throughout one night, so when Selena comes in contact with moonlight she turns into her true form as a werewolf. Numerous cut scenes portray the story of her struggle to retrieve her son from famously known villains of old. Multiple stages allow the player to traverse unique levels and collect power-ups and sigils. In this game sigils, are similar to gold coins except that they reward with health increases and score boosts. There are a number of upgrades for both of  Selena’s forms that improve the crossbow or add more focus attacks for the werewolf. Overall the game poses a challenge and is nostalgic for those gamers that have been playing for twenty plus years and remember the original platformers.
