Right, so I have been a big fan of the RCT franchise because they are awesome games. A few people in the positives have written that it's great to be able to create a park that's 'whatever you want'. Don't get me wrong but what I want from RCT3 is the RCT franchise I know and love, except better.  So the graphics: They are bad. They have dated terribly. (They are old, yes, but they have aged tragically). A few people have said the opposite but look at the pictures with people in and you'll see what I mean. This isn't necessarily an issue, but my problem was that I wanted the plain looking characters from RCT1 and 2. The interface: Again, really not that good. In RCT1/2 the interface was simple. To save you hit the floppy disk. To get rides you hit the helter skelter, to build anything you hit the crane. Well fortunately this system has been replaced with one of excessive numbers of buttons that you will never want to touch. There's no intuitive building system either.  So now to the details of the game: Shops/Stalls: Now have an added feature where you can add toppings/details. Neat, huh? You can decide whether customers get cheese on the burger or not. Whether they have ice in their drink. Ice Cream in their milkshake. Now, that's all well and good except when you're setting the price of the goods it doesn't tell you how much profit per item you will make, which is a pretty important detail. No? And you're not told the costs associated with adding a topping. How useful. I suppose at least they tell you when you're undercharging. 3D rollercoaster rider: Well this is a neat feature. It's not a bad idea, especially when games like theme park had the feature from the beginning. There is an unfortunate problem though, it's jolty and a bit of an anti-climax. It's a neat feature though. 'Parties' of Guests: Another neat feature in concept but really unnecessary. I always figure that if a tycoon game starts to make its customers more complex in a sequel then you've got problems, but for RCT I thought it might work. After all, when was the last time you went to a theme park by yourself? Unfortunately it doesn't seem to operate under general logic. If someone is going to queue up for one ride for a month then surely you'd go and get some food or walk around the scenery? No, you instead clog paths and complain, 'my isn't it busy?' 'Three difficulty modes': Yeah... Nice try at claiming that. What that actually means is you can pass the level in three different ways. The customers aren't more difficult at a higher level, they don't need better ameneties or better roller coasters. Nope, you actually just get a more time constrained (or higher guest count/park value) with less time. Surely difficulty levels don't work that way?!?!?  I am a massive (read: MASSIVE) fan of the franchise and I'd seriously suggest that if you want to give the game a go you should go and buy either roller coaster tycoon the first, or the second. The game is better. The graphics are /technically/ worse but in sum the game is better.  EDIT: Anyone who is acting like I'm saying game graphics are important you will note I am saying that this gameplay is no better... and the graphics have aged badly. //This is a bad combination//. I agree that graphics aren't important, but they are a factor to be considered when buying a game.

This game will undoubtedly go down as my favorite game of all time.  This game is outstanding from a technical and design standpoint. The art is nice and has a unique style, the gameplay is top notch with more decisions and processes in a single room than some games have in an entire level.  Pros - 1. Incredibly easy to learn, yet hard to master gameplay. 2. A solid variety of weapons and passives make good runs alot of fun 3. Tons of unlocks and hidden things make for high replayability 4. Incredibly satisfying when you play well, or beat the game.   Cons - 1. Very difficult. It will take you a long long time investment to clear the game.  2. Drops and consumable items are extremely rare. It makes some runs just a disaster as you run out of ammo and other resources and it essentially becomes unfun/unfair. 3. Some of the guns are so worthless you are almost better off leaving them on the ground. 4. The good items are -REALLY- rare. There are numerous items ive only ever seen once in my 300+ hours of play.  Overall this game is fantastic and worth playing for anyone who likes the twin stick genre.

I have played this game both on xbox 360 and PC with a total of over 100+ hours and can say it is an awesome game!  First you begin fighting the almighty Frieza, then you move onto Perfect Cell and finish defeating the indestructible Buu but that is only the beginning of this journey of a game.  The things you get in this game are cool and worth the you get many ways to customize your character such as skill points, clothing, race and gender. Story line is awesome because it is a little different from many of the previous Dragon Ball games and has a cool twist to the end. Parrelel quests are a good feature, it adds a whole lot more content to the game and cool drops so you can repeat the same quests waiting for a skill or a piece of clothing to drop. I also find that the fact you can change your characters skills into being an all melee type badass to a Kamehameha blasting maniac gives the game a lot of variation in terms of what you want your character to be, also choosing the right race with the right stats can give you a massive boost of power in battle.  I would say the things to improve on this game is more options for your character such as more hairstyles, I would like to have more hairstyles such as Raditz's and Vegeta's style, the ones we get for them are pretty lame as almost everyone in DBZ has a cool looking hairstyle and we get these Also a nerf on the character's skills would be nice as when I like to go pvp you get people maxing out everything into one stat and you get 1 combo'd with melee attacks or an ultimate skill which is not that fair or fun in my opinion.  Other than that this game is good if you are a dragon ball fan or even if you are not a fan pick it up and within a week you could be sat there watching the whole Dragon Ball series! This is the first game I have picked up of the Dragon Ball series so I can not compare it to any others but this being my first game I was blown away when I first got in and was fighting frieza

In this version, the dialogue has been rewritten, and full voice acting has been added, and it is clearer than before what is going on, where you can go, and the impacts of certain decisions. This is a vast improvement on an already impressively solid RPG, and if you had trouble with the original version of the game's first few hours, the Enhanced Edition will much better bring you into the fray.  The new controller interface is also a fantastic addition -- you can now play couch coop with two XInput-compatible controllers, and while it is a little confusing at first how to manage the radial dial-based menus, you will get used to it. Co-operative play adds a surprising amount of depth and lets you fully engage in the duo hero scenario the game is built around, being able to have two humans debate choices as an actual game mechanic, and letting you split your party into two to explore and investigate. It's really a special game, with brilliant writing and amazing depth.

Easily worth the price. This is a great game if you're just looking to fill some time with something both fun and challenging.  At it's core the game is basically a suite of mini-games, each on performing a different function in the overall scheme of the game, from equipment maintenance to operation to economics. What makes this game more than just a suite of mini-games though is the management of all those mini-games. Running one machine doesn't stop the others from running, and they will continue to do so even if you're not in that screen at the time. Running multiple machines at a time is key to success, but each machine requires a certain level of attention to keep them from not only producing oil, but from suffering damage as well. Damage can be repaired, but often only by spending time at the start of each production day, and the more damaged the machine is the harder it will be to repair.  Machines can also receive upgrades over time to make them more efficient, but this can also be something of a double-edged sword. The more efficient a machine is the faster it will do its work, and the more attention you will likely need to give it to keep it producing. So as your machines get faster, so must you in order to manage them. When all is said and done this can result in some pretty hectic gameplay that can certainly challenge the player. The machines you get per stage can also change with each play, so that adds some good replay value beyond just trying to beat a score.  Beyond the gameplay though, another part of the game that really shines is the interface. The audio and visual ambience of the interface on the machines is all very well done. From the hums and clicks of the machines to the almost dieselpunk appearance of the machine interfaces you can tell it was all made with great attention to detail. The hard work the developer put in to the ambience can not be overstated and shows a certain artistic appreciation that can often feel lacking in modern games.  So yeah, for the price you definitely get your monies worth on this one. Sure, you might not marathon a game like this for hours on end, but it's like something along the lines of Tetris or similar games, something you'll just pick up from time to time when you're just looking for something fun and challenging to do. This game fits that bill perfectly.

This review is for 'Dragon Age: Origins - Ultimate Edition', that is, including all DLCs and the Awakening expansion  Dragon Age Origins, despite its...age (dates back to 2009) is still the best classic RPG and the only true spiritual successor of Baldur's Gate.  PROS + The game is huge. A completist walkthrough (there are hundreds of missions and side quests and endless dialogues) can easily take 130 hours. + The tactical combat is very good. + As of 2014 the best storyline ever. + Fantastic characterization of companions, allies and foes. + Truly epic + Strong replayability (different routes, different endings) + Good, detailed graphics  + Excellent voice acting + Easy to use GUI  Cons - While the main story (Origins) is polished, the follow up (Awakening) is still (and will always be, considering it's 5 years old) buggy, with a weaker story line and loses a bit of the high impact involvement with companions. - No voice acting for the main character (for some reason, they did not go the Mass Effect way).  The sequel, Dragon Age 2, is a completely different game (more like an action rpg, buggy, with a weak storyline and silly game mechanics)), so don't bother.  Overall 9,5/10 Dragon Age: Origins stands as the Golden Standard for all RPGs

Recettear: An Item Shop's Tale is a Dark Comedic Capitalism Simulatior Dungeon Crawler game.  I said Dark Comedic because it started right off with our loli main character having to operate a shop along with sarcastic loanshark fairy (despite her denial) capable of taking everything the main heroine ever loved away from her and making her live in a cardboard box with only two internal organs all just because of her chunni father abandoning everything behind just to be a hero. And its all played out in its glorious humor and witty dialogue.  Along the way you get to meet other more screwed up people to assist you in your shop keeping. Examples include: -A swordsman who manage to be even poorer than Recette herself and have problems with his budget management (SERIOUSLY STOP BUYING ALL THE WOODEN SHIELDS WHEN YOU ALREADY HAVE A BETTER ONE LOUSIE).  -A squishy pricky shota black mage who tries to show you how unlikeble he is every moment he is on screen (STOP SELLING ME USELESS STUFF CAILLOU.)  -A drunkard thief who have no qalms about murdering your companion the first time you meet her(NO COMPLAINTS THOUGH,CHARME GOT THAT DPS.)  How do they help you you ask? By plundering and stealing items from the natrual wildlife in dungeons to sell back to the very same adventurers that you hired OF COURSE.  The game itself is split into two parts: Store Management Part and Dungeon Crawling part.  For the store management part you got to place items on your selves,pray to RNGsus that little girls won't flood your shop and haggle with customers in order to rip them off for the highest profit margin ever.(Seriously why don't they buy their candy off the marketplace?) Its much harder and funner than it sounds as you have to estimate what is the best price to sell your stuff at depending on the type of customer you had.(Or just use a guide but wheres the fun in that).  For the Dungeon Crawling part. You can hire adventurers to enter dungeons to fight monsters,plunder chests and all that standard cliche dungeon crawling stuff. Each adventurer have their own unique playstyle such as fighter,mage and archer. Sure it sounds boring,but its the only way to progress the story and not get your loli waifu wind up living in a box.  One of the best aspects about the game is the dialogue and characters. The game never fails to make you laugh at most sentences as each character have their own quirks in their personality that makes their interactions lovable and funny.  I bought this game from the weekend deal for $5. $20 might seem a bit much (IT IS A GAME ABOUT RIPPING OFF PEOPLE ANYWAY) but I promise you every moment of the game is worth your money.  CAPITALISM HO!

Amazing game..I'm close to finish the game and I can say this is my favourite post-apocalyctic game! (there are not many games of this kind but I prefer this to fallout).  PRO:  - AMAZING environment and style, personally I like it (despite the textures are bad..), it truly set you in a post-apocalyctic world!! - nice variety of weapons, gadgets, supplies and ammo you can use. There is a simply and friendly crafting system that allow you to craft ammo, supplies, medikit and gadgets from raw materials. - nice and smooth gameplay, the fights are good, the engine can calculate where you shoots and wich ammo/ weapon you use in order to set a specific animation and all is well fit! - between missions you can reach cities and quest locations with a car equipped with rifles and cannons and have to fight enemies on the road in order to reach your destination safety (or you can try to escape). - playing the game on the hardest difficulty gives a nice challenge. - Good story for an FPS game. - The game run smooth always at 60 FPS (with i5-4670k and 2x SLI of GTX 780) - Didn't found any bug/crash during my playtrough. - A lot of main and side quests and races to do.  CONS:  - the price.....20€ is a steal!! This game is great but it's a port of a 2011 game and it's worth what a I paid durins summer sale: 4,99€. - + LOW textures resolutions. Outside the output is great and you can't notice it but inside you can see how bad the textures are..but personally I didn't care much about it because they have a unique style and let all the enironment look more 'rough', it fit the style. - + There are some 'glitches' with textures that popup late sometimes but I couldn't notice it very much, is a very little distraction and happens just in particularly cases (and more with low spec hardware I think).   UPDATE: I finished the game and I would say something: I know someone can be disappointed by the end but they are working on a sequel...so the 'bad' end it's cause they wanted to make a sequel and I hope it will be awesome :) (at the moment is stopped cause they are putting all the effort to finish DOOM 4 but they will take it after the release of doom)

A nice simulator. Unlike other games that try to give a 'Warfare' experience but instead give you a stupid CQC stage, in this game, it is 'Do or Die', you must team up, but you can give it a try to being solo, of course, it will NOT be like Call of Duty or Medal of Honor, it will be... an unique close-to-real warfare experience.  The game has few guns, but they are unique and can make a difference. The variants are extended, making it a huge library of choices you can have. The bullets have an unique rendering, and you can have tracers (Red, Green.), which can be seen and can help to tell a nearby teammate where the enemy is. The vehicles are decent, most are sluggish and it does not really give you a civillian feeling, but it gives out most of the Military feeling.  Many servers to play in, with different modes: Altis Life, Co-op, Zeus and so on. You can find a game mode you want, and with the huge quantity of modifications existing around Steam and the internet, you will certainly never stop playing, unless tactics or marksmanship isn't your thing.   9.5/10

Dear Esther supposedly transports the player to an island off the West Coast of England. The minimalistic pianomusic and the beautiful graphics stir an eerie feel of being lost in a desolate, deserted environment. As a game, Dear Esther is the sort of interactive medium that transcends the genre. Much like a great David Lynch movie should never be approached the same way one would approach a Star Wars, and is better to be perceived as a piece of art one gets to experience first hand. It hits home on a profound level without feeling the need to comprehend nor to put your finger directly on the heart of the matter. While information is seeping through in a disorderly way, it's only after the first play-through you start to analyse and put things into perspective, There's a reason the final cut scene is followed by a black screen you can only 'escape' from with said key. Still you'll be left with many questions and if you don't want to put hours into figuring it out, I srongly recommend the great youtube analysis on the game's story and events.   For anyone who might get more thrilled by The Stanley Parable than Assassin's Creed, and prefers a good Kubrick or Lynch over an action packed blockbuster.

If 'Waste of Potential' had its own word and was in the dictionary, Darkest Dungeon would be pictured next to it. Below that would be starbound.  Starbound is one of the few games which was more pleasurable to play during beta than release. They took out things that made the game interesting and fun to explore like temperature, randomized planets, and randomized creatures. Those are all gone. Now we have a bunch of in your face drama story that tries to force you along with it by content locking you out of certain things until you do it (This was in beta, but you were pretty much free after the first planet and a mission. Unskippable cutscenes and dialogues full of useless schlock about a run of the mill elder gods story that has any semblance of being mysterious obliterated by shoving it down your eyesockets.  I wouldn't say it's bad, but boy did the development team huff some paint when they decided their open world survival and exploration game with a skeleton story needed quest gating and less randomization.

I mean this in the best way possible: this game is just frustrating enough to not want to give up entirely at quite a few points.  I wound up 100%ing it oer the course of three days, and the way it is presented and developes the player is well thought out and well executed.  I don't want to comment on the difficulty or learning curve outside of 'it's doable'. If you keep going at it there is nothing you can't figure out, and the game makes it very easy to step away from a particular puzzle and come back to it. There is nothing in the game that can't be figured out with a little ingenuity or trial and error.   The art and animation are flawless and very pleasant to look at, and the little posters with phrases on them are a nice touch, even if the hints are a little late to be helpful at times(on purpose, I'm sure). Some of the puzzles are pretty clever and some involve a bit of synthesis.   Overall a really enjoyable experience. Well worth the purchase even at full price, 9/10.

Insurgency is one of the most precious First Person Shooters we have today, its a Realistic and Tactical game, wich needs team cooperation to win the match. Its a very intense game, it makes you go crazy while your killing, and make you fear when under fire.  So Welcome to Insurgency, a TRUE FPS, with , no Hit Marks, no MAP, no HP, no MEDIC'S, no HELP and not ridiculous Killstreaks, its a true firefight Bad guys vs Good guys.  The game counts with a Squad system wich both teams have 2 Squads, each squad have a number of classes that you will use in battle, nothing changes class to class but some classes have Guns that others not, such as Sniper and Support, also the game has Very good maps and map voting system, and one of the maps are from the Mod : Insurgency Modern Combat, wich is great too.  It has 8 types of Game Modes like Push, Skirmish, Strike, Co-op or even Search And Destroy. all of then extremely balanced for both teams. Guns in this game have a incredible realistic damage, so you kill with 1 bullet using a pistol, but you also die with 1 bullet, so do not run arround the map, stay with your team and try to complet the objectives.  The Graphics and Sounds are great, i'm really impressed what source engine can do with big jobs, that game has a lot of details, and a lot of Post-processing effects.  If you guys enjoy this game such as me, try to play the MOD, Insurgeny: Modern Combat, its great, and show how much work this guys did to make such good game like this, years of work made a exepcional game.  So, i'm going to tell you that if you are a Battlefield or Call of duty player, please don't run, try to learn how to play this game, you will be a better player in the future, i promise, but first you going to play Insurgency, and die a lot....A lot. But also Red Orchestra or Arma players are going to be good in this game, they have pacience to play it.   so Just 15$ for that kind of good game? what the hell are you waiting for? buy it...Now!

There are no monsters stalking you, no jump-out scares, No enemies to kill. So this game probably isn't for everyone. It's more of a story you wander through, similar to a visual novel. How much you get out of this game depends on how much time you spend on it. You pick up a lot if you go slowly through this creepy, poorly lit mansion and examine everything. Besides the main story line, you can uncover hints of your fathers professional frustrations, the burgeoning marital problems between your parents, and hints of your great uncle's scandalous past. It's a very voyeuristic feeling, picking through this family's personal belongings and piecing together their personal lives. And a nice shot of 90's nostalgia.  It's an interesting, but brief ride, taking only a couple hours on the first playthrough. I would recommend picking it up during a Steam sale or as part of a Humble Bundle.

Shadow of Mordor is great and a lot of fun! I'm sure it helps to enjoy the LOTR mythos but I still think you can have fun despite that as I found the gameplay to be really enjoyable. It reminded me of the Arkham games (also from WB) gameplay wise. First play through had me finish at 57% (all quests, etc) at 28.5 hours.  Plot/Game: The game follows Talion, a ranger of Gondor and his orc/uruk genocide across Mordor The story is new in that it doesn't exist in the LOTR mythos but WB fit it snuggly in where it doesn't hurt any actual story. I like it. As said above I feel like it plays similar to the Batman Arkham games. There are different side quests between the maps you get access to that can just be skipped if you want to go straight story. They're neat and they give you a lot of back story. There is a central way to skip quickly between different points on the map or you can ride between 2 beasts (though one isn't really fast).   Like Batman you can use stealth or just go swords out and run into 20 orcs. I had a lot of fun hanging from ledges and stealth killing things. The game features what they call their Nemesis system. There are various captains and warcheifs. If they kill you, they become stronger. You don't know what their weaknesses are but they can be discovered during gameplay. There are instances where captains might be fighting each other and you can jump in to kill a stronger one. A neat mission system that acts as a sidequest is when your friends on Steam die to a warchief/captain you will be given a mission to avenger their death and kill that warchief/captain.  Graphics (i5 haswell and gtx780): Nothing to brag about but they're pretty solid. Framerate is stable and even when there are tons of orcs/uruk on screen there aren't frame drops or issues either.  Audio: It's solid, no issues. Nothing that stands out in any crazy way.  Anything bad? I really enjoyed the game and had fun with it, nothing really horrible but some nitpicks. The last 2 bosses are over pretty quick. :(   Other? Torvin is awesome.  Great action game, play it!

I have always had a soft spot for 2D platformers and this is probably my favorite of the genre at the moment. It has got bright colorful graphics, great controls, interesting level design and it offers a good level of challenge without being unfair or frustrating, something Rayman Origins often missed the mark on.  The main part of game world is divided up into 5 different paintings, each with 9-10 levels based on different theme. The paintings all end with a boss and a musical level, where the action in the level follows the background music. I loved the musical levels and I wish there were more of them. There is an extra unlockable painting that let’s you play the musical worlds again with added challenges, but it is basically the same level.  The game has tonnes of content with invaded levels, daily/weekly challenges, and you can unlock most of the levels from Rayman Origins. You certainly get your money’s worth here.  Honestly the only caveat for this game is Uplay...I have had issues with it in the past and I know many people continue to even today.   To Ubisoft’s credit, over the last 3-4 years they have taken Uplay from being an unusable piece of malware to more of an annoyance. So let’s give them credit for that, I guess…  My advice is just log into the Uplay application once, set it to offline and never, ever, touch it again.

Styx: Master of Shadows - 8/10  I came into this game right after playing Of Orcs and Humans, right off the bat you can see that this is not a B list game; the developers have put a lot of effort into making this as close to a AAA as possible.   I've not played many pure stealth games before; even in the games in which you're given a stealthy option I always was going guns blazing. In Styx it's stealth or bust; but that's not a bad thing as the stealth elements are satisfying.  Combat is frowned upon; Styx is usually at a disadvantage when it comes to dueling opponents. To make matters worse the racket sparked from weapons colliding will usually attract other enemies which will have no qualsm in pincussioning you with throwing daggers while you try to block your opponent.   So you're left with the stealth option. There will always be more than one way to get past areas, some easier some harder. Slowly trying to make your way through, killing and hiding corpses or just slipping by.  Mechanics wise, the movement is pretty crisp. The only problems I've had was with jumping/hanging on to ledges.   Story-wise this is a prequel to Of Orcs and Humans and answers some of the questions that're raised in the game. It is also a lot better written and paced.  My favorite part of the game was:    Carrying the sidequest corpse through the area without being seen.    Least favorite part of the game:    Final boss battle, really annoying to not be able to finish it in a stealthy manner. Having to rely on just fighting your way through.

Needless to say, I have played the hell out of this game and will continue playing the hell out of it for a very long time. So, just because I have more cons listed below than pros isn't a bad thing. This is more of a focus list for things that, if fixed, would make what is a superior city builder absolutely brilliant.  What I love:   - The road building system is amazing. You can truly make just about any sort of spaghetti that you want if you're willing to put the time in.   - The visuals are spledid and watching the high volumes of traffic buzz around your city is a joy. Pedestrian AI is also great and I love the fact that they prefer to take the pathways that I build rather than the sidewalks.   - Spending hours developing an area, perfecting every little thing, and then watching it all come to together is one of my life's simple pleasures. This game is so immersive.  What's Disappointing:  - Lack of service structures. For instance, in the education tab we have an elementary school, high school, and university. That's it. There's no variety in the school types (private school, small/large university/campus) or any side options such as libraries. Prisons were added though, so that's a definite improvement to the Law Enforcement tab.  - Some building models look awful. First that come to mind are the opera house and original stadium. These buildings are not at all aesthetically pleasing and the stadium is unbelievably undersized for what it is. This is true for many of the low density homes too where one house is scaled much larger than the one next to it. It just looks bad. Also, parking structures do not exist in this game. Parking garages and parking lots are not available as options in the Transport tab and the parking lots that are attached to office/commercial/industrial stuctures are either very tiny or not there at all.   - Building during the night is next to impossible and rain turns the map into a giant mirror.   - Building along shorelines is a nightmare.  - Some of the default maps are far too hilly. This is a problem because building on slopes in this game can get real ugly real fast.   - While road building is a pleasure in this game, we need proper merging lanes on freeways and dedicated turn lanes/lights in intersections!  Those are all of my qualms with this game. Is Cities: Skylines perfect? Far from it. Is it the best city builder since Sim City 4? Absolutely. If Cities: Skylines 2 is ever released, and it improves upon some of items I listed above, it would be a day one purchase for me.

This game is a cross between GTA and Watchdogs and I'll give you one guess as to if I like it better. The fact that you get to play from a cops point of view is great, I honestly wish that there were more games that were like this. Often times when playing this game on my first playthrough I found myself going 'aw ♥♥♥♥, this is it. This is where Wei gets whacked.' The game is intense enough to keep you wanting to move through the storyline and even afterwards, the umber of side quests you have is astounding.  Where both GTA and Watchdogs lacked, this game takes those aspects and runs with them. Hacking makes more sense in this game than it did in watchdogs, and the storyline is way more engaging in my opinion. The story actually made sense and what you do was for a reason unlike GTA where you go shoot people for no reason (although if you'd like to do just that you can). I also felt like Wei was relatable and easy to feel bad/good/whatever for unlike a majority of the GTA characters as well.  Driving in this game is probably my favorite aspect, as you have achievements unlocked while driving for doing certain actions and that always kept me striving not to cut a corner too sharp or to hit a random person who walked out into the street.

Well, this game is a difficult one to rate, especially since all the hype was generated about the game made it seem so extremely prosperous and grand. Looking on it hard though made the game give me mixed feelings about it.  First off, I've played through FNAF1 already through night 6, and have gotten past night 4 on FNAF2 at the point I'm writing this review, and I've watched many playthroughs by many individuals and have been trying to get a solid view on the game.  FNAF1 was great because no-one saw anything like it before, and was a great and scary surprise to play and watch. The closest thing that it was compared to was Night Trap on Sega CD, which was more of a lighthearted goofy game, than one intended to give you nightmares. FNAF1 also incorporated animatronics that were haunted, bringing a new element to the horror genre that wasn't touched on too much. The game also included the idea of not being able to move about at all, where you couldn't try to run and hide from impending doom. You only had a limited power supply and two doors and a set of cameras to be able to try and ensure your safety. Because of these factors, FNAF1 gained great popularity and hype.  Now FNAF2 comes out, and it (more or less) is the same game as before, with a few more additional animatronics and changes in mechanics (power is now the marionette box and flashlight, freddy mask is the equivalent of the doors). Since most people who came to play FNAF2 had already played FNAF1, mostly everyone expected the same level of fear and fright in the game, without much confusion.  FNAF2 turned more into the 20/20/20/20 mode from FNAF1, and walked farther away from the initial fear factor the first game gave us (though this is in my opinion, yours may not be the same as mine). With the incorporation of the music box and the need to flash the flashlight, the game became more of a camera-mask-swapping-fest than a slower more fear induced game like the first game was.  Now, I'm not going to bash FNAF2 for going this route, as it still is a challenging game to play, but it was brought about to be a 'horror' game, meaning a truly frightening atmosphere, and gameplay that would occasionally cause you to be thrown off on occasion and be put into an alarmed state, pulling you into the game to understand it, and then scaring you to death.  FNAF2 doesn't have as much of the scare factor, or paranormal activity that the first game had, making it a game of just shielding yourself in time, where you essentially 'parry' off attacks from the various animatronics using the mask, while trying to keep the music box wound up.  The idea of parrying off attacks may sound similar to FNAF1, but FNAF1 brought the idea that you aren't able to keep up your defenses forever, that you will have to leave yourself vulnerable to attack on occasion, or else you will ultimately exhaust your ability to stay defended, making each person who played anxious if they were going to make it or not.  FNAF2 sorta had this, but it became more frustrating as the mask could almost be worn indefinitely, and the flashlight usually would not run out of power. The most error prone thing that would happen is having to deal with the music box being wound up, where then that leads you having to step away from the possibly scary atmosphere to wind it up and ensure it doesn't run out. FNAF1 didn't really have you having to that, but you did have to check on foxy on occasion (which you still do in FNAF2 but with a flashlight and audio cues).  Then on top of the music box, the Freddy mask usage with the older animatronics is very difficult, not impossible, but difficult, leading the player to feeling more frustrated than scared in many situations when they are attacked/killed by them.  Like I said, It's a hard game, and is a challenge, but it walked a bit too far from it's roots to keep that same 'feeling' the first game gave you.  The main thing that the game does offer that was a major part of the previous game was the story, where this one leaves more little bits to find to understand what exactly is going on in the little universe of FNAF, but without giving you too much, keeping you pondering and interested. But with more kinda explained, it also kinda removes that curiosity that drove many people to enjoy FNAF1 in the first place, and kinda takes away from the old experience as well (in my opinion).  There also some other factors that made FNAF2 better/worse than the previous game, but I'll list them here in pro/con form.  PROS:  1. New animatronics to deal with, leading to new patterns and strategies to develop  2. Challenging, good for those who wish to try and really beat the game  3. Expansions on the original FNAF story.  4. More of Scott's great artwork for the animatronics  5. Satisfying to beat (for both challenge and story factors)  CONS:  1. No options menu (not major, but would've been nice)  2. Stuck in 4:3 ratio (would've been nice if it rendered widescreen properly)  3. Freddy mask very difficult to use with older animatronics (might not be true if it gets patched/nerfed, but is at this time)  4. Atmosphere just not as 'scary' as the first game was.  5. Little to no paranormal activity (no hallucinations, or sudden changes in how the characters interacted with the cameras)  6. Game mechanics take away from the horror (it's more of a game of luck and snappy actions than one of resource conservation and fear)  7. Less hidden of a story (the details of the story are more obvious in FNAF2, instead of being more mysterious like in FNAF1)  OTHER THOUGHTS:  I think the game could've turned out much better if the game hadn't received as much hype as it did, and was given time to tailor it to perfect specifications. As much as I enjoyed scott's work and his effort, and his early release surprise, the game just lacked that perfect frightening polish that could've made the game really shine, as the framework is certainly there, but it just isn't tailored for the fear factor.  If there could be patches/DLC/Mods/etc that could remedy that factor, then this game may still have the potential to be great. Til then, the game is a challenging one to play, but won't give you that nightmarish experience the first one brought.  I'd recommend you trying out the demo first to see if this review holds up for you, and if you find the game is still enjoyable to play, then go ahead and buy it. If you are looking for that original experience you got with the first FNAF game, you might not enjoy this game as much.  If you haven't played FNAF1 yet, I do recommend playing it and then coming over to try this game out.

I will disclaimer this review by saying I feel the need to give it two very distinct recommendations for two very distinct audiences. For hard-core cyberpunk fans (such as myself) I give this game a very strong recommendation. For the general public or fans of the RPG genre I give it an extremely tepid recommendation.  There is something different about this game compared to its predecessors other than the setting. There is perhaps no other way to explain it other than to say something about it feels awkward.  Let's start by reviewing the elements of the game which are beyond reproach. First is the music. Like the previous games it is enjoyable yet unintrusive. It reminds me strongly of the Mass Effect soundtrack. This iteration, in a nod to the setting, has a subtle Asian tone which works well overall.  The art is similarly excellent, at least for the genre and style of game. Like the music, the scenery was given subtle Asian flair and is overall very interesting. Almost too interesting. It makes you wish there was another zoom level to really allow you to sort of immerse yourself in the ambiance created by the art and music. This almost-but-not-quite is a feeling I will come back to after addressing what is the best (and also the worst element of the game).  The writing. Like in Dragonfall, the writing of Lead Writer Andrew McIntosh is thoughtful, gritty, and engaging. I would buy cyberpunk novels from this gentleman. At the beginning of the game you pick a sort of profession specialty which allows you to unlock certain dialogue options. You can always see these options if not necessarily utilize them. These options show you that the writers have dug down deep and wrote for these characters as if they themselves were playing a role-playing game. Some of the dialogue, especially with Gaichu and Racter seem as if they were carefully researched. This was obviously a labor of love.  Unfortunately, I am going to suggest that the writing is actually TOO good. Unlike the music, which is nice but sort of disappears into the background, you cannot ignore the writing. If you ignore the writing, you ignore what is arguably the best aspect of the game. While it is possible to complete the game without engaging in much superflous dialogue the end result is that you end of up playing a long series of combat events. Tedious and boring combat events.  The combat is, and has been, the Achilles heel of this franchise. The turn based system is overly simplistic (most likely so they can sell it by the boatload to casual tablet players) and not well balanced. A well equipped group can simply run-and-gun through nearly every level. The magic-using classes are mere novelties. Unlike in many fantasy games, the magic is simply not worth it in comparison to a trusty AK-47. The Matrix was revamped as a stretch goal during the crowfunding and is much improved compared to its former iteration. To HBS credit, the Matrix is now much more engaging and gives the player a sense of urgency and danger commensurate with how the deckers actual speak of their time out of meat-space. Ultimately, though it still feels like a mini-game rather than an integral part of daily cyberpunk life. It was great of HBS to make the Matrix not suck but I feel they should have integrated it into other aspects of the game. Perhaps place data access points in non-combat areas. Have areas where deckers congretate in cyber-space. Allow decking to unlock dialogue options or perhaps access to hidden areas or quests. It is actually somewhat frustrating that the Matrix is much improved because it highlights its continued poor implementation and utilization within the game  So here is what ends up happening. You end up engaging all of the extra dialogue choices because you want to extract more life out of the video game, but there is something different in the dialogue department from the previous games. As the setting of this game is a fantastic imagining of China perhaps you will forgive me when I bestow the following moniker to the dialogue: Shadowrun: The Great Wall of Text.  Thats right, the dialogue goes on and on and on. Don't get me wrong, I love it. It devour it. Unfortunately, it is simply not comfortable to stare at a computer reading text for hours upon hours at a time. I do not exagerate by saying there is perhaps 1.5 hours of optional dialogue for every 30 mins or so of combat. It feels less like you are playing a game and more like you are reading a book with music and pretty (if zoomed out) graphics. But the alternative, as mentioned before, is an unsatisfying series of tedious combat events.  Here is where I think the game went wrong. Not the writers, they should be awarded, commended, promoted. The problem here is one of balance and the blame lies squarely with the game directors. I think the directors realized the problem with the game: it has no life to it. Strip away the writing and it feels like an empty neon-lit diner at midnight. Rather than attempting to imagine ways to inject life into the game they simply increased the volume of text. Unfortunately, they leaned on the text to the breaking point and what was once a game became a book.  I will not though, offer empty criticism without suggestions. I think what the directors needed were scripted events thoughout the game. Don't have people stand there in town waiting for you to come by and give them complementary psychological counseling sessions. Have people mug someone as you walk by. Have something happen if you loiter in the wrong place at the wrong time. Some of the third-party mods created by the community for Dragonfall feel like they have more life than this game because of their scripting. I have not myself delved into the scripting tools and ultimately the limitation of how much scripting can contribue to the immersion and depth of the game may be hard-coded and limited by the engine itself. If this is the case, my advice to Harebrained Schemes is if they wish to make another game in this series, either revert to more tight productions like Returns and Dragonfall, or, create an entirely new engine for the next iteration allowing players to finally sink in and completely enjoy the intelligent and beautiful world they have created.  This seems like an awful lot of criticism for a game I've given a thumbs up to but I hope I help potential purchasers of this game go into their purchase eyes wide open. I'd also love it somebody at HBS stumbled upon this and took some of this message to heart. I, and thousands of others love what you do, but you have to potential to turn something alright into a future classic with a bit of work.

Final Score - 11/10  This game's combat is awesome! To give one example: I encountered my first ogre and climbed onto its back so I could stab it in the face with my flaming daggers. The ogre proceeds to nearest wall, crushes my character against the wall, and my character falls to the ground stunned. Then the ogre picks up my character's limp body and tosses it over a ledge causing instant death. Upon retrying, I continually shot it in the face with flaming arrows to which it responds with trying to guard its face from them. Between my main character and my pawns, the ogre was eventually cornered in the same ledge I was previously thrown off of. While it hunkered against the ledge, cowering and covering its face, my warrior-type pawn was seemingly smart enough to shield bash it directly off the ledge to its death. Mind you, this was not a cut-scene, it was just regular in-game combat. I have never felt so satisfied with a monster enconter in a game as I did with that. Overall, all of the combat is superb.  The graphics may seem a little dated, but don't let that deter you. Running this at 1080 with all the graphics cranked up and it looks great. Special effects for spells are beautiful. Environments are a bit low-poly looking by today's standards, but they are very well made and feel pretty immersive. User created characters as well as the NPCs all feel unique and are also very well done. Also, all of the audio is fantastic and the music reminds me a bit of final fantasy games.  I've seen a number of people complaining about the UI, and while it definitely has a console feel to it along with some awkward default bindings, it isn't so terrible once it becomes familiar. Keep in mind, this is a console port, so some of the niceties we're used to on PC game UIs don't exist. As a result, it can be a bit tedious at times when needing to move lots of items around or navigating between multiple menus. Overall though, I don't mind it - and, after only a short time playing the game, it became very easy to use.  Lastly, the pawn system. Initially this seemed really strange and slightly confusing to me. I did have to look up some information online to figure out exactly how it worked. Now that I'm familiar with it and how to use it correctly, it is actually really cool. Don't be frustrated if at first it doesn't fully make sense. As the game progresses, it becomes pretty valuable and is a really unique feature you won't find in other games.  Seriously, if you love RPGs and you love a good challenge, just get this game.

Skyrim was one of the most-anticipated games of 2011, and you can see an amazing amount of polish and detail from Bethesda in some of the changes they'd made to the engine since previous Elder Scrolls games. No 'make your own class', character progression is even more free-flowing this time around. Skills rank up and become more effective as they're utilized, with a bonus based on how well-rested your character is and which mystical standing stone you've most recently attuned to. This allows a character to jump back and forth between more-efficiently raising 'mage-style' skills, 'warrior', and 'thief' abilities. Character level increments as enough ranks are gained in these skills.    Enemies level with the character, but are 'locked' at the level they were when you first entered that area. Cave of necromancers just too hard for you? Leave, come back in five levels. It means that fresh encounters will always be challenging, but rarely overwhelming.   The combat/interaction mechanics are updated so that there's a right-hand/left-hand, left-click/right-click mechanic. This allows the player to wield a spell, weapon, or shield in one hand and something complementary in the other. Two versions of the same spell can eventually be 'dual-cast' to improve effects, and this mechanic also puts a new spin on the two-handed, sword-and-board, and dual-wielding melee combat.   Skyrim is a very open, living realm with lots to do; so much, in fact, that it may be a tad overwhelming. Everyone wants something from the Dragonborn. There's discrete quest chains for the main storyline (Save the world from a world-eating, immortal Dragon who goes about resurrecting his dead dragonbros), the Mage's college (where a character who barely knows how to use magic can rise to the very top, not that many will notice), the Fighter's Guild (by any other name) that introduces some helpful companions and a new way of becoming something that goes bump in the night, the Assassin's Guild (with tales of love, madness, betrayal, and sticking pointy things into others), and a far-reaching, long-spanning saga for the humble Thieve's Guild of Riften that'll send you all across the continent as the stakes get higher and higher. Honestly, the last two seem to dwarf the 'real' plotline, but your mileage may differ.   Aside from (and included in) those, you have any number of random minor fetch, kill, and other classic quest-types, some of which use the new semi-random-generation 'radiant' quest system. They'll never run out, and rarely repeat, but generally are just there so that you know you can always do quests; they're rarely worth it.   So, whether you want to play a stealth-based archaelogist trying to snatch precious Nord treasures from the powerful undead Draugr guarding them, a shield-bashing, axe-swinging juggernaut with shouts powered by the stolen souls of freshly re-killed dragons, or a crafty mage with a penchant for bringing in Daedra friends to do her bidding, Skyrim's got options for you. There's rough spots, there's high points, but it's a great example of a proficient studio kicking out a AAA flagship title.   As for DLCs, I'll likely review those on their own.

Players host their own servers but it's not like BF4, these servers most are locked, others if you d/l this workshop item you can't get in, or if you didn't download another workshop item you can't get in. If you do get in, there's weird rules and no one to tell you what to do.   Conclusion Great platform/developer kit, but i paid good money for a 'game' and as such there's a handful of single player missions, no fmv cut scenes, the 'multiplayer' is not supported through arma only through community, no mission types or user friendly options. soldier animations are oddly dated for an advanced military shooter.

I played this game alot when I was younger. Probably around 6 years old. Most of the time I didn't know what to do, or how to really play the game, but I understood the general concept. Playing the game has made me relive old memories and it's still a good game to this day.   Splinter Cell, the first out of 6 games in the series is a good gem. Released in 2003, this game was quite worth the time. I'm quite surprised by the A.I. in this game due to it being an older game. The A.I. is generally smart and gives quite a challenge even on the easiest difficulty being normal. There are about 10 missions or so, but each mission is realtivley long and you will most likley die alot in the beginning as you need to get a good feeling for the game before you actually get into it. Most likley you'll have to watch a couple videos for throughout certain points in the game. Generally every mission has its challenge, but it's still very fun.  The sneaking aspect in this game is something that I have really never seen in a stealth game. Splinter Cell only offers this type of stealth in there games, where you have to be quiet at all times, move carefully every second and plan your tactics strategically and quickly as possible. Conviction and Blacklist didn't quite follow this same design for a stealth shooter but still made it very fun with a different design altogether. Meaning, the first splinter cell, pandora tommorw and chaos theory are quite unique to their design and make it worth playing.  Lets get into the game itself. The year is 2003. You play as Sam Fisher, a former agent of the CIA is now recruited into americas newest division agency, third echoleon. Along his side is Lambert, a former friend of Sam who helped on previous missions in other agencies like the CIA, and Anna Grmistodder. (I maybe wrong about the CIA part but I have good feeling he's related to that field.) She's a computer hacker that serves the agency and aids Sam in information about various things.  The story itself is quite interesting. It isn't anything too fancy, I haven't beaten the game yet but it is quite interesting as it dives into the political world of countries and the struggles that occour when new leaders or dictators rule over a country. Essentially this isn't any different as the basic story is stopping a dictator gone mad after realising the crimes he has committed. The game breaks this up over ten missions and generally the plot gets a bit more interesting each mission. The gameplay ties into this generally well, but it's more fun as the stealth feature gives it a unique feeling as this game masters the genre of stealth very well. You'll even find yourself going back to some missions to see what you could of done differently, or maybe even see if you're up to a harder challenge. This game makes it so it isn't too hard, but nothing like a cake walk. Generally there are times where you may get frustrated, but once you realise your abilities and how you can tie them in any situation, the game becomes generally easier and just fun to play overall. It may seem that this game wants you to think logically. Essentially, yes, it does want you to use your head and think outside the box a bit from time to time, but it makes it fun to the point where you don't have to worry to much on whether you'll be able to reach your objective.  Overall, this game is reccomended for people who like a challenge, as well as stealth games in general. This game is quite unique and in my opnion, games like these should be made more often rather then the shooters or stealth games we get nowa days.  9/10

I just completed this game. While it had some exciting moments, it leaves me both disappointed and frustrated. For background, I grew up playing the original and was a big fan of Return.   PROS:  -high production values -solid level design (It has arena-style locations, not just a series of tunnels.) -good mix of stealth and combat  CONS:  -poor save placement (For instance, it auto saves before you gather supplies throughout a level to face a boss. BEFORE!) -game strips your meticulously collected gear at the beginning of each chapter. (When Metroid Prime did it once 13 years ago, it was clever because it actually tied into the story; this is ridiculous.) -worthless jump -story manages to be both juvenile and self-indulgent (I don't mind silly, over-the-top nonsense, but when it turns didactic, one can't forgive how the clumsy writing butchers classic archetypes, Hendrix, politics...) -the ending is a turd in the punch bowl. If you insist upon playing this game, do yourself a favor and stop before the end.

app_name: The Dark Eye: Chains of Satinav
review_text: A gratifying yet not quite extraordinary adventure.   This was another very pleasantly paced and assumbled P&C adventure from Daedalic and while I did enjoy my experience in the game it didn't wow me in ways that a series such as Deponia had. The art style can only be described as an animated oil painting, and while the animation was only just adequate, the backgrounds in this game are absolutely gorgeous...the true selling point. They story is captivating and the characters suffient enough to carry it although I did find the end a bit anticlimactic.  I have to say, for a P&C, it was pleasant to find so many straight forward puzzles. I love the genre and grew up with it, but i have low tolerance for frustration and will often 'walk-through' before ripping my hair out trying everything against everything in my inventory. Most of the solutions all made pretty clear sense. Of course in the second half of the game there are a couple of instances of old adventure game pitfalls where I found myself saying, 'come on, really??' when the solution was presented, but overall an easy play for a casual P&C adventurer.   I enjoyed it but I'm not raving. My recommendation is wishlisting it until another sale comes along.

Well that was a waste of my money, my time, and my hopes... I should have seen it coming though, videos I had watched of the game didn't lie about the floatyness of the game. Something I never ever liked in any game. But hey ! If there is good level design, it'll be all right !  Oh.  Oooooh...  Everything feels wrong abut this game. Levels are not as open as they should be, often you'll simply find yourself in a dead end with the only way to go being climbing up stairs. Which of course doesn't mesh with the skates. I might as well not have the skates for unless you're in a slope or have been grinding for a while you hardly go faster than the police officers chasing you. And you trade that modicum of speed you have on them with incredible imprecision over your direction. It's really hard to gain some speed, it's really hard to move around. The game is not snappy in the slightest.  But let's pretend you actually got some momentum. What now ? STOP. Do a graph. Just stop moving. It's time for contextual stick wiggling, harly different from a QTE. It's not very fun, but it is the objective of the game as far as I could tell from these few levels I played. In that regard, it turns the game into more of a platformer as the point is not to build speed but to jump around so that you can reach spots to graph. But again, it's so imprecise I'd rather play a genuine platformer like Sly Cooper.  There is a point system too based on how you trick, but it basically amounts to how long you grind. Which goes in direct contradiction with the tagging objectives that asks you to stop dead in your track, not that it matters considering it's useless and you'll get good grades without even trying. By that I mean that you won't feel like you deserve it and will end up thinking that good grades are actually bad. Seriously. It's that irrelevant. I don't remember any level in the fews I've played that actually recquired you to get a certain amount of points anyway. Why is it even here ?  Let's add to that unskippable cutscene, unsuprisingly dreadful camera, the police forces that feel like they waste your time more than anything else, escaping them stucks you in a cutscene everytime for no other reason but to break your flow so everything about the opposing force, be it avoiding them or escaping them, is annoying, the narrative might as well not be there...  It's not fast enough to be cathartic, it's not precise enough to be rewarding. The levels aren't open enough to give any sense of flow unless perhaps you master them but nothing makes me want to. Graphing is just there to stop you in your tracks. The obstacles on your way are more annoying than threatening. Everything feels wrong. Nothing about it is satisfying.  I want to like this game, I really do but it's all around frustrating... I don't recommend.

I had roughly an hour to go, it was 1 in the morning and I was a few beers in. The game had not yet left me with anything or opened up a few old wound like it had seemed to for other people. Maybe it was build up too much in my mind as the emotional rollercoaster of a life time or maybe it just didn't resonate with me. I had to make this experience my own, but how?  I walked away from the game to slobber on a cigarette trying to convince myself just to finish it and see how I felt. So I sat back down and muted the game and played a random playlist of Keaton Henson's music and my hear started to feel the game and open up to the emotional story that it had been presented with. It someone else telling you to eat their favorite food their way and you being unsure about the taste and texture but as soon as you change the sauce and you instantly sent to culinary heaven.  To cut this short if you want to experience heartbreak hearing Keaton Henson's voice break during his song sweetheart, what have you done to us during the last scenes caused me to weep like I was listening to the story be told to me personally.

It's silly fun. I needed to test out some combinations till I found something I'm good with. Let me summarize you what it is. Third person shooter game. The different thing about this game is gun customization. As far as I can tell (I'm level 7 I think) there are no gun parts that can be bought with money. Only cosmetic stuff like outfits and different taunts, things like that are bought with money. You unlock gun parts as you play the game. You are given 'Blutes' for each match you played through. Then you use those to unlock gun parts. There are daily rewards. They can be certain amounts of blutes or cosmetic stuff. Today I got hip-hop pants for my character :D  When you first get in, you choose a character model. I think there are 3 in total but customization is very nice if you are willing to pay money. Characters are white or black buff man (who looks like Mr. T) with chicken legs and Helga (who is a fat woman). Then you craft your gun. First you choose payload type: Rifle, Launcher, Beam and something I forgot its name of. Then you choose other parts for your gun. There are over 6 part categories for each gun iirc which in turn makes good variety in guns. You can immediately test your gun after crafting it against taunting AI. You should try different guns and find what you're comfortable with. Then you jump in a multiplayer game. There is nudity (cartoony) and lots of gore in the game both of which can be turned off if you like. There is some puerile humor going on in there too. Taunts have a use other than looking funny, stupid or taunting the opponents in Blitz matches where you capture the point faster if you taunt at a control point but it leaves you vulnerable for the duration of the taunt.  There are 'Casual' and 'Competitive' games. Under casual you can play with bots but I don't recommend that. For some reason it wasn't fun for me. Vs. Humans is pretty fun. The game claims to match you with people who are at your level but I saw people complaining about matchmaking. I guess it is very difficult to get this thing right. Always some people are unhappy about matchmaking in any multiplayer game. If you do well in a match, you get extra blutes for that.  There are a bunch of different maps and game modes. In Blitz you and your team tries to dominate the enemy team in a given point in the map several times in a match in order to reach the required point to win the match. In Extraction, one of you tries to collect an ore called 'blutonium' lying on the ground. The goal is to reach to required point to win before the enemy team does. In Jackhammer, you try to steal enemy team's hammer and take it to your base while the enemy is trying to do the same. Hammer is also a weapon with an AoE attack which is fun but harder to use for obvious reasons. Again you need to reach the required point. There is another mode called Kill Snatch where you have to confirm and deny kills. It works like this. When someone dies, they drop a glass container with blue liquid in it. If you killed enemy and pick that thing up or your team mate does, you score. If enemy picks their own blue thing, they deny your kill. When one of the teams reach enough points, they win. I think there is another mode called Annihilation but I don't think I've played that yet. I haven't played any on competitive yet, so I don't know how it's like.  There is another F2P shooter game on Steam. It's an FPS called Team Fortress 2. I had played that before. For some reason I didn't have fun with it as much as I did with this game. In Loadout there is also some platforming and parkour elements apart from gun crafting. If you are remotely interested in shooters (that's me), this game might provide you with some fun.

A Post-Patch review. I aquired this game after it's removal from Steam knowing full well it's problems, and now with a patched game it is in more 'stable' condition to be enjoyed on PC.  After playing both Arkham City and Arkham Origins, the story in Arkham Knight fits perfectly, and adds what you'd expect in regards of new combat moves and shiny new features to enjoy while unfolding the events of the game. The main new toy in the game is the 'over used' Batmobile. Having said that the tank battles are un-Batman like, but do fit with the story and the 'extreme' nature of the stories setting.   Batman's combat flaws still have not been fixed from the other two games (various issues with landing, gliding, takedowns..etc still persist) In Arkham Knight there's a flaw where ground takedowns don't knock out the enemy about 50%+ of the time forcing you to double your time taking down one enemy.  The graphics are very pleasing to the eye, Some of the textures still dont load well or at all in it's current PATCHED form, and the options for resolution and graphics are better after the update to the game, adding in 30, 60, 90 FPS caps, and other good graphics options as well.  I do like this game and I would recommend it after looking into what you were buying first. Even after the patch the game suffers from FPS drops, and texture issues. If you're a fan of the prior three Batman games this game tells more of that story, plus you get to drive the Batmobile. DLC Update! Batmobile racetracks and skins are amazingly fun, and have a fair price attached to them! The 2008 Tumbler was probably the most fun to drive out of the skins so far.

This has been my first experience with the Ys games, and its overall been an enjoyable experience so far. The combat system takes some getting used to. Most RPGs bring you to a Final Fantasy style or Dragon Quest style turn based system, or use something like Zeldas or Secret of Manas action based system where you directly control sword swings and the like, but this game, you run into the baddies. Yep, just run into them. The trick is you do not want to run into them head on, or you will take damage, You want to be off center or attacking at an angle to deal damage without getting hit yourself. A unique method to be sure. The story of the game is pretty good, and the overworld works well due to the villages and dungeouns blending in seamlessly to create a nice single world feeling experience. Also, you can choose which release graphics to use, which is a nice touch. There seems to be a few bugs with the saving system. Sometimes I get lots of lag when I save, and have had one time where my game saves weren't made at all during a gameplay session, ended up losing an hours worth of progress. Overall I would recommend it though, the game is fun and a new experience for RPG fans who haven't tried this series yet.

Is fun ! This game is a Third Person Shooter Genre with fast paced action (almost like a twitch shooter) but this game focus on the PvE. The way the game plays like instanced mission with peer-to-peer connection if you were going to co-op and you choose your gear before heading in (if you have more gears). Some people might feel lost playing this game on what to do, if you do, just complete the map, get Blueprints off the market, try get the resource for it, and build it in your Foundry (something like mechanical bench/construction table). The story will progress itself the more Mastery Ranking (Account Level) you gain, and the only way to level your Mastery Ranking is leveling your gear (Gear Level). Basically, this game is a huge grind. Might want to note this, in the market, you will see lots of gears and warframes being sold in the premium currency (called Platinums), do not assume that this is pay-to-win, This game is co-op, and you can also get them in-game, the only thing that you can't get in-game are the cosmetics (which sometime the Alert Mission (something like suprise mission) give as a reward). Join a clan if you're new, 'cause the clan might help you get what you want and also you can get weapon blueprints in the research clan, also a Dojo, a place that your clan built for a hangout and research place. There's all to it in this game, get blueprint, get material, build, level, rinse and repeat. Basically, this game is a huge grind, but somehow it pulls me back in.

But with the incredible mod support offered through the steam workshop the Spearhead realism mod has made this game enjoyable again and reminds me of countless hours enjoying myself on the original COH.

A decent modern entry into the GRID series.  The look and feel of the game is on par with modern titles in terms of graphics and gameplay. The cars feel unique and handle well. The game leans more toward a simulation title than an arcade racer. If you've played Forza, you will most likely enjoy the gameplay.  The rewind mechanic is still there, which is a nice feature. Cars can flip easily when swung around corners at 180 Kmph or faster, which was a nice surprise and added a level of complexity to driving tactics.  This entry continues the linear progression path where you unlock cars instead of buying and customizing. The game does offer 4 different race 'genres', however progression is still fairly linear.  Overall a good title, however if you're not bothered by having the most modern graphics and are new to the series I would still recommend GRID 1.

Dragons and Titans used to be an awesome game. After several buffs and nerfs, the game wasn't any longer fun to play.    Things that I used to like about D&T that changed:   The possibilty to invite up to 5 friends to form a team.  Having to use all different dragons in a match.  The old controls system without auto-aim.  Anything before the Titan Pass.   D&T couldn't afford itself after the yearly Titan Pass, for it didn't create any beneficial profit.   What D&T should of change is:   Starting with the Titan Pass (Same price for a month is more than enough).  Remove all Runes (stones collected within the matches), since this allows players to easily use hacks and sometimes stay low profile, and as well helps to keep the abilities of the dragons balanced.  Remove the use of repeated dragons in a match.  Weekly updates, including at least 1 weapon or 1 dragon.  At least 3 Moderators per chat (since not all are always able to be online).  Weekly developer or admin visits so players notice the interest of the D&T team towards them. And last but not least, team XP in matches, instead of individual (This will give players a more pleasent and friendly match, since it will encourage all to assist for a common cause).  Some new ideas:  Replacing the collection of stones for Gold pieces to buy some upgrade for their weapons/dragons with a prior limit set, within the match. The upgrades could either have just a few levels but several type that does not mess the balance in match, such as ''Returning to hoard ability cooldown'', ''Short protective shield for teammates'', ''Dashing a small distance'', and stuff like that.  Anyways, there are many things that can be changed to make the game not just as good as it used to be, but greater. Lets not forget the improvements keep players interested, but every change must to be properly debated between D&T team, in order to avoid colliding every feature or element in the game.  At the end, there is far too much that can be improved in Dragons and Titans, but the income must to be well thought, that way the D&T team will always feel excited to work with such awesome game. The concept that this game handled is very insteresting and satifying, specially to those who love dragons. But if anyone wants the players to return to this game, a lot has to be done and responsabilities to be assumed.   Thanks D&T for so many fun moments and to allow me to meet some of the coolest and easy going people to play with.

Edit2: 700 hours in. Woah.  Edit: 300+ hours in. Still great.   I'm writing this review because some negative reviews led to me almost not buying it. I am sincerely glad that I did. This is the game that I've always wanted.  Let me tell you about my first night on LiF. I logged into an RP server and was almost instantly greeted by experienced players who were not only willing to help me with every question that I had - but also fillled me in on all the political happenings that were revelant to that evening. the community, if nothing else, makes this game worth playing.  Before I knew it, they outfitted me with a basic sling and some sling ammo and I was marching off to battle. We arrived through the woods to a small fort in the north which seemed relatively empty - I was disappointed at first: This was it? Little did we know that the enemy was crouching behind their walls equipped with heavy plated armor and bowmen galore. After about 20 minutes, two concussions, an arrow through the chest, and general astoundment that I was still alive - I heard that the enemy king was knocked unconscious and his nobles lay dead around him. Opposed to killing the enemy king, our king inisisted that we let him regain consciousness so he could role play his reaction to the embarassing defeat.   While waiting patiently around him, celebrating our clear victory, we were ambushed by almost a dozen of the fallen king's allies from the west. They streamed in behind us and we fled with our tails behind our legs. By the time we hobbled back home, bloodied and broken, we had only lost one ally - a clear victory if you ask me!   Since then, peace has been negotiated and I've decided to make a pacifist character. - dedicating my time to building a religious school on top of a mountain. That's the best part of this game, you can literally do whatever you want. Want to be a cook? Someone needs you in their guild. Want to be a warrior? Someone wants you for their realm. Want to dedicate your time to stacking wood, mining deep into a mountain, watching wheat grow? Well, you're probably going to have to do it anyway.   Admittedly, I've only been playing LiF for 15 hours (and that is not much for this game!) but i felt like it was my duty to defend it at least a little bit.   Yes this game is still in development, yes there are many features that haven't been implemented yet. But I promise you that if you connect with the right people in-game then its not something you will regret contributing to. There's a steep learning curve but once you get a hang of it everythings really intuitive and well thought out. I can't wait for final release.

As much as I love playing creative indie games, it is really refreshing to play a game with a huge budget where they worked closely with Nvidia to optimize the graphics and make a 3D Vision ready game. What I mean by all of this is: Batman: Arkham Asylum looks gorgeous! There were even a few scenes that had a lot of PhysX effects going on that my GTX 670 struggled with. I wouldn't have guessed that it came out in 2009. One of my biggest pet peeves is when games that look great have cutscenes that have been rendered in 720p (I'm looking at you, Mass Effect series) but the only difference I could see in Asylum was that the cutscenes were not in 3D.  I'm not a huge Batman fan, so I don't know who most of the villains are; I only learned about Two Face when I watched The Dark Knight. Arkham Asylum does a very good job of introducing the characters and giving their backgrounds. They even have a little bio section for each major character with both in-universe and real life facts about them. I could tell that most of the character designs were new takes on those characters, and I definitely think that they were done well. I found it a little odd that almost every single person in the game was ripped, but this is Batman after all.  Mark Hamill did a phenomenal job voicing the Joker. He was easily the most interesting character tho listen to, and I intentionally died a couple of times so I could listen to him taunt me.  Each main villain had a couple of levels devoted to them. My personal favorite were the Scarecrow levels because I got to relive some of the defining moments in Batman's life. By that I mean I got to see his parents die.  The gameplay is pretty basic: left-click to hit people, and right-click to counter when they try to hit you. Some enemy types switch this up a little by making you stun them first or dodging behind them before you can hit them, but ultimately it only adds one extra button that you have to hit before taking them down.  My favorite parts of the game were the rooms where there were a bunch of armed thugs in a large area, and I had to figure out how to take them down without being seen. It reminded me of the scenes in Batman Begins down by the docks where Batman did just that. Unfortunately I wasn't quite as good at it as the real Batman.  The detective 'puzzles' were fairly interesting, but usually very straightforward. I felt like I was doing less detective work and more just following a trail that the developers would only let me see if I turned on the special goggles.  My least favorite parts by far were the boss battles. They were clearly constructed exactly the same way that boss battles were made back in the 90's. I was usually stuck on a platform below the much larger boss, performing the same actions multiple times while looking for patterns in their attacks so I knew when they were vulnerable.  It took me a little over 10 hours to complete the story. It seemed like I wasn't missing much by not exploring heavily; the main missions took me all over the island. It would have been nice to have gotten more of the audio logs, which were pretty amusing, and I only found a fraction of the Riddler trophies. I'm definitely not a completionist.  Overall I enjoyed the game a lot, and I would recommend that anyone who thinks Batman is the coolest play it. It's currently $20, which is a pretty reasonable price.
