KILL. LOOT. RETURN. Also bad Minecraft and some nonsense about a baby.  But okay, more serious tl;dr: excellent main quest and gunplay, mediocre to bad almost everything else.  ++ gunplay is as satisfying as it's ever been in Fallout + gun customization is amazing + power armor feels AWESOME + creature and enemy models look, sound and move great + radiation is actually a relevant mechanic + looting containers without having to open it + excellent voice acting for both male and female protagonist ++ very good main quest, best in a Bethesda game so far + companions are interesting and have distinct personalities + downtown Boston has amazing verticality + sometimes looks amazing - sometimes looks like crap - feels poorly optimized - NPC animations and lip-syncing are distractingly bad - companion AI is terrible -- inventory management is extremely tedious yet mandatory - outdated, clunky UI -- generic procedurally generated quests (kill. loot. return.) -- dialogue system is shallow and pointless -- settlement building is janky and unpolished  INCOMING WALL OF TEXT  Fallout 4 is a seriously dysfunctional game. On the one hand, it wants to be a traditional open-world game with open-ended exploration and base-building. On the other hand it wants desperately to be a Bioware RPG. In trying to straddle both canoes Fallout 4 doesn't quite succeed in either.  The best thing about Fallout 4, the main quest, is amazing. It's the first time I feel like Bethesda's main story hasn't fallen completely on its face. It has both a solid emotional core and thought-provoking moments; quiet introspection and great setpiece action. Without spoiling too much, there is surprising nuance in the main factions, and I had to think long and hard about who my character would ultimately support.  This is helped immensely by the excellent voice acting of the protagonist, both male and female. Having a voiced protagonist in a Bethesda game is a controversial decision, and has certain drawbacks (which I'll get to) but it works extremely well in forging a strong emotional bond to the protagonist and his/her story.  Unfortunately, it's fairly obvious that Bethesda poured all their effort into the main quest. The sidequests are almost uniformly bland and uninteresting. The majority of sidequests are procedurally-generated, so most of them are basically mad-lib 'help X kill Y in Z location'. Even the ones that aren't procedurally-generated are unimaginative and mostly devolve into 'go kill these bad people where they live' with minor variations.  This focus on the main quest to the detriment of everything else is also apparent in the voice acting of the NPCs. Most of them have one, maybe two lines that they say over and over again. Only the guards in Diamond City have interesting things to say based on your exploits.  The dialogue system is streamlined to the point of feeling stripped down, with a four-button dialogue system that roughly maps to query/sarcastic/positive/negative. Done right, as in Telltale's or Bioware's games, this kind of dialogue wheel can be good. Unfortunately, this system just highlights all the more glaringly the lack of thought and resources Bethesda has dedicated to dialogue. The skill checks so prominent in previous games, especially Obsidian's New Vegas, is largely gone. There are only Charisma checks now, and it's mostly just used to wring more money out of poor settlers for jobs.  To its credit, the combat that makes up the bulk of the gameplay is great. It's a massive quantum leap from Fallout 3 and even an improvement over New Vegas. Gunplay is actually fun now, rather than something you soldier through to get to the loot and locations. There aren't a lot of types of guns, but the addition of weapon mods adds a lot of variety to your arsenal.  Power armor is the other massive improvement to the previous games. In Fallout 3, power armor was just fancy super-heavy armor. In FO4, it feels more like piloting a mech, from the lumbering thump of your footsteps to the way you have to step into it, to the Iron-Man-style heads-up display. It eats up fusion cores like mad, but when you're in one, you feel like a tanky Space Marine.  Enemies look and move just right. For example, shoot off a ghoul's legs and it will crawl towards you on its arms. Assaultrons spew fire out their faces if they're close enough. Raiders look actually like opportunistic bandits and not like the half-naked savages of FO3.  The UI is a mess. It's the same PipBoy-based UI as FO3, except that it seems to have taken one step forward and two steps back, with a number of missing features that even Fallout 3 had.  Fallout 4 introduces uses for the random clutter that you see all over the place in Bethesda games. With these materials you can upgrade weapons and armor, brew drugs and build settlements. It is, on paper, a fantastic addition. However, the carry weight limit remains roughly the same as it was in FO3, resulting in you constantly becoming overencumbered. The excessive need for inventory management kills the pace of the game. At one point, before I used the console to double my base carry weight, I was having to stop in the middle of a 'dungeon' and run back to my base to drop off loot.  Moreover, the settlement builder interface is atrociously unintuitive and barely above the standard I expect of a mod tacking on a system the vanilla game wasn't designed for. Building settlements is a compulsive time-waster but not a rewarding or fun one.  Finally, Bethesda's venerable engine is fully showing its age by now. The game is constantly loading low-res LOD textures instead of the proper ones, and the game is plagued with severe stuttering at random intervals. Lighting is also a big issue. About half the time, light placement make characters look pasty and unintentionally eerie.  Bethesda games have always been ambitious, sprawling messes where its individual mediocre mechanics come together into a whole that is somehow greater than the sum of its parts. Even at its best, there's a feeling that Fallout 4 doesn't quite come together. During a key emotional story moment, the superb voice acting and shocking revelation is badly undercut by the poor lip-syncing, doughy face textures, and one character moonwalking into our conversation. Every interesting character moment with a companion is balanced against their braindead AI constantly getting in your way and behaving nothing like a human. Building the perfect little settlement comes after hours of wrestling with the unwieldy settlement builder.  Fallout 4 is a game with nuggets of awesome swamped by lazy or misguided execution everywhere else. Whether it'll be a worthwhile experience ultimately is down to how much you like the Fallout franchise and/or Bethesda games.  My subjective opinion? Wait a couple of years and get the inevitable GotY or Ultimate Edition with all the upcoming DLCs. By then the modding community will undoubtedly have created some truly amazing creations that vastly improve on the base game. In its current state I must reluctantly give Fallout 4 a thumbs-down.  Oct 2016 edit: Tried out the Survival mode that was added. Yeah, still glitchy, unoptimized and shallow. Still not recommended.

This game has an amazing singleplayer experience, I will not be reviewing the multiplayer as I have little interest in it. Please do not take that as me saying its multiplayer is bad, I have never played it myself nor will I.  Pros: 1. It feels like the old doom games(not doom 3), your character moves smoothly throughout the battlefield cutting down enemies left and right. Powerups and health/armor pickups are plentiful with many secrets hidden throughout the maps. If you enjoyed searching every corner of the map for goodies then you will love this game.   2. Music. Normally I am not a audiophile for videogames, but the music that plays throughout the fight sequences fits the theme of slaughtering monsters and really added to the experience.  3. Combat. This comes back to point #1, the combat is very smooth, the weapons feel like they have power. You have a few bullet sponges here or there but even they can't stand up against the bigger guns for long.  4. Executions. This feature lets you instantly kill enemies that are low on life with a melee attack. The game has a plethora of gory executions for each enemy. If will get either health, armor, or ammo depending on which runes you have equiped, which can help you out in a pinch in the middle of a massacre.   5. CHAINSAW. I look at it less like a weapon and more like a emergency ammo refill. The chainsaw once found will instantly kill any non-boss enemy depending on the amount of fuel you have, bigger enemies need more fuel. Using the chainsaw causes a very satisfying execution that upon killing an enemy showers you with various types of ammo. This actions mixes in flawlessy with combat and is one of my favorite features.   Cons: 1. Story. I never really had a moment where I was emotionally invested in the story. The plot just felt like a means to send me to the next area to slaughter more demons. To be fair this game puts more emphasis on the combat then story. If you are looking for a in depth plot then look elsewhere.  2. Length of game varies. You will beat this game very quickly if you are not exploring the entire map looking for upgrades, hidden levels, addons.  3. Lack of good DLC. This is more of a message to the developers, you made a fantastic game. However you keep adding DLC that is entirely multiplayer focused, if you made DLC that added a mini singleplayer campaign then I would buy it. You made a great game that really captured the spirit of doom, you have potential to make alot of money with additional singleplayer content.

While this was an interesting game in concept, execution left something to be desired. The creators actually DID publish that this was a preliminary attempt at this type of point-and-click suspense horror game, and really I can't say that I can do better. My only real complaint is that the 'disturbing themes' of implied child ♥♥♥♥ and murder in a post-apocalyptic world seem gratuitous without giving any reason to the story. He (the character you play as) obsesses over science and logic, though there is no real reason behind having that in there unless it was to imply that the scientific community has no internal sense of morality. It was a very short-lived game, which it was supposed to be. I will give it to the creators that it has the basis of what COULD become a good game, as the lighting effects, sound track, and audio clips of anguish and suffering do add to an air of super creepiness, and that the clues are intuitive, rather than a stretch to figure out. However, I would wait until the creators develop their art and improve on their use of story-telling tools and leave Fingerbones as a project in experimental video game design.

Despite being relatively short, Layers of Fear delivers a very good experience, visual and audio. The jump scares are usally pretty good, except for most of the ghost encounters.  Multiple endings and scattered enigmas make for a good replayability.

The minimalist visual trappings and level design are very clever -- you get both right from the start screen in how the menu operates. Though it is a wee bit cumbersome, it also offers a little practice in driving the pink block that serves as your avatar.   The mechanics aren't difficult to master, but the puzzle designs get very challenging at times, Some are tough more in getting the timing of motions right. Others ask you to navigate mazes with few to no errors as doom approaches from various directions. Throughout the experience, there's lovely music playing.   While it's frustrated me a bit at times, it's the kind of frustration that comes from knowing that making just one more right move or avoiding an errant bit of driving will send me past the obstacle.   In short, it's a properly hard little game with stylish visuals and well-done music. I don't regret a penny.

I know people have been complaining to no end, but I've had little to no performance issues playing this game with- GTX 780, i5-4460 quad core, 8 GB of Ram.   I can maintain 60 FPS (or higher) throughout the entire game on Med & High settings. I honestly don't know why others are having issues because it's fine for me.   The gameplay, as expected, is very good. Some people have been complaining that it's too easy on Very Hard, but that's only if you're indifferent to being stealthy/Ghost/No Kills. The game is actually quite hard if you want it to be, which is really good. There are a very large amount of ways to complete each level as both Emily and Corvo, and you're able to make every takedown as unique as you want it to be. The charm system is very well done, and the results of fully collecting every bone charm can be shown in what you craft. The whole game offers a large amount of replay value, along with being longer than the first Dishonored.   The setting and style of the game is unique and enjoyable (I have a weakspot for steampunk). The levels are very cleverly designed, and there are multiple parts of different levels that blew my mind (   Jindosh Mansion, Timepiece Level  ).  In my opinion, the game is definitely worth the $60, especially if you're a fan of the first/stealth games overall.

Samorost 3  is a beautiful adventure point & click game developed by Amanita Design, also known for their game Machinarium. If this review is too long for you, I suggest you to only read the first paragraph about the game's content and the final verdict at the end.   What is happening in the game:

Playing the demo from Humble Monthly got me quite excited for the conceptual mechanics of this title, so soon after release I bought it without question, but immediately after this review I will be petitioning for a refund. Don't trust its deceitful promotion. It's probably not surprising this game is an empty shell of a story with boring characters, but even the features I expected to redeem it were shoddy at best. You might now be thinking, 'But... but... what about the _______?' 1) Grappling like Rico or Spider-man! Yaya!  No. You'll fall... a lot. There's no freindly blip telling you what's in range (a sub for depth-perception). Many grapple points are placed just out of reach, requiring you to jump and frantically mash the trigger, hoping your aim is exact. 2) Time effects! Just like Superhot or Prince of Persia!  True, those work well... as a convenience. Their unlimited use is forgiving to the point of removing fear of failure completely. But besides that, I never saw either time effect implemented creatively. For the most part, you'll find them most handy in taking back those failed grapples I mentioned. 3) Stealth! I love stealth games!  I know, right?! Theif 2 was my jam in the day! But the AI and visibility mechanics in Shadwen are a step down from those games, not to mention the sub-par level design.  In summary, this physics puzzler only parades as an innovative stealth hit. Though not especially bad, it fails in its main selling points. I hope I was of some assistance in clearing up this foggy ruse. See it for what it is, and then decide whether to buy (definitely not worth full price).

The best co-op game I have played so far. This deserves a spot in gaming history.  In Guns of Icarus Online you play as a member of a crew on an steampunk airship. You can either be the pilot, an engineer or a gunner. Every crew consists of 4 players, usually with 2 engineers. The key to succeeding in this game is efficient communication with your fellow crew members. If you are the captain of a ship you can also communicate with other captains in your team (on other ships) to form an armada that will execute coordinated strikes on the enemy forces.  But whenever you enter a fight, there is a good chance that the enemy ships will destroy parts of your ship. You need to keep your own ship in shape so that it remains fit for combat. During fights you need to decide if you should continue attacking or if it is better to focus on repairs. Many times you need to talk to your crewmates so that some people do the repairs while others fight back.  This game resembles an ego-shooter. But you cannot run around with a gun. All guns are mounted on the ship. But the ship itself moves so you might need to talk to your captain so that he aligns your gun with the enemy.  'We are sitting all in the same boat' is something that makes this game a really awesome experience because of the team spirit you get most of the time. You either win together or you lose together. With that being said, you might run into players who simply don't understand that these things matter and you will lose a game sometimes because of them. But overall, this game is a lot of fun.  There is also the Alliance DLC which gives you gamemodes where you fight against AI ships. These are fun too but my favorite is the classical skirmish mode.

This text novel has a decent, dramatic story, and, if you are ready to dive into little ocean full of korean names, you may also find some political plot. Gameplay consists of interaction with a pair of AI (who, (of course) has  'cute girl'  oblique), whith quite different personalities, to uncover the story of a lifeless spaceship. Interaction with ai is quite limited - binary '1 or 2'. You have to cope with that to open up and read all logs in the ship's computer and download them. Since you are using a remote connection, there is also a terminal with command-line interface (which isn't really useful, comfortable or important).  The main things you need to know:    There are  only two  different AI, and, to be honest, you can count them as 1.3, since second is nearly broken and hard to interact with    Main story is about gender unequality, and there isn't much else there    Interaction with AI is done in a really special way, by asking their opinion about every page you read (that isn't really quick) and responding to their questions to push forward the story     With above said, it has nothing at all to do with dating sims     Music is decent, but there is a little of it    Limitation of CLI may surprise you and question if it is really necessary    There is NO BACKLOG for interaction with AI, and that can be a big problem to progress further in the game    This is a text novel, not a visual one. Text is main vehicle of a narration. Only a couple of sprites are involved in emotional side of a story.  I completed it in 2 hours (lenght depends on your reading speed and ability to miss important phrases no one is going to repeat twice), and while i am satisfied with storyline i got, i think it could have really been much better. In my experience, characters are unequal and absolutely not comparable - to the point of losing interest in second storyline, just because it required re-reading nearly all previous logs (that isn't fast at all with 'blocks' of log interaction UI).  Now, about 'Visual Novel' thing.  As i tried to point out, this is a text game with some visual elements (i would like to compare it to a well-featured OS with a very bad GUI). But sadly, there isn't really a genre for that, and it somehow fits into VN category (at the time of its release, steam still hadn't become a westernized dlsite and didn't offer much to VN/anime fans). And there is a reason for that - this game is about interacting with girls. More than that, it also borrows some traditional elements of a VN - such as defined endings and preferred choice (ihykwim). And this is a feminist game - inside of the enemy territory - sexualising and objectifying genre! That is what makes this game cool. Still, it comes in a different, not well-refined format. That is what probably left me a bad impression and broke enjoyment from the gameplay at some point.  I had a bad experience playing it, while i am not frustrated or disappointed. Overall, this is a good game. I believe many would expect more from game with  'Overwhelmingly positive'  rating, metascore says it. If you want to spend a pair of hours reading sad story - go for it, it has good replay value; If you expect to spend many days and nights with your 'waifu' or leisurely dive into long dialogs and rich, self-consciousness monologue - you'd better not.

review_text: This game has a very similar feel to ICO - if you liked that then this will go down a treat. The movement scheme seems a little odd at first: the controller is split, with one stick and one button for each of the two brothers. This is important, and it soon becomes second nature.  Totalbiscuit listed this as his game of the year. At first I thought it a little pedestrian - TB, you really picked THIS as your favourite? But once underway, you'll see that every bit of it has been carefully crafted. There is none of the filler that you'll find in longer games - this only takes 2.5-3 hours to complete. There's originality throughout, and I found myself saying 'that's cool' at several points. I even approved of the ending.  This is a game I'll not forget for a long time.  Buy it now. Play it now.

Don't listen to the negative reviews about the game being 'Pay To Win'. Warframe is a game where you don't have to spend a single dollar to unlock all of the content. Yes, there are items only purchasable with Platinum (the premium currency) but so far all of these Platinum only items are purely customization items (Syandanas, colour swaps). Yes, you can buy weapons and Warframes for Platinum, but you can also craft them using resources and credits. Crafting takes some time but it's an infinitely better idea than spending Platinum.  Well what if you want access to more customization options? And what about needing more weapon and Warframe slots? If you've already spent the 50 Platinum that you get when you start the game (SPEND IT ON WEAPON/WARFRAME SLOTS). you can easily earn Platinum over time by farming for Prime items and trading them away. After my first week of play I sat down for a few hours, cracked some relics, went to warframe.market (a third party website that helps buyers and sellers find each other) and looked for people buying what I had. Another day later I had made enough Platinum to buy a Syandana.  Yes, earning Platinum takes time but it isn't hard. If you are hurting for Platinum and are too lazy to work for it, you have a chance to earn up to 75% off of all Platinum purchases for one purchase through the daily login rewards. I've played for 220 hours and I'm still enjoying it. Try it for yourself, it's free after all.

I want to start this off by saying I do wish there was a 'I kinda-sorta recommend this game if it were very cheap, or if they changed (insert gameplay fix here)' button. But they dont'. And I've been thinking whether to go up or down with this review.  But decidedly after giving it much thought, I've decided to go negative.  Let me put it simply: it has great weapon feel and sword play, and as a blood-soaked butcher simulator I would recommend it whole-heartedly. It is totally gruesome and I love that, and it's great fun when cutting down demon after demon in different ways.   But here is where it gets bad; as it gets later in the game, at times, there is just way too many things going on at once to even know what you should try and kill or where you are getting shot/sliced/stampeded/blown-up or bulldozed over from. During alot of the later game fights, I found myself getting frustrated by the inability to resort to slicing heads off because I had to implement explosive crowd-control to keep myself alive. And let this be known, I played on casual to enjoy the experience of slicing things to mere giblets. If I were on any higher difficulty, I would have found myself dead just as frequently as a beginner Dark Souls player. And though in the recent patch there was a survival mode added, I would have loved to see a New Game + added, because I was thoroughly looking forward to the point where all my karma skills were maxed out and my guns were fully upgraded and (admittedly) quite amazing. Unless I'm missing something, resuming the game after you've beaten it spawns you after you have killed the final boss, and to me that seems painfully lame.   Overall, I would love to comback to it if there were a New Game + mode, but as it stands now after playing it for about 13 hours, I don't think I'll be coming back to it again in the near future.   TL:DR It's a fun, grotesque and pretty melee fighting game with some awesome gun upgrades. But due to a lack of replayability, crazy rush waves of enemies late game and too much going on at once to even see straight, I have a hard time recommending this to a friend.

Deceitfully easy, I just came from playing through Diablo I(1). I was about to give this game a really low rating, it's just to simple to go through the small closed maps with hordes of monsters coming right at you, you just click and click without much of tactics in your mind. But i got stuck playing this game as intensely as I played Diablo I. And now 38 hours later on my third day playing, I'm through the first two thirds of the campaign part of the game. And I have really enjoyed the game as it has grown on me, with the complex experience points map, the special attack gems and their support gems and slots. It's really enjoyable to go through the inventory optimizing the farmed equipment, upgrading and gambling for better rarity and more gem slots. And then when you're satisfied, just go out and bash through hord after hord of vile and disgusting creatures. My only complaint is that it's just a little bit too easy, or maybe I'm just been lucky with my equipment. I've only died a handful of times, and that's just because I got to casual with my gaming and just didn't see the creature who was pounding my head in. I killed the big bosses after each chapter on the first try just frenetically clicking away with my best attack skill. The games atmosphere is really cosy and suggestive, it's easy to get lured in the dark woods and caves. I'm surprised my laptop (i3 w/ SSD) with Intel HD3000 graphics could handle the game all right, but then again, I'm not that spoiled with eye blinding high def. eye candy. One has to take in to perspective that I'm well in my thirties and I still enjoy playing DOOM I and the likes. And that I'm still amazed with the stuff, for example, the Unreal 3 engine can produce.  If you are still hesitating... just don't! Download this great piece of free to play game, forget about eat and sleep and just click away!

I had doubts getting this game, but I have heard of the Thief series being amazing in the past, so I went with it.  **--Attention, there may be spoilers ahead!!--**  Looking at the old games, they are indeed good. But this isn't something that connects to the old ones.  Here are the game's pros/cons as I experience them (each explained later on). Note though that I've only played the main game and have not gotten around to downloading The Bank Heist yet. Pros: -looks good -a lot of gameplay (if you choose to complete every side quest and get every achievement, also making this a con for me)  Cons: -not much gameplay for people who don't want every side quest and achievement -short story (ridiculous, too) -relatively small and linear maps, lots of points of no return -bad sound mechanics -bad fighting mechanics -bad enemy AI -a lot of stuff you don't learn until you either try every key in every possible situation or hope that the loading screen tips give you actual tips -no challenge unless you force yourself with achievements and side quests  The pros here definitely don't outweigh the cons, at least for me. Now, to analyze;  Beginning with the story, it's short and pretty much meaningless -- the background and city are basically the same as Dishonored, some girl absorbs some magic from some ritual, ♥♥♥♥ goes down and from there on, you're partially Batman, there to save the world from the evil and gloom but only so because the game forces you, while your other parts of the brain are trying to piece together what the floop is happening. Spoiler; you never find out. You're expected to just 'roll with it.'  You steal something for one guy, steal something else for another guy (boiling the gameplay down to that of the first AC game or even less) and after that you sneak around some (if you want to) and the game ends. The characters aren't any better than the story is; there's a damsel in distress, the power-hungry villain (who only appears to be an ♥♥♥♥♥♥♥ good guy), the villain that really isn't one yet everyone wants you to think he is and the villain that appears every once in a while to hurt/chase you. There's also Basso who, for some reason, I like the most. Perhaps it was the slight sense of comedy and irony you got from him. I can't forget Garret, but there's not much to say about him as he's mostly silent. And when he isn't, he's boring. If I had a choice, I'd play the game as Basso. Sure you couldn't run or climb or do anything but at least he isn't as flooping boring.  The Focus ability is never really explained (not like anything is, but this is a thing you're supposed to use all the time). Sure, I get to see things in blue and Quake Pro-like FOV, but it's not until the loading screen tips tell me at the end of the game that I find out I can get upgrades for it at the Queen of Beggars. The 3rd person climbing parts are annoying until you realize you can press 'X' to go/fall down which would also have been a nice tip. You'd really expect to see a manual...  Now I know I'm the kind of person who doesn't like side quests and achievements much, but this game takes my hate for them to a whole new level, solely because over half the game is hidden in there.  The maps are small. Add to that the points of no return (PoNRs) and the maps feel even smaller. There is no room for exploration because you don't know where the PoNRs are and after you pass one, there is no going back unless you reload your savegame. And even though there are sometimes more ways than one to get somewhere, it's always the same effect. Speaking of PoNRs, they take a lot from the gameplay because you don't get punished if you just run around alerting everyone and jump into a PoNR. All alarms stop and nobody chases you after you pass them. Adding to all that 'notactics' nonsense, there's more than meets the eye. You don't have to steal (which is funny because the title of the game is Thief), killing everyone is always an option and being stealthy is near impossible at times (forcing you to bring out the big arrows). Speaking of arrows, fighting needs discussing. You can't melee until you get proper upgrades (and even then you don't get any explanation) and even the biggest arrows in the forehead don't kill aware enemies. That said, you can escape by using other equipment, but that's meaningless since you then either blind yourself as well or make everyone explode, alerting even more guards to your presence. Ooh, and now we're hit guards; how wonderful, it all just keeps connecting. The enemy AI sucks. They're barely ever aware of you, as if they don't want to see you and are just asking for a hit over their head. The hardest enemies to pass are the weird pale monsters, but even those have an algorithm you can easily predict and swoop around.  Sound. Ugh, don't get me started on that. The AIs keep repeating what they've already said, a lot of times they don't even finish the first thing only to somehow be able to speak from their butt to tell me two things at the same time. There's no difference in sound if a guard is walking next to me or if there's 2 walls between us and every time you take a guard down, the sound effects make you think somebody has seen you while you were doing the takedown.  I've saved the worst for last. The game cannot decide what it wants to be. A detective game? Open-world exploration? Assassin's Creed? Free-running? FPS? Horror, perhaps? Who knows? But Thief somehow manages to be all that, and it does not succeed. The main reason is because you're supposed to be a guy stealing, not Batman and definitely not the guy from Slender: The Eight Pages. It wants to be more than all this, but fails to deliver in every aspect. And the most disappointing thing is that it fails to be a Thief game.   I know the review is a bit long and I know it's not all connected as good as it should be. But then again, the game isn't apologizing to me, either.

I concur with others that this game is not yet ready for general release. I say shame on whoever decided to declare this release ready.  Yes the campaign is done and combat works, but there are too many pain points to make this game fun yet. I know it is common if not the norm these days to release a game and then spend the next 6 months fixing it, but I don't feel that is right to your customers. I was a kickstarter backer so I understand there will be rough edges during the process, but setting the game for release in this state makes me fear the game will never receive the attention it deserves. -Camera controls are unintuitive and require constant adjustment. -Mouse input is erratic. -Game engine completely stops switching between units. screen freezes breifly and FPS counter will go to zero each time enemy AI units make a move. -controlling units require too much busy work like changing facing before being allowed to attack a unit instead of just allowing the unit to select between all units in range and just change the facing as appropriate. Doing unit facing changes at the end of each units turn is cumbersome and unnecessary. The UI currently bogs down the game. Turns feel like they take twice as long as they should because of constant camera angle changes and other control issues. Even something simple like selecting a unit is often unresponsive requiring multiple clicks and hunt for the hotspot.

app_name: Ring Runner: Flight of the Sages
review_text: One of the best games I've played all year. If indie games were put into a pile this would be one of the games near the top of the pile. The music is a kind of space themed techno that really hits all the right chords and really brings in the mood. However it reminds me a lot of Pink Floyd and other progressive rock sounds, but that's the kind of music I listen to. The sound effects are top notch as well. The graphics are very good, the background is very busy and colourful, and the special effects in this game were made with blood, sweat, and tears. The best part of the game is the explosion effects. The bigger the object, the more detailed the explosion. The storyline is light hearted and smart, definitely makes me crack a smile and wows me at the same time. The difficulty in this game is true to what you set it as, which means the AI was very well crafted. The campaign is 20 hours, but there is multiplayer to boot, which means you could definitely spend hundreds of hours on this game. There are plenty of multiplayer modes to play, really different from eachother as well. I highly recommend this game.  9/10

Evolve Stage 2 is an apt name for a game that was brought back from the dead after its troubled beginning. It is a better game, a faster one, balanced more in favor of casual fun instead of the e-sports angle Turtlerock aimed when it first launched. Naturally, the e-sports community rejected the forced effort and the game was left for dead... Until now.  Evolve has evolved into a proper Free 2 Play game, with an in-game currency and daily drop-feed rewards, the standard F2P trappings. Monsters and hunters are unlocked with silver dough and they are priced in such a way that makes sure you aren't overfeeding yourself -- it can take up to an hour or two of play time to earn enough cash to buy characters. This will need to be readjusted but the intention by developers is sound: you are always learning new characters and strategies and just when you've mastered each, you'll buy a new one. If you're the impatient sort, high prices would likely tip you over to the paying side.  Both for monsters and hunters you can unlock perks which, when upgraded two times, give you a real advantage against the opponent. This system makes it hard for new players to jump in, as they will always be underpowered when compared to veteran players. Even worse, perks are seperate for monsters and hunters so if you only play one or the other, the game actively makes sure you keep playing those and prevents you from experimenting.   This would all be pretty rotten if you could buy in-game cash. It would be Pay 2 Win. As of writing this, however, you cannot buy anything with your money. Presumably, Turtlerock are keeping that turned off until they finetune the progression.   But is the game any fun? It is. As I said before, Stage 2 is more casual, matches usually go on for about 10 minutes and the tools hunter have available to them make sure that you can catch-up with the monster and fight it even if you don't kill it. The major complaint of there being too much running is still true -- Evolve can be a running simulator. Because the movement system is rudementary and generally unengaging, there can be boring periods in every match. But when the fight starts, it's all adrenaline.   All in all, Evolve Stage 2 is a step in the right direction. It's not perfect by any means and the unlocking of characters is probably not enough to keep most people playing.   But, the foundation is strong and when it evolves into Stage 3, Evolve will be a great game.    Thankfully, Turtlerock and 2K seem committed to making that dream happen.

My feelings about this game are mixed. I love the presentation, I love the idea and the execution is all right -- but the story feels a bit stock, the characters are pretty bland and the difficulty is brutal -- for the wrong reasons.  I don't mind a difficult game, but here it feels very uneven and punishing. I don't always need my decisions to go my way, but it often feels like it doesn't matter -- making it feel like my input doesn't really play any part in the game at all. I might as well flip a coin.  Another issue I have with the game is that the outcome of one scenario won't affect the next one, so if my partner dies during the very intense torture sequence he'll still be alive and kicking as if nothing happened as the story continues, which takes away any impact from what happened.  I want to love this game. The game jam scenario was incredible and I gladly support the developers. Perhaps a sequel (or a similar game in a totally different setting) might be better, but it's hard for me to recommend this with these issues.

I Really enjoy this game! Sure, it has its flaws (such as it randomly logs me out when I'm in the middle of playing from time to time, and the somewhat buggy group system) but all in all, I really enjoy it. I really like the experience of being able to play the elder scrolls with friends, and I LOVE being able to travel across most of tamriel.   I really like the PvP, although I would like it even more if they would put a better ranking system in the PvP, where it puts you up against people your level. It also takes a long time to find any action in the PvP, unless your playing with a HUGE group of friends, because you can't fast travel to a keep that's under attack, so you have to run to the keep nearest to you, and travel to the keep nearest to the keep thats under attack, so by the time you get there, the battles mostly over. But when you do find a group, and go for a good, long run, it is REALLY fun and addicting.  The quests are kind of repetitive, but there are LOTS of other things to do, like going dungeon crawling with your friends.   I also really like the feature of being able to create a guild and build up members, until you have a HUGE community of people in the guild.  I really recommend this game to anyone who likes MMOs and/ or The Elder Scrolls!

In this game, a type of miasma that wiped out earth has created a destructive field you are searching to find out clues as to why it's spreading. You will be tasked with building a fleet to discover what is going on. You will need to gather resources and use them to open new technologies and then can study and add various parts to your ships as you continue along. There are various factions that you will need to pick and choose between, as you will be pitted against others by working with or choosing not to work with them during your main questline. I have not found a whole lot of significance in picking or not picking one over another so far, but I am not very far into the story itself, so I am not sure what effects this might have later on.  The plot itself takes place in space, and the visual style is very appealing, I think. I also find the music to be very relaxing and not too overly annoying, even after listening to it for long periods of time. Some game music will grate on me after a while and I start to notice it too much, but this one is fine so far for me personally. Anyway, as you move forward and investigate various plot elements, you will be presented with dialogue and choices, which will advance it further along.  The key is to find a balance between the ships in your fleet, returning to base periodically as you gather more resources to further enhance your current build as you progress through the storyline. The mass of various types of ships, and what classes of weapons or equipment that can or cannot be added will determine what roles your ships will likely fill, and as you unlock more technology, you will start to get a better idea of what kind of build fits what you're trying to do. Again, I will emphasize, the key is finding the balance you want, and keeping the fuel levels adequate, while dealing with random buffs and debuffs that happen as small events during your travels in space, which I really enjoy. I have not been able to find any way to save ships if you don't want them to be actively in your fleet. You have to remove their equipment/weapons and dismantle them from what I can tell, but it would be nice if they could add a feature to save ships if you wanted to use alternative formations.  The battle system is not too overly fancy, but the way in which you can use various weapons and ship builds to engage in combat is an interesting aspect to the game. You can set individual ships strategies to be more balanced, or offensive or defensive, depending on what their strengths and weaknesses are. You can choose to let the battles happen on their own at a faster rate with the Auto button on the right side, but I have found it difficult to play with the auto battle settings on so far. The AI doesn't seem to work as well as if you manually battle, so I end up trying to toggle between speeding it up on auto during the opponent's turn, and going to manual before my attacks. It would be nice if there was a way to set up and customize how your AI fights battles in a general sort of way, prioritizing certain attack patterns or strategies.  One thing I do not like about the battle system is that each turn takes a bit too long it seems, in my opinion. After you select an attack, the speed and wait between attacks beginning and ending is a bit too long. I am not sure if there is any way to make this faster, but I've found no option as of yet. Another thing about combat I think should be fixed: if you pick an attack in manual mode and switch to auto before the attack actually happens, the move will cancel out, and it will pick its own attack sequence. I find this a bit problematic if I hit the auto button too fast on my last attack of a sequence, and hope this can be fixed at a later time.  The weapons can have varying effectiveness as well, depending on type. Most direct hull (ship's HP) damage weaponry is shorter range, in general, so you will likely have to take out the front line of ships first, and then the bigger support and defensive ones after. You can attack the hull directly, which will mainly go after the shields, depending on the type of weapons used, or try to lower the numbers on individual parts of the ship you target. Lowering the shields first will help most of the conventional weaponry to take off bigger numbers to individual parts of the ship, and I have found taking out the armor to generally be the most effective way to eliminate a ship from the battlefield.   Each turn, you can attack or defend, and weapons consume power every turn. This is alleviated by either waiting in defense mode each turn, or using support ships to give energy to other ships when they are too low to use various attacks or skills on their given turns. I really like the resource gathering system in the game, and gaining new tech as I go. Each battle yields a little of each of the types of resources, and once you have ships in your fleet capable of using probes, you can try to scavenge planet bases, as well as directly salvaging materials from the planet itself to gain even more resources. If you run out, you can simply go to another planet and return and this will replenish your probes. Returning to base will accomplish the same thing, restoring your fleet completely back to normal.   This is great to do in the side quest mode, which you can basically use as a sandbox mode to gather as much as you want, while fighting battles as you go. I have had a glitch happen as well to one of my ships where the power was stuck below a certain amount, so I could not attack, and I had to return to base and remove its equipment and then re-equip it all in order to get the number to return back to its normal amount.   The missions seem rather simplistic, and in a lot of ways I think this is a good thing. I like the dialogue that is involved, . It is nice you can go back to previous acts if the new ones seem to be overly tough. I've spent the majority of my time so far in free roam gathering resources so far, and this aspect of the game is quite addictive, in my opinion, and I'm having a lot of fun learning new technologies, and collecting resources to learn the various blueprints and then add them to ships. You can also create an extended version of the blueprint, raising the stats of each type of number, but at a cost of more mass. There are various types of equipments to increase aspects of the ship such as hull, mass, power, etc.  In summary: The game is surprisingly fun, not too flashy, and visually appealing. The music is a plus, since it is subtle and relaxing, and for me it fits in with the rest of the game well. The basic gathering aspect is highly addictive and fun, and the progression is a bit slow, I think. It seems to speed up as you hit new acts though, as the need for more resources continues to increase. I enjoy researching new tech, and it's quite rewarding unlocking new ships and weapon and equipment blueprints. I would like to see the battles go by a little faster at times though, and your AI be a little bit smarter about how to attack the enemy fleets. You can gather in side missions until you are ready to move forward, so you can pace yourself in a non-linear way. I'd love to have more options in prioritizing the AI during the auto battles, and/or have ways to speed up manual battling. I'd also love to see a hangar feature implemented to save ships you may not actively want in your fleet instead of having no choice but to dismantle them. A couple of possible bugs are the regenerating probes, and the one ship's power number getting stuck where I could no longer attack. Another possible bug is the cancellation of the manual attack if you switch back to auto mode before it has gotten to a certain point in the attack phase.   I give this a solid 8.5/10 out of what I've played so far. Great game!

ICEY is basically a one hour Strider-like with some meta-commentary on 'the nature of gamedev' stacked on top of it. It's gimmicky nature is ultimately saved by the unique novelty of the Chinese gamedev perspective. I tend to detest games which revel in indie navelgazing but ICEY is ultimately a lot more good-natured than something like the Stanley Parable. ICEY's Narrator oscillates between pathetic and relatable in a very human way, which is endearing, and makes some of the twists and turns the game can take when you thwart him a bit more exciting, it's just a shame that the underlying metroidvania-layer of the game is pretty meh from a gameplay perspective. Great art/style, though and for ~10 bucks it's hard to argue that I didn't get my money's worth.

Did you ever enjoy Diablo? This game is for you.  Didn't play Diablo? Well, if you like RPG's with progressive skill trees, different character classes, lots of monsters to kill, and great dungeons to explore, this game is still for you.  In either of those cases, at $20, this game is well worth the money. However, at the $5 sales which happen a few times a year, this game is absolutely a STEAL. Incredible value for the price.  The comparisons to Diablo are well deserved, but the fact of the matter is that this game does play different and in a good way. Anyone who has played Diablo 2 probably remembers that 95% of items you find were useless, and the in game gold was almost worthless. This is absolutely not the case in TL2. The item system is deep and complex, with multiple ways to gear any given character ranging from tankiness to pure glass cannon DPS. And gold is extremely relevant as the shops sell good items, additional spells, and the gambling store rolls uniques frequently enough to spend your actually hard earned gold there. As for item drops, you will often find useful gear and have to make decisions like 'do I want that extra armor, or that little bit more damage?' all the time. Eventually you will be looking for some very specific gear which will be hard to find, but it takes a long time to get to that point and even then you don't often blindly sell all but 1-2 items on a given run.  Also there are personal stashes for each character, as well as a shared stash which mixes the best of both worlds between Diablo 2 and 3's stash systems. You can use characters as mules like in D2, but you can easily transfer items between all your characters through the shared stash. If you like to use one character to provide good equipment, or just pass down such items, to your lower characters it is a piece of cake. This is also helpful because of class-specific uniques which drop.  As far as the skill system goes, it is very diverse which each character having 3 skill branches. Each branch has 7 skills and 3 passives. Skills have tier levels when 5, 10, and 15 points are placed in them to unlock extra bonuses, but the passives do not. Skill points are actually limited though. The maximum amount is 132, but if you play for a while you'll find yourself wishing for a few extra points to throw into skills. This gives the game great build diversity, and often will leave you desiring to have more than 1 character per class to explore all the different options.  The combat system is great as well, and the monster difficulty scales well throughout the levels. Depending on your setup, some areas will be easier, but others will put a lot of stress on you. If you play carelessly, you can die really fast. The multiplayer system is great too, and some characters even have skills which lend themselves assisting your teammates in multiplayer but are useful enough in single player to allow you to boost them and then play either way you wish.  The only downsides to the game to me are in two areas. One is the story is underwhelming, and really didn't interest me. That said, most people don't play Diablo for the story so that probably isn't a big deal. The other thing I dislike a bit, and this is probably just more of a personal issue than anything, is that just like Diablo 2/3, Act 2 is a huge desert act which can be somewhat annoying to navigate. I would have been nice to have a change of pace from the same old desert crap. Although at least the bestiary is substantially different.

review_text: This is really my favorite game of all time. It took a strong dark route as opposed to its more lively counterpart.It tried new things to keep the sense of kotor still fresh and that the different actions you do affect your companions according to their personalities. This imo is the highlight of this game. Its a well thought out mechanic of the game that made me want to explore how much I could influence other party members. This and the characters themselves made this adventure a blast to play. The combat is the same,expect some new force powers but other then that, its pretty similar.The extensive dialogues are back and still very good. The writing I think is stronger in this entry managing to fit with everyone's role very nice. Idk, just buy this game. 9.99$ is not to steep for such a lengthy story, if its on sale, there should be little reason to not buy this game.Before i end this i urge you to get the TSLRCM mod. It restores most of the cut content AND the Droid planet.Both sadly cut out from the game.   10/10 buy it you   meatbags

NO. This developer made so many empty promises it's unbelievable.  Read my reviews from BETA forward below....  BETA Review: So far, being a beta, planetary annihilation has what made Total Annihilation one of the greatest strategy games to date. The gameplay, experience and management makes the game appealing to both fast-paced and slower base-building players. That being said, the constant online feature is a headache and something that I hoped developers would've realised by now is a bad idea. It's the only reason I do not enjoy the game as much as I know it has potential for. I've read that it's only until the game is out of development. It's a great game, make no mistake, but I hope they'll bring an offline mode (as they have mentioned) when the game is done or I won't be recommending it at all.  EDIT: Post Galactic Update. Game runs smoother although the AI still focuses on swarming rather than strategy, turning most games into a bit of an irritation in the longer run. I love the new commander spawn and everything feels slicker and better. No crashes yet from the new update.  What started off as a great idea turns into something that I never wanted for it. First 'exclusive' content is already available in DLC form and those who bought on early access (supporting the game from the start) get the short end of the stick and zero reward.  It's an excuse to call it a completed game. There aren't even factions. To call this game Planetary Annihilation is an insult to Total Annihilation, which is still a better game. Buy it on special, then it's maybe worth it. I'll never support an early access game from these developers again.  EDIT 2: Post Offline announcement. This still can't make me recommend the game. They made a promise, they broke it. I'm tired of supporting companies who don't keep up to their end of the bargain.  After all that's been done, and with a ridiculous kickstarter budget, yet half of the promised content....I will never support Uber again.

Far Cry 3: Blood Dragon for Steam had all my attention. 80s Nostalgia, Action Heroes, Cyber Commandos, Tron colors...sign me up! There's just one problem the game failed to tell me about--it's virtually UNPLAYABLE. In truth, it uploads another DRM onto your PC called uPlay which is barely functional. If you can even get it to work, the UI plays on top of Steam which does nothing except slow down the game to the point of agonizing levels. Just save your money and get the game on Xbox 360 or PS3, instead! It'll be DRM free and run like a champ!

This game is practically a dream come true. Crypt of the NecroDancer combines two of my favorite genres of games (Rhythm and Dungeon Crawler) in a completely awesome way. Move to the rhythm as you traverse an ever-changing dungeon as a variety of characters, while picking up awesome weapons and spells to defeat the baddies that stand in your way. The game is very solid for it's basic premise, and you can't tell it at a glance, but it has a very good story. A good game I can relate this to is The Binding of Isaac, because it has plenty of re-playability value that makes you wanna keep diving back into the dungeon for a different experience. Whether you're looking for a Rhythm game or a Dungeon Crawler, this is a 'must have' for your Steam library.

app_name: Paranormal State: Poison Spring Collector's Edition
review_text: I want to like this game but its hard. The game has nice points for it. The graphics look good and clean and clear. The sound is nice, the voice overs are ok. The issue I have with the game is the puzzles are a little strange to be honest. The fusebox puzzle for one and they all seem like someone is checking them off from a list. They are mostly unfun.  The graphics while clean are strange. You get a full screen shot of someones head and there talking but no lips are moving. You get a lot of static shots of the locations and people. The team of people your with dont seem to care much about the living. In the first scene someone is talking on there cell and she screams and the line goes dead. Do they call the police? Nope they wait around while you search the area for a way to get into the house. There is another scene where your character falls thru a hole in the floor of the house. Do they offer aid and try and get you out? Nope just a well I know you can make your own way out. Wow thanks for the concern there fellows.  The thing is there are already games out there that do this exact formula and do it a lot better then this. Examples would be Abyss: The Wraiths of Eden, Nightmares from the Deep: The Cursed Heart, The Midnight Mystery games. These games have better puzzles and the stories are a lot more interesting and they tell a good story. Buy these other games first and if you can find this for cheap as I did then pick it up if this kind of game interest you. NOt a terrible game but could be so much better then it is.

As a fan of the franchise since the first game, I must say that this game is the best finale for the series. The main story is pretty satisfying, with many decision branches that affects the ending that you will get. Side quests also provide depth as everyone of them has a story of their own, rather than just generic ones, and some even affects the main story itself. Same can be said with the expansions, with their own interesting storylines. All in all, it is the greatest game I have played.  Edit: For the steam awards, facial reactions, the general acting as well as voice acting, makes this game as good as any portuguese soap.

The Good: Magic Duels has that special 'Magic' feeling about it. It's free to play but unlike its main competitor it does not seek to drain you of your hard earned cash by using multiple kinds of currency to always leave you with a desire to spend real money to progress and get new cards. Magic Duels has one kind of currency and you can only get new cards by buying booster packs. Pure and simple. It feels honest.  The Bad: Less honest is the way the AI for Magic Duels feels. Theres no other way to say this: It cheats. No really. It cheats. It knows what cards you have in your deck and in your hand at any given time and I've personally seen it have more than 4 cards of the same excact kind in its deck. It has an infuriating habbit of pulling a rabbit out of its hat, and just happen to have the excact card it needs to foil your plans. I know developers has to make up for AIs just not being able to put up as good a challange as real people but I really wish they would have spent more time on this AI.  Overall: SO close to being a really enjoyable Magic game. One that I really wanted to love. Sadly I can't recommend it unless you are planning to play this with friends only where only the crappy 'random' draws ruins the fun. The single player stuff is vile and will give you heartburn.

God Games have always been one which you've have the overall power the wield the world at your fingertips, with each God Game type having their own different style to give, showing the emphasis of what type of god you are in that specific universe. Sometimes the wielding of nature (which is most common in god games) is used to a much greater advantage, one of which provides the player to have much more strategy that simply clicking on the environment. From Dust perfectly does this.   From Dust developed by Ubisoft is a game where you play as a deity which has the power to shift the lands underneath the native people's feet for their own means of survival. From Dust's story synopsis is about a group of native in which their land is torn by the natural causes, until they summon their god (the player) to help them strive to a new land in which they may find a peaceful location to settle on with any nature causes harming their colony.   The Story is told through a more native style tale which help give a more tribal and authentic feel to the games' art style, with such a story it can be consider to have contain a hidden message or parable. (Though I'll overlook that)  The gameplay is pretty much like other god games. You have to move around the natural element of the world to help suit the needs of the native people, to get from point A to B, which 'B' is a tunnel to the next level. The native people set up camp near a tribal totem pole in which it gives you various additional power to prevent natural disasters or pave way to an exit. This is the games' strongest point: the powers that the player has. The game makes it clear that you can grab and bend the natural element to your will. Ubisoft was clever with this technique as each level gives a limited amount of supply of a certain element. This is to make you strategise the current situation and makes you focus on rationing certain elements. Sometimes they give too much of an element and its up to the player to prevent that specific element destroy the tribes people. Sometimes this can done through kites which give special powers prevent certain elements from destroying the tribe.   The art style of the game is somewhat unique, in that it uses more tribal colours and texture which exist in more tropical environments, like Hawaii and Togo. The graphics are technically well designed with very little glitches or flaws that exist within the game.   The flaws that exist within the game are the sometimes unfairness of a certain level, elements attacking the tribe too quickly and giving a game over. Another is the AI which has a lack of pathfinding when going to a location which is perfect valid with no obstacles in the way. Last but not least is the somewhat lack of a sandbox mode or more time to use your creative skill in building nature constructs. Personally I had a great time using some elements to create damns and bridges, changing the flows of certain elements is what is highly enriching besides the strategy element of the game.  Overall From Dust is a great game which I highly recommend to anyone who loves God Games and want a bit more strategy as well.   7.75/10.0 (Very Good)

I love me a good choose-your-own-adventure, and the Assassin series is a solid option for those looking for a lot of charming little split paths. Orlandes in particular I feel is a better put together tale, and although the choices you make have less impact, it's a fairly well written story. Just keep in mind that this is a two part series, so to get the full charm and see the series miniature example of developer evolution, this isn't a bad place to start. The story follows your share of tropes and leads us off on a very gentle cliffhanger. The use of old school dice to determine encounters and sneak detection is a nice touch, and going on the highest difficulty will garuntee some roadbumps along the way and some surprising challenge for a choose your own adventure story, which definitely extends it out a bit.  The story is fairly standard, all being said, but what is important is that it's an enjoyable little journey. Is it worth the 10 dollar asking price? That's...tough. Even with all the branching paths, Orlandes is a short novella at best. The sequel, which has the same asking price, has a ton more content. On sale? Definitely worth the buy, but the price tag as of now is hefty. Orlandes is one to keep an eye on, and it will probably elicit that nostalgic feeling, even if it doesn't quite capture the charm of its predecessors.

I've tried many times to write a decent review of Mankind Divided, but I failed because it all comes down to preference. Do you like the setting? Do you feel that microtransactions are not invading your gaming experience? Is the storyline enough to keep you entertained for more than 10 hours? Then this may be for you. However, although I recommend the game to a veteran Deus Ex player, I cannot recommend it to new players. The storyline is bland, excessively diluted, and requires multiple media to be enjoyed fully. If you dont buy the prequel - the novel Black Light, which bridges the gap between Human Revolution and this game - and Children's Crusade, you miss many points of interest that would have let you experience the story in the correct way. This is not acceptable in my opinion. And it is not acceptable that a new patch causes more troubles, or even opens more bugs - for example last patch saw a main characfter spawn two times and be alive and dead at the same time -.  That being said, this is probably the closest 'positive' review to a 'negative' that I would give. I dont want Adam's adventures to end, but the developers need to do at least three things in the next installment:  1 - hire a better writer 2 - find another publisher or fight off the bad influence of corporate businessmen that just want to fiddle with the game to gain more money 3 - focus on what Human Revolution offered and return to it's glory as close as possible  I have enjoyed the game mechanics very much, but everything else needs a major return to HR's pacing, art style and characters.

Good entertainment for an afternoon  Jetdogs have a few hidden object titles on Steam and this is one of their best from the games I have played. It's an enjoyable romp through atmospheric scenes and puzzles that won't cause too many problems to solve.  In fact the puzzles are rather easy, thus this would be a great game for kids or beginners to the genre. Only one puzzle took me more than a few minutes (a tetrahedron, and even then it only took about 15 minutes), so I would have appreciated a more challenging experience. I played it on the harder level so It must be basically just a playthrough on the lower setting. In fact you are often told exactly what you need at a given time. For instance, when arriving at an impasse, you will be informed that a crowbar would be useful. As you pick up the crowbar very early on, there is absolutely no challenge in going to your inventory and then using it.  The music throughout is very good. Different classical pieces really add to the absorbing locations. The story itself makes sense and is reasonable for a casual HOG. The story held my interest and was a motivating factor in completing the game.  I enjoyed Frankenstein and it was a welcome break in between playing more traditional, tougher point and click adventure games.

I have to admit, I went into this game skeptical. I love the story that the RPG usually holds, yet I despise both RPG styled fights and bullet hell; both of which are key elements in this game. However, my skepticism was thrown out the window by the time I got past the first boss. The quick time events in battle kept me paying attention. The 'attacks' were driven by observation. I felt my progression with every enemy I spared. A sense of accomplishment trailed me while playing this game. The gameplay was excellent, in addition to the story, characters, setting, lore, humor, and the SOUNDTRACK. When playing, you really feel the determination mentioned with each save point. But as fun as this game is, it does delve into a rather horrific storyline. I wouldn't recommened it for the meek. I'm about to face the final boss, who I've seen online, and I think that's where it starts dissolving from clever humor into disturbing horror. The game also has some parts where the fourth wall is broken; not as a joke, but as the in-game characters questioning their exsistence and remembering past save files even if you hit reset. I just wanted to warn anybody getting the game, as I didn't have that warning myself, and it really freaked me out. So if you like an engaging, detailing game with unique characters, humor, and gameplay, Undertale is probably what you're looking for. Stay determined!

10/10  A very complex game which requires ONLY 2 things to enjoy (specially when playing without a party)  1. Do not feed  - Basically, feeding your enemies will make your teammates hate you and curse you  - Learn when or when not be aggressive. Study hero skills.  Play more to gain experience and also go to  dotabuff.com  to lookup heroes which are very successful  against a particular hero you want to learn / your favorite hero  - Do not defend towers alone against 2 or more heroes. Just backout if your teammates are not going  to defend the towers.  - Backout of your position when you are alone and visible on the map and opposing players  are nowhere to be seen as chances are the opposing team are coming to gank you  - go back to base if low HP and you dont have life regen items  - Do not tag along with a hero with an escape mechanism. chances are in bad situations   you'll be left alone and get killed by the enemy.   Only tag along when there are 3 or more of the team  - Always bring TP when planning to go deep into enemy territory  - Practice your hero against bots before using it for the first time!   2. Join your team  - Basically DOTA is team game. When you die while with the team, no one will blame you (much)  unless of course you did not use your skills properly during a fight  - When teammates requests for you to join, just join them  - It is quite sad not being able to join team fights. Just make sure you have enough  mana for your skill combo  - Augment a 2 man tower push to become a 3 man push especially when the tower's  health is more than 50%.

I'm 39. I've been gaming since I was 8. I created my own game at age 12. Of the endless stream of crap titles I can probably count on one hand the games that really held up to their hype. Test Drive Unlimited 2 has found it's way onto my very short list of what I would call a 'must have' or 'must play' game. TDU 2 isn't just another multiplayer racing game, in fact it's an MMO with hundreds of players that spans 2 islands to scale! The driving is fun and acurate enough for even the more sim oriented fans. The graphics are great and the game seems to run smooth even on machines without the latest hardware. TDU 2 takes the experience even further by allowing you to ride along in with your friends in their cars while they drive or vise versa, invite friends into your houses, start a club with many choices of locations and buildings, host social gatherings, and for a small fee you can unlock the Casino which hosts Poker, Roulet, Slot Machines, and a consistant 'nightlife' 24/7

Pretty game with smooth combat and some breathtaking vistas. I have played to the end as a Mage, and have only a few real issues. I will not call them complaints, because I enjoyed the game and may dust it off again someday. As a veteran RPG fan, I enjoyed the skill trees and equipment customization. I was disappointed that I reached the level cap about halfway through the game, playing with all DLC active. Changing skills resulted in measurable changes to one's fighting style: swinging a warhammer and stumping around in plate armor feels nothing like hurling chakrams while flash-teleporting through a swarm of enemies to set them up for a blast of fire. The voice acting was generally good, and the tutorial explained all the mechanics without being too heavy handed. The Lore is well-spaced and well written, and completing a set of lorestones give a reward for bothering to listen to the entertaining stories scattered around the world. If I had complaints, it would be that, first, some abilitiess are heavily overpowered. Once I learned 'meteor' and specced my fate to archmage, I pretty much pressed one button to end a combat. Secodnly, some skills are under-used. I never once needed to pick a pocket or advance alchemy past the point of being able to mix potions with four ingredients. Thirdly, the sense of being in a single-player MMO is sometimes overwhelming. There are repeatable fetch-quests, it is easy to join and advance in all factions (no matter your ability choices), and actually leading a faction seems to provide little to no benefit.  If you enjoy RPGs even a little, I highly recommend picking this up the next time there is a sale. It may be rough, but it is a gem. Pity the publisher will never get to improve on the benchmark they've set.

Few games can truly claim to walk the fine line between fun and frustration as much as The Binding of Isaac, another hellishly difficult title from Edmund McMillen: the man behind Super Meat Boy. It’s a title that delights in pushing players to the point where they want to start taking swings at their computer before attempting another round guiding its titular tyke through a series of nightmarish and randomized dungeons.  A brief animated intro sets up the threadbare plot: Isaac is a young boy living a happy life at home with his mom until she starts hearing the voice of God and hiding him from the evils of the world. It isn’t long until Isaac is permanently locked in his room and awaiting execution at the hands of his mother at the almighty’s behest. Escaping into the basement, Isaac begins his bizarre journey.  The Binding of Isaac somehow manages to be disturbing as it is endearing with its balance of gruesome monsters and matching setting with its adorable, wide-eyed sprites. Dungeons are splashed in every foul bodily fluid imaginable—blood, guts, tears, urine, and even an uncomfortable amount of feces. Imagine Hellraiser filmed through the lens of a Saturday morning cartoon for kids. The bestiary is equally grotesque. Armies of maggots and flies are frequent foes, and headless children spewing fountains of blood from their necks are a common sight.  Bosses take things a distasteful step forward: fetuses, conjoined twins, fat slugs urinating on the floor, and even Isaac’s mother are just a few of the end-level bosses that need to be dealt with. Keep in mind that the protagonist is also a perpetually-naked boy using his tears as projectiles. The Binding of Isaac is a pretty sick game with aesthetics that would appeal to one’s inner horror fan and Splatterhouse enthusiast. It isn’t going to be to everyone’s tastes, but those that enjoyed Super Meat Boy are probably going to find themselves like McMillen’s perverted sense of style.  Since the dungeons are randomized, there’s no telling what you’re going to find whether it be a useful power-up or more monsters. This makes memorization of each level impossible and keeps the experience fresh every time. On the downside, this means success depends on good luck and players will often lose because they were dealt a bad hand. Every time Isaac dies, players wind up right back at the start of the game and even the most patient of individuals will become infuriated by such a design choice. After putting in so much effort and acquiring a myriad of useful abilities, it feels way too harsh.  The randomness extends to the power-ups themselves, as there are well over a hundred of them and they don’t always have the expected effect. Some even hurt Isaac and if used in a tight spot are going to guarantee death. At the very least, they can be used in conjunction with other power ups and there’s not much of a limit on how many of them can be used at the same time. Still, there’s nothing worse than getting bad power-ups and items with no end in sight. At that point, it’s probably best to just quit and start again.  There’s even some replayability to the title with extra characters that can be unlocked and a bit of exploring to see if there’s anything particularly useful in a given dungeon. The Binding of Isaac is a purposefully short title designed to be played many times over, not just due to its unforgiving nature, that will yield a surprise here and there when players think they’re seen everything. Its ability to keep you guessing is its greatest strength and weakness as it violently seesaws between an enjoyable 2D romp and unbridled frustration.  The Binding of Isaac is not for those that are easily deterred, and it’s a safe bet that any Super Meat Boy fans are going to sink their teeth into it. For such a small title, there’s a lot of content packed into it and it’s an experience that isn’t always the same from one playthrough to the next. Players will question whether it was their own skill that carried them through to the end or if it was sheer luck, but regardless of the difficulty it’s an approachable game with a unique charm that boils blood just as easily as it encourages laughter at Isaac’s cartoonish spectacle of childhood trauma.    A Must Play

XCOM: Enemy Unknown is hard. Your soldiers will die, sometimes for pretty stupid reasons. Countries will panic and abandon your cause. There's a good chance you won't beat the game on your first try without external research of some kind. And that is why XCOM is such a fantastic game. I'd recommend it for those new to the tactical turn-based genre on merits of the slick, easy to use UI and the smooth learning curve. I'd recommend it for those with more experience if they are seeking a challenge. And I would recommend it for everyone just because it's so damn fun.

Fable - The Lost Chapters, a classic game that originally released in October of 2005 on the original Xbox, September of the same year on PC, ported to Steam in December 2011 and recently had an HD re-release on Xbox 360. Fable is a RPG in which your decisions dictate where the story will go. You can be a Hero of purity and good or a Hero of deceit and evil. This was a groundbreaking feature when it first released because no other game on the market had that 'I really control this character's life' feel to it. With a unique skill leveling system, you can make yourself a burly warrior, a gifted mage, a craft thief, or as I like to do, a 'Jack-of-All-Trades' sort of build.  When the game begins, you are still just a boy in the town of Oakvale. It's your sister's birthday and you need to find her a present. Your choices start at a young age, as you decide where you want to do good or bad deeds to make the few gold needed to get the gift. Shortly after you get the gift, bandits raid your little town and slaughter everyone. You're saved by a member of The Heroes Guild and taken there where you'll grow up and learn to be a Hero. Will you be The Hero of Avo or a Servant of Skorn. The choice is yours.
