I love the other games in the Arkham series, and played them with no issues. Frankly I play a ton of games both old and new, and rarely do I find a game to be just totally unplayable on any of my computers. It was a huge disappointment that this game had so many performance issues it was really unplayable to begin with, and even after their couple of patches it is barely playable with low fps in many areas and still some graphical artifacts.   If you do somehow manage to get the game running at a decent speed (which requires a better graphics card and more memory than any other game I have come across to even begin to play), then the game itself is okay but has seemed to me to be the weakest in the Arkham series since it ends up centered so much on requiring the batmobile. It is a lot of fun to have a new toy, but you are required to use it so much that it ends up feeling like a racing/tank shooter game half the time. The rest is pretty good and most of the things you liked about the previous games (if you haven't played them, you should) are still in there to some degree. I am sure it will be a great game in 5-10 years when it is patched either officially or unofficially the way it should be and when most people will actually have computers that will run it reliably.

I FINALLY completed this game, so I decided to say my thoughts.  This is a pretty interesting game. It makes gun usage a lot more complicated than it normally would be in other first-person shooter games. There's no ammo counter. You have to manage what ammo you have yourself, and do every bit of the reloading process that other games usually do for you. And there are also different mechanics to each weapon that you have to deal with. I felt a little overwhelmed at first, but it takes a little time to get the hang of it. I find the revolver a little easier to use.  But this game can be quite frustrating as well. Sometimes you spawn right where an enemy can see you and start shooting. Sometimes you spawn with only a few bullets, and loads of enemies. Sometimes you go down some stairs towards a doorway and are killed by an enemy you can't even see because you haven't even gotten to the bottom of the stairs so you can see them. Sometimes the frame rate drops in some areas and makes it hard to shoot a drone flying straight at you (or that might have just been my laptop). Sometimes the game lags when you enter a new room, and it's not particularly helpful when there's an enemy around the corner. And sometimes you might have to enter a room with four or five drones inside. Good luck with that.  But the biggest problem I've dealt with is the wasting of bullets while trying to disable a turret. I know what parts of the turret I'm supposed to shoot in order to disable it, but sometimes, I end up wasting an entire clip of ammo because it won't work. It feels like the hit detection is off or something sometimes.  But overall, I rather enjoyed the game.

review_text: I have found out this game dull regarding its main story. I did not dislike at all the stealth game system. I would not pay the 49 Euros price. I would wait for a discount. The scenarios of medieval towns are remarkable though.

This game was on sale and i decided to give it a try, i mean why not. The game itself is not as enjoyable as other stealth based games. The city itself feels narrow and linear the stealth mechanics are some what fine but not the best. The main quest is confusing and i find myself wondering as to why to i care about Garrett when he comes off as a cross between Batman and The Crow some of his dialogue come of as cringeworthy. The puzzles you are required to do to obtain a certain item are excruciating and could have been handled better.  This game had the potential to be great especially since i don't mind stealth games but i ask myself where is the loot where is the fun of doing these easy missions that take only minutes to complete. You don't have to kill anyone and by pass the level with ease. I wanted a challenge instead i found myself thinking that i could be doing something else with my time.  What is great about the game are the visuals especially with the lighting of certain levels and the city itself looks like a real filthy and damp city and certainly keeps you immersed for a while. The weapons are creative for the most part but i felt there could have been more effort put into it.  I would recommend the game if it goes for cheap on a steam sale but in my opinion it's worth a try just don't pay full price or you'll feel ripped off.

I love this game! The best thing about it is definitely the high replayability. Even the people who are not so good at it are still trying to beat it after 1k deaths. Well worth the money. The concept is simple yet well executed, the graphic looks really good and the controls feel amazing. Personally I like to connect my controller to the PC and customize a few inputs, but you can use the keyboard with no problems.  Finally, though as I mentioned the game is replayable just in itself, you get the daily challenges, where you compete against other players to get your score on the leaderboard. There is a CO-OP option and a VS mode, for those who like to play with friends. Finally, you can make of this game what you want: a score run, a speedrun, a pacifist run, ect. 10/10  You can play the old version for free on the official website, if you want to try the game before buying it.

Challenges can be fun. And Gauntlet has the fundamentals of a great challenge: interesting tools for split-second decision making, and difficult encounters that demand you play your very best.  Unfortunately, there are some awful design decisions and sloppy glitches that spoil what would otherwise have been a classic action game.  First, the pacing: Gauntlet has historically been a very fast-paced game. But the obstacles in this Gauntlet are often extremely tedious. Monster spawners take ages to destroy, and you'll have to do it over and over again. Once you've found the right strategy, you'll just be repeating that approach every time, while hoping that one of the game's many glitches doesn't ruin your attempt.  Among these bugs: Enemies will get stuck, invincibly, inside walls; the player character will get stuck in the world; collision detection screws up frequently; attacks will miss enemies directly in front of you; small elevation changes will render abilities useless (God help you if you try to fight on stairs); your defensive skills will sometimes fail to work; and relics can cause your character to lock up.  Any of these glitches can lead to a cheap death, and that often means losing 20+ minutes worth of progress, since the game's rewards are gated behind challenges like 'complete three levels in a row on the hardest difficulty without dying once,' while your character dies in a split second if you screw up, or the game does.  While Gauntlet seems suited for a quick jump-in, jump-out pace, the level structure actually requires completing several very lengthy levels in a row, which gets very tiring, especially when they consist of the same obstacles over and over. The level design itself is poor, with lots of boring, winding paths, thoughtless blob-shaped rooms, and cheap traps. In one of the randomly-selected sections, there's a room that can trap your character by requiring a key to exit yet not including a key anywhere in the room, or any way to backtrack, forcing a restart.  There are fun elements here, but it's a sloppy product.

It seems when you set out to make a point and click game, there are several characteristics your game must have. It must have visual and audio effects that reflect a relaxing atmosphere. It must have a simple interface that makes you feel like a complete dullard when you inevitably fail to understand what you're supposed to do. Each solution must incorporate a logic that is completely unique to the creator and makes everyone else say, 'Umm okay... I guess that makes sense...' when/if they find the correct solution. And finally, it must have gameplay that encourages the player to rub every item in their inventory against every interactive surface in the game, in hopes they'll get lucky and stumble onto the creator's train of logic.  So with that in mind, let's go down Fondling Teddy's checklist. -Calming visuals? Check. -Soothing ambient music and sound effects? Check. -Ridiculously simple interface? Check. -Puzzles who's train of logic just pulled in from the twilight zone? Double check. -Gameplay and level design that makes the player wander aimlessly back and forth throughout the game, mashing random objects together like a confused, alcoholic alzheimer's patient that just pulled themself from a car crash? Ultra check.  In fact, backtracking is such a huge part of Finding Tiddy that it should be one of it's big selling points. 'Now with 75% more backtracking!', the store page would say. 'Do you enjoy seeing the same 8 screens over and over? Too bad!', the store page would exclaim.  But all the backtracking pales in comparison to the absurd 'solutions'. The breaking point for me occurred when I eventually got sick of the usual, 'mash item X against surface Y in a vain attempt to understand what I'm supposed to do' technique and committed the ultimate and most dishonorable sin of all: looking up a walkthrough. As it turns out, I was supposed to play a series of notes from my newly acquired sheet music notepad thing to make a rock happy. Which would make sense, but here's the catch, over half of the music notes were missing from the sheet music notepad thing and I had no donkey punching idea how I was supposed to get the rest of music notes. Or what order I was supposed to play them in. Or even if I was supposed to play music at all. None of this is explained. Even after I copy-pasted the solution from the walkthrough, nothing is explained. There wasn't any sort of message saying something like 'hey you figured out the right music notes by giving a sensual massage to the tree of pounding' or 'it's a good thing you booty bumped the stone of throbbing cause he had right idea' or anything at all to show what planet the developer was on when they made that puzzle. All I can assume is that I was   supposed   to look up a walkthrough. As far as I can tell, that was the developer's   intended   solution.  In short, Fondling Tiddy is your typical, mundane, nothing special, point and click game. Much like a stripper, it's fun to look at but once you get down to business, you'll see why looks aren't everything.  If you want a good point and click game, then check out Machinarium and Return to Mysterious Island. Those games do point and click right.  Machinarium:  http://store.steampowered.com/app/40700/   Return to Mysterious Island:  http://store.steampowered.com/app/277110/

So to be fair and fact: Platformines shows more as it really has, it could get pretty fast boring coz its everytime kinda the same: run into the cave - loot - back to base - sell - run into the cave. Also coz that the cave IS SUCH HUGE AND RANDOM i miss really someone on my side who helps me out or i could share the fun...It plays just 'unfinish'...it really feels like the creators missed something, srs.  So for my opinion, BEST try FIRST the DEMO and make your OWN Fact about but i -CANT- say its a bad OR a good game.  So i would give it a 3 out of 5 star voting.

At first glance this game didn’t look very good. Just some other F2P FPS and soon to disappear. Although after only playing a few hours I can happily say that I was VERY wrong. Once entering the game it’s a bit confusing although it is pretty simple. There are many classes within the game specifying what the character can do. This aspect is very similar to TF2. After a bit of time playing the game you will earn in-game currency that can be used to buy boxes or you may earn them through playing and will be randomly award with a boxes much like CSGO cases. The difference is that you don’t pay with real money in any way because there aren’t “keys”. The load out card you earn for your character can be applied to them which will change things like their guns and/or abilities. This feature really makes this game different from others and I really enjoy it. Overall the gameplay is solid and runs well. I rarely get frame drops or skipping. The maps are also really cool; easy to understand but yet they allow you to move behind the opposing force or go directly into them. There are also many different missions from map to map giving you different game modes. I have also noticed a competitive tab in the menu all though I have not tried it. If you have considered downloading DirtyBomb I suggest you do. It’s worth your disk space.

So after 240 hours in 2k16 and 20 hours in this, I can really say it improved in almost every point. So I am just going to make a quick pro and con list: pro: -new handle system is easy to learn but hard to master (you can really create your own combos and make some nasty ankle breakers) -graphics and sound improved even more -AI improved by a big margin -Blacktop now has people watching you play, cheering etc -the soundtrack is once again amazing -playing as a team (for me personally) is way more fun in this part -the story and the cutscenes are amazing and they seem to never stop -endorsements are awesome (you can sign with a shoe company and get your own shoe for example) -the dynamic duo system is fun -the halftime/pause shows -you can rewind the whole game in instant replay now -much more which I fail to remember right now but those are enough  cons: -somewhat underated player ratings (Isiah Thomas, Dwyane Wade, Damian Lillard..) -already 90+ ovr players in mypark -it takes way to long imo to get a new upgrade from working out -some weird sound and animation bugs but they aren't a big contra  All in all I'd give this game a 9/10, it's so much fun and even more fun with friends. app_name: LEGO® STAR WARS™: The Force Awakens
review_text: This game is addicting. As soon as you get into it, it feels amazing. The graphics are wonderful(for a lego videogame indeed). Everything seems new from their previous installments. I completed the game, and the completing part was as fun as the story part. I must say that the game wasn't that hard, but it was still fun.   Pros :  -The story goes perfectly with the movie -The voice acting is awesome -BB-8 is as cute as ever -It's Star Wars -You can actually know what happened between the sixth installment and the seventh which in my opinion is absolutely awesome. -Kylo Ren is a Darth Vader fanboy -The new cover system is cool and feels so great in a Lego game  -The cutscenes can be pretty funny -You can play as some of the characters from the 6 first installment by collecting Carbonite Bricks -If you get to the 'last level', you'll have tons of fun!  Cons: -The AI is extremly dumb, he will wait for you to do everything for him, he doesn't do anything on his own.  -The game as a whole is pretty easy -If you use a ps4 controller, the in game buttons won't be circle/square etc. They will be button 3/button 1 which can get pretty confusing -The game crashes sometimes -You can't play as mickey mouse -The water crossing part in the Rathtar Hunting level feels like a Xing Tian ult  To conclude, this game was awesome and I recommend it. I hope I will be able to play as mickey mouse in the next lego star wars game!

This is a really fun game if you can get past how stupid and unlikeable every single character in the game is.  The open-world movement is lots of fun, combat is quite fluid and easy, and the game generally looks quite nice.  You can eat innocent civilians for health, or just openly murder them without penalties, and all of the enemies in the game are part of a giant 'conspiracy' to test viruses on the citizens of New York. There are literally thousands of soldiers and scientists that are in on it, which makes the conspiracy about as unbelieveable as is humanly possible.   The main character is a 'badass' in a sort of 'we made a 10 year-old the lead writer' kind of way.  Despite all of that, this game is lots of fun. I didn't have memory leak problems, but also have 16 GB RAM, so I can't comment on people with frequent slowdown. I think the game looks like any other last-generation-to-pc-port. Higher resolutions, much better draw distance, more shadows, etc, but in reality an engine that doesn't try to do too much.  I'm having a great time 4-5 hours in, and it feels like i'm about halfway done the main story. If you like the game, get the 'Radnet' DLC. It adds a bunch of fun challenges to the map - should really be included in the main game, but has no plot and is really just a bunch of superhero sports to try out.

app_name: Prince of Persia: The Two Thrones
review_text: The Silver Egg in the trilogy, the best of both Sands of Time and Warrior Within, minus a ♥♥♥♥ ton of glitches WW had.  So sometimes you change into the Dark Prince, like Dark Link, except he merges with you and has a nasty habbit of leeching your health, in which if you don't kill enemies and smash pots for sand every now and then you'll find yourself dead faster than you can say   'Remember that one part from WW where you were the sand wraith and thought you had to rush a parkour sequence because your life was ticking away every second but actually didn't have to because it doesn't kill you unlike it does in this game?'  Yeah, thats always fun. Then theres some somewhat clunky chariot races of death straight out of The Prince of Egypt, except more death hazards and extreme faceplants into fortified walls.   One of the new/best parts of this game is some new parkour elements, and stealth (sometimes right after a parkour sequence, for example: where you slide down a crevice and assassinate the dude while his buddy isn't looking with quicktime events varying in difficulty depending on how tough the enemy is). Instances of stealth can vary, but one of the most useful parts is the sand portals: Parkour and execute the stealth QTE right, or get a ♥♥♥♥ load of reinforcements to kick your ♥♥♥ for failing.  Then one of my favorite parkour areas, the massive (probably shorter than Dubai's, LOL.) tower. In which you will ♥♥♥♥ your pants the first time one of the invisible enemies hit you.  Assassinating enemies whilst on a chain as Dark Prince is always fun, and bosses are (mostly) fun and challenging to fight.  So if you liked the first two games in the trilogy, this ones a no brainer.

Just had a very agreeable day with Gunpoint.   The plot is an entertaining and irreverent take on the noir and robot trouser genres. If you fancy some indie fun that features: progressively intricate levels, puzzling, stealthing about, leaping with a callus disregard for gravity, and (when the time is right) kicking bloody doors in. Then this is the game for you. The gameplay is well balanced you feel badass, but extremely vulnerable at the same time. The story shows hidden depths and just like the building offers you a degree of choice about how you tackle it. Primarily how serious you want to take it. The pixel art is pitch perfect. It hits that level of representing what's being shown, rather than looking like a compressed image or generic block figures. However pixel art does make it harder to judge if a character is supposed to be a classic femme fatal.  I'm looking forward to an expansion pack or whatever comes next from this developer. In the mean there's a level editor and a growing list of maps in the steam workshop. Few really stand out yet but I'm sure it's just a matter of time.

LoG has often been presented as a homage or even a remake of Dungeon Master (DM). Its main gameplay strong point is its puzzles. There are a good bunch of them, very evolved and clever. But DM wasn’t really puzzle based. Moreover, the dungeon exploration is a lot more straightforward than DM ; it features a more linear and less mystified progression which requires none or only very little backtracking or roaming (except for 2 or 3 levels) ; the whole progress is made even easier by the auto-map system. And as LoG’s modern 3D engine lets you see more than 3 squares away, you almost never feel lost for the whole game.  Another weak point is its combat system. It’s very simple and basically requires you to strafe-turn-strafe-turn non-stop while waiting for your attack cooldowns to reset. Even worse, if you have mages in your party, you have to enter complex rune sequences while you’re strafing & turning. It’s like that for hours till you beat the game. If you don’t strafe & turn around, the monsters will kill you very quickly.  Presented like that, it seems to be very close of DM, but actually, DM’s combat was better : - melee weapons actually had 2 or 3 different attacks, so you had to think a bit and try out to find which one was the best in the present situation ; it wasn’t a lot better than LoG, but still it was more evolved and a bit less boring & repetitive over the course of the game - it also required complex rune sequences to cast spells, but as mana was pretty limited, we could cast much less spells, and often had to prepare the spells before the fights, so it was less annoying  Anyway, personally, I think this kind of system is really outdated, and today it’s possible to create more engaging & less repetitive combat systems, either by going in a more arcade direction, or a more tactical one (like Dungeon Guardians ;-) ).  Another weak point is the story and its background. There’s not much going on there. But in the dungeon crawler genre, it’s not really penalizing.  But there’s a last severe weakness : the ending level(s). Without spoiling much, let’s say it felt out of place, rushed, and not satisfying for the gamer (ie: no real sense of achievement).  And still, LoG is a good game (great some people might say if they didn’t get bored by the combats). Mostly because except its puzzles, it has another very strong point : a great atmosphere, brought by a finely mastered artistic direction. So globally, we still get a good adventure in an oppressing dungeon.   So in short, that gives :

EDIT: I changed my review to 'recommended' since I love the gameplay and I hope they are still working on it. However be careful since this game has some issues. Read my old review for details.  Full Review: I recommend this game for its AWESOME gameplay!! Especially if you wanna play alone. If it is on sale. Get it! have some fun!  As soon as you want to play with friends, this game shows its ugly unfinished beta-face. Besides the plenty of bugs the matchmaking for friends is absolutly hell. Don't get me wrong, I had some very fun hours with this game. But let me just say this: It is possible to invite your friends to a party and queue for a game, BUT you will very likely not end up in the same team. :D This is ok, for the first match you end up in, because it already started. But the game sometimes can't even manage to place you in the right team for the second match.  Especially if you are more then 3 players it is impossible to play together. This disables this game to be any more then fun for a few hours. I voted it down because I am so disappointed. As I said, the gameplay is one of the best I've ever encountered, and they ruin all of this with matchmaking. What a shame. Another thing, which makes this game 'casual' is things like a flawed display for the health of the titans. This is one of the most crucial elements, and it doesn't work. Also, the game has plenty of lag-spikes, my connection is fine, I had a ping of 20ms, but I guess the netcode is just bad.  Another complain I have with this game, is that it is not marked 'early access' within the store. This game is far from finished.  TL;DR: If it is on sale. Get it with a friend and have one, or two evenings of really good gameplay. Don't get it if you are searching a shooter you can get into, and play often with a fixed team.  Also, developers: Please finish this game! The gameplay is soooooo good.

While I can't speak for the quality of this game on a console, I suppose a review of that would be irrevelant since this is on PC. This is an absolutely terrible port of the game. The control scheme, while interesting, was not ideal. Not unplayable, but not easy to get used to. The gameplay itself was ok, but the movement and the action was the worst part. I had huge difficulty getting through one of the first fight scenes. I can't say whether this was from the difficulty curve being too steep, or the controls not functioning properly, or just simply the game mechanics being terrible, but whatever the reason, it wasn't enjoyable. To touch briefly on the movement, it goes along with the controls being bad. Again, I'm sure if I had played more, I would've gotten used to the button mapping, but I've played a lot of games with a lot of different control schemes, and this was by far the most unique. To use mouse 1 to sprint, and to repeatedly tap it to run faster is unheard of. I'm aware that I could remap the buttons, but I shouldn't have to remap almost every button. Overall, the game was just not impressive, and frankly, not very playable. If you're are a very forgiving and lenient gamer, then you may enjoy this game, but I simply could not overlook the broken mechanics. Perhaps on a different console, it would be more enjoyable. 3/10.

A historically accurate 1v1 tower defense game where you control a boulder using the rolling physics to maneuver the boulder to bust down the enemy gate's HP while also defending your own gate's HP. I found it fun for a short while before quitting due to lack of interest as it can get pretty repetitious because for the most part you're basically just maneuvering a boulder through or around the enemey's tower placements with jumps and the basic movement controls, though the boulders can come in different variations within the match you're currently in should you purchase them with the money you earned destroying structures and smothering people.

Insurgency is one of the best shooters I have ever played. Somewhat difficult game to grasp, yet very rewarding. There is no 'run-n-gun' in this game, it is entirely tactical based. Every decision you make can ultimately be the deciding factor if your team wins or loses.  What this game does great at doing is actually making you feel like you are in a REAL war--other games can't seem to do that in my opinion. Every weapon holds the power to 1 shot 1 kill another player (not from any distance of course) and it really impacts the way you play.  For 15 bucks, you can't go wrong. This game will hold your attention for hours on end, that is, if you are smart enough to learn from your mistakes. In the beginning, YOU WILL DIE ALOT; however, if you pay attention to what you do wrong, your skill level can only go up.  Last thing to mention, for anyone who is interested in the more competitive side, there are already tournaments being set up. The community for the game is fantastic, everyone is willing to help one another out.  BUY IT!!!!!

This game has to be one of my personal favorites. I always recommend it to all my friends who think Skyrim is the only good rpg. Oblivion, personally, take the lead of my favorite TES games. The atmosphere is different everywhere you go, graphics aren't the best, but aren't the worst. Gameplay was short of perfect in my own opinion. I played this a ♥♥♥♥ ton on my 360 until it ogreheated, then I moved it to PC, before I ever heard of Steam. Every time I play, it's always a new adventure. There's literally no end to the fun. Althought I recognize it isn't as good as Morrowind, you should still give this a try if you haven't let Skyrim ruin the Elder Scrolls experience for you. The dialogue is entertaining sometimes (i.e, powdered dear ♥♥♥♥♥), but had more to say then the Skyrim dialogue. I could compare this to the height of the World of Warcraft experience before it went downhill. Oblivion is the Elder Scroll's Lich King expansion. Unlike Skyrim, you can talk to everyone at anytime (usually). The whole world is beautifully detailed and urks you to explore it. Climate changes when you get near to any of the cities (Snow near Bruma, Sunny near Anvil). This game was very well done.   bad things about this game: It has many game breaking bugs (ie duplication, stat boosting, etc.) but if you steer away from it, the game is a great experience for all. Voice acting is very repetitive (voice vise) due to lack of funds for voice actors. You can get lost easily in ruins if you're not careful to tell where you're going (but shouldnt pose that much of a problem).    Overall, I'd give it an 8/10 play it if you like WRPGs at it's finest

This game is pretty much everything what anyone would want a Survival game to be.  Thou there could be some improvements of course. The feeling is there and the basic game play is mostly enjoyable, after the slightly stiff start.  The only things, that I think, could be improved, are the lack of placing supplies to a vehicle, so you always have to carry them on your back. You can't seem to give supplies to your fellow survivals either, so that they could carry them. A co-op atleast would be a nice addition and it would solve these problems, when you and your friend could carry their own stuff.   Other than that, SoD is a nicely done game, which is pretty loyal to the Zombie genre (except for the special zombies). Although some parts were frustrating for me at the end, since I'm not spectacular at action platformers, I think this is quite possibly the most fun action platformer game that I've ever played, including classics like Sonic and the Mario games. At least for me, this games art style, story, enjoyable characters all with good voice acting, combat done well, enemy variety, story, music, atmosphere, setting, humor, and overall emotional investment the adventure mode allows the player to have with the characters all come together to make a very fun and enjoyable experience.  There is some cheesiness in some of the dialogue and story, but in a funny, well placed way. Beyond that, I feel like some of the different moves that can be pulled off can be a little awkward, but it depends on the control setup I suppose. All controls are easily rebindable, as is joystick sensitivity, so I might need to tweak that more, as some of the mild floatiness and jumping precision were probbly on my end, as everything else felt very tight.  One of the most fun aspects of the game for me was how much the overall style of presentation encourages a very 'go get 'em' attitude. The characters can take quite a number of hits before dying, and coupled with the kinds of scenarios that the characters encounter, often the best way to approach a situation is to just dive in and tackle it head on. One of the main themes in this game is the idea of believing in ones self, and not only is this conveyed through the story, but also in the very nature of the gameplay. Many times the character will be dropped into a situation with many enemies closing in on them in a very impromptu way, and though it might seem like a bad situation, if the player believes they can come out of it, and make use of the skills they have, they generally will. It was a very good use of theming through gameplay, and gives the the player a much more meaningful connection to the story in the adventure mode and the characters.  The main story isn't that long, but it's well suited for multiple playtroughs, and with different characters to pick from. There are also time attack and training levels to hone one's skills. A single playtrhough can be done in one sitting, especially if the 'classic' mode is chosen without the story. And with that, the adventure mode is just long enough to say what it needs to without overstaying it's welcome.  Overall I'd say this is a great game, especially for it's price. I can easily see myself coming back to this game over and over when I feel like a good action platformer, especially since most of the levels can be played as a fast paced speed clear, or a more elaborate maze. It's a great value, and one that I would recommend highly to anyone that like action, adventure, and/or 2D platformer games.

Space Run is an interesting take on the typical Tower Defense gameplay, and while I'm not a huge fan of these games I did enjoy this one enough that I felt like I got more than my money's worth (Note: I paid ~$5 but I do consider it worth $15 if you can't get it on sale).  Instead of building towers on a stagnant map with a maze like path for lines of mobs to run through toward their goal, you build on a platform (your spaceship) which 'flies' through space and you encounter asteroids, fighters, and larger spaceships. Beyond that it's pretty standard Tower Defense fare, kills things for points, use points to build more towers, complete map for upgrade points to buy upgraded towers, rinse and repeat.  This game also has fun little snippets of dialogue between missions, and good quality voice acting. The graphics are decent, as is the UI, and there were no major bugs during my playtime (the worst things I had happen was it would occasionally glitch after resetting a map, but exitting out to the mission select and starting again always fixed it.)  I had some issues with the game though. For instance, I really didn't care for the tedium of being forced to repeat missions in order to get enough upgrade points to not gimp myself in future missions. Nor did I like the cargo loadout part of the game, that just increaed the odds I would end up handicapping myself since I'd have no idea which parts of the ship would be hardest hit during that mission on the first playthrough. It also seemed like a mistake to force the 'ship' design on the player when in my opinion that should have been a big part of the upgrade system, do well, get a bigger ship, etc. In that way the 'ship' could have also been a persistent RPG element and made the game more interesting.  Again though, that first half was a lot of fun, more than I have in most other games at the same price.
I beat the game in around 8 hours, I did some side missions like liberating and sailed around discoverying things. I suggest getting this game on sale, it really is enjoyable.

Stellaris has been great fun so far. The sci-fi nerd in me absolutely loves the atmosphere and exploration in this game. It reminds me of the feeling the original Mass Effect gave me, an open unexplored unknown. With a great sound track to boot.  Civ V is the only other 4X/Grand Strategy game I've sunk a lot of hours into and you can draw a lot of comparisons between them. If you've played Civ the tile working system will just make sense, the income system will make sense and so will the technology + research system.  Stellaris does a great thing with the research system by randomising it. In Civ V you basically go down the same few routes every time. But in Stellaris it's mostly impossible with a randomised 3 choices for each tech type being generated. And what happens when two of those technologies you really want come up at the same? You have to make a tough choice. With the inclusion of specific researchers who have specialities and a finite life span you have to manage your leaders properly.  The custom ship designer is another awesome addition. You have to choose between rail guns, lasers, missiles or other weapons you’ve unlocked for your arsenal. You have to pick the right combination and then make sure you have enough juice to power it all. Deciding between extra shields or extra armour may depend on your neighbours and the kind of guns they’re packing. Combat can be as in depth as you choose to make it.  Overall Stellaris in it's current form is extremely fun and extremely varied. Currently there are only two victory types: Conquest and controlling 40% of the galaxy. But the journey to get there is exciting and different every time. Don't forget you can spend ages designing you own race from traits, personality, cosmetics and their home world. My review is only just touching on some of the best mechanics in the game, but there is much more on offer.

I started this game on PC, but ended up finishing it on PS4.   SOMA stops just short of brilliant.  It's a big game, even though it's short (I'd say about 6 - 9 hours to beat it on average), so you might have to divide it into sections when reviewing it.   Controls  It's pretty much Amnesia in this regard. It has the 'click-and-drag-to-manually-interact-with-the-world' kind of thing going for it. Grab and throw objects, sprint, climb, interect. It's all very straightforward, and there's an option to simplify it if you want to, y'know...   ...ruin your experience. :(   Story  Very rarely do you find a story in a movie or game that sticks with you, but SOMA's is just amazing. It touches on themes of consciousness, suicide, and what it means to be alive or human. It's a slow-burning story that has plenty of huge, incredible plot twists, but the game doesn't rely on them to keep you interested. Prepare for your existential crisis!   Stealth  SOMA's stealth is actually a little lesser than Amnesia. While you might be able to croutch past an enemy or stop moving so they don't see you, it's good, but a little on the lackluster side. There's a few missed opportunities for more dynamic gameplay, like places to hide in or things to throw. It's not bad, it's just not what I expected.   Running  When all stealth fails, you run. Oh, boy will you run. When you run, chances are you'll have the enemy right behind you or sprinting in your direction. Simple, yeah, but it'll get your heart racing.   Puzzles   The puzzles are a little scattered. They aren't bad all the time, but they can hold you up. Puzzles are known to do this, but sometimes they're misplaced and can jitter the pacing of the game. They're nice since they add a dimension to the gameplay, but some of the puzzles feel like they were put there because gamers expected them.   Exploration  This was my favorite part aside from the story itself. While this isn't a sandbox exploration game by any means, there are parts of the game where you're free to wander around - sometimes even when you don't mean to. Even puzzles can require you to poke around a little. This allows you to fall further and further into the rabbit hole. With each audio clip you listen to, each paper you read, or each data log you skim, you can see just how deep SOMA's world gets. (Bottom of the ocean, no pun intended)   What did I think?  What I didn't get from in-depth stealth, I got in running like mad, a great story, and extremely entertaining gameplay. SOMA, to me, was just shy of perfection, and it's a game I'm going to be talking about for a while      TL;DR Here's my much more entertaining, much less formal  video review  of SOMA

review_text: When ever I played video games around my grandfather he told me that war is not as fun as games represent it to be. He told me that I'll never understand his pain and that I should be ashamed of who I am for loving them. That it's an absolute desgrace to the memory of his friends and family who died in combat. I shrugged off his words and continued to play games like COD and Battlefield. My Grandfather died two years ago and I wasn't phased, I felt somewhat relieved that I no longer had his judgement for doing the things I loved.   I recently got this game and after only a few hours of playing I had launched a Dog into the air on a baloon, made a horse crap on the body of an unconscious man, blew up a truck of small african children and made men fall in love with me with a poster of a bikini clad girl placed on a cardboard box I was wearing. After all these things happened, I sudenly realised what my Grandfather was talking about, I understood the pain he felt. War is hell.   #RedWedding out of 10, would buy it again for myself.

Might be a case of incompatible and outdated hardware but for me, recently upgraded to an R9 290X before picking up this game and I am able to run the game just fine on high settings. No crashes or anything else to speak of yet. I'm thoroughly enjoying the game, especially after just having played through all of Rogue.  To be frank this game does a lot right that the previous games could have done better and there's two very important things that I can mention:  1. Offering a reward system for 100% synching a memory. If you have more incentive to do the optional objectives, it makes the experience more enriching and it makes you actually want to accomplish those objectives. Putting optional objectives just for the sake of doing them for a % completion doesn't offer much incentive.  2. Changing the combat so that every weapon isn't an insta-kill monster and this goes hand in hand with number 1. You will actually want to obtain and upgrade armor and weapons and the such now that there's a different impact on combat and you're not tearing up groups of 20 enemies with relative ease.

In VR, the game is incredibly bad performing. On my system it runs with >60 fps on highest settings at 1920x1200 in the normal mode, but in VR it's stuttering when I go beyond the lowest settings. VR needs a serious performance update to be feasible. Lowest options will make UE4 (one of the most advanced engines in existence) look worse than UE2 from more than 10 years ago (which is not exactly furthering the immersion), stuttering will get you motion sickness.  Games of this kind have to be played standing (except you have a solution for the headsets cord wrapping around you and your seat), without being able to freely rotate you will get uncomfy very fast. The devs are working on the neccessary adjustments for inventory handling, but at the moment they are not released and you have to rifle through all your pesky items every time you need something. The VR controls do not allow strafing (walking sideways), which is very annoying as you have to rotate all the time to make a step around an object (which may give you a headache over the time), will hopefully be fixed soon.  While The Solus Project offers an interesting world to immerse yourself, the technical side of the game in VR is severely lackluster at the moment. I'd recommend to put it on your wishlist and wait for the neccessary patches to be implemented. Review will be updated as the game was.

An enjoyable survivalist romp with some rogue-like elements. Think FTL in a Max Max setting. A very satisfying value while on 50% sale, but not at the full asking price. Several missed opportunities prevent this title from being an all-star, most notably the lack of content.   Pluses: + Intriguing real-time tactical convoy-based combat. You must maneuver your vehicles to protect your transport while giving them an optimal firing angle. + Good battle pacing. You *almost* have enough time to pay attention to everything, which brings to each fight just the right amount of harried eye-hand flicking. (But see UX minus below) + Varied vehicle builds. You can build ramming tanks, light DPS machines, mine layers, and other configurations. + Charming retro soundtrack. + Witty writing and flavor text. Some 'choose your own adventure' gameplay that doesn't involve convoy combat. Lots of geek references.  Minuses (more of missed opportunities): + Desperately needs more content. There's only one base map which never changes, just the quest markers on top of it. The 'randomized' quest comes from a small pool that will repeat often. + UX and grpahics go a little too far into being retro, so that they don't provide the player with needed information.  + What's with the default resolution being boxed? Yes, you can stretch to fullscreen, but then the retro graphics get really ugly. Perhaps done for a future tablet port, which makes sense, but this choice cheapens the PC experience. + RNG a bit too random, denying opportunities of player choice. (You may never get a 3rd or 4th vehicle for your convoy due to chance, severely limiting your play.) Some form of crafting/salvage system would solve the random problem, and be very in-theme.

«Yeeaaaah, that one's heading for the bottom!»  - Flavio Piccolini with his probably most repeated line ever in this game  I'm actually quite surprised to read so many negative reviews about Aquanox. It's one of those games I always wanted to play back in time, but never really had the chance to do so. Cyberpunkish submarine dogfights with crazy characters at 5000m under the sea, with sea monsters and ♥♥♥♥? What could possibly go wrong?  Many years later, recently, I finally put my hands on it. Contrary to my expectations I find that the game aged pretty well. The colorful graphics, although from 2001, are still nice to look at with all those light and plankton FX. Characters and voice actors are indeed cheesy, but really do entertain. So does the soundtrack, that  could  have been composed by The Prodigy in the flesh... Gameplay is fast and uncomplicated, there's even some nice cutscenes - what more could one want?  There are still some remaining issues like the default sensitivy with modern mice or the really unvaried mission design. In most levels you just dive from point A to B and shoot the ♥♥♥♥ out of Bionts, Crawlers or later Sea Monsters. The conversation sequences between the missions are also not everyone's cup of tea, although I was actually fine with it.  After about 10 hours you'll see the ending flickering over your screen and you may be dissapointed by the rather open ending. But it's definitely an entertaining experience after all, and that's what counts.  The Good: + Colorful and detailed graphics (for its time) + Interesting concept + Entertaining singleplayer campaign + Fine gameplay + Memorable music + Multiplayer + Cutscenes + Cheesy but solid voice acting  The Bad: - No real variety in mission design - Not a too wide selection of submarines and weapons - Story and characters not everyone's taste  The Ugly: -- Control issues with modern mice   8/10

app_name: DARK SOULS™: Prepare To Die Edition
review_text: This game...  It's better than when it first started out with GFWL, but if you're using a keyboard don't expect this game to be as user friendly as you may have come to expect with many mainstream games.  That said, the problems with playing on keyboard are easily remedied, and once you get into this game you're not going to want to stop until you've beaten everything.  The game starts off pretty easy at first, timed blocking, dodging, etc., but after you finish the tutorial the game basically drops off you in the middle of nowhere and says 'Alright, have fun!' and throws a bunch of fire ants down your pants.  It leaves you to figure out the game on your own from there, with just enough knowledge that you don't feel like you need to immediatly seek out a wiki (depending on how much of a little ♥♥♥♥♥ you are). From there each area is just full of surprises from well-made enemies and bosses to hidden traps and tresures.  In short, if you're looking for a challenge, try this game out.  As a tip from me to you; try to hold off on looking up walkthroughs for as long as possible, half the fun of Dark Souls is figuring out where to go and who to kill by yourself.

app_name: Tom Clancy's Ghost Recon Future Soldier
review_text: This game. I can't praise it enough. My first two hours in this game was basically spamming 'OMIGOSH' to all my friends because I had never seen something as amazing as this.  Beautiful gunplay, really advanced customization menu for guns (though you should just listen to your benevolent overlord who recommends everything for you before the mission), there's so much you can do in combat, from super-stealth to Sync shots, to infiltration (slow-mo retro style shooting included), prioritization of targets during normal combat, marking out targets with airborne devices such as drones, etc.  You also have X-Ray vision and sensor grenades (which are pointless once you have X-Ray vision). Your team is equipped with extremely futuristic weapons, gear and outfits (you actually wear a Chameleon camo that changes your color according to the surrounding, making you nearly undetectable). The cover system while firing is extremely impressive.  The main missions are co-ordinated and always point you in the right direction, so you don't have to worry about wandering off into the deep forests of nothingness.  Conclusively, amazing game, totally worth it, definitely something I'd play repeatedly, however, there is a small issue. This game does not run from Steam independently. Lauching it actually lauches uPlay (the Ubisoft content delivery client) which requires you to register/sign in and then use the CD-Key provided by Steam to activate the game. However, this is only initial and you can launch the game normally after that.  Too long, didn't read: Buy it. Right now.

Personally, I found this game very under-rated. I thoroughly enjoyed it. The combat system was fun to use and adapt to and can make your character look like a total badass if you've got it down, or a blind 10 year old waving a stick if you don't. Hell, maybe you're even a badass blind 10 year old with a stick. In any event, I found the combat very satisfying. As for characters, Roy (The protagonist) was great. I loved the way he sounds, his word choice no matter what option you choose, and just the way he lookes (having a main character with heterochromia just looks cool). I also liked the other characters and how you can influence them and converse. And as far as weaponry goes, you get a huge selection of makeshift, modifiable weapons and explosives.  I can see why some people might have less praise for this game than myself though, it isn't a typical game like many of us are used to. It's not like any shooter here, and not even that similar to other RPGs like Fallout or The Elder Scrolls. Nonetheless, I would recommend this game to anyone looking for a good game with a good story, unique combat, and well rounded (and yet somewhat mysterious) characters.

app_name: Deus Ex: Human Revolution - Director's Cut
review_text: Played the original version back in 2011 and loved it! One of the best games I played of last generation and a worthy follow up to the masterpiece that is Deus Ex!  A really engaging story which had me interested from the get go, some really well designed environments that let you choose which approach you take to complete objectives. Personally I was more of a stealth until someone sees me then kill everyone in sight, but if you want to stealth the whole game you can. They have also fixed the messed up boss fights in this version, no longer will you be stuck with no ammo and forced to fight a boss. Also this version comes included with “The Missing Link” a 2-3 hour side mission which was originally released as DLC, it is not part of the main game and I really enjoyed it as I had not had the chance to play it!  Graphics still hold up pretty well, and I must admit I like Jensen's voice actor a lot despite him coming across sometimes as very monotone.  If you have never played Deus Ex and want to see what it's all about then pick up this little gem! And remember when you're backed into a corner with ♥♥♥♥ all ammo just remember that you never asked for this!

Frankly I feel dissapointed for spending so much time on this game. I really only have one thing to be said on this game...it has good production values. Maybe the music if nobody tries to add lyrics. But when it comes to story...oh boy just forget it and look elsewhere. ANYWHERE. This might have been worth it if the story had in any way come to paint the hero as a redeamable character...but any chances of that ends with the first part of the trilogy.  Rufus is the main character, and can be safely classified as a sociopathic anti-hero. The least of the things you will get to do as the 'hero' in joining Rufus on his journy would be to break up a black woman from her boyfriend and trick her into indentured servatude, turn baby dolphins into tuna, repeatably mind warp and physically abuse your 'love interest', poison your friends, and my personal favorite being FEEDING CHILDREN TO A SLIME MONSTER (tortilla wraps, gravy, and even puting them in the mouth yourself).   The 'jokes' are not funny, usually aiming for the lowest denominator. Expect sexism, racism, misogamy, and even gay jokes just to be hip. Expect our 'hero' to remind women to not be in a resistance movement, to consistently make fun of a speech impediment, to basically just throw words at you and jangle the keys. It's really sad that my personal dislike of Rufus and the jokes were so bad that even doing the tired 'crotch meets hard object' joke didn't even make me crack a smile. They even have their own laugh track out of nowhere for one scene...it's about as funny as it sounds.  So my final advice to you is to avoid this trilogy. I spent roughly 20 hours on it. I want my money, my time, and to wash myself of the whole experience like it never happened. It takes a lot for me to utterly find a character irredeemable and Daedalic has succeded of going even beyond that. To the point I now question the ethics of the developers themselves.
It was better when it was out of steam, now is the biggest ♥♥♥♥♥♥♥t to try to play.

What starts out as an intreguing, enigmatic experience turns into an irritating triviality when you have to constantly repeat unreasonably difficult quick time events (QTE's in a story-driven puzzle game? You couldn't do better?). The story had enough intregue to make me want to know more about what was going on, and I do quite like the voice acting, but the horrible QTEs in the beginning made it unplayable. I say 'in the beginning' because I never got past the Windmill. Why? Because my character kept blacking out because I didn't press E every millisecond when it showed up. I kept having to redo that section, and it interupted the flow of the story and frustrated me to the point of quitting. I'm not spending my time on frantic QTEs when I could play much more fulfilling games (some of which are also free). Sorry, developers. I can tell effort was put into this and I would like to know how the story goes. But seeing as the very first bits of gameplay are clogged with QTEs, I will have to recommend one just watch a Let's Play. That way you'll get the story, and you wont have to do the frantic button pressing! The reviews say that the puzzles for this game were well-done, but I never got to any real puzzles because I was blocked by the least relevant game mechanic of the century. Oh, yeah, and the save game doesn't make sense. It says that you'll have to remember where you last were, but as far as I could tell you couldn't just type it in, or select from a list. Was it really so hard to have a typical save file, guys?

But that doesn’t make up for the lack of contents it provides, especially in now day gamings standards, do Capcom think this is still the 90s? Do they think they can get away with a half finished game and ship with just because they’re Capcom?? The extreme lack of quality in certain areas (The Character Story Mode art, who did it? How the hell did low quality, minimal effort roughly drawn art like those get a pass???), and the unstable server issues, the still unavailable characters, STILL no Arcade mode, poorly designed survival mode, and the ridiculous pricing. The ‘extremely late, patched in Story Mode was solid and well done, it actually provided a lot of stories, backgrounds for this game and links with previous games, I think if Capcom had this at launch, people will be more forgiving, but they got cocky.  A solid fighting game, lacking extremely in single player replay values, too much grinding to unlock characters. Does not worth the full price at current time, NEVER get it unless it’s on sale special.

app_name: STAR WARS™: The Force Unleashed™ Ultimate Sith Edition
review_text: The game is pretty good. Decent story. But were it really shines is combat and movement. It has revolutionary combat which utilizes corners and walls like never before. Let me elaborate. so say you're fighting a boss and he has his 'special ragdolling super-move.' the dude gets close to you and you're now trapped between ragdoll god and a wall. he's ragdolling you constantly, you can't get up. What do you do? hope to god you can un-ragdolls for the 0.03 seconds it takes to jump out of there. This only happens about 20% of the time If you have good reflexes (or mash spacebar uncontrollably). Never before have I seen the environment integrated into immersive combat so effectively. The second great aspect is the movement, or more specifically the fact it doubles as a surfing simulator. Sometimes you might get in a battle that killed you 6 times and took an hour and a half to complete. You've finally done it and you walk a meter away from the intended path and BOOM. you've found a sufable corner. The surfing animation is without a doubt one of the best I have seen in a game to date. the only downside is that usually after about 10 seconds, the physics engine decides you've been surfing on the spot for so long you're probably at like 200 kph by now so it rockets you outward into the firey abyss of doom (or some other abyss of doom.) Fortunatly because of the amazing checkpoint system, you now get to fight ragdoll-god once again and continue trying hopelessly to escape that corner while getting ragdolled for another good portion of your life.  So, is the game good you ask? YES! You should certainly get this game. 11/10 would ragdoll for 17 minutes straight again.

I'm just gonna give this a thumbs up review because it deserves it. For all those short narrative focused games on steam that just bore your pants off, this one nails it. Sure it's a point and click with puzzles, but they aren't crazy or outlandish like in most of the great classics, so even if you're not any good at solving puzzles in point n clicks this one should be doable. All puzzles can be solved by using plane logic (unlike so many other point and clicks), so they are easy enough. I was delighted being able to get through it without needing a guide - something I consider a good thing.  It's fast paced and therefore doesn't get boring. You progress fast and get to see new things. The downside of this is that it's pretty short. It took me 2 and a half hours to complete. However I didn't get stuck much, so you might get some more time out of it.  I'd recommend getting it on sale (like I did :) ) then you most likely won't regret buying it if you enjoy a nice little Sci-Fi Short Story.  On a last note: Some review mentioned bad voice acting. Initially I would have agreed regarding the captain. But as soon as I put 1+1 together the 'bad' voice acting actually added some charm to the character, seeing that he's a Russian or some other Eastern European speaking english in a flat and bored tone. It also added to the whole theme that he's an unlikeable ♥♥♥♥♥♥♥♥. I thought the main characters voice acting on the other hand was decent.

As someone who was previously a fan of causal puzzle games (apart from the pesky F2P model they were built on) and an even bigger fan of anything pornographic, this game imediately spoke to me. Just having a very fluid and stratigic puzzle game without the pesky developers begging you BUY MORE MICROTRANSACTIONS is reason enough to get this game. It's leaps and bounds better than 95% of the crap you find on the Play Store or App Store.  However, what really sets this game apart is the adult side of it. The dating sim, while resembling very little of a traditional dating sim, was simple and very enjoyable even when a role in the hay wasn't on the table as a reward for beating it. I absolutely LOVED the idea for the girls to send you nudes on your phone as you progressed through the game romancing them. MUAH! Briliant! However, I do reccomend you either buy this game directly from the developers or go to their site to download install their nudity patch for Steam because Steam's vanilla version is unfortunately censored.   The girls are all nicely varied with none of them having a clear nice-girl/mean-girl attitude as with many pornographic dating sims. The characters are all well designed both asthetically and personality-wise, and their voice acting is top notch...especially when you finally get them into bed. There's even some secret characters that aren't entirely human if you're into that. No fetish gets left behind! Still, even though its far from the raunchiest game ever made, I found myself often opting to play this than go enjoy some actual porn due to the massively erotic characters and voice acting. Another game of RPG Maker. I really love playing RPG Maker games back in the day (around 2007 where I use RPG Maker 2000/2003 to make a game). Speaking of which, this game battle system is similar to the one I played with the game from RM2K (sorry I forgot the game name). As a someone who used to work with RPG Maker engine, I'm proudly say that this game is a top notch RM & it's free! The game itself actually not a really action RPG to be honest, but that isn't turn base either (my definition of action is a controlable character that can damage your enemy without taking damage to yourself on a basic of the basic without using skills or anything). You can see your enemy and attack it on touch (or interaction). You don't mash button to attack, just push the enemy with the arrow keys (I repeatedly press Z because I think the attack is manual). I think the battle close to Ys I. The difference with Ys I? So many. But in terms of battle system, the enemy reflect damage (without skills) in this game. I haven't dig enough time to go on (just a few minutes as my review here). I found this game interesting, given it is free, no harms there. The artwork is nice too, tutorial is well made on each stage. About the story, I can't comment on that (it's translation from Chinese some said it's weird at some part). The boss battle also interesting (just fought the first slime boss battle, and it's well made).  TL;DR If you like RPG Maker games and semi action & platformer (scripted in this game) this game is for you. If you want a button mashing, beat 'em up, or hack and slash, this isn't one of them.

Gives a tantalising illusion of depth, but is in reality dissapointingly simple. Its like playing an even more depressing version of the Sims; a game which will test your patience and devotion to emotionless stick figure characters, rathen than any skill. Team 17 have made something similar with The Escapists- a game which had such incredible promise in concept, but quickly reveals itself to be a monotonous endurance test.   This is not to say that there is nothing in the game that I liked. The art style is effectively depressing and dim, the overall mechanics are solid- but there just isnt enough meat on the bones on this one to keep you going for any longer than the 20th in game day.  Get Fallout Shelter instead- its much better made and best of all, it's free.

Only buy this game on sale if you are interested, anything else would be a waste of money.  Pretty decent point and click adventure game, but it has many shortcomings. First off, the animations are sluggish and choppy, in general objects seem to move faster than the animations, creating a gliding effect. Many of the enviroments are very well created and thought out, but the interactive objects and puzzles are often obscure or difficult to figure out by logic. If you try to open every container, try to combine every item, you'll have no problem. But there are sections where you'll have to do the same action twice; the first time you'll die, the second time you'll get an essential item. It just doesn't seem well thought out. You'll spend half your time in this game reading various logs and pda's, which is not a good thing when the writing in general is pretty weak, it gets better as you progress but not by much. The game is not very scary, it does have some atmosphere, but the aforementioned faults does not help the general impression. The game feels like a mix of ideas from various series, but it does not bring anything new or memorable to the table.

8.1/10 - A New Player Has Arrived In the Clicker Genre!   This is one of the only clicker games I can honestly say surpasses clicker heroes. The game has everything you love and adds some really interesting elements that take the game to that next level. You do have a band of heroes; but you can only play 10 of them at any 1 time. The position of these heroes determines their bonuses and overall effectiveness, a perfect integration of the strategy element.  The game also has monthly events, a chat box to keep you from playing alone, tons of upgrades, levels and characters to alternate between. There is the ability to passively play and farm gold for upgrades or you can choose to push forward and actively participate in epic battles. I'd personally pick this game over clicker heroes by a hair; but overall it seems to have a little more depth.  It's Free. You should definitely check this one out if you like clicker games!     Accidently Reviewed

UPDATE: Nominated this game for the 'Most Deserving of a Second Chance' award  I'm going to do my part in giving this game a thumbs up since it breaks my heart that two of my favorite recent stealth games have been given mixed reviews on Steam.   Dishonored 2 is a FANTASTIC game. We all know the problems with the performance and I certainly don't approve of how they handled the game's PC launch; however, now that I've installed the beta patch and begun playing it runs buttery smooth on my GTX 970, and the gameplay is absolutely sublime.   The biggest drawback of the game in my opinion is the story, honestly. Perhaps I just haven't gotten far enough in it yet, but there doesn't seem to be as much of a sense of revenge building up in the player throughout the game like in Dishonored 1. In the first game, you initially want revenge on Daud and the Lord Regent, but then you're betrayed and your lust for revenge gets even stronger. In this game, other than wanting revenge on Delilah, there isn't much that drives me.  However, what DOES drive me is the gameplay and level design. Both are expertly crafted, with power usage responsive and allowing for even more creative ways to deal with your opponents than was possible in Dishonored 1, which was already an enormous number. Add that on top of Emily's powers, and this game has a lot of replayability.   If it didn't have the initial performance issues, it would be a 10/10 from me. But alas, I'll have to knock one point off.  9/10 - DO BUY if you're into stealth games or anything like them

This is really fun. I just stand in the park and watch all the people and pet the dogs. I got two around me and then I noticed the guy sitting by the tree with his dog. I couldn't pet his dog until he got up. I was so happy. Then he bad mouthed me and I knocked him out. I was upset that the car almost hit one of them and then lost track of them down the street, but I heard their barking and where two of them were! Now I'm dancing on the sidewalk with a lady who didn't like me at first but is now dancing with me. The two dogs are sitting there watching the traffic go by.   third edit: At some point, I am going to play this game, but for now, it would be hard for me to stop how far I have walked on the streets of San Francisco with two dog companions! I do hope that the city fixes the water main that I exploded right outside the park to see the graphics. It has been two full day/night cycles and it is causing all kinds of commotion in the area. Surely, this will be a candidate for game of the year. The level of immersion is mind-blowing. I had no intention of playing this game the way I am at the start. Alas, I am a huge dog lover and interacting and wandering the streets with two lovely companions is just fabulous! I do hope that there is some way that further interaction and communication with man's best friend in Watch Dogs 2 gets implemented. Kudos for doing our animal friends right this time. Genre : Adventure - Walking Simulator [Warning : No actual walking]  Dinner date is a non-game again. Its supposed to be narrative driven and have a quirky concept. But really all it is is some guy getting stood up on a date in what is presumably his flat, so you push keystrokes to make him eat bread, fiddle his thumbs to waste time, or have his stare at the chopped veggies or the clock. What you do has no real impact and the whole thing is over under ten minutes. It is supposed to be cinematic and immersive because of the first person aspect of it. And maybe for a film student who is unfamiliar with video games this can be a powerful way of introducing the medium. But even when this game came out in 2010 computer gaming had matured past the need for such a limited showcase of its potential and ultimately any excuse made for this game nowadays will outright sound pretentious.

Compared to its predecessor, the gameplay is infinitely better, and pretty much every other area as well. Almost all the weapons are very fun to use and the fact that weapons gradually wear out in use -- making them less reliable to a point where they simply break -- is a neat little touch, though the guns sometimes feel perhaps too flimsy.  The enemy AI feels very poorly designed, every single enemy having the tenacity and willpower you would only see in the most battle-hardened movie heroes. No matter what you do or where you are, they will see you, they will chase you, they will appear in sometimes seemingly endless numbers in incoming vehicles.  The missions are all frustrating, either in their simplicity or when you decide to do the extra missions by your partners, always making the missions end in you having to save them like damsels in distress. The story is rather dull, you're just a random mercenary, doing what you're told to do (rather poorly).  The game has a lot of potential to be an entertaining sandbox game, but it lacks in some critical areas, making it a very mediocre game overall. Only get this game if it's on sale for chickenfeed.

If you're a fan of 4X and Sci-Fi, then you will love this.   Build improvements on your worlds (you start with earth and mars if you play as the humans), use the resources generated from those improvements to build starbases and ships and explore the galaxy, meet aliens, negotiate with them or destroy them!   The map size choices are amazing. Playing a full game with the largest map size will take weeks, or using the small size play through in a few hours if you want a quicker game.   The different races you can choose from really are different. War based, science based, trade based, cultural based. Play it however you want to play. You can even make your own custom race.   Build scouts, contruction ships, colony ships, warships, fighters, carriers. Use the ships already built into the game, customize them, or build your own fleet from scratch. I had a great time optimizing ships for specific tasks (low engines and life support on smaller ships with maxes weapons and shields for planetary defense, or gigantic capital ships with all the bells and whistles for leading a fleet of escorts into battle)   Great game.

My experience in Magicka 2 vs Magicka 1.  I'll start it off with the first time I played Magicka 1. The moment I started to grasp the concept of regular combinations, element combinations, and the special spell combinations, thats when I begun to experiment on so many spells (be it regular spells or the special combination spells, or both), and found it absolutely fun at how I can play the game differently each time and would still enjoy it nonetheless. Thats when I absolutely fell in-love with Magicka. It promised a sort of freedom to your utilization of these spells. And the better you got at utilizing regular combos or utilizing multiple special combination spells, the more satisfying the gameplay was.  People tend to have different opinions and first impressions with this game, so I'm going to state my opinion and hope people would understand that I, as a person writing this review, do not present the entire community who plays magicka.  As I have loved Magicka, I got excited with Magicka 2. It promised something a bit new. Movement while casting a spell, and I thought 'This is amazing! Just with this Magicka would be even more fun!'... I didn't pre-order. I waited about a year because I had other stuff to do. And when I was finally free, I took the chance and bought this game.  The campaign was never spoiled to me. I never knew the story progression. And I certainly didn't look up all the available magicks, whether old or new in Magicka 2.  So I got in here. Bought the game without hesitation, downloaded it, load it up, and played for hours. It was hard! I loved it! I was doing it alone as single player, so naturally, it was difficult progressing. And I just wanted to finish the campaign so that I can make a new game and search all the spells. And then join public games.  -Now this is the part where the disappointment comes in-  As the game ended, and I finally opened up my web browser to search for all the spells. I was shocked to know that so few spells were actually in the game. The old ones that I loved and even the old ones that I rarely used (but still used nonetheless) weren't there anymore. Magicka was more about weapons and dodging, and thinking of clever use with the upgraded regular spells. (Absorption aura is awesome, I'll give them that. but I now have to use more than one element multiple times just so I could be immunite to it. And you can only ever be immune to just 1 element. And that got me sad.)  The madness and chaos of using spells like Thunderstorm, Meteor shower, Vortex, Conflagaration, Phoenix, Invisibility, and even the meager Rain spell... was nowhere to be found. (I used to do Rain spell + Blizzard spell back in Magicka 1. I loved freezing my enemies)  It might be found in the other modes. I don't know, I have not checked. But this wasn't the magicka I know.. The idea of making incredible combos and whatnot were slightly there. But the larger special combination-spell combos (Like Rain + Blizzard) were gone. And I couldn't help but feel, for the first time in the entirety of my Magicka gameplay experience... feel disappointed. (Again, this is just my point of view)  Magicka is much more crazy now with some few added spells and new upgraded-regular elemental spells. But I think the lack of... Chaos... that the old spells gave... made Magicka lose a bit of its charm.  I will still play Magicka 2. It is fun, there is no doubt about it. But I can only wish and hope.. That one day a dlc campaign will be added. And all the old spells added into the game, as well.. A man can dream, right? A man can dream.  I just wanted to get this off my chest. Because it just saddens me on how I miss my old spells.  Well, that's all I've got for you folks. Time to jump back into it... Happy spell-ing! XD (*casts thunderstorm on unsuspecting allies*)

Nihilumbra is both a very interesting and very divided game. It was presented to me as 'Limbo meets Portal,' but in practice, I found it to be like neither of these things. The unfitting dark creatures roaming about the relatively normal environments felt more like Heart of Darkness, and the emphasis on the 'paint mechanic' (there are no portals) gives it more similarities to Tag: The Power of Paint. I was also told that the game made figurative waves on the iOS market, but that is a market so inundated with inane and uninteresting nonsense that the comment meant exactly nothing to me, so I went about my playthrough of Nihilumbra determined not to give it a free pass for standing out from mediocrity. So let's get to it.  I said that I felt that the game was divided. The gameplay is divided into two distinct modes, the story mode, and the void mode. The latter is unlocked after completing the former. The story mode is what you'd expect; it tells the story. The biggest problem people will have with the story mode is the narrator, or more specifically, the narration design. The decision to have a narrator in a game about nihilism is questionable. I'd think silent, empty text would serve the presentation much better, so that's what I went with when I played it. I don't think the voice is necessarily terrible, but I don't think the presence of a voice in this game is necessarily warranted, either. The narration is also triggered by your position on the different screens within the levels, and if you die and return to a checkpoint, the narration will repeat every time, and this can get grating. Fortunately, for the people who will be too lazy to mute the narration, the story mode is almost too easy.  Void mode is the game's answer to that. It's the obligatory, 'you can't spell 'indie' without 'die'' departure from the almost insultingly easy story mode. This section of the game is of a very much 'Oddworldian' difficulty. There is plenty of trial and error to go with the more challenging puzzles. You will die in void mode. I've seen void mode criticized a lot, but again, these are (mostly) iPad gamers we're talking about. Fortunately, you are given a reprieve from the narration, but this can be both good and bad. You are introduced to void mode with the promise of closure to the game's somewhat bitter epilogue (What? Did you expect a good ending in a game about nihilism? DID YOU?). While you do technically get that closure, it's accompanied with absolutely no narration or text, so it's a bit of a dull reward for anyone who played through void mode because they liked the story and wanted that elusive happy ending. The only other thing you get (besides the achievements and accompanying bragging rights) is a 'secret language,' which changes the text of the narration and makes it the complete antithesis of taking itself seriously, so if you thought the story was pretentious garbage, hated it and want some cheap catharsis, you might enjoy replaying story mode for that, but it will just end up leaving a bad taste in everyone else's mouths, especially with the internet memes which have basically already made the 'humor' rather dated.  There is appeal to be had in both the story mode and the void mode, but I think the whole package kind of antagonizes itself. I don't see many people enjoying the whole of the game. Most people will probably heavily prefer one half over the other, and people who prefer the story mode will have to settle with the bitter ending, and people who prefer void mode will have to trudge through the story mode. Whether you decide this game is worth it to you or not will depend on which of those two types of people you decide you are, and whether you feel that you'll only enjoy one half of the game.
