GameGuru is an ok engine for people who don't have any programming experience, however if you think you're going to make a serious game from it think again. While it may change names again in the future, it will always be in beta regardless of what the product states. While the loading times have improved from the original FPSC, there are too many issues tied to it's source code that probably will never be fixed. I'm sure many non-bias people who can think for themselves will agree if you look at it's history. I've actually lived through it since 2005.  Furthermore, this engine like it's predecessors seem to all have the same motive: making some quick money then going mia until people forget about it. FPSC, FPSC x9, FPSC X10, and now GameGuru seem to all follow this pattern since 2005. It is however a great platform for new programmers to learn LUA, and the community is very helpful and knowledgeable. I will say it's the community in my opinion that keeps me coming back, but I regret investing my time and money into a dead end product. Also, the mickey mouse encryption which didn't exist before is ruining everything. If you're trying to protect a developer's intellectual property/content wouldn't it be a good idea to have it exist in the first place? What's the point of protecting something that doesn't exist? It can't even copy texture and basic text files right or even at all for that matter. GameGuru is the only engine where after I build my game, I have to go into the folders and manually check that it copied all the files over correctly. Then I have to manually copy the missing files over, and if an encrypted file was corrupted during the build I have to add and re-encrypt the whole build myself with a third party program.

This is not a 'game' in the traditional sense of the word. Its more of an experience, a window into the life of someone living with depression. I'll tell you right off the bat: this game is not very fun. However its not about being fun. The game exists to provide understanding. I read some of the reviews for this game, and several of them came off as shallow minded and superficial. Phrases similar to like “Was listed under the RPG category but its not an RPG its just a text based game.” I’m not saying that people aren't entitled to their own opinion. I’m saying that a person shouldn’t take this experience as just another free to play game. One must look past the simple categorization and actually see Depression Quest for what it is. The developers never had the intention of making a fun game, nor did they ever want to make money off of it. I believe that this game was created because someone (namly a Zoe Quinn) simply wanted to tell a story and get a thesis across. I came into Depression Quest with low expectations as I do all free to play games. I found that the ambient soundtrack was appropriate for the subject matter. The writing wasn’t anything revolutionary, but it told a narrative that I saw to the end. The story had its moments where I related to the characters and felt real emotions like pity. I was told that the game has 5 endings depending on the choices you make. I only played through once to in order to not dilute my experience and impression of the game. Like I said before this game was not fun and it was never meant to be. Depression is a real world issue and it affects real people every day of their lives. Simply creating a triple a Sims-esque simulation game would possibly serve to detract from the subject matter. Bottom line: If you go into this game with a sense of superficiality or immaturity you are going to leave feeling like you wasted your time. However it you read into it and get invested the same way you would any other form of media, the experience will resonate. I’m not saying it will affect everyone like it has me and many others, but it will one some level.  If you do have depression. Seek help. It is out there.

This game is surprisingly emotive. Your team are civilians in general, they experience moral qualms which affect their state of mind. Killing others to survive is abhorrent to them, as are other criminal activities such as theft. But sometimes bad choices or misfortune means theft is the only way to recover. Strangely, there doesn't seem to be any moral repercussions when killing involves a criminal. Time is both your friend and enemy as each day highlights the scarcity of resources yet brings the war closer to its end. Sometimes events help to offset the bleak surroundings as people help one another. The next door neighbor may have vegetables they would share. You may find other people who seek to join your ragtag band. Will you accept them into your home or harbor what little resources you possess against the hope that you make it through the war? The first few games are more challenging due to a lack of information on the tech tree within the game. After some experience, play becomes more manageable as such other events such as weather and misfortune start to creep in. Its not a cheery game, but it does provide somber insight.

Advantages :  - An interesting reflection on transhumanism - The atmosphere midway between Bioshock and Amnesia - Rarely takes the player's hand, without being too difficult - Rhythm mastered quickly the introduction - No jumpscare  Drawbacks :  - Very classical in form - Worries optimization - Slow start  Summary : SOMA is an interesting reflection on transhumanism that leaves the player think intelligently about events without taking her hand. SOMA is not perfect but it is where we expect it, that's the point.  16/20

Frictional are fast becoming my favourite developer (along with CD Projekt RED) because they really look to improve with every game, and they have truly succeeded here. Penumbra was about immersion, Amnesia was about fear, Machine For Pigs was about story, and they have combined all of the elements with beautiful/horrifying graphical elements to create an amazing experience. 2/10. This is one of the first games I bought on Steam, probably around 2009 or 2010. It had problems with randomly crashing in the middle of a battle and erasing all progress. When I played it again in 2014, it still had problems with random crashes in single player campaign mode. The single overriding factor in rating a game is that the game can be played. If the game can't be played, it's a brick. For the twenty or so minutes that the game is available to play, it is fun. Kind of repetitive, and annoying to try to find the target you're supposed to kill in order to win a round, but fun nonetheless. There was an expansion pack called Scorched Earth that used to be available. It's no longer available in the Steam store or for purchase from anywhere else, which means that content is permanently inaccessible to people who didn't buy it when it was available.

app_name: Game of Thrones - A Telltale Games Series
review_text: Game of Thrones is a game that had so much potential, yet somewhat failed to live up to expectations. Now don't get me wrong, the game is good. But choices mean little to nothing and not much is resolved in the finale. Mainly they used the popularity of the game as an excuse to not finish the story and instead announcing Season 2, which hopefully will fill the numorous plot holes that was left behind. But it still has the Telltale charm...you give a crap about the characters. The illusion of choice is just that-an illusion-but it is still fun nonetheless.  Pros: -Characters/Character Arcs -It genuinely hurts to be a jerk -Choices can be extremely difficult, making them realistic -Intriguing plot line -Manages to maintain flow throughout multiple character switches -Telltale still goes for the feels -Game. Of. Thrones.  Cons: -Episode 6 -Little choices matter -Graphically unsound  Final Score: 7/10 - Good

The combat, story and activities are not good enough to warrent it being this long. -The parkour system often makes you jump in strange directions, often to your death. -There are at least 50 towers to climb, needed to reveal the details of the map. Turmoil  is a lot of fun!  While there is nothing super ambitious about this title, what it sets out to do - it does quite well. The game mechanic creates an interesting strategy/puzzle aspect that can be quite addicting.   Essentially you are trying to turn profits by drilling for oil.  Your success is largely dependent on things such as what strategy you choose to employ to find oil and extract it out of the ground. You have options to upgrade your equipment and abilities through spending your hard-earned money. This is definately a time-based game where you will drill for oil for a set amount of time, which obviously puts more emphasis on strategy.  What I really liked about  Turmoil  is that it is pretty laid-back. I sat on the couch and only played with the mouse. My wife even got interested, which is rare. Nothing is too complex here - in fact this would probably do well on mobile. I hate mobile games (as many of you know), but this has just enough complexity to transcend that genre.   Pros:  +Music is fitting, and quite nice. +Game mechanic is simple to learn, yet takes a bit of time to master. +Quite addicting. +Price-point is definately spot-on for this title. I have no problems with the pricing.   Cons:  -This would be  perfect  for local hot-seat versus mode. Shouldn't be terribly difficult to implement (I'm not talking online mode, mind you). -From looking at other reviews, mileage seems to top out somewhere between 10 and 20 hours (closer to 10). I'm fine with this, and think its appropriate, but just know this isn't going to be infinitely replayable IMHO. -Mouse cursor needs to be darker or have more contrast (or at least have the option). This should be an easy fix.   Yeah, definately a fun title to spend the weekend with.  I'd recommend this at full price if it looks like your thang.

Total War: Warhammer might be the best total war game I have ever played.  Every single faction is unique, they all play differently, they have character and quirks that mean every one of them is a new experience.  Regarding not being able to conquer the whole map, it makes sense from an in-universe perspective, the Empire would have no idea how to run a Dwarf hold and Chaos don't want or need cities anyway.  The magic is fun and well balanced once you understand what the different spells are for, what it is do and why they do it.  As for DLC, as long as they keep being awesome I'll keep buying them, if someone wants the content but thinks they're too pricey then just wait for a sale. The game is perfectly playable without dlc and it does get Free-LC as well, like Wurrzag with his own Orc faction, a new lore of magic and it's accompanying empire wizard or the upcoming Bretonnia, so it's not like all extra content is completely cut off.  All in all Total War: Warhammer is a blast to play, and will probably keep me and a lot of others playing it for a long time.

PLEASE READ: Due to the PC version lacking uplay (which seemed like a godsend at first), you cannot get certain items within the game that you can get on console versions. Effectively, that means the PC version has less content.  If you're still interested in getting the game on PC or just want to know if it's worth it at all, read on.  Overall this is in every way an improvement over the last game. It feels like a proper sequel; it's bigger, better, and still maintains what the original game was all about.  There are a few minor things that I consider problematic - the major one is listed above. That is the game's biggest flaw, that its versions are not consistent across platforms. This is a big deal in 2016. It is, however, possible to get all the achievements in the game and the devs did at least say they'll look into not making the content unavailable on PC appear in the trading cards anymore which was originally what led to a lot of people's confusion about 'missing content.'  Other problems involve some stutter that just barely avoids qualifying as lag. It can screw you up on challenges but I did still manage to complete them all and I am not a very skilled gamer. I am running a fairly high-end PC though and I am unsure how it would perform on lower-end machines.  And finally, there is the problem that even if the challenges aren't...well, always challenging, I personally liked the old system of finding things in the world and tossing them into scanners for completionist's sake, it was usually less stressful. I suppose the main issue I have with the challenges though is, the whole goal is to jump through hoops, literally. It's pretty easy to lose track of the hoops you're supposed to jump through and can sometimes make you repeat a challenge. I think having either some kind of dotted line to guide you or having a counter for how many hoops remain on each challenge would be helpful in those cases since you are timed during them.  The good points of the game are, not only can you scan plants just by grabbing them and pressing X (or square if you're using a PS4 controller) but now they also actually do something. You can plant them anywhere you want on the map and have them grow a new plant. It's a pretty good way to cheese challenges or just goof around when you're bored.  The newly-added POD view is also a great new feature, especially since you can be easily disoriented in this large new world you have to explore. It also comes with a waypoint system which I used constantly to find my way around.  All the familiar things are back, you still use tele-routers to get around, you still unlock new suits for abilities, and you still collect crystals to become super OP. It's overall very rewarding now and the crystal radar has even been improved to extend its range when you only have a few crystals left so you aren't left stumped as often.  I absolutely recommend this on consoles and do recommend it on PC if you don't mind that you are missing out on a few things. There is likely a video online that'll show you all the differences if you're interested.

I HAVE A JOB SO I CANNOT FULLY COMPLETE THIS REVIEW BUT I HAVE A TIP FOR PEOPLE WITH FPS PROBLEMS:  When I booted up the game I was having fps problems in the first dungeon, like 10-15 fps and it wasn't smooth at all. I'm embarrassed to admit it and was suprised to find out the problem was because my computer decided to run the game with my integrated card instead of my GTX 960m. Needless to say I swaped the default around, and would recommend disabling the integrated even and I got a smooth 60 fps on highest settings. Now this might not be your problem, but remember to do things like check drivers, maybe even shut down background programs and use something like MSI afterburner to see where your problems are. The game might have legitamate optimization problems, but I'm getting none of it with that change I mentioned. Make sure everything is right, and if you have lower end specs you might have to suck it up and go low quality, which still doesn't look too bad if I'm being honest.  Ok the boring part out of the way, as I mentioned I want to review this game in full but right now I just want to point out some things I have already been pleased with.  Art: Truly Tsunako's best works are in this game, neps are neppier and it is adorable. I have an artbook from Tsunako already and have gotten a sneak peak at the art already, and it's simply fantastic. Also watching her progression getting better and better as the series has evolved is quite a fun thing to observe.  Music: I have already listened to a few tracks and I really am liking them all so far, the atmosphere in the very early game already feels to be a pretty drastic improvement over previous games.  Neps: The most ♥♥♥♥ing holy and purest life form, so amazing it cannot be possible in real life and leave your friends (which I don't have anymore) and family dissapointed in your life choices. But that doesn't matter when you have Nepu and friends.  That is all I have so far, but message me if you are having optimization problems. I don't know much at all about anything but maybe I can be of some help.  But for now I leave you with a KANGAROO NO YOU NI!!!  (play it in Japanese if you are reel weeb hours)

Each unit uses up a portion of your food while buildings do not. - New music for the game  Negatives: - While some parts may be considered a reskin the game presents quite a few different ways to play compared to 8bit armies.  - minor AI improvements needed - 'Vehicle crate' *gets horseman* (Modern terms for crates) -'Aircraft crate' *gets dragons* - unit pricing may need some tweaks so you can't make a massive army without plenty of extra cash  This will be heavily updated over time since I have not yet played the a whole lot. I also would like to share that the developers from petroglyph are always open to talking and answering questions through steam and discord.

review_text: If you like Ghost Recon, chances are - you will not recognize it in this one. It's nothing like we had until the Advanced Warfighter 2, and I would say it's the worst Ghost Recon ever.  Another thing that's different from previous parts - Future Soldier was made in Kiev, and so it's full of anti-Russian BS. If your language is Russian, you will enjoy totally awkward dialogues and accents, as well as a lot of foul language in written form.  Previous parts of GR, especially the AW2, are made for endless evenings full of fun. This one - is a one-timer. I went through it to forget. Methinks for many it will be the first and the last GR.

If that's your cup of tea, get this game. Get this game.

Normally I don't do reviews but I felt that w/ all the negative reviews since release I should do so. I have 528.5 hours in this game, so I can safely say that I've been through a few releases up to version 1.0. I can't speak for the bugs folks mention, as I have never run into any that were game-breaking myself, but I can say for the folks complaining about lack of tutorial is that it's difficult to write a tutorial for a sandbox game that leaves the details completely up to you. I can recommend you watch youtube videos, there are some excellent let's plays that will teach you the basics. You're also able to turn off enemies if you want to go through the learning steps a few times.  Some cons though: Now let's be fair, this doesn't have the complexity of Dwarf Fortress, I don't know if that's possible for any game right just because of what I know about DF. But it doesn't say that it's trying to be a DF clone so that's OK. One other thing is I hope the developer continues to develop the game and add new features, such as more workshops, more armors, more weapons, more traps, etc...right now the game has JUST enough material to justify $15, but I can only say that because I've played it. If I did not play it maybe I could not be convinced to pay that amount, I don't know.  All in all I DO recommend this game, if for nothing else but a step towards Dwarf Fortress.

Evolution of JRPG from point of view of US is main concept of this game. The concept is very interesting, but this game failed to realization. Why did this game mix FF and Zelda? They are another species, it's like confusing with the dog and cat. Why did this game implement 'HD-UI' before 'Pre-rendering graphics'? Why did this game start by monochrome graphics? First zelda is colored. Why did this game take Diablo-style? 'Hack & Slash' style like Diablo is not typical style of JRPG. If this game's purpose is follow all RPG, this game lose Ultima/Wizardry/Dungeon Master/Rogue/Might and Magic/And more.  But, the above dissatisfaction is trivial. The biggest problem of this game is 'boring'. In FF-like part, ATB is very simple. You can win the enermy just to select 'Atack' and 'Heal'. (MP is no limit.) Strategy has been lacking this game. In addition it, In this game, frequency to encounter the enemy is very high! Zelda-like part is just deterioration version of zelda. I got flustrated by slow speed of walking. Boy does this bring back memories. Many times over. I owned this game on PC back in the day when it was called, 'Out Of This World', I bought it again when it came out for the SNeS, I bought it again on GoG, and then again on Steam. I play it a few times a year.   It's not a long game, but it's unbelievably fantastic game. The graphics are just a fantastic art form. Both the original and the 'updated' looks. The score is perfect for what it delivers, loneliness, urgency, drive, and finality. The gameplay is great overall. The controls are what you would expect for a 1990's game, a bit clunky, but it does fit this game.   I definitely recommend this game to anyone interested in not only a piece of gaming history, but just on the merit that it is really a fantastic game for it's time, and even still now.  Eric, hats off to you for a fantastic game!

This is a better version of Audiosurf. The song selection now allows for SoundCloud songs to be played directly from the client, along with anything you have on your computer. Unlike the original Audiosurf, it is cross-platform friendly and runs well on Linux, even on slower integrated cards like Intel HD Graphics 4000.   The gameplay is nearly identical, except now the track can do barrel rolls. There is also extensive support for leaderboards and user-generated content.  Worth picking up for newcomers or fans of the original that want a fresh coat of paint.

I would 100% recommend this game to anyone that enjoys story rich games. It deals with issues of racism in parts, and deals with corrupt leadership in others. The storyline was well put together, and immersive. As soon as I finished my first playthrough, I turned right around to play it again. And after this one, I'll probably play at least twice more. The decisions you make feel like they really matter, and the relationship development is interesting and fun to watch, as well. Personally, I didn't play the games before it (The Longest Journey, Dreamfall: The Longest Journey) and still found the story to be wonderful and fun. The puzzles can be a bit conveluted, but it's mostly a case of finally understanding the puzzle and then thinking, 'Well, maybe I'm just stupid.' because you didn't notice something. I kept mentioning to a friend that I was 'lost' and 'confused' on where to go in a couple different areas toward the beginning, and they mentioned that it made the game sound bad. By all means, it wasn't a bad game, just a new area I hadn't explored that confused the everloving heck out of me. Even then, I still loved it. Like I said, I'd recommend it to anyone.

Amazing game! It is very relaistic with big and accurate maps. There are different scenarios each are actual battles from WW1. If you like call of duty or battlefield this is NOT the game for you. This game requires patience and you have to be prepared to die a lot! The graphics look amazing on ultra settings. It is also a very balanced game which requires attacking and defending (you will die a lot in attacking but get a lot of kills while defending). If you like fast paced games again this is NOT the game for you as you cannot just run out your trench and expect to get kills. It requires thinking and planning ahead as to where you will go, which leads onto my next talk of map design. There are plenty of places to take cover on the game like craters and grass. There is also barbed wire which will kill you. There is only one problem with this game and that is that there is not enough players on the battlefield so if you like historic warfare then this is the game for you!

review_text: This game is wicked fun. Instead of shooting zombies... er, mutants... with the mouse you type words. This game has so many problems, not sure where to even start. Both technical and design.  A glaring technical problem: the frame rate is locked at 30 fps. This is absolutely unacceptable in 20-freaking-16 on PC. The screen follows your character as you walk around, constantly scrolling to keep you near the center. Why does this have to scroll at 30 FPS? The animations are fine to be 10 fps or whatever, because they are hand drawn and look cool. But why does the screen have to clunk around at 30 fps? After 10 seconds it's downright nauseating and it never gets better. It's annoying every single minute.  The physics are really bad. Enemies getting stuck out in empty areas beyond your reach, your character getting stuck on crystals randomly. 'Trees' where sometimes you can walk through them and other ones you can't, with no visible difference between them. There are these crystal spike traps that damage you when you walk on them. About 20% of the time my character would get stuck in the middle of them while trying to 'warp' past them and get damaged or killed instead. I couldn't figure out if it was something I was doing, so I spent about 5 minutes and 20 lives trying to figure it out. Never could detect a pattern or cause. But I did find that if you walk halfway into them, and then warp, you don't get stuck as much, maybe only 5% of the time.   There is no consistency to the auto-save. Sometimes it saves each time you enter a screen. Sometimes it doesn't. One time I died and I had all the items I had before dying. One time I died and it went back 20+ minutes and hadn't saved anything.  The game play is excrutiatingly boring for two reasons:  1) You start with a sword and quickly get a gun. There is a grand total of 2 moves: swing your sword and shoot your gun. That means after you swing your sword a couple times, then you get to shoot the gun a couple of times. Oh and then swing your sword some more.   2) 99% of enemies drop nothing, and you gain absolutely nothing by killing. They serve no other purpose than to put a barrier between you and the things you want to do, like explore the game you just bought or fight a cool boss. The same enemies are in the same place and respawn every time you die. That means you get to enjoy repeating the same fight against the same enemies in the same places over and over again! Yay!  If this game was trying to be a 2D Dark Souls, it failed miserably.

My expectations were set extremely high for this game because of The Walking Dead season 1, Season 1 was my game of the year and easily makes it into my current top five games of all time.  Telltale followed up their masterpiece with another work of art, a rich story driven game, beautiful visuals and once again; near impossible decisions.  The walking dead season 2 is set just a little while after season 1, you play as Clementine in this survival story.   Clementines character is really well developed and right from the get go you will feel strongly attached, especially after the events of season 1.  This game is a must buy for anyone that has played season 1 and if you haven't played that then you should definitely add it to your wishlist.  Overall   Pros +Amazing story writing +Great voice acting +Thrilling and exciting +Beautiful visuals  Cons -Episodes still seem rather short

As with all iOS games sold to the PC, the lack of actual complexity becomes a glaring issue quick. For what its worth, the game couldn't have been named more aptly. Its a good breach and clear simulator, but has barely any game worth mentioning outside of it. Which is a pity, for the combat is fun and functional, without ever feeling too micromanaging which is often a problem with turn based squad tactics.   If devs would actually take a game like this, and flesh it out with an actual economy, politics, base-management, an overall objective or even an open-world perhaps. Basically anything resembling a game outside of the combat simulator, this would actually become a quite good. As with pretty much all games you can play with your thumb on a tablet, this is a waste for PC. Do not buy at full price.

Metroidvania with a really good balance between combat and platforming. There's a little bit of backtracking here and there but mostly for items and not actually progressing the main quest. The story, characters and script are original but the dialogue (especially among your party characters) can be hit or miss at times. The music score is good. It reminds me of the music in Super Metroid, mostly serving as background environment music, so you'll either notice it or you won't. (Except for the warp rooms. You'll always notice the music there.)  There's little reason to play this game a second time though, unless you're hunting for achievements. And if you're hunting for achievements, keep in mind there is an achievement that requires you to beat the game within a time limit of 2 hours.

Unfortunately, I cannot recommend this game, even if it does use the Steam additions for games effectively. I got this game for free from voting on Greenlight, but it was 5 months wasted of waiting to see how this turned out. To be honest, I would preferably give this a 'decent' option if the Steam Reviews had those, because it wasn't as awful as others I've played, like  Pixel Piracy.    The visuals were nice at first, but they got more boring and had the same old cartoony look most platformers I've seen have. Nothing too eye popping for me, but it isn't eye meltingly horrible as other games. Not much to say on the visuals other than it seems to have the same old, cartoony, bland look to it.  The gameplay was actually enjoyable at first, but it really doesn't bring much to the table here. It was solid, the puzzles actually require thinking, and the level maker was pretty fun to use. However, the earlier levels have some kind of huge difficulty spike in them. Yes, I just said they actually require thinking, but it's better if they actualy make them a bit more simple at first, and then have the difficulty go up and up. The difficulty spikes were not fit for a game like this to me, unfortunately. Also, I can't stand hitting Enter every single time I want to restart a level. Why not just use the LMB? It makes life a lot easier, because using Enter just seems like a weird way to do things. In it's defense though,  Mass Effect 2 was similar to the style RRR had, and that execution turned out to be not much better.  Not much to say, other than the annoying abuse of Enter and the fact nothing was really added to RRR.  The story, well, there isn't really much of one. You save robots for a vague reason. It's straightforward for the most part, but maybe they could've explained the robots' story some more? Pretty vague, but at least it isn't nonexistent.  The audio is probably the best thing to the game, shockingly. I began playing my games with headphones recently to add on to my experiences, and I realized RRR has a pretty impressive main menu song, and the songs that play in the levels sound great, too. I highly praise this game's music. The sound effects also sound nice, but at times, they sound way too loud.  The replayability is hard to determine at the moment (look at my hours) but it does have a level editor, and you can also get levels made by the community in the game's workshop, so that's a good (and rare) thing in a game like this. The high scores seem meaningless, but I was never much for high scores anyway.  A major pro to this game is that it implements the Steam additions effectively. It's one of the main reasons this game gets the 'meh' and not the 'bad' score. Also, the dev seems to be very generous because they're doing it with other games, too. They're also dedicated to the game, even when it isn't in Early Access. Kudos to them for being dedicated.  In the end, RRR does NOT suck, even with it's plenty of flaws. However, it falls short of being a good, fun, cute game and has too many flaws, and rely heavily on some pretty major things I've only seen few games do. In the end, you could get the game, but you won't have as much of an experience as other puzzle games out there.  Graphics- 6.5/10 Gameplay- 6.5/10 Story- 4.5/10 Audio- 9.0/10 Replayability- 7/10  .:6.2/10:. +Great devs +Awesome music +Solid controls +Uses Steam additions effectively -Relies too much on the Enter key -Massive difficulty spikes on some levels -Annoying and loud sound effects -Bland visuals -Has room for a stronger story (Not surprised, but seriously could've had one)

It doens't offer enough new content on its own to replace CIV 5 and without the ability to mod it myself and play others' mods, it really wasn't worth the purchase to me.  That being said, it's certainly not a bad game and was released in a more finished and interesting state than most civ games are. If the lack of workshop content isn't a big deal to you, go ahead and pick it up. I had fun for a few games and ended up going back to CIV 5 and 4 again. If you liked the original or the remake, then you will like this version. The game has options for the original tank controls, or position oriented control scheme. Not much has changed, except a graphic overhaul and a bump up to 1080p resolution, from the GameCube version. My only gripe is that you cannot use DSR and crank the resolution up, like with most newer releases. The game is still rewarding and challenging, and now there are three difficulty settings. I picked what would equate to normal, and it is still challanging enough to feel like a true survival horror game as it is meat to be. Tight corridors and tense camera angles will have you sweating. From a technical standpoint the game runs without many issues, your gun will clip through walls if you are really close and aiming that direction, and a few minor bugs here and there. However they are not enough to take you out of what is otherwise a compeling and wonderfull puzzle filled horror adventure.  9/10 pros -tense action -stays true to survival horror of the original -graphics are pretty nice -difficult  cons -minor technical bugs -no DSR options -cannot skip the door opening sequence, even if you have been through the same door 5 times

Dirty Bomb: It's a good concept, bridging the gap between super casual TF2 and super competetive CS:GO, so it's got that at least. Gameplay is smooth, the wall hopping mechanic is nice (if a little useless), and the characters are all colourfully voiced, and varied enough visually to be pleasing, although some seem to be very different in functionality, although I've probably not played enough for the nuances to come out. The teamplay focus works perfectly, the exp/credit incentive is enough to have teams working together (if not communicating well) and this makes for interesting and balanced firefights and skirmishes withing the games A few problems though: The guns feel floaty and insubstansial, kills and hits don't feel 'Meaty' enough, for want of a better word, and this makes kills somewhat less satisfying that they should be. (The melee attack is rubbish, there's almost no point using it apart from as a finisher for downed players) The maps and time limits seem geared heavily against the Defending side for the objective game mode, as some defenders hold on for as lost as 10+ minutes, only to lose from there, this is neither a rewarding feeling for attackers, nor a a fair fight for the defenders, as they have to play consistantly far better than the attackers, as one 'wipe' will usually result in an objective loss. Overall: The team focus is nice, the guns are weightless, and the Mercs are all cool as hell Verdict? Play it if you're a fan of less conventional shooters, it's worth a try, and the final release will probably be slick as hell.

Unlike in some word games where the lenght of words is really important (Letter Quest), or games that are really difficult if you're not a native speaker (looking at you Lexica), this game hits the right spot between difficulty and fun.  Words lenght can be important if you want to get 3 stars in every levels, but even with a couple of short words you can still get the 3 stars rating. The goal of this game is to bring the characters to the bottom of the screen by using the letters on the grid, many levels aren't timed but gives you a limit of words to use which allows you to take your time and find better words instead of rushing and just doing 3 letters words.  So if like me english isn't your first language and you are hesitating to rather buy the game or not, let me tell you that as long as you have an average vocabulary this game is fun and not too difficult nor too easy for people like us :)  Have a great day.

app_name: The Metronomicon: Slay The Dance Floor
review_text: A cute little silly unique rhythm game with RPG elements and good humor.   This game will force you to learn to multitask with your brain on a fairly high level. There are three difficulty options that change the note density with the hardest not being too bad so the game is accessible to an average playerbase. For anyone who isn't amazing at rhythm games it presents a fun challenge with coordination and it additionally has RPG elements that require a more open-ended classic strategic element commonly seen in RPGs. It takes a mastery of the physical and mental parts of the game to challenge the leaderboards but it is not necessary to be a master to progress in the game.  If you know this is something that interests you then you will probably like it. If you aren't sure be wary because the gameplay is what it is for hours on end song after song. But I recommend it for what it is if it is your thing.

Fun platformer that rapidly progresses from 'this doesn't seem so hard' to 'WHAT IS THIS AND WHAT IS MY LIFE?!'. There are certain companies in the gaming world that dominate a specific genre of games. In TellTale's case, it would be point-and-click adventure games. These people know how to make a fantastic adventure game with wonderfully designed puzzes and great story execution.  SBCG4AP is based off Homestar Runner, a long running and very entertaining flash cartoon website with a fantastic cast of diverse and easily recognizabe characters. As such, being a fan of Homestar Runner is definitely reccommended for full enjoyment of this game, otherwise you'll probably have no idea what's going on, and in the case of a few of the puzzles where a bit of knowledge about the characters really helps, you might find yourself having a lot of issues.  As for the puzzles themselves, they seem vary in difficulty from episode to episode. I feel like episode 4 is the easiest, but I don't really know which episode I should consider the hardest. Puzzles in just one episode can also have varied difficulty, meaning they don't just increase in difficulty as the episode goes on. And since this is a Point-and-Click Adventure, that of course means that getting stuck will probably lead to the age old method of trying everything with everything. However, you can turn hints on if you have too much trouble, or if you want to hear additional funny dialogue, whichever works.  The control is as simple as you would expect in a game like this, point to where you want to go, and click to move there. Hold the mouse to move faster, and while I know you can run, I'm not entirely sure how to get it to work, I think you double-click, but it doesn't always work. Moving to where you want to go can be a bit finicky though when you're on the grassy roundabout for a majority of the game's locations (Bubs' Concession Stand, The Stick, Strong Badia, The Cool Car, The Whale and the like). Your inventory is easy to find and navigate and talking to the other inhabitants of Free Country, USA is as simple as clicking on them and choosing an image from a little chat bubble for your response. Saving your game is easy and in general the game is very streamlined to make playing it simpler and removing a lot of needless menu navigation.  In terms of the graphics, voice acting and sound, they're all pretty darn good. The character models look like the characters' 2D flash puppets were just taken and rendered into 3D. All of the locations that Homestar Runner fans recognize are here and accounted for, and there are even other references like the Geddup Noise, that painting of a demon holding a torch (why is he holding that torch?), and there's even a minigame in one episode where you can make a Teen Girl Squad comic. The voices are done by The Brothers Chaps and Missy themselves, and they deliver the characters just as they would in the cartoons. And the music is perfectly like the cartoons, and some are just taken directly from them (One for example is specifically taken from 'Strong Bad is in Jail Cartoon'). Essentially, they feel like you're playing an hour or so long Homestar Runner cartoon, and it's a joy from beginning to end.  Overall, a fantastic buy for Point-and-Click enthusiasts, especially those who are fans of Homestar Runner, but you might be able to enjoy it just fine even if you aren't a fan.

What can be said about the game Alan Wake, for starters it's pretty amazing.... I would highly recommend if you don't own the game you should stop reading and immediately go pick it up.. Need a little more convincing where here goes..  You find yourself playing the mysterious and successful author Alan Wake. Finding himself with a major case of writers block, Alan and his wife Alice decide to take a trip to Bright Falls, hoping the distraction of a 2 week vacation in a beautiful setting will help shake out of the cobwebs. What could go wrong, a beautiful woman, a beautiful scenic cabin and 2 weeks of silence..   The game was originally supposed to be an open world game, and what a game it would have been. Unfortunately while the open world portion of the game was cut, the parts that remain are fairly large although kind of straight forward. Yet exploration by the player is rewarded with additional supplies or collectibles..   The combat.... it's unique and interesting, although can be a bit repetitive as only a few different enemy types exist in the game.. The major way this game excels where other 3rd person horror games have failed is with dodging... and trust me you'll be doing alot of dodging.. Outside of that it's fairly straight forward, point your flashlight at enemy, break the darkness shield and then fire away with one of your weapons (Handgun, Shotgun, Flare gun,) it's easier than it sounds although at higher difficulty levels the limited supplies will more than likely see you running to the next check point..  If you're a fan of horror games, mystery movies, 3rd person games or just games in general you will probably like Alan Wake, the only real downside of this game would have to be the lack of enemy diversity. Although the story more than makes up for the short comings of the enemy diversity.

I've recently tried several games that fit the description of 'first-person interactive narration', which I feel is an adequate categorization for this game. There aren't puzzles to be solved, necessarily, but there are multiple (and often beautiful) landscapes through which the narrator tells his tale. The game isn't very long, and I didn't find the story to be as compelling as those in games like Gone Home or The Stanley Parable, but I was drawn to finish it and found the scenes to be quite spooky. The story itself unfolds slowly, and you're not sure when the next narration is coming. As an audio-visual experience, Dear Esther was compelling, but it wasn't really a game, and if I'm asked to rank similar titles in terms of story, Dear Esther would fall below Gone Home, The Stanley Parable, and To The Moon, the latter being my clear favorite, if graphics aren't a major part of your rubric for scoring these kinds of titles. It was enjoyable but I never felt wholly satisfied with the purchase. I recommend it as an experience but not as a game, and would recommend it at 5 dollars or less only. I won't comment on the story itself, as that's up to you to experience...

app_name: Dark Messiah of Might & Magic Single Player
review_text: Preliminary Review As I've only put in one 3 hour session of this game, this is simply a preliminary review of my experience so far.  Pros: - Mechanics: The game mechanics are quite interesting. Some might say 'ahead of it's time' others might say 'just plain wierd.' It isn't like a traditional RPG in a lot of ways, with no clear stats to manage. But there is gear and skills to choose from, and different ways to approach the games problems. All of those are quite solid, although the game doesn't do a very good job of telegraphing any of it, or explaining it. The underlying cake, while a bit old, is still reasonably tasty. - Atmosphere: While visually dated, the tone of the game is fairly robust, with consistent motifs playing through with nods to old Might and Magic storylines. What this boils down to is the cream of the cake, which while basic, is still ok.   Cons: - Graphics: Visually the game has not aged well. Compared to Half-Life 2 which also used the Source Engine, DM looks craptacular.   - Difficulty curve: The game throws you to the wolves quite quickly. Given the ways that the combat, stealth and magic are unusual, this learning curve can be quite sharp as not only is the player having to learn new systems for a game, they have to learn new systems FOR THE GENRE. Comparisons abound between DM and Skyrim, and while they are significantly different, what has made Skyrim remarkably successful is it's approachability - something that DM clearly lacks. On the flipside, the combat and mechanics as mentioned above do allow for a deeper experience than Skyrim, but it does require more work from the players part in order to get the full enjoyment from that experience.   Summary  If you're looking for a something quite different, and don't mind picking up something that is a little dated, give Dark Messiah a go.

Payday 2 is a action-packed multiplayer co-op shooter.  The gameplay is unique in that there are many different ways to approach each heist. You can go in guns blazing, try to sneak in and out without anyone seeing you, keep everyone down so the police don't ever hear about it; Or even gun down everyone to leave no witnesses, if you so desire. The gameplay creates a genuine adrenaline rush, as you can never be sure you're going to make it out alive, which pushes the game's heavy risk/reward aspects (You leave with what you have, or risk it all for a chance at more loot.). As well as this, each heist is randomized, creating a different experience each time.  Overall, I would strongly recommend the game to anybody.

LEGO with guns.  You might take one look at Ace of Spades and think 'Minecraft Clone!' before moving on to the next game. If you do, you'll be missing out on a fun title that takes pieces of multiple genres and snaps them together like children's building blocks.   At it's core, Ace of Spades is a competent shooter with most of the staples you'd expect today: classes, weapon selection (albeit limited) and large, open maps. What differentiates it is the fact that everything on the map can be destroyed. I mean it - everything. I'm not talking pre-designed collapse in a building from a tank shell or artillery strike, but rather block-by-block devastation. Add to this the fact that all classes are able to place blocks (or pre-fab structures) down on the map at will, and you'll see the landscape of a round change completely in only ten or so minutes. Forts are built and destroyed. Snipers erect networks of towers that fall to crafty engineers. Vast, sky-bridging platforms extend from one base to another, only to be eliminated by a stray rocket that plunges six men to their doom. Opposition too stiff on the front lines? Mine out a tunnel into their base like a medieval sapper and hit those pesky snipers from behind.  Ace of Spades offers several game types, although only two of them are consistently played on populated servers. Team Deathmatch needs no explanation. The second is Zombie mode. In this, the start of the round sees one or two random players become the zombie. They must hunt down the other players using blips on their minimap and claw them to death, thus creating a new zombie. Non-zombified players will erect vast defenses in the vain hope of staying alive for a few extra minutes. Overall, quite a lot of fun. I wish more of the modes were played because some of them are really good, but it's still a lot of fun and worth the $15.  Server load is low at this point, and was even when I first got the game in 2013. You might find 2-3 full or semi-full servers in your region depending on time of day, but it's usually enough.  To sum up,  Pros:  - First Person Shooter goodness on maps that can be completely destroyed by the players. Watch as landscapes rise and fall all through human effort. Dig secret passages into enemy bases, destroy their carefully-built structures and be a general nuisance. - A mild building element allows players to construct temporary defenses to aid in their skirmishes, leading to interesting scenarios as players deal with what are essentially castle sieges. - Fun, fast-paced action combined with sneaky digging and relaxed building. The game rewards you for doing basically anything that you'd like to do. It is, after all, a LEGO set with guns.  - Pretty much bug-free. I've had one or two lock-ups in nearly 40 hours of play.  - Casual-friendly. Great for short-period time-killing or for longer stretches.   Cons:  - Snipers. Every single round sees 75% of the players taking the role of sniper. More variety would be nice, especially given the fact that other classes can manipulate the map in far more interesting ways. - Autobalance issues. Good luck joining a round with a friend and getting on the same team. Autobalance kicks in even during the opening moments when players are loading between maps, and it isn't possible to change teams (in spite of a change teams button) unless you quit and re-join. On the other hand, if half a team drops during a match the game won't autobalance to adjust for that, so you can sometimes see 10 v 5 scenarios.  - Liberal kick-votes. It seems in most rounds that half a dozen players or more will be the target of an unexplained, undefended kick vote. Usually for 'Hacking,' even though I have yet to see a hacker in many dozens of rounds. In spite of the map being easily destructible (and rebuildable, like a LEGO set), many players will take offense if someone so much as damages a block they've placed down with a stray bullet. Erroneous 'Griefing' kickvotes pop up all the time - usually because a sniper gets mad when someone blows up their tower. Never mind that they can just build a new one in half a second. If you get irrationally angry when people damage something you've made, even if you can rebuild it quickly and with minimal effort, you might not like this game very much.  Overall, Ace of Spades is well worth $15. If you consider the price of a movie in theatres and the amount of enjoyment you could get out of this game, the price is a steal. If you wait for a sale you'll be even better off.  DISCLAIMER: The video on Steam shows players flying around with brown jetpacks, painting things and building complex structures. I think that was the developer's attempt to show an artistic representation of their own map-building process. If you buy this game thinking you can join unlimited-time servers and tool around like in Minecraft, you'll be disappointed. Rounds are about 15 minutes apiece and anything you build can and *will* be destroyed.  Additionally, many of the reviews for this game are quite negative. Ignore the old-timers comparing it with the free version from ages past and just take it for what it is - a shooter with maps you can blow up at will. I have to wonder if some of these people even know what fun is that they consider Ace of Spades a bad game. It's ok.  Now seriously, the game is only good if you can deal with a bunch of farming and grinding as it mostly focuses on leveling up your equipment and aquiring new gear. Most warframes and weapons are really easy to obtain, while others require you to do special missions which are time consuming and require dozens of runs to obtain what you need. That's pretty much what you'll be doing most of the time. Currently, the game lacks end-game content, so when you max out your favourite weapons and warframes the only thing to do is farm stuff that you don't really care about. There's also a glaring lack of story to the universe. The developers give us larger story-ish based events every once in a while, but all we learn from those is what's currently happening in the game's universe. The origins of both your enemies and warframes is completely unknown. Speaking of devs, Digital Extremes (devs of Warframe) communicate with their players very well, there's livestreams every week where they talk about upcoming changes to the game and such. As for the gameplay itself, it's quite good. There's new mechanics introduced every few months, the game runs smoothly, gameplay is really enjoyable and diverse(you'll still be farming, but you have a wide variety of equipment to use when farming). The game is really enjoyable with friends, clan mates, ect. Another thing to notice is the lack of dedicated servers, so if you have a ♥♥♥♥♥♥ connection - tough luck.  TL;DR Like farming and grinding? This game is for you. Have friends and wanna play something together? Right here. Enjoy immersive storylines? Look elsewhere. Like co-op? Bingo. Like going solo? Well, you can, but it's really boring and repetitive that way.

Murdered: Soul Suspect is a great story. It's a mediocre game but I enjoyed the story so much I have to recommend this. It has great atmosphere, very nice graphics and a very appealing main story. It's a unique take on detective stories with a supernatural flavor. The investigations are easy but the findings are not trivial and kept adding real new information throughout the game. Only when I was very close to the end of the game was I able to figure all the pieces, meaning the game keeps the tension very well. However, Murdered: Soul Suspect also has linear progression, too trivial investigations, many collectibles which I did not find interesting and sort of bland side investigations and strange stealth combat.  For me the game really picked up when I stopped trying to collect everything and doing every side mission. So, I recommend this game with a suggestion to stick with the main story.

War Thunder is absolutely worth a go if you're into WW2 simulators - Being tanks or Aircraft. While there is A LOT of grinding, if you decide to go 100% F2P, it still feels somewhat rewarding and it keeps feeling like you're making progression since aircrafts/tanks have upgrades as you unlock them for each faction.  I've just recently, before making this post, moved my WT client to Steam so the hours aren't correct on here. That being said, I'm not pro/amazing at the game - but I feel I've given the game enough hours to give you an idea of what to expect.  +Very pretty game, the graphics are crisp. +A lot of vehicles and they keep being added. +In RB (Realistic Battles) Gaijin try to make sure that flight characteristics are accurate for specific aircrafts. (Exclude Russians, see; negative points) +Often in-game events are being held to earn Golden Eagles. (Currency which is bought with RL-money)  +/- You 'need' friends to play. The game becomes, just like many other games, x10 more enjoyable with a wingman.  - Some would disagree for what I am about to type, but it's very clear. There's a Russian bias towards the game. Russian aircrafts are performing way too good. The in-game jokes are that they're made from 'Stalinwood'. - BR-system is flawed and broken in several places. BR = Battle rating. While each faction/nation has Tiers of aircraft, different aircrafts have different BR in a, sadly, weak attempt to 'balance it further'. This is being abused and people try to bring high tier aircrafts with low BR to lower tier games. - The community is what it is. My suggestion is that you try to join a squadron of people with the same mind-set, or create one and set your own rules.   I'm sure I've forgotten a ton of things, both good and bad - However, mix em' all together and you get a game which will give you a lot of hours of fun, IF you are into these types of games. I think you 'have' to be a bit of an history-geek and be interested in WW2 to really fall in love with the game.  An overall score? 7/10 and I would love to give it 8 but with the current Balance and BR-system, it's stuck at 7.

Stop me if you've heard this one before. 'I bought this game on a Steam sale...' I picked it up because it was cheap and vaguely remembered the Kickstarter. I never really thought I'd play it but once I started the game I powered through in just under three days (~8 hrs). The game's combat system and world are well realized and there are multiple characters and builds to play as (with more coming). It's a classic Metroidvania style game that hits the progression loop well.   At times I found the combat frustrating though. Many of the game's enemies can get you in a stun lock if you're not careful and the bosses in the game come with a three step recovery program. 1. Get your butt kicked, 2. Try again and do marginally better, 3. Take the boss down once you get down the pattern. This works well but until you understand the boss the battle can be rage inducing and life threatening to your controller of choice. Also I wish you could see a unified map rather than just pieces of it. Overall though if you're looking for a fun, well done, combat oriented Metroidvania I'd recommend the game.

Dex is a 2d sidescrolling metroidvania set in a futuristic cyberpunk world where corporations secretly rule over the impoverished masses. Set in a location known as Harbor City, you play the hero Dex, a young woman with latent and mysterious cybernetic powers who is caught in a conspiracy involving various shadow organizations scheming to control the world.  The majority of your time in this game will be spent in one of two worlds: meatspace, where you run around on foot and battle with various enemies, and cyberspace, where you move around an avatar and break various defenses in order to hack systems. The meatspace parts of Dex are fairly simple and straightforward and also offer a kind of “hacking” via augmented reality, allowing you to disable opponents and cameras or hack turrets so they fire on enemies instead of you. Combat can be resolved by either gunplay or melee, and both methods are equally effective when done properly. In cyberspace, you have a variety of obstacles to negotiate and 3 special types of attack, each suited for different kinds of enemies (along with a few one-use items that can do things like clear the screen or shield you).  Besides combat, you will have to interact with many different kinds of people as you unravel mysteries and complete quests, and the game gives you a multitude of different ways of dealing with each encounter. You can opt for the more diplomatic route (charisma) and convince people that your way is the best way, or just put a gun to their face or use some form of deception to get what you want. Each choice you make will have actual consequences later on in the story and sometimes even immediately afterwards. Exploration has the same level of variety, with different avenues depending on your skill-set such as a door that can be lockpicked or a vent to crawl through.  Graphically, the game is kind of a treat with the way it pulls off the whole retro-aesthetic. At first you might feel that it’s kind of deliberately ugly but after a few hours of play it sort of grows on you and becomes pretty in its own unique way. The devs were also not afraid of portraying a believable cyberpunk universe in all its grime, blood, and general unsavoriness. The musical score is catchy enough and the sounds reminiscent of what one would expect from a title in this genre (little bleeps and boops in cyberspace for example).   Dex is incredibly well rounded for what it’s worth, I only felt that maybe it could have possibly been two games as the last portion of the game seems almost like it could have been its own adventure. Additionally the sound could use some normalization for the cyberspace sequences, which are way louder than the rest of the game and forced me to reach for my volume dial each time. Overall though, I had no real problems with the game itself and would recommend it to anyone who enjoys the cyberpunk genre, especially if you liked (D)eus (Ex).

Quick message regarding those complaining that their FPS is capping at 30 and they don't know why; there's a setting in the options menu to change it from 30 to 60 or 120.  Stunning game, fun open-world gameplay and interesting planet generation. Unfortunately it has some performance issues involving memory and rendering:  As a Playstation port it has different ways of controlling the performance. Foliage, ores and animals render in around 50-100m in front of you and/or your ship even on the highest render distance. The terrain also has a tendency to 'phase in'.  Then there's memory. After an hour or so of playing the game it slows down from about 50 fps to around 5-10 fps. I was confused at first, wondering if my GPU had suddenly died, but I later found out after some Google'ing that it was infact a 'Memory Leak'. A quick save and a restart fixed the issue, yet it still comes back after an hour-maybe-more.  Speaking of 'saving', the game has an odd 'Save' system, at first I thought you had to go to an outpost of some sort and hold 'E' on the save beacon, but it turns out you can just leave your ship and it will save. I suppose it makes sense, considering I wouldn't want to load in while entering the atmosphere or leaving a space station right as 2 groups of AI are duking it out and my shields are low from a previous scrap.  TL;DR, Pros:  It's a fun game, has an incredibly high potential to be quite possibly the most spectacular open-UNIVERSE game. Every planet you go to 'has its own laws of physics' (quoted by a developer) and procedurally generated terrain, plant-life, ore formation and even animals.  TL;DR, Cons:  Poor performance involving rendering and has constant memory leaks after an hour in one session.  However I am hoping they will release a few patches to resolve the main issues that I have mentioned in this review, so for now I am leaving this at an optimistic 'Recommended'.

I would play everyday after school. Trying to beat my fathers ridiculous score. I never did beat his score, and to this day, I am still nowhere near it.  This game is a great puzzle game. I've waited for all the episodes to come out (yes, all the episodes came out already- go play it in case you're one of these people who wait until the finale comes out before playing a Telltale game) before I'll write my review: Tales from the Borderlands is one of my favorite Telltale games (among with Telltale's The Walking Dead: Season 2 and Telltale's Game of Thrones) and overall favorite games. Tales from the Borderlands was overall an amazing game with perfect premiere and finale episodes. As someone who played all previous games in the Borderlands franchise (Borderlands, Borderlands 2 and Borderlands: The Pre-Sequel) and really loved them all and played many other Telltale games (both seasons The Walking Dead, The Wolf Among Us, Game of Thrones and Minecraft: Story Mode), the combination between the two was spectacular for me. Some of the things I really liked about the Borderlands franchise is the rich story and characters and the amazing world, and having them all in a Telltale game is amazing. The writing, the humor, the story, the soundtrack (the main theme is amazing, and it's orchestral version is even more amazing) and music effects, the design, the decisions, the action- everything was just amazing. While I do think that episodes 2-4 had their highs and lows, I think that the first and fifth (finale) episodes were just perfect. I personally really hope that Telltale will decide to make a second season of Tales from the Borderlands and I personally cannot wait for it to come out. If you haven't played it yet- now is the time.

There isn't much to say that hasn't already been said. The Mystery P.I. series is among the earliest and more popular Hidden Object Only games. This particular edition asks the player to find 25 keys and casino chips in order to unlock 'bonus' gameplay material. What the game doesn't tell you is that you score a million point bonus if you also manage to locate 25 fingerprints sprinkled about the game as well. All of this uber-casual gaming fun is, of course, in addition to the hidden objects in each area. One negative reviewer mentioned that the same areas are repeated several times as you play the game. While this is true, the list of objects does randomly change so you can't just zip through by remembering the first batch of object you found in the earlier scene.  HOG have become much more sophisticated over the years, with the Mystery Case Files franchise pioneering woven story threads and sometimes maddening puzzles that must be solved in order to complete the game. Fans of these 'deeper' games number in the millions and sometimes it seems as if they could each play different games without any two folks playing the same one. There's a saying in the Southern United States... you can't swing a dead cat around without hitting a few of them. It definitely applies to this genre of casual games. BUT... if you have children that are just honing their nascent gaming skills and observational abilities, or older relatives that are new to computing (I know they're out there because I have several relatives that are just learning how to use computers) this series is the perfect introduction. Each of the Mystery P.I. games is educational, entertaining, and is an excellent tool to assist elderly folk beginning to deal with the onset of Alzheimers or any of the vile diseases that plague us in our golden years. I still drag these out of my 'completed games' folders every now and again and tear through them just for the nostalgia value, so I definitely got my money's worth out of them!

Note: I was one of the original betatesters for the original release, as well as one of testers for the Steam release.  The Princess’ Heart is another RPG developed by Roseportal Games. It was their third commercial game, after successes like Whisper of a Rose and Sweet Lily Dreams (both available through Steam). It does not share the same universe as those games and looks and feels more like a traditional JRPG.   The Princess’ Heart has a nice plot, if a bit short, however its characters make up for it quite well. I found them very unexpected and they often surprised me. There’s 9 party members in total, one of which is optional. I would have liked to see some more party interaction.  Graphics are pretty standard, but used wisely and correctly in my opinion. It also feels more traditional, because of the standard graphics.   As for gameplay, there’s no difficulty modes, but as the battles are well balanced and the monsters don’t respawn until you’ve left the dungeon completely, it doesn’t feel necessarily. I liked that I could change the party formation easily, which is quite necessary as most battles require you to do so. Some of the dungeons are mazes and loop, which I quite enjoyed as a challenge.   All in all I recommend this game to every strategic RPG lover.

My first playthrough took me about an hour and a half to maybe two hours. So far after about 5 hours straight of playing this game I would say for the price of 2.99 that it is ATM, I say buy it if you can.  Okay now to pros and cons  ____Pros____ Superb writing Charater creation melded well, with out feeling to out of place Good illusion of choice (I'll get to this in a second) Passages don't get to long easy to stay focused good amout of replayabilty super fun great characters   ____Cons____ Now back to that thing illusion of choice, what do I mean by this. Well after going through the story four or five times I began to realize that some of the options you pick don't actually do anything  as far as I see. Nothing game changing but I was kind of dissapointed that they did not do anything The stats besides the fighting ones are never really explained at all and you are never really shown by the game what they do to your story. I think they alter what option you can pick at the end (No spoilers) but I have yet to test this theory.   All and all its a good game for the price it's at. while I feel I could get a flash game with the same amount of content. The writer and developers did a very well job and I'm happy to support them.

So here's the opinion of one of the veterans of the game. (400+ hours)  I've been playing this game for 4 months and I must say to those that believe that its a mobile game that they are mistaken. It's a browser game that became a FB game and then moved to mobiles and now comes on steam.  Is it pay to win?  I'll tell you from my experience. No it isn't. We had someone in my city that spend 200$+ dollars on the game.   I left him miles behind because I played smarter before I even spent my $20 on the game.  I played this game 3 months without spending a penny on it, after that I decided to purchase THE THING REALLY WORTH BUYING IN THE GAME- The Wizard Package.  Before that, I had been making money by selling Golden keys for 100,000,000 that I got from raids. There are other ways, but that was the most efficient one.  The other things are cool, but honestly they cannot compare to crafting the final item in the wizard package, which nowadays (that I have focused production on it) makes me make more than 2G a day (2,000,000,000 gold)  The game doesn't become really fun until the moment that you start cooperating with your citymates to build your city and then it becomes even more fun when you raiding and pvping.  This game is worth playing and a great time burner.  Enjoy.

I really wish I could recommend this game. However, after the Parity Patch... no longer. That isn't to say it was perfect before, but imo it was a HELL of a lot better before. Parity Patch trashed this game. No more recoil, so it feels like a straight up laser gun with almost any receiver, leading to very OP weapons and underpowered weapons as well. People can actually snipe with LMGs and pistols from across maps, it's ridiculous. Previously the recoil system was quite dynamic, and it required good customization builds and training to adapt to the recoil patterns. Character customization was also a plus, with speed builds, heavy builds, as well as middle-of-the-road type stuff. Now, however, it feels like they decreased run speeds as well as cut stamina, so now infinite running kinda renders the old speed builds obsolete unless you focus upon melee being a strong point. I hate to say it, but if you're a CoD fan looking for a F2P shooter, I guess this can be your new baby. But for vets such as myself (I've played off and on since closed beta), Hardsuit Labs has, unfortunately, turned this into a huge disappointment. They removed all kinds of other features such as datanodes, mail, and global chat as well, and I don't see how there could be an allure any more. It isn't really... unique. It just plays like CoD. There's not much community any more either. All they needed to do was merely add new content like maps, weapons, gear, and fix some of the bugs like the elevators (which were atrocious) and random server disconnects and it would have been fantastic. But they decided to port a shoddy PS4 version for us instead. I'm sorry to leave, but HS Labs ruined this for me, as well as many other old players I know that feel the same way. This used to be the BEST shooter I ever played.

For people who are interested in game design,  Starseed Pilgrim  is a very interesting game to study. The player is dropped into the game world with little, if any, instructions or hints. They must experiment with the mechanics to discover their behaviour and relationships.  While players looking for a fun, familiar puzzle game won't find it here, players willing to dig deeper and figure out how the game works for themselves will be rewarded. Once past the initial confusion, the game's minimalist approach is refreshing. Each new discovery or 'aha' moment is totally worth it, because you will have earned it by yourself.

As far as interactive story games go, this is at the top of my list. A $20 price tag may seem a bit steep for a game which can be completed in its entirety in about two hours, but you'll almost certainly be able to find it for less on either the Humble store or a Steam sale. The depth and detail that was put into every little aspect of this game is amazing, and I would definitely recommend it to anyone who wants be part of a great story.

app_name: Sword of the Stars: The Pit
review_text: I'm a huge fan of rogue-like games such as FTL, Dungeons of Dredmore, and Binding of Isaac, etc, and I've played these games as far back as Temple of Apshai on C-64, so I'm no stranger to the genre.  I really like some of the ideas introduced in the Pit (I especially like the sci-fi setting), but the game just feels too long for the type of 'throwaway' deaths it inevitably throws at you. FTL and Binding of Isaac have great short game times (about an hour or so on average full game), while Dungeons of Dredmore did tend to drag on too long imo. But while Dungeons of Dredmore had some long playing times, you would never (to my memory) run into an instant 'you die'! scenario that you find in The Pit. And by instant 'you die!', I mean situations that don't have a defense and result in immediate or eventual loss of the game when they occur. I.e. use the human mod station and become 'ravenous' for food, trapped doors that you are forced to pass through that destroy your food/weapons/armor/etc, disintegrate yourself when trapping a machine, or get knocked unconscious in a single shot and then be subject to whatever grab/acid/radiation weapon types that will ultimately kill you, even if you don't die in that encounter (missing your weapons, or radiation which doesn't have enough 'cure' items). I also hear you can be vaporized by a single enemy shot, regardless of health, but I haven't encountered that yet.  My own opinion (and everyone is enttiled to their own) is that the game is not bad, but might have been better off with shorter playtimes to keep me compelled to keep trying, instead of spending hours to die to something unavoidable. And even if the death is avoidable (such as not using human programming machines EVER), the rate at which you learn about the death traps is a little too slow for my tastes.  Bottom line for me: too much time investment for what little knowledge (if any) you gain with each death (most of them unavoidable with no defense possible)  The Pit has potential if the devs shorten the game time and remove some of the instant deaths.

This is one of the best train simulators out on the market. The graphics are nice, the sounds are pretty good, the driving is realistic. For me, what makes this one of the best is that it's the most realistic railroad simulators available to the public.   But with everything nice about the game, there is also a dark side to it. The number one thing that I really dislike about the game is the abundancy of payware. If you don't want just the couple of routes to drive and/or crash trains on, you have to pay hefty prices on some routes. Even though you do have to pay $5 or $6 for the cheapest route (Game of Gnomes), some of the routes make up for the price by adding new locos, rolling stock, and other stuff (Cajon Pass for $18). I really also don't like the amount of freeware assets that rely on the overpriced loco addons, such as Blue Peter ($20).  All in all, even with its major dark side, I like the game and enjoy driving trains and building routes in this simulator. I recommend this game to people who like the more realistic simulators and to rail fans everywhere across the world.

A decent addition to the Borderlands franchise that will likely satisfy fans, but leave newcomers somehwat dissapointed.   It is important to clarify that Borderlands the pre-sequel as a game is not Borderlands 3. By no means should it be considered a fully developed sequel that is helping the franchise progress leaps and bounds.  Instead it feels like an extension or traditional expansion pack to borderlans 2. Whilst it experiments with some new features such as Cryo, Lazers and Jump-Smashing, the experience really doesn't feel like it's come that far from that which it is meant to be the pre-sequel for.  If you love the borderlands franchise, and have friends to potentially play with then the pre-sequel is defenitely worth investigating. However if you were only a moderate fan of the previous games, I'd wait for the release of Borderlands 3. If you're completely new to the franchise I'd advise you to pick up Borderlands 2. Borderlands 2 seems like a more complete game with better writing and characters. The presequel instead feels like what it is, a side project that is farmed out to a different international development team.   The story is average, but I have never really been that impressed by any of the borderlands storytelling. It fills a few of the gaps between the main 2 iterations of the story, but the characters and progression are much less compelling than those stories too. Being the villain, or at least having a more ambiguous agenda in the story is different, and fairly refreshing, though again the narrative did leave a bit to be desired overall.    Conclusion
