It seems I have a terrible issue where I can't stop buying every version or re-release of the Metal Slug games. The games are just that darn addictive.  SNK Playmore has blessed PC owners with a port of Metal Slug X, which is basically the director's cut of Metal Slug 2. Now some people prefer the original version of no.2 because of it's less forgiving difficulty (which by Metal Slug standards, isn't say much) while others appreciate the added variety of enemy, power-up and weapon placement seen in X. Personally I enjoy X more than no.2 but hopefully SNK will bring over no.2 to make everyone happen.  The performance of the PC port is good, but suffers from slow down when things become hectic and this is more apparent during the last mission. Granted this rarely happens, so it never becomes a problem and I've seen worst lag in some of the weaker Metal Slug ports, like no.2 on the PS3. Video options include resolution, full-screen, aspect ratio and scanlines. Plus controller support with optional button mapping. Online leaderboards are also here, so you can see how bad you fared against the rest of the world. Sadly this port is just the original arcade machine, so the Combat School or Another Mission from the PS1 port are MIA. Yes the modes recycled assets from the base game, but still offered great challenges that expanded the game's replay value. I'm also disappointed to see the art gallery didn't make the cut and would love to see all the art work in tasty HD clarity (and without enduring long PS1 fetch times).   The important thing is the main game is here and it still rocks. For those who haven't played Metal Slug before, it's a fast paced, frantic 2D shooter with beautiful hand drawn graphics and catchy music. The game is only one hour long, but they still crammed a lot into these six mission. Caramels with mission guns mounted to them, alien space ships, mummies, mutants and trains that want to run over you. Bosses that try to crush you with tanks, food that makes you fat and shoot bigger bullets, Metal Slug's trademark humour is always a breath of fresh air, especially during today's more serious approach to game design.  However this game was originally designed to eat dollar coins at the arcade, so it's brutally difficult with one hit kills happening every 10 seconds for those who don't pay attention. Fortunately the port comes with unlimited continues and adjustable difficulty. The port also retains local co-op and Metal Slug has always been a multi-player favourite with my friends. Sadly SNK just doesn't know how to make a good netcode for their games and renders the online completely useless.  Online issues and missing features withstanding, Metal Slug X's chaotic, humourous action has successfully made the jump to Steam. I recommend it to die hard Metal Slug fans or anyone who enjoys the odd arcade shooter.

Dragonball Xenoverse - Big Potential of which is only the half is used - Kinda nice Graphics (Because how do you want to make an Anime as a Game? with Reallife Graphics?) - Unbelievable annoying Music (At least in Menus and Tokitoki) - Disturbing RPG Loot Drop (Repeat a Mission 150 Times and you'll have one Outfit) - Sometimes kinda stupid KI's (Ultimate Gohan always use his Evasive Attack and spams it as example) - 'Incomplete' Story (Janemba, Turles, Slug missing for example) Beside these really dumb things, this Game MAKES DRAGONBALL GREAT AGAIN! (kinda) - Nice Gameplay (Beside some Details... *cough*Super-Saiyajin*cough*) - Ability Point System   (3Points per Level in HP, Ki, Stamina, Attack, Super Strike, Ultimate Attacks and Evasive Attacks) - Max Level is 99 (but after Level 80 its difficult to level up since you need much more EXP) - Unbelievable Variety on Outfits - Nice Character Editor (Kinda lacks on Options... but its Okay) - Well Edited Story   (With Animations and Stuff, not so Stiff anymore like earlier Games...Besides Gohan(Kid) and   Broly...which tend to AHHHHHHHHHHHHHHH always ._.) - Huge Collection on Super Strikes, Ultimate Attacks and Evasive Attacks  (Somewhat 200 according to Xenoverse Wiki) - Different Races as Character available (Namekians, Freezer-Clan, Majins) - The Main Story (Somewhat 15Hours according to different Sites) - Maaaaaaaany Side Quests (Parallel Quests) up to 55 and with the DLC's +11  (They vary from fighting to collect dragonballs which you can't keep ._.) - Also a Master-System where you choose a Character from the Dragonball Universe as your Master  (You're able to learn their Skills from them and they're always switchable, beside they get salty when   you leave them even when their Training is over xD) - Coop-System  (Kinda buggy in Toki Toki, but it's nice to play some PQ's with your Friends) - You can collect Dragonballs by fighting Time Soldiers which sometimes appear in PQ's  (PQ 2 and 12 are good for Dragonball Farming :P) I GUESS ENUF SAID Its definitevly playable for every DBZ-Fan Even when there are DLC's (Today u'll get the Season Pass for 6€ when you're searching good enough^^)  IN THE END I give DBZ Xenoverse 8,5/10 Because the Loading Sequences sometimes feel like they are unnecessary (.__.) But definitevly worth everything under 20 Bucks  GAME ON!

review_text: Praise the sun..
Really trashy game but very addictive. I would not recommend this to anyone but dare you to play it if you like pain.

Pros:  - Unique concept - nice challenge - pixel art - zen difficulty option - many characters and monsters to unlock - level select at new game, keeping character level-ups  Cons:  - too fast, so you have to write short words to deal damage fast - you do not enjoy the fights, as you focus your eyes on the letters - many obscure words, so you need to be creative - no ending (and the game tells you that in a very subtle way) - armor and weapons just add skill points, they do not behave as in-game objects - no fullscreen support (you can make the edges larger in the options screen, but you cannot make it go fullscreen) because the author decided this was the only option he had (see Discussions for this game on Steam).  So yeah, I recommend getting this game, it is fun if you enjoy word games, even if it is flawed. And, if you can wait, get it in a bundle like me or wait for a very good deal. No reason to pay full price on Steam, anyways :)

Firewatch is a wonderful vignette into the lives of two flawed and lonely people who form a connection. What type of connection is shaped by the way you play.  It also has the finest voice acting I've ever heard in a video game. It is worth the price of admission for this alone.   It is not a grand adventure in which you play the hero solving problems and resolving loose ends, and those seeking that sort of experience will be disappointed. Its story (and ending) captures the complex and muddy nature of real relationships. It is not a long game (around 5 hours), but it is the perfect length for what it is, and it will stay with you for a long time after playing.  I would highly recommend this, especially if you like games like Gone Home. I would especially recommend it to people who are older (maybe late twenties plus); you will recognise the muddy moments in life where there is no right choice, only choices. And it is beautiful for it.

As a gaming old-timer, I've pretty much seen it all. Lots of bad, plenty of good, and way too much awful. Truly unique and fun experiences stick out in my memory like events on a timeline... Pong, Space invaders, Pitfall, Diablo, Ultima (all), ET, Zork, Bard's Tale, King's Quest, Doom, Madden, Zelda, Quake, Starcraft, Sword of the Samurai, Warcraft, Civ, SimCity, Age of Empires, Half-Life, TIE Fighter, Neverwinter Nights, GTA III, Unreal Tournament, Sims, Deus Ex, Morrowind, Oblivion, Skyrim, Grim Fandango, WoW, Fallout, System Shock 2, Bioshock, XCom, Duke Nukem, Pirates!, Knights of the Old Republic, errrr what was I talking about?  Magicka is great fun, in any case. I love the casting system... being able to create a nearly endless combination of spells is very cool and challenging. In my first session, about an hour in, I dropped a massive magic boulder on my head and died. I thought that was pretty funny.  I picked this up in a bundle... and I feel guilty, because this is worth far more than the 37 cents I paid for it. At $9.99 it's a definite buy.

Fortunately, it is positive.  ----- So I played 40 minutes of this (only need less than an hour to get the concept and the mechanics that comes up as you progress for this game) and cleared 30 levels (some took a few retries to complete or go for 3 stars 'cause OCD). It's a cute puzzle game with cute square cows, hedgehogs, rabbits, fish, etc. and a cute way to squish weeds that look like Venus Fly Traps. Fun for kids and puzzle lovers alike.  Some of the levels may be a breeze, however some require thought and planning to get that 3 star.  There's not much else to say to be honest.  There was one level, 16, where the perfect move counter needs to be higher. I cleared it in 17 moves left (which should be the maximum unless you can clear it with 18 moves left), yet the perfect counter is set to 5. I did point that out to the dev and hopefully they'll adjust that.  -----  Check out more games I see that deserve more attention from my curator group:  BluePaw's Underrated Games List  Join and/or follow if you wish.

After Quake's 1996 debut, multiple trends of 3D First Person design came about, one of which was Valve's Half-Life in 1998. In opposition to other FPSs that focused on a more heavily arcade-influenced gameplay (secrets, par times, quick action), Half-Life was almost entirely designed around a linear first-person narrative structure, watching the events unfold around them through the eyes of Gordon Freeman.   Half-Life's blend of story and gameplay is what sells the game. You participate in this fast-paced horror/science-fiction/action journey through an enormous scientific complex and it feels incredibly satisfying to do so. Watching the enviorment go through various shades of Underground Laboratory Bunker gives you a real sense of scale at how Black Mesa is.   The weapons are a huge component of the game's atmosphere, with different areas giving you different weapons, all of them fun to use in some way. The game takes full advantage of its weapon selection not just from a story perspective but a character perspective as well. Gordon is a young, relatively inexperienced researcher who fights against the expected norms in this scenario by repeatedly fighting his way through Black Mesa backpacking all manner of experimental and unconventional weaponry. Learning how to use all of them makes a playthrough so much more satisfying than if you were to just sprint through with just the first four guns you grab.  The first Half-Life stands well apart from other expansions, mods, and even its own sequel. Opposing Force and Blue Shift, while satisfactory expansions don't maintain the uniquely original aura of the first game. Black Mesa, despite touting itself as a remake, really only succeeds in presenting a more modern and realistic setting. The rest of the mod is just poorly-playtested gunk. It's just not as fun to play as the original game. Ditching Xen to chase the multiplayer carrot didn't help either. Half-Life 2 just isn't as interesting a game as the first one. It manages to simultaneosly have worse gameplay, story, and artstyle than the first game.  *unintelligble soldier noises*  Gordon is punished for his lateness and everyone suffers.  marvel at the questionable FPS game design choices of yesteryear.  whensblackmesa (just kidding we already know it's broken as much as it fixed)

Bioshock Infinite is absolutely amazing, the shere attention to detail and the gripping story made all the time spent looking in every corner of every random lane and fighting wave after wave of interesting (but occaisionally dull) enemies a blast and had justified the 20+ hours I have spent on this game.   I found the story in the beginning a bit slow and uneventful, but the bright and glamorous world made up for that with interesting dialogue, spoken by the people lining the streets and the great showcase of the indoctrination of the people by Father Comstock. As the story/game progresses things change dramatically, throwing in plenty of combat and spicing up the story perfectly.  After ruthlessly slaughtering countless numbers of enemies I found the combat got a bit dull, and in a way a bit easy after getting most of the upgrades and new weapons/vigors. The enemies are also a bit on the bland side, since most of them are just a darkish colour and little detail on the uniforms I found my self killing what I thought was the same person over and over again, this was especially prevalent towards the end of the game.  The combat incorperates a lot of 'fantasy' into it, with things like Vigors and more modern guns with a little less realism and less punch. The physics of the weapons felt great too, they were not way too unrealistic but they weren't too reaslistic either, which provided a nice blend making those heads look as bigger target as always. If I were to have 1 major complaint about the guns it would be how underpowered some guns can be towards the end of the game, where I ran out of ammo for my sniper and had only a pistol as a replacement. The pistol been my main weapon at the beginning of the game and could take down enemies in what felt like 1 blow, but the pistol had no such effect when taking down the same enemies later on, making one have to completely spray all their ammo at the opponents head to actually take them down.  At the end of the story things get very weird and confusing but when the game ends, most previous events in the game begin to come together, but some do not leading to a lot of confusion and possibly sequels (?). The end left me wanting to play again, so that I can find all those little events that link to the main plot and then giggle, thinking about 'how could I have not noticed this before?'  This game is one of a small ammount of games that have left me happy and satisfied at the end, and I can definately see myself playing it again sometime in the future. To get a game this great for only $10 was itself amazing. I have seen games that cost a lot more and has been realeased a lot earlier, that are absolutely terrible.   Overall I believe Bioshock Infinite to be well worth the time and money, and that Irration games & 2K have done an exellent job, creating what is probably, a masterpiece.  I would definately recommend getting this game, and I guarantee (A guarantee? Who has time for all that paperwork?) you will have fun!   I give it a 10/10

app_name: DARK SOULS™: Prepare To Die Edition
review_text: This game is for people who are actually able to learn from their mistakes and turn their deaths into some sort of useful knowledge that they will be able to use the next time around. The story may be a bit vague, but it is extremely deep and actually rather sad if you are the type of person to pay attention to details. The atmosphere is wonderfully and masterfully presented, and the game really immerses you into a believable dark fantasy setting. The soundtrack is not present for most of the game, because most of the areas are silent. When you do hear it in boss battles and in some areas, you will hear that is actually well done and underestimated. The theme of the final boss is a particular song that is great. I'm sure you've heard that this game is difficult, but I would recommend getting help on knowing where to spend stat points if you aren't confident in developing a proper RPG character. Extensive planning for the type of equipment is needed as well. You will need equipment that compliments your characters major stats. I found that most 80% of this game's difficulty come from its level designs. Many of them, (The late game areas especially) are designed in a way to make life miserable for you like with a narrow bridge leading to big enemies that can easily make you slide off. If you are expereinced with hack and slash type of action and dodging, will find the combat to be a bit easier than those who are not as experienced. This game is not for the faint of heart, and if you are willing to pull through to the end, you will be proud of yourself and glad that you had done so. It is completely worth it. Praise The Sun!!!!

app_name: Brothers - A Tale of Two Sons
review_text: I came into this game knowing it was the single player co-op game, many friends had recommended it, and not much else. That is the way to do it. It is absolutely worth the 4-6 hours it will take to complete and every new chapter managed to again take my breath away. It is an incredible story of loss and growth set in an utterly realized yet fantastical world. Sometimes controlling the brothers at the same time is confusing (it's the video game equivalent of patting your head and rubbing your stomach at the same time), but it is not enough to take away from this journey. Immediately jumps into the top of the 'video games as art' conversation. Play it now.

Despite peoples claims for it being a 'Pay2Win' game you'd be terribly mistaken. All frames are obtainable through regular play. You can choose to invest real money if you so wish to get the frames you wish or you may choose to get it as you would with any other game.  It's not a mmo, more of a 4-player Co-op Third Person action game. Allowing you to take control of a Warframe. Refered too as 'Tenno' the race controlling it. (Basically you're a space ninja.)  You get to zip line, Run along walls, back flip walls, slash a big ♥♥♥ sword and generally kick ♥♥♥. There are three factions: Corpus, Grineer and Infested.  Infested: The Infested are mostly melee based. With strange alien/corrupted creatures that can inflict deadly poisons or maul you to death.  Corpus: The Corpus are like the evil science type guys. These have Moa robots. (Practically guns with legs.) They mostly use lazer weapons. Not only that there are melee corpus who make use of electric prods. (nasty) As well as the annoying shield spray robots which can repair the shields of foes around it,  Grineer: The Grineer unlike the corpus have more 'standard*' type weapons. Less futuristic. They also have melee uses who try to slice you. You'll find them doing a lot of damage if they successfully land a hit.    Originally this game had started off as something so very small. With only a few frames: Loki, Excalibur, Volt, Rhino and Mag it slowly started growing and becoming so much more. Really basic levels and basic movement and have strongly benefitted from constant player feedback improving the game with every update.  The game is extremely responsive however be prepared to get a hand ache after hours of play with how much you need to keep moving to avoid enemies and help allies. One minor issue for me is that this game lacks any cover mechanic. (enemies can cover but you are unable to.) Blocking is in the game but it also feels rather pointless since I've found myself in little to no situations where it was actually neccesary or helpful. It is also assigned by default to mouse button 4. If you're old fashioned like me and have a mouse with only 3 buttons you may need to assign it to a spot on your keyboard.    The game is simple, the graphics amazing. It works on my really sub-standard PC with a graphics card as basic as an ASUS EAH6450 with a simple (God-awful) AMD Athlon 64 Processor 3200+ 1.99 Ghz and 3.00GB of RAM running Win-XP SP3.  (Yes this pc is about as old as the hills.) You may not be able to play with friends with a PC like this but you sure as hell can play the game at well over 30fps and as far as I'm concerned that is absolutely astonishing. Considering many other games with worse graphics than this run about as fast as a snale on salt.   Do I recommend this game? Hell yes, I would recommend it to everyone. Even if you wouldn't like it. Try it anyway since it's free to play and you really just can't complain. (I've had more hours out of this beauty than I have out of any AAA $60.00 piece of over hyped trash.)

WARFRAME is an excellent co-op TPS that rewards your creativity and patience.  The premise is simple- you are an ancient warrior, awakened from a deep slumber by the mysterious Lotus to face the Grineer, Corpus, and Infested enemy factions. You do using a wide arsenal of different Warframes, as well as primary, secondary, and melee weapons. Each frame has its own abilities and strengths, and most weapons are unique and each serve a different purpose.  There are three methods of progression in the game- Mastery Rank, Equipment Rank, and mods. As you play through missions, you'll gain EXP, which in turn increases the rank of your equipment. Leveling different pieces of equipment also increases your overall rank, known as Mastery Rank. Certain pieces of equipment aren't available until you attain a high enough rank. Leveling up your equipment also increases the number of mod points it has, allowing you to install more mods.  Mods themselves are the core of the game, and are used to modify and tune your frame and weapons to your choosing. There's a large amount of mods, and each mod provides bonuses. For example, a Serration mod will give you a percentage of bonus damage for Rifles. Another example would be a Vitality mod, which increases your frame's health by a certain percentage. There's plenty more, too- Critical hit boosting mods, elemental damage mods, ability mods- by utilizing these properly, you're able to create the perfect killing machine tailored to your needs.  Actually utilizing your gear is a blast, too- WARFRAME's combat is fast and invigorating, and using your abilities and parkour movement is a lot of fun. Although there are only three (technically four) enemy factions, there -is- a wide variety of enemies within each one, and you'll encounter new types of enemies as you progress through the solar system. The missions themselves are entertaining, and there's a variety of modes, all of which invole killing things. My personal favorite is Survival, which has you pit against hordes of enemies as your scramble around the map looking for life support in an attempt to last for as long as possible.   PROS: ♥ Exciting combat system ♥ Wide variety of weapons and characters to choose from ♥ The progression system is fresh and interesting, and has a lot of room for flexibility ♥ The development team for the game is very active with the community, which is nice ♥ Grinding is involved, but the game's gear and gameplay allow you to keep things fresh.  CONS: ■ The music gets very bland very fast. There's roughly four or five tracks (if that) for every mission in the game. ■ If you're not a huge fan of grinding, things might get stale after awhile.  Check out WARFRAME if you like: MMOs, Borderlands, Mass Effect 3's multiplayer, space ninjas, co-op shooters  ▶ Kat gives WARFRAME an 9/10.

app_name: Castlevania: Lords of Shadow - Ultimate Edition
review_text: If you're the type of person who really enjoys backtracking over the same levels two or three times (minimum), and you're really excited by the idea of never actually getting *stronger* and instead just getting more consumable gadgets to throw at stuff, then this is the game for you! If you're not the type of person to whom this stuff sounds appealing, move on. This game is plagued by a lack of imagination, ♥♥♥♥♥♥ camera angles, and even a little contempt for the people foolish enough to play it. Example: the Chupacabra. Rather than any sort of goat-sucking, the Chupacabra exists just to waste five minutes of your life. It shows up twice, steals everything but your main weapon and consumables, and then you have to chase it down -- but you never get to kill it. Or smack it. It just boogies on out of there after being a ♥♥♥♥♥♥, and adds literally nothing to the overall experience. I got up to chapter nine (I think) and uninstalled it when I realized it was just going to be more of the same, especially once I finally gained the ability to double-jump (!!!) and would have to go back through six or eight previous levels to get the upgrades I was unable to reach before. That's not _fun_, that's a way to pad the 'hours played' numbers. It's dull, tedious, but it's absolutely on par with the rest of the game.

Excellent game. It might not be an AAA title, but damn if it doesn't try.  The aesthetics are really sweet even if the graphics technology used feels a bit last-gen.   The missions are varied, most of them have interesting victory conditions to offer players a challenge different to just carving a way through to the end.   There's quite a lot of weapons, and each of them feels distinct from the rest.  Every character uses different weapons and thus plays differently. The wizard offers the most unique experience, but even the dwarf is a fresh take on the warrior archetype - in contrast to other characters, you never have a clear overview of the situation on the battlefield because of your puny height! It might sound lame, but it really feels fun and unique once you try it out.  The controls are solid. They're not just mindless W+1 (forward and left mouse button), you actually have to get a feel for each weapon's speed, strength, range and special traits to be efficient with it. Once you learn to control the weapon's weight, you might even catch yourself physically shifting your own weight with each swing.   Two of my main gripes with the game is the performance and lack of versus play. I've got a pretty beefy PC, yet Vermintide runs at below 20FPS on highest settings. I hope the devs do get around to implementing versus some day.  Despite a few flaws, I highly recommend.

The combat, while enjoyable, can get repetitive, but that partly depends on whether you choose only one method; between fidget's projectiles and all the different melee combos at your disposal, switching things up should be no problem. Overall, it's an excellent experience, and leaves me wanting more.

The tactics are varied and interesting, and there's a lot of room for risky play. Anyways, I'm probably more coherent when I hate a game. It's hard to praise a game as much as I have, so I'll get to the cons: -There's only one game board. -There's a pretty small character selection.  That's uh, about it. review_text: This was one of the very first games I played as a kid, and it's a pleasure to see it brought back to life on today's machines. You may need to do some digging on the internet to find a patch to get things running smoothly on your machine, but please don't let this scare you away. I got mine working perfectly after browsing Reddit for fifteen minutes, but has rewarded me with hours of classic FPS action. It may be dated, but if you miss the good old days of pressing the spacebar to shoot and cutscenes with real actors in them, you won't be disappointed. 5/5

+ Very good for its time and has the catchiest start screen music I have ever heard in a long time. + Four-player is always fun in games like these, though this might be the only way you will be coming back to this one. + Truly the only thing that will come close to Scott Pilgrim vs. the World: The Game on the computer. ○ Game-breaking bug where you see a loading screen wtih Mikoto indefinitely and has yet to be fixed. - After the start screen, the music gets pretty mediocre as the game goes on. Not worth getting the soundtrack. - Too much grinding and not enough variety in moves to be comparable to cult hits like Guardian Heroes. - No online co-op, though most of my friends in my town play games like this anyway so I can always find someone myself.  Still, despite all of this, especially the middle part that only ever happens to a certain amount of users, such as myself, this game was fun for the time I did get to spend with it before I began trying to get a refund for it due to the fact that it does not work anymore and it feels like I wasted my money, especially since they have yet to update the game.  But, this game still works with enough people where you would be likely to fall into that same in-crowd of users. Only people with Windows 10 (for the most part) have ever experienced problems, though it is peculiar to experience any problems with Steam games, let alone game-breaking bugs. So, the choice is up to you.  In spite of all this, once you do play this, you will have a hard time stopping.

The nice art, beatiful soundtrack, funny narration and voice acting just add up to a very fun gameplay, making this game really enjoyable to play. Ok, so I'm not a huge racing game fan. I've never really been into need4speed, gran turismo, project gotham and so on. This probably has a lot to do with a friend who would take great pleasure in showing no mercy to noobs to racing games he'd been playing for months. Getting repeatedly lapped sucked the joy out of most of these games for me. Then I discovered Forza 2, a game that had somehow gone under his radar. And having not lost the will to live whilst playing it I actually got ok at it and played it upto the point when the endurance races begun. Then my friend discovered it and finally here was a racing game that I could beat him at. So the Forza franchise will forever hold a special place in my affections. But sadly Microsoft have not ported any version of Forza to the PC, which sucks.  Step up Grid 2. Grid 2 has a feel of Forza about it. It looks beautiful, with graphics that are comparable to Forza 5. It boasts game play that can be exhillerating and infuriating during the same race. It also includes Brands Hatch and two F1 tracks in Yas Marina in the UAE and the Red Bull Ring in Austria. In short I have been looking for a racing game that I can find as enjoyable as I used to Forza. And although Grid 2 lacks the depth of Forza, I have found it every bit as enjoyable in the few short hours that I have played it, and for me there can be no higher praise for a racing game than that.  8/10 (would be 9/10 if it was Forza)

Hours upon hours of hair pulling frustration!  Do you enjoy playing racing games that make you want to pull your hair out till you go bald? That make you want to throw your controller at the wall, and to email the developers and ask them what the hell was going on in their minds when they added certain features to their game whilst insulting them and their stupidity at the same time?  Well, if you do, this game is for you!   Highlights of this game that will make you want to buy it and play it:  - AI racers crashing into non-racing AI but not being removed from the race, even though if you were in that situation you would have to start over from the start because you crashed! - AI racers that crash will automagically get placed on the track again, whereas if you crash you either need to painstakingly reverse to get back onto the road, how completely fair, amirite?! - Getting to the end of a race being first and then having a non-racing AI stop right before the finish line, making you crash into them! - Getting to the end of a race and crashing and having to start from the start again! - Activating your turbo behind an AI racer, only to have that racer stay in front of you!  - Having to spend 5 minutes on a race, only to crash, and then needing to start from the start again! As I said, hair pulling fun! - AI racers in cars that are crappier than yours, but can accelerate twice as fast as you! - Cop cars that gang up on you, and forget about taking down the other racers! - AI racers will crash into non-racing AI, and then you will crash into them, because of their stupidity! Nice coding, whoever made this dumb game! - Police road blocks!  - An uneccessarily long final race, over 10 minutes long that will require you to restart several times because of the points I have mentioned above!  Sounds like fun, right?! Well if this review entices you, go out and buy this game immediately! Personally, if I had a physical copy of this game, I would throw it into a fire and dance around it right now! I would have more fun eating dog poo in a 40 degree van than playing this game!  If you were like me and tried to get Distinction/Gold on every race (I personally got it on every race except for 2 or 3) you will clock 26 hours on this game like I have, which includes time spent restarting heaps of races!

Don't want to read through it all: This is an awesome game. You should buy it and play it. I'm super-cereal.  I've changed my previous review to 'Recommend' as Ubisoft decided to play nice and eventualy came up with a half-arsed solution to provide it's customers with additional activation keys above the initial 3, IF it is needed.  SO this IS a pretty great city builder/strategy in the Anno series.  Player gets to choose between two (and later 3) fractions which have their own different agenda, philosophies and approaches to development. You get to combine them as you progress so it doesn't really matter. You get to build, plan, explore and have fun on land, on and beneath the sea.   However, playing with the Green Initiative had me really think about choices we humans make and how those affect our environment. I was aware of the ecological impact we have on our planet prior to this (duh), but this game really gave me the opportunity to see the possibilities in action.  For instance, I was playing it cool and neutral, minding my own business and left Sokov to mercilessly extract oil and pollute his own islands. His business, not mine, right? And then this MFin' tornado showed up and destroyed everything on its path diagonally across the map including my own beautiful and serene (and neutral) islands. This is when I decided No More Mr Nice Guy and during rebuilding I took a different approach. I started investing resources into bigger military power and took an aggressive stance towards everyone who messed with Mother Nature. Sokov had to go. And he did. Thousands of my dead citizens were avenged and there was peace again. Peace and prosperity and clean air. And it felt good.

Anno 2070 is a good game with a very nice blend of city building and fighting. I wish the campaign was longer but it was fun to do and the storyline drew me in, that is the point and it worked. You could get lost in the game and easily spend many hours before you realize it, anouther tell tale of a good game.   Now for the inevitable downsides, if you buy this game I would suggest doing it through Ubisoft, nothing against Steam, but when you buy that way (like I did) it takes forever to start the game. First it has to go through steam, then activate U-play and finally start the game (side note, if either one has a problem starting you have to start all over). Second would be the build interface. When you go to place something in build mode it is jumpy and what you are placing will randomely change directions on you causing you to place it wrong, without an undo button your left with destroying it and redoing it again.  This is neither good or bad but I wish you had the option of starting as the techs. You have to start with either Tycoon or eden and wait to get the techs later. If not then at least let us build underwater before you get to techs.

Yes, the game isn't great, but it also is terrible like everyone makes it out to be. Many reviewers over exaggerated this game's terribleness. The reality is that with nearly 13 years of hype, I can't say I blame them though. Duke Nukem Forever is an 'okay' game. It's no 5 star blockbuster like everyone wanted, and it's sure as hell not the pile of trash people are claiming it is. The gameplay is 'okay', there's nothing special to it appart from the item system featuring cans of beer and steroids. Graphically, this game is horribly outdated; games from 2008 looked better than this. The story is terrible but then again, it's Duke Nukem; were you really expecting Metal Gear Solid quality story telling? The game's only saving grace is Duke Nukem himself. He's a loveable character even in these tough times. Spewing out silly one liners and owning a business empire centered around himself has made him even cooler.Jon St. John returns once again to play the voice of Duke and as always does an amazing job at portraying him. Now that the initial wave of hate has passed over, I can safely say that this game is worth giving a try if it's on sale or if one of your buddies own it.

* Started as a random count in Ireland in the 8th century.  * By 800 I had become King of Ireland.  * By 1000 I had established the Empire of Britain.  * By 1100 I had conquered all of France.  * By 1150 I had taken control of all of Italy and crowned myself Holy Roman Emperor.  * By 1160, I had launched a war on the Byzantine Empire, won, installed a Catholic on the Byzantine throne and by 1190 the Orthodox Church had been completely eradicated. * By 1200 I had inherited the Byzantine Empire after marrying its Empress, and promptly established myself as the true Roman Emperor.  * By the game's end, the Irish Roman Empire controls the entirety of Western Europe and North Africa all the way up to Lithuania, Somalia and Persia, has entirely reversed the Arab conquests and seized control of Mecca and Medina (confining the Muslims to Eastern Arabia and Pakistan), and has even established several trade posts in India, effectively beginning the colonial period several hundred years early. Technology is incredibly advanced, heresies are spreading like wildfire and nobody really seems to care, so effectively the Enlightenment has begun several hundred years early as well. To top it all off, Iceland, the British Isles, most of France, most of North Africa and most of Hungary are now fully Irish.  This is just one of the things you can do in this game. Just one. Do I recommend? YES.  (Lag gets a bit bad in the late game, apparently because all the Greek characters are using up processing power trying to figure out who they can castrate out of all the characters in the game. Yes, you read that right - the game runs slow because the Greeks are trying to castrate the entire world. Other than that simultaneously annoying and hilarious problem, the game is great!)

I love the Mad Max movies, and my love for Mad Max got reinjected with Fury Road.   With the game, I first noticed that it was developed by Avalanche Studios, a swedish game studio known for Just Cause-series. One of the absolutelty best Open World experience there is. So with the Mad Max game, I get the best of two worlds, being able to play and live as Mad Max and enjoy the wasteland as an open world.  My first experience with Mad Max was with TotalBiscuits PC-Port Report, and after that I decided to get the game.   So after a +25h of gameplay I am very glad that I did purchase the game, because it is really worth every little $$$ spent on it. The game is so rich of content, even if some may find it very repetitive, but I love it. I love Open World and I love exploring. The graphics is really amazing, and I can't wait for Just Cause 3. Even if I run the game with a 660 TI OC, I can still cramp up the graphics quality. I run smoothly at 60FPS.   The gameplay itself I find very enjoyable, I fínd a good balance between fighting with vehicle and fighting on foot. I love the fact that it really takes time to save up scrap (money). It is really amusing to just drive around and doing silly stuff or just waste time. I do agree that the story telling aint that strong, compared to other games, but I don't really see that is what Avalanche Studios wanted with this game, they wanted a beautiful Open World game with so much content instead. It wouldn't even surprise me if this was a intended joke, to other games in the genre.   It is above average, it is, but for me, I really do enjoy it, and I do not regret buying this game at all. It is worth all it's money.

For anyone who misses L4D, don't read this, buy the game.  It plays very similar: 4 player co-op, special Skaven with similar abilities as the infected attempting to disrupt your positioning, health and special items scattered around randomly to help you carry on.  In combat, it's much more melee-centric. Ammunition is limited, and many classes are built with stronger melee in mind, so their ranged choices are for dealing with special Skaven quickly, and less effective against the hordes. Waywatcher, Witch Hunter, and Bright Wizard have weapons that can make crowd control easier. The specialization of each character improves on the L4D model by not giving everyone the same vulnerabilities and dependant on the same strategies for survival.  I've yet to play all the missions available, yet, but there appears to be more campaigns than L4D + L4D2 + DLC combined. In addition, there's a simple loot and reward system that takes mere moments to manage between missions, and in most cases, you'll directly go to another mission rather than waste time not earning loot for upgrades.  I've been playing near-nonstop since release, and yet to feel like it's becoming repetative, despite only having played 1 of the 5 characters.

This game has only one original thing about it: The Nemesis system. It's really, really interesting, and I can't wait to see other games steal it and use it.  Everything else is cribbed from other games, most notably the Assassin's Creed and Arkham franchises.  I want to be clear, though, that I don't think that's a bad thing. Everything here works together well, and was very enjoyable.  This is probably in my top ten for 2014, and I really wasn't expecting more than mediocrity.   My only complaints are plot/story based. I'm not a huge fan of the main character, and the closed system of the LotR cycle makes me feel like a real payoff isn't possible.  What I'd really like to see in a sequel is treating it like Rocksteady's Arkham games, where the world and characters are obviously Batman, etc, but it's not beholden to the continuity of any particular movies or comics or whatever.  Also, for the love of god some character development for Talion. Please. I'm not saying 'forget about your tragedies and go out wenching', but time should begin to heal wounds.

As the titular Styx you'll be facing several missions to find out why your memory is so jumbled and how to stop your ability to seemingly hear what most people are thinking, a thing that is driving you mad: while every area is full of enemies you'll count on your skills in stealth and assassination to even the odds. Master of Shadows is a bit of a mixed bag with its good stealth action inside huge levels paired with clunky jumping/platforming, forgettable graphics and atrocious open combat: it's still quite fun if you can forgive its shortcomings.   PROS:   -Styx is an interesting character, a true antihero stopping at nothing to reach his goals: he also has a nasty sense of humour  -the character models are pretty detailed  -Styx's voice actor does a very good job  -the levels are huge with several floors, shortcuts and small places only you can fit in, this promotes exploration and gives you many choices when planning your route to the objective  -good amount of enemy variety  -the actual stealth mechanics are pretty good and less forgiving than other games, shadows only partially hide you rather than making you invisible and a muffled kill can still be heard if someone is near your location: the expansive levels really add lots of ways to stealth your way through like using small tunnels, hiding under tables, jumping on high ledges etc  -you'll be able to unlock several skills and upgrades, the clone ability is the best of the lot letting you manipulate things, poison food or create a distraction without putting yourself in harm's way  -quite long at 15+ hours   CONS:   -graphics are weak overall, they look muddy and aren't very detailed  -jumping and hanging on ledges feel very awkward: jumps lack some precision while hanging on ledges require you to walk slowly towards their border but doesn't always work. This can get frustrating in certain areas and/ or ruin your stealth thus forcing you to quickload and try again  -open combat is bad as you're locked in a quicktime event where you have to parry X amount of times before you can deliver a killing blow, if more enemies are present you'll still take hits while fighting one of them. You're basically better off just reloading the save  -enemy AI is hit or miss, they'll sometimes notice you right away or hear you dropping on the floor even from a decent distance but other times they'll just get stuck on a wall or not respond properly to your actions  -areas are recycled in later levels, despite the different enemies and patrol routes this repetitiveness gets dull  -the overall plot is a bit too murky to be interesting and suffers from pacing problems  -last boss encounter is honestly 'meh' at best   http://steamcommunity.com/sharedfiles/filedetails/?id=520136655   Styx: Master of Shadows looks dated while you play it and some of its design choices fell old aswell ( like having to unlock kills from corners, from above etc): there's no denying that the game offers an enjoyable and challenging stealth experience though. As a goblin your combat prowess is questionable at best, but your small size, agility and bag of tricks will let you move unseen while leaving a long trail of bodies if you're so inclined. It's also refreshing to see that, being an antihero, Styx has no problems with killing enemies that'd be classed as civilians in other games: unless a mission restricts you in some way you're free to do as you wish. The huge levels are also nicely designed and you'll always have several routes to your objectives, some more risky than others.  I got Styx for 8$ on sale and, for the price, it offered an enjoyable and lengthy experience despite its noticeable flaws and perhaps being a bit too long. Would I recommend it? It really depends on how much you like stealth and how patient you'll be with its blemishes: if you're ok with avoiding combat, having to resort to quicksave/ quickload to make sure clunky controls don't get in your way and the fact the later levels can get somewhat dull then go for it when it's on sale to enjoy its proper stealth and challenging missions.

NEStalgia is an MORPG that takes me back to the early days of my gaming life. You play in a world very similar to Dragon Warrior, with turn-based battles, quest chains, and multiplayer parties to adventure and fight with. You can recruit monsters to fight with you and solo if that is your preference. The servers are small in scale and there are cliques, but people seem friendly. Expansions and updates are free, after you purchase the full game, which is always a good deal for a fun game. You also host private servers just for your friends. If you enjoy retro RPG's, you are going to love this game.

Mass Effect 2 continues the epic, cinematic sci-fi action-RPG story which you began in the original game. You *did* play the original, didn't you? If you did, dozens of choices you made will have consequences large and small in the sequel. Even though the first game is less polished, it's worth starting there. It's so rewarding hearing your achievements discussed by NPCs in ME2, or even having those actions take your story in a new direction.  Play the first one, and then fall in love with Mass Effect 2- a milestone in RPG action games.

app_name: Valiant Hearts: The Great War™ / Soldats Inconnus : Mémoires de la Grande Guerre™
review_text: My summary: BUY IT! Valiant Hearts is a perfect blend of story, diverse gameplay, and puzzle solving. Rooted in historical events, it is a powerful game that can move you to tears. You follow a story which separates father and son on two different sides of the war. Joining them, a US soldier with nothing to live for (save his friend Emile), a French field-medic trying to reach her father, and a loyal dog separated from its owner. They will all struggle in the war until the end, and no one will ever be the same again.  Pros: I really want to say my favorite game of all time but I think that would be going too far. I think it may be my favorite indie-esque 2d game of recent memory, perhaps only with Bastion and To The Moon as competitors. What I do like about it is it has less gameplay than bastion but more gameplay than To The Moon. For me, it is the perfect mix of the two, and does both well.  Cons:  In general some of the puzzles are so not-obvious it becomes frustrating but they do provide a neat optional hint system. The difficulty is also appreciated rather than dumbing them down completely. Additionally a few elements like the medic revive functions (just mashing buttons in guitar hero fashion) is a bit of a downer to such an exciting game, but it is a very minor component of the game.  Story (9.5/10): Amazing, there are very few video games that can compare. I have a few minor complaints about it but they aren't worth mentioning and I do not want to spoil the surprises along the way.  Art (9.5/10): Really unique and awesome, my ONLY complaint is that it can become a but blurry and pixelated when some of the sprites zoom in during cinematics.  Sound (10/10): From the mutterings in every language to the music to the narration this couldn't possibly be better.  Gameplay (9/10) Because of a few annoying puzzles that are almost intentionally obscure and the medical revive system which is bashing buttons, I can't give this a perfect score. But everything else about it is perfect. Seriously, it feels so good and just when you think a mechanic is getting old, you get put in a tank, car, or are dodging mines or gun fire.

Levels are short enough to pick up or end the game at a moment's notice, and with hundreds of levels as well as a survival mode, you'd be hard-put to say anything but that One Finger Death Punch offers extensive gameplay. Played for a few hours now. Likely won't be bothering to finish it.  Good things about it: with proper hardware, have not experienced any crashes at all. No apparent glitches (things that aren't supposed to happen).  Things I'm not thrilled with:  - chromatics? wearies the eyes - checkpoints? well, you can get used to them, but being forced to replay a hard-won fight just doesn't cut it for me - overly complex attribute system, at least for me. Some might enjoy this sort of thing. Boosting my attributes did not really seem to matter as I progressed in the game, causing me to feel I wasted my time on those screens. - weak dialogue. If you're going to add interaction, then add it, don't just make a stab at it.  Things that put me off this game:  - KEEP THE DAMN CAMERA FOCUSED!!!! I cannot hit an enemy if I cannot see him because the camera goes from a horizontal view to a vertical view. Have it back away so you can see the field AND your opponent. - my character moves (during fights only) like he's drunk. Reaction time sucks (and that is with weight being 1/4 of maximum capacity). - weapon's reach is too short (I've got a LONGsword, why doesn't it hit my opponent unless I'm standing on his toes???!!). - too many keys for different moves. A light attack key and a heavy attack key? Why not just hold the attack key down a bit longer to initiate a heavy attack? I'm killing a monster; hit it hard, kill it fast. These aren't duels, I don't get points for style, only survival. - being able to see would be nice. In the area of the monastery, I really do appreciate how accurately the environment of a destroyed, deserted monastery was recreated. BUT, I only detected some of the infected because their health bars appeared. Torches, lanterns, something would have been useful to see where things were. I played in a very dark room and still could not see half of what was around me. Hard to explore when you don't know what's nearby.  My hardware consists of a 4-core CPU running at 4.3GHz, 24GB of memory, and an nVidia GTX970 w/4GB DDR5. I use only a keyboard and mouse.  Remember these are just my opinions after some hours of playing this game. Check out the gameplay videos on YouTube, and decide for yourself if you want to give it a try. Personally I think there are more rewarding games out there.

(Review for Albion Prelude Addon since it's awesome. Terran Conflict has more story but is less optimized. Also my hours sank into this game at release, before steam)  Forget about half ♥♥♥ casual games like Elite: Dangerous. This is the best space game so far. It has everything you'd want: A LOT of different ships to fly, exploration, itemization, complex economy, several factions to interact with different personalities and likes/dislikes, build / take space stations, stuff to do / explore even late in the game and a huge amount of freedom in what to do. Many different items and customisations for your ship or goods mean to read about them. Some information is not easy to find at first, but it's worth it. It's a deep and complex space-/fight-/trade simulation which is it's strongest point.   What I'd want from x4: Stay the same. Be complex, be deep! We have enough of the shallow crossover plattform games with the fly-in-emptyness-and-don't-do-much-at-all ♥♥♥♥. 8/10  This War of Mine is a fantastic new take on the survival genre. I really love the game and have played 4 games already. The campaign is short, about 8-10 hours of gameplay, but by war's end you are left with a great feeling of accomplishment. My only complaint is that the combat controls still feel clunky, and can bug out on you from time to time. The game could also use some more options like a character creator, and the option to continue playing after the war ends in a sandbox mode. It would be nice to experience the city with open stores and interact with your neighbors after relief supplies get distributed. It would be a good way to witness the devastation and also a way to get all your characters back on their feet.   Considering the patching that has already occurred, I can say this dev will have the game a 10/10 eventually. Until then I think it deserves a solid 8 out of 10, and totally worth the price of $20.  Here's to hoping they add in a multiplayer feature, with combined houses, raid defense mechanics, and multiple scavengers during the night phase.

app_name: METAL GEAR SOLID V: THE PHANTOM PAIN
review_text: Gods, I could talk for days about how much I resent loving Metal Gear Solid 5, how glad I am that Kojima won't be making another one, and how excited I am about the prospect of a MGS6 without him at the helm, but I will spare you. Cutting to the chase: MGS5 is the best stealth action game on the market, and it is worth full price, but there are several caveats:  - About halfway through the game it adds a pretty robust management sim. Great if you like management sims (and I do), not so great if you were hoping to spend all of your time playing an uninterrupted stealth action game. The sim walls off upgrades that affect the stealth action part of the game, so engaging it is not what I would call optional.  - After completing the stealth action part of the game, the sim becomes tedious quickly, because its most important timers are based on the in-game clock, not real time. Part of the reason I have so many hours logged is because I would just let it run while I was at work to try to make some progress. Note that you cannot boot the game and Alt+Tab out to do something else; Alt+Tabbing pauses the game and the timers.   - One of these important timers is the one that manages the diminishing returns on repeatable campaign missions. The missions are your best source of in-game currency, and being unable to repeat them for full rewards makes late-game unlockable progression extremely tedious.  - The Metal Gear Solid story continues to be insane. Ridiculous characters are thrust into even more ridiculous situations, and nothing in the plot follows logically. Magic is now so commonplace in the setting that Kojima apparently no longer even feels the need to handwave it as pseudoscience. The game does a great job of sustaining tension throughout, but it never resolves. You may have heard that the story is incomplete -- it is, but it is important to note that this does not matter. Completing this story would not have saved it from itself. Play this one for the gameplay, not the story.   - The game uses an algorithm that analyzes your playstyle and reacts accordingly, making it harder to play the game the way you prefer to play. This would make sense for, say, a combat training simulator, but as this is a game, the result is pretty frustrating after a point. If you like non-lethal sniping, you will eventually reach a level in the game where every enemy is wearing a helmet and a riot suit, even when you complete the management sim objectives necessary to cut off helmet and riot suit supply lines. Challenge is all well and good, but how about letting me play the game I bought the way I want to play it?  - The open world does nothing to improve the gameplay. Stealth action is by its nature linear -- get from Point A to Point B, achieve Objective C, and extract at Point D, all without being detected. There is not a single mission in the game that is not made easier by the player's ability to approach Point B from any Point A they choose. Those few missions in the game that are genuinely challenging are challenging largely because the game removes this choice, rendering the open world irrelevant. This game is not Grand Theft Auto or Saints Row -- it is not a dynamic environment. It is two handfuls of heavily-defended mission locations separated by vast tracts of beautiful but completely empty wilderness.  - If you accept the game's ToS, it becomes always-online and your base is under constant threat of auto-opt-in PvP resource theft. In my experience, if you don't increase your PvP rating by stealing other players' resources, you can generally avoid being raided, because you will stay in a lower bracket where no one is raiding. This is no guarantee, however. You can refuse the ToS, but once you accept them you cannot rescind your acceptance, so it is important to know this going in.  -Cheating is rampant, even in the automated sim league, which is sort of like fantasy football with private military corporations. Who cheats at fantasy football? These guys.  In short, the gameplay is excellent but Kojima's bizarre design decisions cast long shadows, just like they always do. If you know what you're getting into from the start, and it sounds like it might be worth a shot, I say spend the $60.

I am a fan of Puzzle Agent. It's a point-and-click adventure game, but the story weaves in 20 logic puzzles that match the theme of the game.  There's not a lot of pointing and clicking -- mostly, the game moves along quickly from puzzle to puzzle.  What's interesting, though, is that the storyline is surprisingly creepy. It's like a very good episode of The X-Files (or Fringe, for you youngsters out there). This is not good material for little kids. It gets a bit intense.  The puzzles in Puzzle Agent aren't terribly difficult, which I think is a good thing for this game. Huge roadblocks would only get in the way. Instead, the adventure and the puzzles create a framework for a pretty good story, and it moves along at a good clip. If you want killer difficult puzzles, look elsewhere.

app_name: DARK SOULS™ II: Scholar of the First Sin
review_text: After finishing Dark Souls III I wanted a little more so when I saw Dark Souls II: SotFS up for sale I jumped on it and for the most part I'm glad I did.  First I want to talk about the port, which is great this time, the complete opposite of Dark Souls I: Prepare To Die on PC which we all know has a laundry list of issues and is only made somewhat playable by DSFix but I'm happy to say that's not the case here, I was getting a locked 60fps throughout on max settings and I didn't have a single technical issue in over 40 hours. The game looks, sounds and runs really nicely.  Content wise it's a big game with the included DLC, I actually still have two optional bosses left to kill and I've sunk 43 hours into it already so you're getting plenty of bang for your buck (I'd highly recommend shopping around, I picked it up for £9.59). On the whole the game is enjoyable in a Dark Souls sort of way, but there were a few areas that went past 'Dark Souls' and just ended up being extremely tedious, not hard or challenging just plain boring and those areas did tarnish the overall game for me a bit, but thankfully there's only a few truly poorly designed areas in the game so in the grand scheme of things it's not too bad.  I found the bosses to be on the whole too easy, the base game bosses especially. I killed every boss in under 5 tries apart from a select few. The DLC bosses are much harder in general though, and the final boss of part II is currently kicking my butt after many tries, he's by far the hardest boss in the game and one of the hardest I've faced in the 'Soulsborne' universe [EDIT: He's down!]. I haven't been using phantoms or summons either, so I imagine if you do get truly stuck using these options will help you out a lot.  For me it's worth playing if you enjoy these types of games, I actually put it above Dark Souls I purely because of the quality of the port, it was just more enjoyable to play without all the issues, but it's a few pegs behind both Bloodborne and Dark Souls III which is the top of the pile IMO.

DS2 was better designed and overall a much harder game but this still a great game for the most part. It has better graphics and some of the areas are better designed than prior games in the series but I feel that the other edition of Dark Souls did a better job in every other department.  One thing I should add is that if you're buying this game for long term use I think the other DS games are a better choice because in this NG+ consists of no extra content, they simply added about 40% extra health and damage to all enemies and that's it. Very, very disappointing and lazy way to 'increase difficulty' without any actual effort.  If late game PvP is your goal then you may want to go back to DS2 as well because the servers here are laggier than any Dark Souls game prior except perhaps Dark Souls 1 release servers. There is no lag compensation here which means you'll have to forward your swings and hope that your opponent runs into it. This gives the PvP combat the feeling that it isn't very responsive. Every time I get a kill on someone I feel that I didn't deserve it and when I die I feel the same. That doesn't isn't a conductive feeling for a competitive PvP environment.  Aside from these negative things the actual graphics of Dark Souls 3 have vastly improved over Dark Souls 1 and 2 although I would say level design wise DS3 is very off and on. Some areas are superb and others feel like they were thrown together to annoy the player and weren't thought out properly.  Movement feels more responsive and jumping is easier now in Dark Souls 3. Dodge rolling is smoother than ever.  Combat is about the same except I feel that almost all weapon combat sets have been messed with disasterously. The only weapon I actually feel comfortable using is straight sword but most (not all) other weapons have had their attacks changed drastically. Some weapons that used to be the best at clearing groups of mobs have had their attacks changed to mainly straight downward slashes now and to be frank it makes me not want to use any other weapons.  In short, if you enjoyed Dark Souls 1 and 2 then you'll certainly enjoy this, you're just going to be disappointed in certain aspects of the game, but to be honest even with these negative features the game is still absolutely worth playing.

When I first saw this game, I was not very excited, 'bullet hell' tends to ring a no with me because I find it a chore. But this game pleasantly surprised me and I did not think that it would be such an emotional or pleasant ride from start to finish. Bullet hell was doable and was not a huge drag on the atmosphere, it fit quite well with trying to figure out how to passively defeat foes. This take on the 'lost child in mysterious world of creatures' never gets old in Undertale because you are not truly tasked with being a hero or leaning to chaotic-- it is very much on you as the player to make these subtle choices that affect the gameplay. I really enjoyed how the player character is left androgynous and 'silent' which usually gives me a greater slate and connection to the world and the characters.  Some people may have issues with characters being annoying or some other negative term, but I really don't see it. For a game of this length, sure there is not huge amounts of development on the surface; however these characters really do shine when you complete various routes or pay attention to small visual cues therein. It doesn't need to be overall complicated with the sort of game that allows you to be a pacifist or to destroy everything in your path. Many of your reactions and interactions will be different based on your tendency to be violent or befriend. For me, I could not and will not be doing a genocide run and I felt like I got my full money's worth from this title. The presentation will have you a bit confused at first, but this entire world and the set-up grows on you and you will find yourself in the feels department before you hit the big finale.

OVERVIEW Ace Of Words is a casual, anagram-esque game developed by Zerobit Games and published by Ocean Media LLC. With an easy interface, and an appealing look. The concept is just to try to spell as many words as possible with 6-8 letters available. From there you can spell up to 100 words and you have 120 seconds to get them all.  PROS + Easy Achievements   Guide To Achievements   + Steam Trading Cards + Steam Leaderboards + Colorful + Easy Concept + Fun  CONS - Overpriced - Not A Big Enough Dictionary - The Highscore Resets Everytime You Restart Game - Looks And Plays Like A Mobile Port  CONCLUSION This game is not a big hit nor is it a miss. I have heard of some people having malware problems with this game but I have not experienced that. It is tons of fun, and has a solid concept that is executed fairly well. They just need to work on the dictionary aspect, its a small dictionary that is missing plenty of words and plurals. For example, the word 'true' is not in it, nor is 'toilets'. Yet it has ' Hitler ' and ' Trump '. Also $5 for a game with such a small dictionary is absurd. I am not recommending this because I think it is not worth the price, and has too small of a dictionary.  3.5/10

Like Adr1ft, Breached is a combat-free scavenger hunt in which players must sluggishly traverse some stunning scenery in search of (1) supplies and (2) a story. Breached takes things a few steps further, in that there are three different zones to explore (a canyon, seashore, and crater), and that the game is divided into a limited number of days, each of which is preceded by the opportunity to read a hyperlinked series of journal entries, digging down into the cause of the accident that resulted in the protagonist's current situation, as well as the harsh external environment.   Unfortunately, the gameplay boils down to a repetitive series of tasks; on each day's two potential excursions, players will be dropped by one of the extraction points and given free rein to harvest up to three supplies. Alpha, Beta, and Gamma minerals are required in order to play a logic-based synthesizing game, where players must use trial and error (and their limited quantities of each mineral) to come up with a nine-element combination that'll replenish their fuel. Players must also find the often hard-to-reach capsules, in the hopes of breaking down these artifacts for the parts needed to repair the oxygen system.   The problem is that the gameplay makes all of this excessively unenjoyable. The first-person drones piloted by the protagonist accelerate very slowly and are often stopped by the slighest collision with an object, even one that's out of view. The electromagnetic anomalies that are the only 'lifeform' wandering the desert (and deserted) planet are consequently difficult to avoid, especially the yellowish ones, which seemingly have the ability to tractor-beam a player, resulting in a wasted excursion. The Canyon level is especially arduous to navigate, as there are no clear routes to follow, only a positional tracker that points players toward the nearest object of interest in a 1000m range, regardless of whether it's reachable.   I wanted to enjoy Breached, but too much of my experience within the game felt like dumb luck, and I had little inclination to battle with the controls for future excursions. The story is interesting, but not enough so; better to play a more polished title, like The Fall.

app_name: Higurashi When They Cry Hou - Ch.3 Tatarigoroshi
review_text: After playing this, I could definitely see why many tout this as one of the best installments of the Higurashi no Naku Koro Ni series.  The series is known for pulling the rug under your feet with mood changes from cutesy to the horrific, often bordering on whiplash between the two, but this one in particular makes a bleak and hopeless portrayal of domestic abuse the main setpiece for the story, the point upon which everything unfurls this time in Hinamizawa. Ryukishi is said to have been a social worker for a time before writing Visual Novels, and in this episode you can really get a glimpse of the squalor he must have seen from his time there. The feeling of powerlessness against an injustice that thrives off bureaucracies' dogmatic adherence to regulations, the futility of going against those regulations with good intentions leading to the worst of outcomes, How people break under abuse, abandonment and betrayal. It's a very different tone from the previous works that is really heavy.   Between the bleakness however there are also some really emotionally stirring scenes, especially the bonding scenes between Keiichi and Satoko. The development of Keiichi and Satoko's relationship from something like friendly rivals to a love shared between siblings, paralleling her relationship with her actual brother who is missing is done really well and... The night time walking and conversations about nothing in particular, just enjoying the small periods of life where the good times knowing they won't last forever between the gang... Beautiful moments as dark rumbling machinations lurk just on the brink... You experience all the joys and terrors with them... Ryukishi finds a way to charge into your heartstrings and pulls at them, and really shows us how good he is at writing a story.  There are a few little hiccups here and there in the presentation. Music isn't as varied as it could be, though each track used is used to good effect, maybe a typo here and there (I believe I recall a her pronoun used when they were referring to a male character), and there's something about a sprite being removed, which honestly doesn't affect the overall work that much. One could say its addition served an important purpose as a characters view of another changes right at that moment, and it drives the point home, but you can still get the sentiment even with it gone I think.  All these aside, it's a really great entry into a great series. Highly recommended for all who enjoyed previous entries, and if you are somehow reading this without reading the previous episodes, you really ought to go and read them. They're all good.

Don't ever build any foot units or archers, even if you need to scale a wall with ladders or a siege tower, just let the horses run up it (trust me they can if you give them enough hay). That is a sad truth that both players and the C&C series itself have been stripped off of its well deserved proper ending. Thus dies C&C from house Westwood.  Adding injury to insult the new hybrid of a game is not even enjoyable to play. Tactics and strategy were limited to just positioning your mobile base and produce produce produce units. No money means just keeping the number of units at maximum and trying to utilize their not so useful abilities to win a mini skirmish after another in hope of reaching the mobile (sic!) enemy base to destroy it. The process gets boring after an hour of gameplay. On easy, the AI is practically dormant. On normal, computer produces units all the time that it becomes overwhelming.  I won't rate multiplayer as I dont care about it. I wanted a good and fitting story to end a legendary plot. What did we get? A slap in the face. They might as well just put the credits at the beginning and be done with it. A MAJOR disappointment. I hope someone picks up the torch where C&C 3 left it. Kane WILL be back.

You play as the original Big Daddy of Rapture, trying to take back the first Little Sister from Mother Lamb. The game just feels like a continuation that did not need to happen. But aside from that, it is an improvement from the previous title.  First of all, hacking is a lot more convinient and easier than the 1st. I used to never hack because of how difficult it was, but now hacking is do-able. You now have a drill, which is a lot cooler than a monkey wrench. And you get more audio tapes to learn more about what happened before the Rapture chaos.  Also, there are crashes again, so you NEED to solve them from Googling.  Overall: I have to give this 7/10 It's a great game, a continuation of what happened, but there is no huge improvement from the previous game. It's great to get a better understand of what the hell happened to Rapture, but bioshock 2 could have just been a short expansion from bioshock 1. I'd still recommend this if you enjoyed the first bioshock, but don't expect to see something amazing.

Bioshock Infinite is worth the money at all times, sale or not. Although it is part of the Bioshock Universe you should have no trouble getting along with the story (unless you play the DLCs, maybe) because it takes place in a new setting with a new story; You're Booker DeWitt, the false Shepherd, and you are in Columbia, a floating city in the year 1912.  The Gameplay is what you would expect from a singleplayer shooter: Walk the path, enjoy the scenery, look for items, shoot bad guys. And Bioshock offers this in an insanely good way, with a story and lore that really entertained me. From the ultranationalistic and christian extremist columbians to the Vox Populi, everything about the world entertained me, and even if you don't like that kind of story, the artstyle provides enough to keep you entertained, at least if you like the american Stars and Stripes aesthetic and 1910s-1930s look.  Really recommend this game, 10/10, it deserved the game of the year award.

My boyfriend got me to try this game last November. He has played it for years, so I had him to help me along the way. Once I got used to the movements and game play I had a lot of fun with it, and soon became very interested in the story line. (Killing Floor Wiki page offers a lot of information, it's great!) There are 6 different perks you can play as - I usually choose berserker because I like using the melee weapons. You can level up other perks no matter what perk you choose, so I've found the best strategy for me is to play as a berserker (you run faster, can't be grabbed by clots, etc) then buy some sharpshooter weapons to level up my sharpshooter perk.   All in all it's a very entertaining game with different maps, and different modes...so there is enough of a variety to keep things interesting for awhile. Sometimes we will play in objective mode for awhile and just keep killing the 'mutants' until we feel we've earned enough kills. I just hope they still do the Hillbilly Horror theme this Halloween, since I haven't played it yet and it looks fun!

This game!  So addicting and painful and addicting and painful.  I have never been able to experience the things I have in CK2 in any other game I've played in my life.  You really take on a dynasty view, you are playing as a family throughout history, not as a nation, general or single badass and it changes everything.  I loved the way wars were fought (and not fought) diplomacy feels very real and the use of medieval legal systems to justify wars and claims was very well done.  I have been in so many insane situations that just had to be real and feel like I can connect to the kings of old, struggling to live another few years into my old age so that my second son (first died of sickness) can turn 16.  The kingdom cant afford another regency like the one before it that sent my Kingdom into civil war...and with that greedy Roman Emperor always lusting after my glorious kingdom I cant take any risks.  This is a game for longterm planning, investing, scheming and manouvering around your opponents...and sometimes losing.  Its ok to lose sometimes. Especially if your boss has a problem with you. Sometimes its best to give up land without a fight if its a fight you cant win. Gunpoint is a 2D puzzle game where the basic goal on each level is to avoid/kill guards and hack a computer, then escape. The main gameplay mechanic involves rewiring switches and electronic devices. For example, if you need to open a locked door, you can link a light switch to the door so that it opens when you press the switch. As the levels progress you can create lots of complex solutions. Walk in front of a surveillance camera to summon an elevator which sets off a sound detector which opens a trap door causing a guard to fall to his death. The story revolves around corporate espionage and murder, and the whole game has a great sense of humour. Character dialogue is text only, and the graphics and sound are basic, but the music is pretty good. Unfortunately Gunpoint is quite short. I finished in under 3 hours, although you can make choices that change how the story plays out which adds some replay value. Its definitely worth playing, but perhaps best to pick it up on sale.
