Ignore the complaints about Dante's re-design. While it's true cosmetically he's changed the vast majority of his personality remains unscathed. Except rather than being arrogant and cocky in the manner of an anime badass his dialogue is a lot more natural and far less forced. The same can be said for his appearance with him looking a lot more human, both of these factors made it easier to empathise with Dante while witnessing his origin story. I think for all but a small niche the new Dante is likeable, relatable and much more natural as a character.  The game plays in a similar manner to other action games of this genre, throwing a group of enemies at the player and encouraging them to combo their way through them to advance the story. The better the combos the more rewards along the way. This works really well thanks to two mechanics, switching weapons mid-combo in DMC is very quick and simple allowing the user to swap weapon sets in real time at the push of a button. While this is a series staple DMC adds greater emphasis to the mechanic with enemies that can only be killed by certain weapons, something that encourages players to think tactically about how they approach a melee. The other can only be described as a god send for the series, in a similar manner to DMC4's 'Devil Bringer' players are given a weapon early on to drag enemies towards them so as to keep combo's going and create a faster flow. The twist this time round is that there's also a weapon to drag Dante TO the enemies which doesn't sound like much but married with the previously mentioned 'X weapon kills X enemy' system this creates a small ecos ystem the player has to manage in each battle. Just hacking away will not wash in DMC. In what has become a staple of the action genre now the game also features your typical 'rage mode' which allows Dante to regain health while dishing out extra damage, against regular enemies this more will also slow down time and launch all the enemies into the air for you. It feels a bit like a get out of jail free card on the first run through but on higher difficulties you'll be thankful for the few sweet mnoments of down-time before the bloodbath resumes.  One minor caveat to this though (one that is not so minor for the more hardcore DMC follwers) is that the average level of difficulty has been nerfed. While this is dissapointing for some it never mars the experience on the first run and with harder difficulties available after completing the game it's not a big enough issue to put you off the game. Another drawback in this new entry is the camera system, there's no lock on option available and while this isn't so bad as you can work around it it does make it unnecessarily difficult early on when trying to target particular enemies.  For many players Ninja Theory's decision to use UE3 was a strange move, on consoles at least the game is capped to 30fps a bold move by any action game let alone it's founding series. Thankfully this not an issue on PC as the framerate has been unlocked to reach that silky smooth 60fps and boy does it look fantastic. This is quite easily the best looking game in the series by a long shot with many levels featuring a wide colour palette in the majority of levels (sorry no 'UE3 browns here). The most enticing aspect of the levels though is how they move and shift as Dante progresses through them, you really get the impression that even the world itself is against Dante in his quest with large chasm's appearing as a building seperates itself into multiple pieces or a plethora of deadly buzz-saws appear as if out of nowhere. This is made even more epic by the fact that Dante is able to traverse these new dangers freely with the weapons mentioned earlier allowing him to drag himself accross the map or pull platforms out of walls.  While the overall story isn't much to write home about it is engaging enough the first time through but not something you'll be talking about in years to come, or even weeks later. It does present it's characters well with all the main characters avoiding the cringeworthy dialogue the series is known for whilest managing to get a few pot shots in on modern culture along the way. All of this is voiced well with Dante and Vergil being the standout performances. The other star on show is the music in what can only be described as a perfect marriage between dubstep and death metal the games soundtrack fits right in and manages to help make each area feel unique.   Despite it's minor flaws and the vocal niche baying for it's blood DMC is a highly enjoyable action game that manages to be accessable to newer players while still providing a good time to series veterans. It doesn't offer up much in terms of replayability other than some achievements and artwork and the expansion DLC while offering a new character mars the experience with a poor plot and recycled enviornments.   In conclusion if you're an experienced fan of action games or a newbie looking to get into the genre you're likely to have a great time with DMC.

Reus is simple enough to get into for a casual half-hour game here and there, but if you are a big fan of strategy games then the resource development trees offer enough complexity to keep you coming back to better your scores and get more achievements (unlocking more things to place/build).   I hit a wall after about 8 hours when I first started playing Reus and left it alone for a while (a recent splurge meant I had other exciting new titles to switch to). It was a few months before I came back. I found the Walkthrough page on  wiki.reusgame.com  gave me enough pointers on my return to properly get into more advanced town-building tactics, focussing on using the symbiotic relationships a bit better. I do still have to pause the game a lot to check reference charts, but with regular playing they're becoming more familiar.  I am a big fan of strategy games, and I think if you're not then your interest in Reus may be short lived. Except that I guess if you like god-games for the 'Release Godzilla!' type of actions, then Reus will allow you to build a world and then watch it burn in a variety of ways. And what an attractive looking world it can be.

Warframe is a coop, 3rd person shooter, dungeon running game with various game modes (survival, defence, boss fights, etc.).  I love co-op PvE games in general and Warframe ticks all the boxes for me. It gives you plenty of reasons to replay various missions and keep you busy with unlocking more characters and weapons as you progress through the game.  Being a free game, Warframe has a very fair business model. The weapons in the marketplace is a bit on the expensive side, but most of those weapons can usually be crafted with little effort. The community is very active (at the time of writing) and are usually very friendly if you are looking to buy items or blueprints off other people.  The crafting system in the game feels a bit like a mobile game, where you have to wait hours (sometimes days) for weapons to craft while you can rush it with platinum (premium currency). However, if you plan ahead this shouldn't bother you that much.  The clan system is implemented very nicely, where you can comfortably manage a clan with only a little group of friends or with a large clan (of up to 1000 members). The clan dojo is also a great place to spend time with clan members and feel at home.  I dislike the peer-to-peer multiplayer however, since this can cause quite a bit of lag if you are playing with a group of 4 people on slow connections. There is also a couple of issues with the game servers as the unnessesarily many hot-fixes (up to 4 or 5 a week that I know of) and login errors has cost tons of mission rewards and progress.  The developers also needs to add more content for the archwing part of the game, as it feels a bit incomplete at the moment.  TL;DR: Warframe is a solid coop experience. It showcases a fair mini-transaction based business model. Weapons and warframes are expensive to buy for premium currency, but the in-game crafting system allows you to craft everything without paying a dime. Apart from some connection issues lately, I fully recommend this game to anyone that thinks playing as a space ninja that looks like a rhino armed with a laser pistol, a rocket launcher and a giant fire axe with friends seems fun. And it's free so its worth a try if you are at least curious.

The minds behind Bastion, Supergiant Games ,are back with their newest game: Transistor. If you've played Bastion before, you can clearly see that this is indeed, the same people making a different game. I played this game a few months back, and i still find myself making questions about this game. It's just... Too much to handle.  The world of the game is quite opposite from Bastion. As Bastion features this beautiful, broken world , Transistor gives us this utopian perfect world that on the surface seems absolutely troublefree.  As i quite obviously foreshadowed in the previous sentence, Cloudbank (the so-called perfect city) has it's flaws.  The storyline of this game borrows one thing from Bastion; the game just throws you in the world and gives you a vague hint of the story and let's you figure it all on your own. The game starts with the Camerata trying to assassinate the main character, Red with their weapon, the Transistor. (roll credits please). Emphasis on the word 'attempt' and instead of Red dying, a another character, a nameless man gets killed and stuck in the Transistor.  The Story itself features the revenge for the said assassination attempt and the story reveals more than one side to the motive on the attempt.   A one more thing about the story; prepare to get sad. Whereas Bastion relies pretty hard on the 'everything is going to be fine' attitude on the matters at hand, Transistor does the opposite. Go figure what that means.  The game is very heavily driven on aesthetic side of things and on the absolutely gorgeous soundtrack, that i'll tell you more about in the next part of this review. I purely bought this game on the impulse of seeing this game on the Steam Marketplace (on a sale if i recall right) and i instantly fell in love with the games visual side. After the installation and first try of the game, i instantly fell for the audio side of this game.  Darren Korb (the composer/producer) who worked on Bastion's Original Soundtrack is also back in this one, except two things have changed. The game features a beautiful female vocalist (Ashley Barrett) and instead of Bastion's brighter, cheerier major-sounding tracks, the tracks are clearly very much darker, sadder minor sounds.   I personally find myself having the Transistor soundtrack constantly on loop and i highly recommend you to do the same. But after the finishing the game of course, since the OST's vocals are more or less connected to the storyline.      Without getting too hard to the infamous spoiler-territory: i'll say that after you've played the game, you'll find that the track #23 on the OST hits you really hard.     The mechanics of the game are quite refreshing, the game features this 'pause the game and plan your next move' mechanic that is quite... different from what i've before used in my quite lengthy gaming journey. The game has quite good controller support, but the aiming on the controller isn't that  fine tuned and it usually required me to aim on the mouse. And that sometimes made my game glitch.  The game really enjoys the contrast between a few things. The main character, Red is a singer without a voice and the man trapped inside transistor is a man without a body.  9.5/10 Buy it, Love it , Cry a little bit.  Who am i kidding , cry a river.

The Quick Review:  Though it can be unpredictable at times, I feel as though that adds to the challenge of it. Why bother building another town if you know exactly how it’s going to play out? I’ve enjoyed this game so far. Even only six hours in, I can tell I’m going to get a lot more out of this, because I clocked those six hours in almost one sitting. Plus, the mod community is alive and kicking for this game, so even more game play styles are at my finger tips.  Some quick highlights:  Quick to pick up and learn, but not dumbed down to the point it’s no fun Mods are beyond easy to install, enable/disable, and browse in an in-game menu Using the same strategy each time won’t work for the most part, giving you a different experience each time you play Good time scale/pace, not too slow, not too fast Bobby the stone cutter got crushed by a rock. Damn it Bobby  The Report  One thing in this game that really hurt my soul, but wasn’t the game’s fault, was the time control. During the tutorial it said, “to speed up the time, press F1, and click fast forward.” I sat there in my chair, dumbfounded. I was just waiting for “but it will cost you this many units of this currency you have to buy with real money.” I couldn’t believe it, I was almost in tears. Build a town/city without micro-transactions. I didn’t think that kind of thing existed anymore.  But it does! It does exist, and very well in Banished at that. It’s up to you to get your new settlers living in a stable home. Believe me, it’s not as easy as it sounds sometimes. One time I started a village and had fish coming out of my ears with just two fishermen. Every other settlement I’ve tried to make I can’t get enough fish to save my life … literally, they all starve to death.   That’s the thing about Banished, there are several ways your settlers can die, and it’s no easy task keeping them alive all the time. It is, however, very entertaining. As mentioned in the Quick Review, the user interface is super simple and quick to pick up. It may seem at first as though there won’t be enough to keep you interested, but you’ll soon learn that not having enough different buildings to build is the least of your concerns. You won’t be building new york city overnight either. Even with the time boosted at 10x, things still take a good bit to get done … then winter comes.  There are no enemies to fight other than the fragility of your own settlers’ bodies, so if you’re looking to conquer some land through means of war, you’d best look elsewhere. If you are looking to build a settlement and just try to survive, then I highly recommend this game. It is most definitely a fun one. No DLC (as of now), no missing features promised to be added later, just a game. It’s not perfect, I’ve seen some of the settlers take some questionable routes, but I’ve not seen many games like this where that didn’t happen at least once or twice.   I grabbed this game at 50% off, which was $9.99, and I believe it was worth every penny. Even with a full price of $19.99, I don’t think that’s too bad, considering that other game developers would probably sell this at $40 or $50, and probably take away some features you really need and sell them as DLC.  Wrap Up:  Very fun game, great time killer. Easy to lose track of time while playing and relaxing … most of the time. Be careful with the mods, they can make things way too easy. Of course, that might be good for some folks while not good for others.

app_name: Joe Dever's Lone Wolf HD Remastered
review_text: Epic narration. Superb artwork. Amazing 'living' illustrations.  One of the best and longest adventures of Lone Wolf saga I have ever read. Usually I could finish a normal old Lone Wolf game-book in 3 hours, but this new 4-acts adventure took me about 34 hours! (about the same time I needed to read the first old 10 gamebooks!) So.. ten times of fun for about the same price!! Awesome!!!!   Also, I like so much Joe Dever's voice between chapters. It' s cool. It creates a great epic atmosphere and It gives me the sensation to be in a real role playing game party session with a very (very!) good gamemaster. For this reason I'd love to hear his readings in EACH page of the book! (Maybe a premium gourmet fun edition? :)  I hope next episodes will come soon!!!

Stellaris is a very good game with a few serious flaws that affect, well everything.   The good points:  1. you can design your own race, probably could use more points in traits to actually be able to customize your race a bit more, but still its a good attempt.  2. graphics are quite good when they are working correctly.  3. overall game design is good with a few caveats.  4. you can design your own ships, sort of.   The bad points:  1. As with most 4X games, this one really suffers from a poor AI. I won't go in to details, it would be a very long list.  2. I just started the game and my combat fleet has 4 ships, my neighbor i just met has thousands of ships.  3. Ship design: it would seem like you can custom design your own ships but here are your choices: (depending on the platform) you can put 1 large weapon, 2 medium weapons, or 4 small weapons. Quite often it won't let you build the design you want without pressing the 'auto-complete' button which changes your ship design.  4. Combat: you have no control over your ships once they engage. You put all long range weapons on your ships, guess what ?, you are going to point blank range for fighting.  5. Game killing mid game slow down: has nothing at all to do with how good your system is according to the developers, even on their beast machines they get ridiculous slow down. Rather than build a new platform that uses multi-threading and optimization, they used an older gaming platform that assumes you are only using a single core with limited ram. By turn 200 you are experiencing a massive performance slow down of the game, by turn 300 (if you can actually make it there) the game is unplayable, a single click on anything in game may take 30 or more seconds before it responds, if at all. Making it to end game is all but impossible. So you play for a bit, then start a new game. Frustrating.   So in short:  1. you can design you own race, poorly.  2. you can design you own ships, poorly.  3. you can engage in combat, poorly.  4. you can play the game to a point, then you have to start a new game  If they fix the game slow down issue, this game will be playable. In its current form we are beta testers

If you don't like to read, you can always watch my first look at the game  http://youtu.be/dU-XJjTcqx0     I picked up Ziggurat for two reasons, it was on sale and Totalbiscuit recommended it. I knew it was a first person Rouge-like and that was about it. The thing I did not expect going into the game that really hooked me was the speed of the gameplay. Imagine Quake and you have an idea of the speed I am talking about. Ziggurat is much more a FPS with Rogue-like elements thrown in. On of the tags on the Steam page is Rogue-LITE, which I didn't know was a term, but fits this perfectly. There are just enough mechanics from games like Binding of Isaac thrown in without it getting too carried away. In one sense I miss that, but with the fast twitch shooter gameplay of Ziggurat it fits quite well.  The game is not perfect, enemy variety, and overall polish could be improved. The learning curve is steep as well, and I actually had to turn the difficulty down to make any sort of progress. Overall it is a fun game that combines genres in a way that I have not seen done before and I highly recommend it.

As a preface, I'd HIGHLY recommend you play the first two games in the series before giving this a try. While ZTD can be played as a standalone title, you'll be missing out on a lot of crucial information and you won't enjoy it to its maximum potential.  Another hit in the Zero Escape series which has yet to have a bad (or even mediocre) title. The overall premise is similar to the two previous games: 9 people are stuck in an underground bunker, where they are forced by the antagonist (a peculiar geezer named Zero) to play a game ('the nonary game' in the previous titles, 'the decision game' in ZTD). For each person that dies, a password will be obtained for the X Door, which when provided with 6 passwords, will open and result in freedom for whoever opened it (and whoever passes through in the ten seconds after). This is the basic premise, but the story goes MUCH deeper than this and anyone who has played a Zero Escape title beforehand will understand what I'm saying.  I've personally already completed the 3DS version of ZTD, so I can say with absolute certainty that the story is great. My only criticism comes in the form of the games ending. Without spoiling anything, I believe that if this was meant to be the conclusion to the series, they have left the ending a little open ended (I'm hesitant to call sequel-bait, but it's a distinct possibility). This may result in another game in the series being released (which I'd love), but based on the wait many fans have had to endure for ZTD, it's a distinct possibility that another may not be made. Don't let this put you off, however - the journey through the entire game itself is incredible and it's on par with the previous titles (which are also held in a very high regard by most people who have played them). Some of the plot twists will blow your mind!  In terms of gameplay, there's not really a lot I can say. The game consists of a mixture of visual novel elements along with 'escape the room' style puzzles, with a large flowchart full of branching decisions. The game handles this in a very unique and interesting way, however, allowing you to jump between fragments of the timeline, not actually knowing where they place until they're completed. This allows you to jump right into whichever fragment you desire, and play the game in whatever order you choose.  The PC port is absolutely fantastic, and I have been getting consistent 60FPS at 1080p with full antialiasing with an antique GTX 650 and AMD-4170fx rig. The animations may be a little jarring occasionally, but the voice acting more than makes up for this.  Overall, Zero Time Dilemma is a fantastic entry into the series and I'd highly recommend buying it ASAP. Do yourself a favour, and play the first two though. You'll never regret it.

app_name: METAL GEAR SOLID V: GROUND ZEROES
review_text: At the time of writing this review I have played 7 hours. After coming into this from games such as Arma, Dayz and other shooters, I was not sure what to expect. I did play the old version that came out on the Playstation in the late 90's, and that was a great experience. But this... BLEW MY MIND. Graphics are amazing. This game runs so smoothly. It says that it is optimsed for Nvidia, I have a Radeon and it was perfect. This is a very attractive game to look at.  Gameplay is fun and there is no steep learning curve. Once you get a handle on the keyboard controls (Also can plug a controller in) it is very simple to make Snake as deadly, or not, as you wish. There is a lot of replay to be had. Once you complete the first mission, new ops open up for you. And you can't help your self, you just want to get better and faster at completing them.  I bought this during the Christmas sale but honestly, now that I own it and have played it, I would have payed the full asking price.  This Is a must have in your library even if you have never played any thing like it before. I am now frothing at the mouth in anticipation of the next installment 'The Phantom Pain'.  In conclusion: Graphics 10/10  Perfomance 10/10  Game Play 10/10  Enjoyment 10/10  Value 10/10  Enough said. GO PLAY.

While I don't normally post reviews, after playing this game I just couldn't resist. It is a shame I did not know of this game until today, since psychological horror is one of my favorite genre of media. Fingerbones takes hold of your mind and messes with your intuition from the very beginning. The music, sounds, and the atmosphere give an immersive feel while you are trapped in this little world. The lighting is beautiful and the more pixelated graphics give an unnerving feel to the world. The storytelling is told through notes you find and leaves you asking 'why', but that is one of the strong points to a game of this genre. Moral questions are weighed in interesting ways that make you think and digest the story afterward. The concept of why we have emotions and if we even need them in society, or how mankind is just another lifeform that has the sole job to reproduce in order to survive. While there are no jumpscares(except for the generator that I turned on without having a flashlight which made me jump out of my skin from the noise), the atmosphere and how the story pans out makes you inticipate a jumpscare. The story is not 'for the faint of heart' so to say, but don't be a wuss. In conclusion, I would say the story and atmosphere are very well done and captures your attention throughout. Fingerbones definately diserves 20 minutes of your time, as well as the extra time you will spend afterward pondering what you have just played. 8/10  TL;DR: Crazy father that is breaking bad takes his daughter to his survival shelter and has a teaparty with her then reflects on his scientific ideals through reading notes. You will learn about fingerbones. 2spooky4me

I can't find anything good to say about this game. Honest. Screenshots look okay, but the game's hardly polished and the interface is terrible (no shift-clicking, crafting/furnace window is not intuitive).  Downfalls: - Tools have no durability, and the pickaxe tool seems to work on everything, regardless of the material (dirt, stone, wood). - You also cannot jump and place blocks directly underneath you, so I hope you like digging stairways.  - Trying to build a bridge out from a hill, the game won't let you place blocks unless you hang off the block and place while jumping. (At least in Minecraft you could sneak off a ledge and slap a block on.) - Physics on this just feel wrong: jumping from block to block you may sometimes miss or just jump up faster than you anticipated. - The textures are atrocious and hurt my eyes after a while. With no outline of the box you are targeting, things can get disorienting, especially in caves.  Also, many maps are ravaged: trees gone (floating bushes), obscene words spelled out in text, abandoned dirt huts, etc. It just feels like the land has been pillaged for someone's personal cache.  TL;DR - Crappy F2P voxel game with item mall to get you to spend money. Take your hard-earned cash and go buy Minecraft instead. It is a much more satisfying experience.

Where is an appropriate place to begin to describe Borderlands 2? I suppose first, it would be fair to point out that if you have an overwhelming body odour problem that's so bad that people can smell you over the internet, you have no friends or you just plain hate people, this game is going to suck holy balls for you - especialy if you went to the trouble of buying ultimate vault hunter mode. Make no mistake; at this point in the game, it'll suck hard for you, and you'll be forced to use a GIB editor to make yourself a level 61 character, which pretty much invalidates the point of the game, as you can just go ahead and make a save file with all the legendary weapons and cash you could possibly need.  However, this game is for you, if:  1.) You're some kind of special hentai freak that likes to fap over moxxi or lilith 2.) You have friends (4 of) 3.) You have a joy for explosions 4.) Like me, you have way too much time on your hands and can, at the time of this writing, sink 324 hours into the game. Seriously. My friends, despite loving this game are telling me that I quite urgently need a life.  It has to be said, that this game improves substantially the things that Borderlands 1 Lacked, which isn't to say that both didn't have their charms - it's just that this sequel has approximately five times the amount of boobies, and almost doubles the amount of sequels, although it has to be said that not being able to just skip through the god damned cut scenes after 324 hours of playing is making me want to cave my god damned skull in, and I'd very probably actually consider giving randy pitchford the sloppiest, gayest ♥♥♥♥♥♥♥ he's ever had in exchange for being able to do so.  That said, I think 324 hours is a testament to either the replayability of this game,or to just how bad my memory span is. Either way, buy the game. It's worth it just to hear Krieg's inner monologuing.

Tomb Raider Underworld is definitely Lara's loveliest outing IMO (including the reboot). The graphics and animations are fantastic. The only complaint I have with this game is the way Lara looks. Don't get me wrong, she looks incredible and her character model is well detailed. My issue is that she looks nothing like she did in Legend or Anniversary. In fact, many of the characters look nothing like their old selves. Amanda looks plastic and artificial, like an emo Barbie, and Natla just looks too... nice... She doesn't look menacing like she did in Anniversary, and her long hair has been cut short (granted, it WAS completely burned off in a volcano). Even characters like Allister and Zip look completely different! What happened to continuity? Those issues aside, the game plays moderately well. Lara will go over cliffs/mess up jumps at times because of a weird camera angle every now and again, but it's to be expected with Tomb Raider at this point. The save system is a bit wonky, it's hard to tell my own save file apart from the autosaves. On the plus side, the puzzles and platforming sequences in the game are wonderful. These puzzles seem really well though out, and it brought my mind to the classic Tomb Raider games in their quality. Also, this game plays much better on a keyboard and mouse setup than Anniversary does, just because Lara's movements are that much more fluid. I still say using a controller is the best way to play this game though. I'm disappointed that I can't get the DLC levels, but that can come later I suppose. If you like Legend and Anniversary, this is a worthy follow-up, and wraps up the story nicely. Highly recommended!

Forget everything I said about this game. I've preordered the best edition and purchased every DLC, played all of them religiously, and I sit here very comfortable with myself uninstalling it. For a few reasons.  But mainly, and, if anything, only, because OVERKILL Software are lying ♥♥♥♥♥.  Before release, the developers, and I quote, 'made it clear that PAYDAY 2 will have no micro-transactions whatsoever (shame on you if you thought otherwise!)'. Accompanied by a full statement article on GameSpot, and yet two years later here we are, with weapon crates and skins and keys. And not only that, the new cosmetics also have additional stats.  The Hype Train website promised $250,000 in prizes for PAYDAYCON, a sum promised but never delivered. The community funded the convention with full intent of having the prizes disseminated, and the developers pocket the money.  Publisher 505 Games also stated pre-release that safehouse customisation would be a thing, and yet it's come to a point where players are mocked or smirked at for mentioning it. You'd think by now we'd have the right to demand it when the system and plans are already in the bloody game.  Players were also promised 'a fair and equal shot at all content', and throughout the game's life cycle we see many masks that are limited release only e.g. E3 and PAYDAYCON (a ♥♥♥♥in' venue requirement), Humble Bundle (promotional period), Alienware Alpha (another platform entirely) and the Hotline Miami game purchases.  These and many other reasons are why I and many others - it's more than half, the figures are out - are dropping this game very hard and never coming back.

I decided to delete my original review after the whole microtransactions update came.  What can i say, it's a game i loved at first sight and have banked 700+ hours into. The game is fun, it's hella fun if you're playing with the right people and even solo. Sadly, the game is far too easy. The game before the microtransactions update was still too easy for my taste. Now that you can buy guns that have increased skill means that you can buy skills which is just dumb in my opinion and makes it even easier than it is. Death wish doesn't feel like something hard to achieve, although it sounds harder than it is. Now, i'm not a person who cares or even enjoys skins. So they don't affect me at all.  I haven't played since 2015, but at that point i had around 98% of the achievements and took a break after. It seems that the update that came recently fixed a lot of problems, which it did, and the community is responding positively to it. Still, i'd love a skill increase in both stealth and loud missions and less achievements that are 'kill 250 enemies with a specific gun', which for most people takes literally no time to achieve. Make it 2500 kills instead. Or, 'beat a loud heist solo death wish no AL'. Something that takes time to achieve. Since leveling takes forever, it's out of balance in my opinion.  I only speak for the hardcore stealthers. I know me and a few others, enjoy stealthing dw at max detection, and i'm pretty sure they agree with me saying stealthing is too easy. I'm not complaining, i just wish that they added an extra difficulty for the more hardcore players.   Would i recommend this game?  Absolutely i would, you're going to have a lot of fun!

app_name: Brothers - A Tale of Two Sons
review_text: Roughly 3 hours of gameplay. Great visuals, great music, and a simple but innovative control scheme. The first Chapter is a bit slow but after you work your way through to the end (and epilogue) it becomes a falcon punch straight in the feels. Highly recommended.

app_name: Brothers - A Tale of Two Sons
review_text: I have very mixed feelings writing this. The game itself was a lot of fun, and I really enjoyed the unique way of controlling both characters at once. The puzzles were mostly easy to solve, but I like that. It allows me to enjoy the experience without getting frustrated and still concentrate on the story being told.  Which brings me to the story. Tragedy and sorrow can be powerful and valuable things to explore through a story. I've read plenty of sad stories before that left me feeling sad but uplifted. You just have to be careful about how you present it and make sure you show the little rays of light that give value to the struggles the characters are going through.  This game does none of that. The closing scene is the most depressing and worthless end to any story I've ever read before. There ARE good things they could have shown to help bring a feeling of worth to everything you went through in the game, but instead they just depress the heck out of you and leave it there.  If you're ok overlooking that, the game itself is a LOT of fun. Controlling both brothers to solve the challenges they face is both challenging and rewarding, and I honestly enjoyed that part of the game.

For an indie game, this little gem has definitely surpassed my expectations. From making one laugh out loud at running gags (yo, Jorji) to questioning one's moral standpoints, this game has all the elements that makes it one of the smartest games I've played all year.   You're allowed to make choices - but with natural consequences that affect how you manage to care for your family, how loyal you want to be to your country and other random things one would otherwise not think about. Do you want to let this woman in to see her long lost love? Do you help the rebels trying to overthrow the government? Do you accept the bribes given to you in exchange for entrance into your beloved country?   The game can also be replayed a number of times without it getting tiresome or tedious, which is great plus point. Best $5 spent in a good long while.

Another rule broken is that the computer can have as many orange legendary cards on the field that they want. It is normally limited to just 1 at a time. More of an annoyance than anything to me. Multiplayer: Not recommended, due to bugs Single player: Recommended, if you want a straightforward mech romp splatting bugs  Gameplay is you're in a suit of badass mech armor with fun weapons and unlimited ammo, stomping through a gigantic space hulk - a ton of ancient ships fused together. The plot guides you through objectives as you lay waste to hordes of aliens that die in a satisfying shower of giblets. Performance is not well optimized so be ready to turn down some settings if your comp is not high end.  In single player you have a wide array of abilities and weapons that unlock as you complete missions, along with a skilll tree that provides passive buffs, new abilities, and squad buffs. You have 2 supporting squad members, one heavy fighter and one healer. The healer is pretty dumb, you have to manually tell it to heal everything. The AI is okay but not impressive. There's not a ton of tactics required but the atmosphere and combat are both fun.  Multiplayer largely follows a similar mission layout without the cutscenes or collectibles. There are several classes, each with much more restricted abilities and weapons than single player, but it works well. You go from objective to objective, clearing rooms and hallways. It never gets more complicated than 'stick together' and 'focus the big guy or high damage enemy'. It's simple but very fun. Biggest gameplay frustration is each mission you start over at a low level and have to re-unlock stuff you just had, but that goes pretty quick so it's not so bad.  The problem with multiplayer is the bugs. There's so many that it has hurt the ability for my group of friends to enjoy the game. Here's a list of what I can think of right now: - I crash about 25% of the time when going into my inventory/loadout (playing as host, not sure if that matters). - 3 friends have had all their attacks stop working, multiple times. They can move and look around but not attack. They were only able to fix by exiting game and restarting. - Random crashes, seems like my friends crash about once per 2-3 hours randomly. - Random lockups, sometimes the game will freeze for 2-5 seconds and then let you resume. While locked up you'll keep moving in one direction and often end up somewhere bad. - Mission objective markers and info rarely show up for non-hosts. This results in non-hosts getting lost/frustrated as they feel like they're just wandering around with no objective - Mission objectives can get screwed up by someone joining the game. I had an objective to take and hold an area, we were halfway across the map and someone joined and it started the timer and we won without ever reaching the area. - Even as host sometimes my objective breaks, so we end up wandering around. One mission we beat when I got a timer bar with no explanation of what it was, and it took about 10 minutes to slowly fill in. - There are some objectives that are to break an object. This information doesn't seem to share well between players. Someone will keep shooting the object still because it doesn't look broken for them.  When things are all working, it's a ton of fun. But I haven't played a single game where everything worked smoothly, and I haven't completed 2 missions in a row without at least 1 friend suffering a catastrophic failure. Hopefully this game gets patched to at least be more stable because it is everything I wanted from a multiplayer power-armor romp through a giant space derelict.

Resident Evil / biohazard HD REMASTER is the same title released in 2002, albeit with even further-improved visuals. The 3D and 2D textures have been markedly improved, but only so much. While some shots will delight fans of the original remake, others reveal its true age. Still, a side-by-side shows extremely impressive improvements.  For veterans of the series, very little is new, and what is new is optional. New fans who would struggle with tank controls can opt for the alternative free-moving setup, but it's only an alternative that has its own share of problems. The visual update includes 16:9 widescreen, but one can opt for the original 4:3. They also include the options for 30 frames per second or 60. Though it seems like anathema to PC gamers, I opt for the 30 frames to recreate the cinematic feel to the game, as 60 felt wrong.  While the game has aged, it's aged well. Returning players will most likely remember the mansion they loved to fear and can hit the ground running. I found myself breezing through puzzles and remembering tricks to get around certain areas. The game no longer instills a fear in me like it did before, but this is the sign of a more experienced, older player, not an empty title. Still, not 10 minutes into the old horror I found myself cautious about what lie around the next corner. The din of moans around a corner, the shambling of footsteps, the sudden crack of a window, the sound of silence and wind, and even the sudden turn of a character's head when they saw something off-screen still made my heart beat faster and my hair stand up.  From the eyes of someone who played and beat this many times before years ago, buying this left me seeing the title as a solid 8/10. For new players, looking for a true survival horror experience, this lives up to the fantastic 9/10 that you've likely heard so much about. Once you get by the control scheme which may seem awkward and novel, and realize that it's as much a part of the experience as the zombies themselves, you're in for quite a treat.

Alright, so having just finished BBP, I can say, I'm satisfied. For 5 bucks, you get what you pay for. Now, I'll be honest, the gameplay is nothing to write home about. It mostly consists of running to an enemy, smacking them, then running away. But, the worst part: The Glitches. Seriously, I actually doubt if they playtested this. To name a few: Famine will do nothing if you die once and fight her again, dieing in Hell results in the camera being stuck at a close Bird's eye view, preventing you from seeing jack ♥♥♥♥, and I constantly got stuck in the wall during the Garmr fight. And that's just to name a few. But, the appeal is not in the gameplay. It's in the soundtrack, and great sense of humor. Seriously, the shout outs, jokes, and sountrack are worth 5 bucks alone. app_name: Microsoft Flight Simulator X: Steam Edition
review_text: There was a lot that could have been done to make this game great, but someone dropped the ball. In order for you to be able to play the way you want to, you need to buy more dreaded DLC. The controls re complicated and many. Unless you have a full working sim cockpit it is virtually impossible to do certain things. Such as auto pilot. For reason ine continually crashed whenever i was trying to get out of game, or something simple like finish a tutorial.I would have to exit all the way out then get all the way back in. Very annoying.

I got this game a while ago in a bundle and was very excited to play. I had heard a lot of good things about it, both on and offline, but to save myself from spoilers I hadn't looked at any videos. I really wish I had. Toren was just one frustration after another, leaving me a grumpy mess in the end and causing me to write my very first review on Steam. You did it, Toren, you broke my silence.  Cons:  First of all, the controls are abysmal. I used the Steam Controller to play and thought at first that maybe I had picked the wrong setting. After a few tweaks...and a few more...and then more, I gave up and realized it's not me, it's you. I reset the setting to default and got on with it. Twenty minutes later I'm ready to destroy worlds in my frustration. I cannot tell you how many times I ran off cliffs, or almost did, and had to here 'oh!' 'ooh!' 'ohhh!' as she cartwheels her arms. I had barley started turning her and she was already falling off again. Girl, get your act together and just turn around!  There were also odd little moments where I would walk by something, like a tree branch or a whole in the wall and, without my prompting, without me even moving towards it, I would suddenly be walking across the branch or ducking through a hole in the wall. Why? Seriously, why? I was just trying to walk past, and because I got within ten feet of it I'm suddenly going that way? It was then a case of trying to run around, get off the branch and walk away without triggering it again. And then triggering it again anyway. Or ducking back under the wall three times because her turning circle is utter bums.  The checkpoint system I'm a little two faced about. Possibly the game is trying to punish the player by making them trek endlessly back from the beginning of a 'dream' sequence if they die. Possibly the game just can't get its head together and play nice. If the first, fine, it's teaching you to play slow and learn and take baby steps so you don't run off the same cliff you're just trying. to. walk. by. If the second, then...then whatever, I'm disappoint anyway, I guess I'll just play this same bit of the game over and over and over ad nauseam.  Lastly was the camera system. My first indication that I was going to have a fair little fight with it should have been the little into movie. It 'clicked' around. I can't think of a better word to describe it then that. It seemed to click, or jump, from one scene to the next, like I had blinked and missed something, but it was just the camera moving. It wasn't smooth and it just looked weird. It was the same as I was playing the game. I would turn a corner, or climb up something, and click! new angle and now I'm falling off a cliff because I'm no longer going up while I'm pushing up, I'm going right. Right off a cliff. THANKS. I struggled with this the entire time I played because it was so unpredictable.  I had to stop playing near the end. I paused the game, got food, and when I came back my controller was nonfunctional. By the time I got back into the game, I had to reload from a checkpoint right at the beginning of the board and my sanity just said NOPE. So off the game went.  Pros:  The music I suppose? Although it was mostly just soft backround noise, it worked well with the game and the quiet little sadness the game seemed to try and portray. Woo?  The story was very beautiful, what I saw of it. And seeing/finding all of the statues and trying to guess what she had been doing before she froze was pretty cool. While I haven't finished the game fully (sorry my sanity is more important Toren, but I'll finish you when I'm not a huddled mess of grump and annoyance) what I have seen so far is enough to make me want to come back some day and see the rest of it. So that's good, I suppose.  The art wasn't terrible. A lot of it was really beautiful, what I got to see of it. Some of the most beautiful things happened while I was reading the wizard guys words. I either missed what he said, or I got to see just a half second of something very pretty. Not enough time to read the story, or not enough time to enjoy the beauty around me. Like I said, though: at least the story is good.  All in all, while I'm quite sure I will go back to Toren and finish it, I'm equally sure I will never play it all the way through ever again. I like my sanity the way it is, and would rather not lose what little is left on a game I'm only getting to half enjoy.  Poor little baby-bubs game! You had so much potential!  Not recommended.

Short version: Phoenix Wright meets Zero Escape. More flawed than either, but still stands on its own.  Long version: It's almost paradoxical in some ways; the overwrought character archetypes actually develop some interesting personality, and the ridiculousness and Japaneseness of certain scenes somehow make the gravity of goings-on even more pronounced. It does a really good job of drawing you into the crushing atmosphere, but it kind of sucks at foreshadowing. I guessed two of the biggest endgame plot twists the moment they were first hinted at, and figured out a third on my own time several hours before it was revealed. That said, the class trial segments take enough twists that SOME things are bound to take you off guard.  My biggest complaint with the game is the inexcusable amount of padding. For everything that could be summarized in one or two lines of dialogue, you are guaranteed at least six. Fast travel via the map is a godsend and the game would be borderline unplayable without it, but room transitions STILL take far more time than they need to purely in the name of flaunting the game's low-fi popup book style. The overall message of the game also gets ridiculously heavy-handed in the end. That said, it was a thoroughly enjoyable and original ride and has me imminently curious about the sequel. I'm glad I got the bundle!

app_name: A Study in Steampunk: Choice by Gaslight
review_text: Pros: -Decisions that matter -You can be gay -Stats actually count toward something -A plot that feels like a Sherlock Holmes novel -Romances that actually contribute to the plot -Tense action sequences that make you wonder what the best action actually is -Lengthy, but far from tedious  Cons: -Can only play as a man  All in all, this is legitimately one of the best games Choice of Games has ever put out, and you shouldn't rob yourself of the chance to play a game that genuinely cares about whether or not you have a good experience.

The mulitplayer is solid COD twitch shooting with some cool tack ons, in addition to the great SIngleplayer and fun multiplayer the Zombies coop mode is also good fun with friends, in which up to 4 of your friends call fight endless waves of zombies with traps, and weapons its good stuff. The only downside is there are only 2 zombie maps for the standard game mode. The rest are 15 bucks a pop for the DLC map packs. Which is pretty insane how old the game is but Activision doesnt seem to like to price down their games even years after release. Hence why Call of Duty is 20 dollars... Anyway I highly recommend Black Ops to any franchise vet who has not played it or anyone looking for an enjoyable singleplayer with a solid multiplayer accompanyment. 8.5/10

Magicka is a hilariously satirical take on the typical fantasy RPG/Action game, and is ridiculously fun to play. The bugs that crippled the game early on are fixed now and it's time to put on your wizard robe & hat. Be warned: the single-player game does have a bit of an initial learning curve and is challenging even on the tutorial level. Once you get used to the control scheme for casting spells, though, you'll be having too much fun turning goblins & beastmen into chunky gib salsa to notice. Where this game shines, though, is in the multiplayer modes: Online Adventure (read: Co-op), Survival, Arena & PvP modes provide variety and the frenetic pace of dodging enemies (and often, your own teammates' attacks) makes for a great, quick multiplayer experience. The base game is a good deal at $9.99, but catch the Complete Pack on sale if you can, since the Vietnam and Marshlands DLC's extend the game with new missions & stories. Great, addicting game - I give it 9/10.

I captures the atmossphere so perfectly that I really wish it was a sandbox that I could explore for hours. The peace, the eary sounds, everything fits. This looks like it might have been a good game in the mid-nineties. The devs put a lot of detail in making the game seem like it was an old classic that had not aged well through the multiple ports, and the way they put in the auto-crash surprise complete with text dump really does make one sentimental for the old DOS days. But probably the most impressive example of their attention to detail has to be in the options panel. These sly characters purposely set the mouse scroll speed so fast it takes a true gamer to master the challenge of actually getting the option controls open. But there is a further easter egg, because you find there is NO SCROLL SPEED CONTROL! Now how perfect is that? Just exactly what you would expect of some 1995 game that came on both 3.5' and 6' floppys in the box!   tl;dr These guys built a masterpiece of parody, even though they released it in 2010 you would never know. You would think it is much much older.

The fighting is very enjoyable and can be actually challenging, instead of spam clicking the left mouse button over and over and over. Easily among, if not, my favorite RPG(s) of all time, Mass Effect is an extremely unique game. In my opinion, it is already a classic that I would place among other masterpieces such as the early Fallout games or Planescape: Torment.  Story: Probably Mass Effect's greatest aspect is its story. The game's universe is extremely interesting and immersive. Everything seems scientifically plausible and is put together in a very desirable package. It is often stated Mass Effect is a Space Opera, and I believe that label is more than perfect for this game. Beyond the backstory and universe, Mass Effect's characters are very well-written and interesting, even the mundane humans have interesting stories, such as the pilot of your ship who has a bone-weakening disease. The plot itself is also captivating, bringing in influences from Lovecraftian horror to the science fiction shows and movies of the late 20th century.  Gameplay: Mass Effect's gameplay is overall pretty decent. It does feel clanky and aged at times, but it by no means makes the game unplayable. There are certain levels of the game I loathe to encounter whenever I replay it (Feros...), but sticking through those rough times and comparing it to the much more fun levels ultimately means I can't say the gameplay is bad by any means. A feature frequently criticized are the (mostly) optional levels where you drive around in an APC called the Mako. I personally enjoyed them but that could likely be just my driving desire to explore the various worlds of the Mass Effect universe.  Replayability: I would recommend most people play Mass Effect at least twice. There are many reasons to come back to Mass Effect such as being able to play as a either a male or a female, romancing different people, or making situational choices. The dialogue your character can say can range from being a kind and noble person to a borderline sociopath. For example: You can use charisma to charm the audience of a reporter trying to make you look bad, or alternatively you can tell her you've 'had enough of her snide insinuations' and punch her in the face. In addition to this there are multiple classes you can play as, from a decidated soldier to a technology specialist  Ultimately, even if you think Mass Effect has fallen from grace (I did not like Mass Effect 3 in the slightest), Mass Effect 1 really has standed the test of time has an incredible RPG and one of Bioware's finest creations.

review_text: GOOD:  -It is an harder version of Tales of Phantasia, and this is good. -The story is simple but well written with the classic trio of strong, kind and knowlegiable characters. -It is labeled as an hard game because you cannot save alway and because of the fights, but when you die you can choose to restart the last fight, giving infinite possibilities to beat the hard part you are facing and making the whole game easier than it looks.   BAD:  -The main character has NO special abilities and swings the sword from the beginning to the end with no real improvement. -Disease are too strong for what is given to counter them until later in the game...you do have only items and EVERY fight you get poisoned or confused or asleep. -There are too many forced fights...some are justified throught the game story but others are not... -No support for compatibilities like OS or Joystick (had to play it on window mode).  PRICE: Worth less then 4 euro, 75% discount  RECCOMEND: I do only reccomend this game to those who enjoy hard fights, love the genre and are willing to close an eye (or maybe both) for more then few 'not well tought' events.

Getting this clear: This isn't exactly a game. It's more of a visual novel-esque type thing. This is a sort of game set in it's own category due to it's game play, and for sure this game play is NOT for everyone. Not every likes games that just have you explore your surroundings and listen to the story being told. But I'll tell you this is great in the respect of the story and exploration being told! That is what drives this game and it's what the folks over at the Chinese Room are wonderful at doing! So if you like games that involve more play and less story, then this just isn't for you.  Now this game itself is about our unnamed player who came to the island to remember things that happend in his past. You get to learn about why he is at this island and about this 'dead Esther' whom he speaks so much about. This game will draw you in from the vast island's sceanery and the brilliant dialogue. Soon enough this little 'game' will draw you in to where you just want to know the whole story it's trying to give, even if it can be a bit uncomprehensible at a few moments, but you'll soon pick it back up into understanding.  All in all, this is a very unique and interesting game and if you really want to play, but feel unsure about it, just wait until it goes on sale (That's what I did), and I'm sure you'll either be satisfied with it or not mad that you spent too much on it.

This is probably my favourite assassin's creed game so far (I haven't played Black Flag yet though).  The combat is pretty nice (although, not much different from earlier games). The counter system has been changed, and now gives you the option of throwing/disarming/killing your opponent with either primary or secondary weapons; but the enemy types remain roughly the same.  There are now more stealth opportunities, with the ability to move through dense undergrowth undetected, and some of the optional (and main) missions reflect this. There's still no option to stealthily move around normally though.  Calling for support has also been changed, with a range of different options instead of just being able to call for a target to be assassinated or support in a fight. Some of them feel like they need a bit more work, particularly ambushes and the fake guards thing. Ambushes are slightly irritating in the rather long time between them being triggered and something actually happening (although, this also happens with the standard assassination and marksman skills, but I tended to use them in less time sensitive ways). Fake guards can spawn either colonial or redcoat disguises, but there's no way to specify which, so you have to hope for the right one.  Money is slightly problematic in the game. You no longer recieve any money for completing missions, but there's also very little to spend money on. The (dyed) outfits may be worth spending money on, and the maps are handy, but the better weapons can be crafted for less money (and there doesn't seem to be much difference between different swords etc. Pistols are a little more noticeable in their differences, since some fire multiple shots at a time and some can be fired twice without reloading). The ship upgrades are largely worthwhile, but the naval missions aren't really particularly important in the game so it seems a little dissapointing only spending money on upgrading the ship.  The naval missions are damn fun though. The difficulty of them seems a little easy though, since the Aquila appears to be nearly indestructible compared to the enemy ships. A lot of the optional missions make it seem like controlling it should be harder, with stuff like '25% or less damage from hitting rocks' appearing a couple of times.  Most of the optional missions are pretty well chosen, adding some difficulty without being too annoying. There are a few exceptions, like when you have chase someone down busy streets without hitting anybody.  The Tyranny of King Washington is damn fun too, with the animal spirit powers modifying the gameplay style a bit. I would recommend playing the standard game first, since there are no tutorials for anything other than the spirit powers (and the control system is fairly different), and the collectibles do include spoilers for the main game (including the ending of the game). My main problem with it is that you have to find additional weapons in treasure chests, and you're never given the opportunity of deciding to keep what you already have equipped rather than what's in the chest.  There are problems with the game however. It's quite buggy. Some of the bugs may be due to me playing the game on a laptop that's a little below the minimum specs (although the game largely ran fine for me).  The most noticeable is that dyed outfits don't show up in cutscenes, and instead you always wear assassin white clothes (the unlockable outfits do work though). Sometimes, weapons mysteriously unequip and have to be re-equipped (this mostly happened with Captain Kidd's sword for me, but I also lost a tomahawk during the Tyranny dlc). The magic lanterns in New York (and one of the Boston ones) were blank, leaving me to solve them based on brief flashes of the symbols during transitions from the top to the right slots and youtube videos. Changing weapons/outfits are the homestead is also ridiculously slow, with massive pauses between pressing the button to change to the next weapon and the actual change happening.  Cliff climbing is also introduced in this game, as well as free running through trees and the ability to quickly go over fences etc. instead of climbing onto them and jumping off them. These are all largely good, although it can be difficult to tell where a cliff can actually be climbed and where it's just slight bulges in the cliff. Tree free running is pretty good, particularly when it's been snowing, but there aren't really enough branches for them to actually go where you want (particularly when you're running around outside of missions).  The actual plot of the game is pretty good, but it suffers one problem: Conner comes across as quite dull. I'm not sure whether it's a problem with character being unlikeable or the monotonous voice acting though. As in this review, there's so much else going on that the plot feels more like another distraction than the main bulk of the game.  And while, yes it is a game by Ubisoft and so it needs uplay to work, Uplay does seem to mostly keep out of your way. There's a little delay at launch while it loads, and it likes reminding you about the dashboard when you start the game, but otherwise it's pretty much invisible. Except when you complete an achievement/action. It does feel like it shouldn't really be needed though.  TL;DR: Pretty good game, with a few problems.

This my first Roguelike (you have to start from zero after death and levels are randomly generated each time) and I love it!  Addictive gameplay and upgrade system accompanied by awesome and fitting soundtrack. Great value for my money: The campaign takes less than 1 hour to finish (IF you can finish it), but the randomness, unlockable ships and achievements drastically increase the replay value. I've poured dozens of hours of fun into this. There are several vastly different play styles depending on your ship, your ship upgrades and your crew (which, by the way, can be made up of any permutation of different alien species, each with different abilities).  Some of my favorite tactics include: 1) Disable enemy weapon system, so they can't attack. Disable their oxygen system, so that they'll suffocate. 2) Teleport my combat trained crew onto enemy ship and kill their crew. 3) Set the inside of the enemy ship on fire and let its crew burn to death.  I'd say definitely give this one a try.

6.5/10 Good game.  Theres alot of hype around this game, some of it is justified, some not so much imo.  Likes:   Nice story, nothing ground breaking though. Decent voice acting. Being searched for by the Alien while your hiding gives a nice sense of fear. Decent graphics. Superb ambient audio. Lots of gadgets to tinker with. True to the movies.  Dislikes:   The map, when your on a different level to your objective it can be painful to work out where you need to go. There is a marker on your hud showing your direction but no way to tell which level your on in comparison to your objective. Once you get the flame thrower the hardest part is trying to find fuel for it or not running out of what you have. Again the flame thrower removes alot of the suspense to the point where you can walk up to the alien blast it a couple of times and carry on. When your using a computer or lock mechanism/mini game you can be attacked, the only way to tell is a few faint grunts from your character, no visualisations. Towards the end for me it started to drag on a little making me hope there wasn't much left to do.( Reach point X - You made it- Oh wait no you didn't something bad is happening) Ripley is like the grim reaper, to know her is to get killed faster than an extra on an away mission in Star Trek. You don't really need all the gadgets, I finished the game on hard setting without using a couple of them, like the flash bangs. Weak ending.   As many Cons as there are pros but I would still recommend playing the game, it's good for about 20 hours of gameplay, if you can pick it up in a sale. I couldn't see myself paying full retail price for this game if I knew what it was like, probably wouldnt be too happy if I did.  Tips:   Watch the overhead vents. Check behind you often when you hear something, the androids aren't fast but they can be damn quiet and have an extraordinary reach. Save at every opportunity.  In Space no-one can hear you scream....or yawn once you get the flamethrower.

A truly great game, very tense, and of the ~20 hours I have spent *trying* to complete this game, around 16 of them were hiding in lockers, air vents, under tables etc etc.  The final third of the campaign dragged on a little too long, which is a shame because some things became a little repetitive. However, overall its a game I'm happy I've spent £30 on, and I'll be going back to play it when the new DLCs come out.

Unfortunately not as fun as DMC 3 (the only amazing game in the series). Camera gets lost too often, controls are more loose compared to previous DMC titles. The weapons are fun (though not guns) and chaining combos with various weapons is enjoyable. Some opponents and most bosses are not fun to play against. They're not difficult (finished on Nefilim/Hard equivalent, hardest difficulty you can get on first playthrough), just super annoying. The art design of the levels is occasionaly very good (one of the levels mid game is amazing), but the actual layouts and traversal are uninspired. Platforming elements are very weak. Altogether gameplay is ok at best.   What is truly terrible is the story. DMC series never made much sense and was always cheesy, but at least it was amusing most of the time. This game takes itself very, very seriously. Characters make fun of Dante for being an emo kid, but it actually is a spot on description. 90% of the cutscenes are cringe worthy and/or boring. At least you can skip them even on first play through. I did in the final third of the game once my patience ran out. I doubt it picked up in quaity.   Ultimately, not a terrible game, but average/decent at best and does not kick ♥♥♥ like the previous young Dante. With so many cheap, good games available, do yourself a favour and spend your time playing something else. If you really want to play a good DMC game, buy DMC 3 Special Edition.

I'm not going to tell you anything about how this game is played, except that it falls into a category some of my friends have deemed 'walking simulators.' Your interaction with the world consists of walking through a large, strangely empty house, and picking up and manipulating the things that you find there, in order to piece together a story. But, and I can't emphasize this enough, all of that is woefully underselling how fantastic this game is. If you care about games as a medium, about the state of games today, or just about learning how to tell a great story, I implore you to buy this game--without watching any reviews or let's plays. Giving anything about this game away would take away from the experience and the wonderful ability Gone Home has to play with your perceptions and preconceptions. Gone Home is my game of the year for 2013, and I think it should be yours too.

Hi, for those who don't like to read longer game reviews I have only 3 words : One big frustration.   Please don't get me wrong, this game is not ment to be easy one - everybody knows that, even I do. But from all perma-death, player-♥♥♥-whooping games, this one - is the worst game I played so far... sadly.  And it's not about stealing all your gold pieces when entering the castle, or increasing price of every singlie upgrade just because you managed to buy one after all. Also it's not about this stupid, irritating visual mods making screen upside-down or blurred or changing nice colors to sepia... Ok, partly it is about all of the above, but...  NO.  It's just because the most unpredictable component in this game is your hero - in a bad way. And I don't agree with people saying that fighting mechanics is great - no, it's not. It's buggy. Try to turn around and attack quickly - you can't, your hero or heroine will be butt facing the opponent. WTF is this ?  This kind of games require some skills to play (I mean to survive long enough for the run to mean something). But correct me if I'm wrong, skillful play is not about overcoming character control issues, is it ?  I don't remember me swearing so much in any other game simply due to technicallity issues (maybe beside playing Mortal Kombat II against my brother on one keyboard, but that was actually lot of fun, this game isn't).  I know that there are players finding fun playing this game after puting several hundreds of hours in it. But I can't afford to waste so much time for a simple game just to be playable.  Game graphics an musics are pretty cute though.   Let's wrap it up: Pro: - visuals - sound & music Cons: - control issues, small but ♥♥♥♥♥♥♥ off - where is 'hardcore mode off' switch ?  So in the end, word of advice: If you like pain - buy this game,  if you don't - certainly you won't (or will regret).

They should have made this a spin off. It feels almost nothing like older Sacred games. Just calling it 'Sacred: Uprising' or something would rise the reviews up to 70%'s.  Before I bought the game I read the reviews, I played the original Sacred and loved it. But I bought the game anyway because it seemed like a good couch co op to play with my family. And this is what this game is all about, a co op action rpg.   Keyboard/mouse controls are not that bad, but turning animation is non-existant. I recommend playing it with a x360 controller. Keyboard/mouse + x360 co op is working thankfully.  Graphics are really good actually. A little bit too bright from time to time but really good visuals. Aside from non-existant keyboard turn animation, all other effects and animations are very well made. There are so many different backgrounds to fight on, I wonder if they intended to make a instance based a-rpg when they started.  Music and voice acting are also good. Not too great though, dialogues almost always sarcastic, most of the time it will make you smile but sometimes it gets too corny.  Story is not all that great, I only got upset a bit when *spoiler alert* our narrator got captured and we had to rescue her.  Gameplay mechanics; everything works fine, abilities and heroes compliment each other, balance is good. Weapon spirit mechanic is creative and fun. Your weapon spirits will make voiced commets while you play and it can be really funny. There are many different types of minions but types of mini bosses are only two. Actually there are some more but differences aren't noticable. Fighting big bosses can be really fun or sometimes really frustrating. Co op mechanics exists like one player turns a wheel wile other tries to fend off minions. They made really polished create opening animations but they only drop health/energy orbs and gold. Weapons, armor and weapon spirits are upgraded at certain hero levels. Weapon spirit drops are even worse. They drop randomly and cover half the screen when they do. There are items which drop at side quests such as potions and wards, I with other items droped like that as well.  Conclusion; I'm going to give this game a thumbs up, it frusturated me from time to time, it wasn't what I expected from Sacred but I had fun with my family and this game looks, sounds and plays too polished to get a negative from me. I didn't let my past experiance ruin this game for me. If you have one or two controllers and a friend to play with, this game is a good co-op experience.

app_name: Command and Conquer: Red Alert 3 - Uprising
review_text: great expansion, poor delivery. This expansion added a bunch of nice new units to the three factions of red alert 3 which leveled the playing field for all comanders, aswell as making the vets to adapt and form new tactics. The base game of red alert 3 felt like it was missing something unit-wise, and this game definatly delivers for that aspect. This expansion also has a new rewarding challenege mode aswell as three new mini campaign for all three factions as well as the japanese commando's campaign... so you learn how the OP cute little japanese schoolgirl got her powers :D.. So now to state the problem with this package........ NO MULTIPLAYER !!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!! I mean seriously EA? you spend alot of time making a great expansion for redalert and dont add multiplayer????????? We dont want to play only against AIs we want to go online and use these new units to wreak havoc online, but EA failed us once again. If you are dominantly a SOLO player i recommend you to pick this up, for that you'll have many hours of entertainment. but if you are a multiplayer/COOP kinda of guy/gal i would avoid this one.

80% posittive reviews? This is overrated. The game is truly average-average with little attention to details. I would only grab it at a serious sale.  It just doesn't feel right when playing. A lot of details are not well set or not well throught out.  Where borderland move and shoot felt right, this feels a bit awkward with the low gravity, enemy movement (a lot of their animations are too fast such as the soldier's strafe and bug's jump), some annoying new enemy AI behaviors (AI taking cover for TOO LONG, forcing you to chase corners instead of having quick and satisfying dog fights), the size of some maps, and the slow slow SLOW spawning mechanics that makes you sit at a door and wait for enemies to spawn one by one.   It takes a while before you get the right items (air speed and air control stuff) and 'the hang of it' to make floating around fun and efficient.   Where the previous borderland's progression felt natual, this one implements a system similar to dragon age 2, where enemies keep spawning as you fight and only as you fight. Meaning if you are fighting some insects, instead of walking into a group of 10 of them, you'll instead find 2, then 2, then 2, as you fight more appear out of thin air and makes you STAY LONGER in that area than necessary. Instead of fighting 10 and moving on, you have to wait for the others to slowly spawn, either that or get into the habit of running ahead without killing everything, in which case why bother playing at all? This causes another problem, when you kill something and move ahead, often enemies will spawn ALL AROUND YOU, even in angles you had secured... this really makes the game feel like a game (spawn scripts, annoying whack a mole mechanics) and kills immersion.  In similar open spaces games I liked moving in to a bandit camp, sniping obvious targets from far then moving in to clean up with shotguns and SMGs... In this instalment this is impossible, closing in to a camp you will see no one, it is until you walk inside it that enemies start to spawn, and not all at the same time either. Again this kills any planning or tactics. You basicly just have to aim and firing faster... which is too simplistic a system for a game of this size.   The story is not very well put together, you start right into the action and i really didn't feel like i was attached to the character or immersed. You just go shoot that, move that, search for that, and so on without understanding what is going on... Ok so they are shooting the moon, but why? And why do I hate my allies and sympathize with my ennemies so much? Feels like I am on the wrong camp, and there is absolutely ZERO decision making in this game. Again this hurts immersion in the story and the world events. I found the early story and fights boring (basicly soldiers are boring to fight, they spawn too slowly and take too much cover, dragging fights for no reason), only the last 20% is fun because you meet new enemies and fight challenging, epic bosses.   The thing that bothered me THE MOST is that a lot, if not most of side quests require you to go back to the NPC for your reward when you finish the objective... making you walk a long while or even jump to other zones just to get your reward. A lot of times you have to go back to a mission board, and those are seriously ill placed... the one in jack's office in particular is horribly placed... go back to another area, jump, and walk a long way just to deliver a mission? Come on. Seriously, in 2014 do they really need to inflate game time with such gimmicks? It's just annoying and discouraging. I ended up abandoning side quests entirely and focusing on the main story at first because i could not be bothered to travel back and forth anymore. HELL you can't even see all your quest positions on the map at the same time!! meaning when you travel to an area, you can't optimise your route to do all the quests objectives that are nearby... you have to keep switching quests... very stupid way of doing things.  But most of all, progression and the skill system is meh, very bland... i spent 10 minutes staring at the skill tree trying to choose which way i want to go, not because there were too many awesome skills and perks, but because everything felt weak and bland, choice in skills was about choosing what is least boring. 90% of skills are about doing more damage, shooting faster, healing faster, and they don't really change the tactical layer at all... you still just move in, shoot everything, and move on. There are so many games with interesting skill system that it is a shame they did not provide a deeper, more tactically-varied selection of skills. Some skill trees are entirely too tedious and will be skipped altogether... like the melee branch of the gunslinger / sheriff... huge open spaces, slow no gravity movement and jumps and you want me to chase enemies around to melee them? No thanks... Maybe if they had put some kind of teleport / power move / hook ability to close up to enemies, but again they don't seem to have thought deeply about the skill trees.  This is not a bad game, but it ends up being way average instead of truly excelent. It's one of those titles where you play and keep thinking about all the possible improvements they could have done, mostly removing annoying and needlessly time consuming mechanics.  Not a horrible game, just averagely good, and preferably something to grab on a sale. Only buy if you are a true fan of the series and need to play all / know all about it. Not recommended because at full price it is a drag, only grab it on a sale.
